    钦定四库全书　　　　　子部十二
    　　太平广记　　　　　　　小説家二【异闻之属】提要
    　　【臣】等谨案太平广记五百卷宋李昉奉勅监修同修者扈李穆汤悦徐宋白王克贞张洎董淳赵隣陈鄂吕文仲吴淑十二人也以太平兴国三年八月表进六年正月雕板印行凡分五十五部所采书三百四十五种古来轶闻事僻笈遗文咸在焉卷帙轻者往往全部收入盖小説之家渊海也后以言者谓非后学所急收板贮之太清楼故崇文总目不载郑樵号为博洽亦未见其书通志艺文畧中遂谓
    太平广记乃太平御览中别出广记一书専记异事悮矣其书虽多谈神怪而采摭繁富名物典故错出其间词章家恒所取资又如皇览三辅决録三国典畧晋阳秋晋中兴书齐春秋唐歴益部耆旧传汝南先贤传防稽先贤传古文语琴清英世语符子金楼子诸书世所不传者断简残编尚间存其什一尤足贵也此本为明嘉靖中右都御史谈恺所刋卷页间有阙佚无从校补今亦仍之焉乾隆四十四年二月恭校上 [1]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [1]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 钦定四库全书 - Formal title indicating the work was officially compiled by imperial decree.
    • 子部十二 - Classification within the imperial library system, indicating the work belongs to the 'Miscellaneous' category.
    • 小説家二【异闻之属】 - Further classification indicating the work falls under the 'Tales of the Strange' subcategory.
    • 提要 - Summary or preface providing an overview of the work.
    • 奉勅监修 - Indicates the work was commissioned and supervised by imperial order.
    • 同修者 - Refers to the co-editors or collaborators on the project.
    • 表进 - To present a written document to the emperor.
    • 雕板印行 - Refers to the process of carving wooden blocks for printing and then printing the work.
    • 轶闻事 - Refers to unusual or extraordinary events and stories.
    • 僻笈遗文 - Refers to obscure or forgotten texts.
    • 渊海 - Metaphor for a vast and deep body of knowledge.
    • 言者谓 - Refers to someone who said or argued.
    • 非后学所急 - Means 'not urgent for later scholars.'
    • 收板贮之 - To collect and store the printing blocks.
    • 总目不载 - Indicates that the work is not listed in the general catalog.
    • 号为博洽 - Refers to someone known for their extensive knowledge.
    • 未见其书 - Indicates that the person has not seen the book.
    • 艺文略中遂谓 - Refers to the statement made in the 'Art and Literature' section of a catalog or record.
    • 专记异事 - Means 'dedicated to recording extraordinary events.'
    • 虽多谈神怪 - Indicates that the work contains many stories about supernatural phenomena.
    • 采摭繁富 - Means 'richly compiled from many sources.'
    • 名物典故 - Refers to proper nouns, historical events, and cultural references.
    • 词章家恒所取资 - Means 'constantly used as a resource by writers.'
    • 皇览 - A historical text compiled during the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 三辅决录 - A historical record of the Three Auxiliaries (regions around the capital).
    • 三国典略 - A historical text about the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 晋阳秋 - A historical text about the Jin dynasty.
    • 晋中兴书 - A historical text about the revival of the Jin dynasty.
    • 齐春秋 - A historical text about the Qi dynasty.
    • 唐历 - A historical text about the Tang dynasty.
    • 益部耆旧传 - A historical text about the elderly and distinguished people of the Yi region.
    • 汝南先贤传 - A historical text about the distinguished people of the Ruyan region.
    • 防稽先贤传 - A historical text about the distinguished people of the Kuaiji region.
    • 古文语 - Refers to ancient texts or sayings.
    • 琴清英 - A text about music and its refinement.
    • 世语 - A text about the language and customs of the world.
    • 符子 - A text by Fu Zi, a historical figure.
    • 金楼子 - A text by Xiao Tong, a prince of the Liang dynasty.
    • 诸书世所不传者 - Refers to various books that are not widely known or transmitted.
    • 断简残编 - Refers to fragments or incomplete texts.
    • 尚间存其什一 - Means 'still retains a small fraction.'
    • 尤足贵也 - Means 'especially valuable.'
    • 恭校上 - A formal expression indicating that the work was carefully proofread and presented to the emperor.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 钦定四库全书 - A massive imperial library project commissioned by Emperor Qianlong during the Qing dynasty.
    • 子部十二 - The 'Miscellaneous' category in the imperial library system, which includes works on various subjects not fitting into the main categories.
    • 小説家二【异闻之属】 - The 'Tales of the Strange' subcategory, which includes works on supernatural, mythical, and unusual events.
    • 提要 - A common feature in Chinese bibliographies, providing a brief summary of a work.
    • 奉勅监修 - Indicates the high status of the work, as it was commissioned and supervised by the emperor.
    • 同修者 - The co-editors of the work, who were prominent scholars and officials of the time.
    • 表进 - A formal process of presenting a completed work to the emperor.
    • 雕板印行 - The advanced printing technology used during the Song dynasty, which allowed for the mass production of books.
    • 轶闻事 - Reflects the interest in extraordinary and unusual events in Chinese literature.
    • 僻笈遗文 - Highlights the value of preserving and studying obscure and forgotten texts.
    • 渊海 - A metaphor for the vastness and depth of knowledge contained in the work.
    • 言者谓 - Indicates that there were differing opinions or debates about the work.
    • 非后学所急 - Reflects a practical concern about the relevance of the work to later scholars.
    • 收板贮之 - Indicates the physical preservation of the work through the storage of printing blocks.
    • 总目不载 - Suggests that the work may have been overlooked or undervalued in some catalogs.
    • 号为博洽 - Refers to the reputation of scholars for their extensive knowledge.
    • 未见其书 - Indicates that some scholars may not have had access to the work.
    • 艺文略中遂谓 - Refers to the classification and evaluation of the work in a catalog or record.
    • 专记异事 - Highlights the focus of the work on extraordinary events.
    • 虽多谈神怪 - Indicates that the work contains many supernatural stories, which were common in Chinese literature.
    • 采摭繁富 - Reflects the extensive research and compilation involved in the work.
    • 名物典故 - Highlights the importance of cultural and historical references in the work.
    • 词章家恒所取资 - Indicates that the work was a valuable resource for writers.
    • 皇览 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
    • 三辅决录 - A historical record that was a source for the work.
    • 三国典略 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
    • 晋阳秋 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
    • 晋中兴书 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
    • 齐春秋 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
    • 唐历 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
    • 益部耆旧传 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
    • 汝南先贤传 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
    • 防稽先贤传 - A historical text that was a source for the work.
    • 古文语 - Refers to ancient texts that were sources for the work.
    • 琴清英 - A text that was a source for the work.
    • 世语 - A text that was a source for the work.
    • 符子 - A text that was a source for the work.
    • 金楼子 - A text that was a source for the work.
    • 诸书世所不传者 - Refers to books that were rare or obscure sources for the work.
    • 断简残编 - Highlights the value of preserving fragments of texts.
    • 尚间存其什一 - Indicates that even small fragments are valuable.
    • 尤足贵也 - Emphasizes the importance of the work.
    • 恭校上 - Indicates the formal and careful process of proofreading and presenting the work to the emperor.

    The Four Treasuries: A Comprehensive Collection of Chinese Literature
    Volume Twelve: Miscellaneous Works
    Tales of the Strange (Second Category)
    Summary

    Your humble subjects respectfully present the Tales of the Strange (Tai Ping Guang Ji), a work of five hundred volumes compiled under the imperial decree of the Song dynasty. It was supervised by Li Fang, with twelve co-editors: Hu Rui, Li Mu, Tang Yue, Xu Chuo, Song Bai, Wang Kezhen, Zhang Ji, Dong Chun, Zhao Lin, Chen E, Lu Wenzhong, and Wu Shu. In the third month of the third year of the Tai Ping Xing Guo era, the work was presented to the emperor. In the first month of the sixth year, it was carved onto wooden blocks and printed. The text is divided into fifty-five categories, drawing from three hundred and forty-five sources. Rare tales, unusual events, and obscure texts from ancient times are all included. Works of lesser length are often included in their entirety, making this a vast and deep ocean of tales.

    Later, some argued that the work was not urgently needed by later scholars, and the printing blocks were stored in the Tai Qing Tower. As a result, the Chong Wen Zong Mu (General Catalog of the Imperial Library) does not list it. Zheng Qiao, known for his extensive knowledge, also did not see the book. In the Tong Zhi: Art and Literature section, it was mistakenly stated that Tales of the Strange was a separate work from the Tales of the World (Tai Ping Yu Lian), dedicated solely to recording extraordinary events. While the work contains many stories of the supernatural, it is richly compiled from numerous sources, with proper nouns, historical events, and cultural references interspersed throughout. It is a constant resource for writers.

    Additionally, texts such as the Huang Lian (Imperial Records), Three Auxiliaries Decisions, Records of the Three Kingdoms, Jin Yang Qiu (History of the Jin Dynasty), Jin Zhong Xing Shu (History of the Revival of the Jin Dynasty), Qi Chun Qiu (History of the Qi Dynasty), Tang Li (History of the Tang Dynasty), Yi Bu Qi Jiu Zhuan (Biographies of the Elderly and Distinguished of the Yi Region), Ruyan Xian Xian Zhuan (Biographies of the Distinguished of the Ruyan Region), Kuaiji Xian Xian Zhuan (Biographies of the Distinguished of the Kuaiji Region), Ancient Texts and Sayings, Qin Qing Ying (Refinement of Music), World Language and Customs, Fu Zi, Jin Lou Zi, and other works that are not widely transmitted still retain small fragments, which are especially valuable. This edition was carved by Tan Kai, a Right Censor during the Jiajing era of the Ming dynasty. Some pages are missing, and there is no way to correct or supplement them. This edition is preserved as is.

    Presented respectfully for proofreading in the second month of the forty-fourth year of the Qianlong reign. [1]

    总纂官【臣】纪昀【臣】陆锡熊【臣】孙士毅
    　　总　校　官　【臣】　陆　费　墀

    　　钦定四库全书
    　　太平广记卷一
    　　神仙一
    　　老子　　　　木公　　　广成子
    　　黄安　　　　孟岐
    　　老子
    　　老子者名重耳字伯阳楚国苦县曲仁里人也其母感大流星而有娠虽受气天然见于李家犹以李为姓或云老子先天地生或云天之精魄盖神之属或云母懐之七十二年乃生生时剖母左腋而出生而白首故谓之老子或云其母无夫老子是母家之姓或云老子之母适至李树下而生老子生而能言指李树曰以此为我姓或云上三皇时为中法师下三皇时为金阙帝君伏羲时为郁华子神农时为九灵老子祝融时为广夀子黄帝时为广成子颛顼时为赤精子帝喾时为禄图子尧时为务成子舜时为尹夀子夏禹时为真行子殷汤时为锡则子文王时为文邑先生一云 [2]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [2]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 总纂官【臣】纪昀【臣】陆锡熊【臣】孙士毅 - Titles and names of officials responsible for compiling or overseeing a project. '总纂官' means 'Chief Compiler' or 'Chief Editor.' The use of '【臣】' indicates that these individuals are officials addressing their emperor.
    • 总校官【臣】陆费墀 - '总校官' means 'Chief Proofreader' or 'Chief Reviser,' another official title.
    • 钦定四库全书 - '钦定' means 'imperial decree' or 'ordained by the emperor,' and '四库全书' refers to the 'Four Treasuries,' a massive collection of books commissioned by the Qing Dynasty.
    • 神仙一 - '神仙' means 'immortals' or 'deities,' and '一' here likely indicates the first volume or section of a series on immortals.
    • 生而能言 - Literally 'born speaking,' an idiom indicating that the person was exceptionally wise or knowledgeable from birth.
    • 指李树曰以此为我姓 - Literally 'pointed to the plum tree and said this will be my surname,' an idiom suggesting that the person chose their surname based on a sign or event.
    • 上三皇时为玄中法师 - '上三皇' refers to the mythical Three Sovereigns, and '玄中法师' means 'Master of the Mystic Center,' indicating a high-ranking Daoist figure.
    • 下三皇时为金阙帝君 - '下三皇' refers to another set of mythical rulers, and '金阙帝君' means 'Imperial Lord of the Golden Gate,' another high-ranking deity.
    • 伏羲时为郁华子 - '伏羲' is a legendary figure in Chinese mythology, and '郁华子' is a title or epithet for a deity or sage.
    • 神农时为九灵老子 - '神农' is another legendary figure, and '九灵老子' means 'Laozi, the Nine Spirits,' indicating a divine or semi-divine status.
    • 祝融时为广寿子 - '祝融' is a legendary fire deity, and '广寿子' means 'Son of Longevity,' another title for a deity.
    • 黄帝时为广成子 - '黄帝' is the legendary Yellow Emperor, and '广成子' is a title or epithet for a sage or deity.
    • 颛顼时为赤精子 - '颛顼' is a legendary ruler, and '赤精子' means 'Red-Blooded Son,' another title for a deity.
    • 帝喾时为禄图子 - '帝喾' is another legendary ruler, and '禄图子' means 'Son of Fortune,' another title for a deity.
    • 尧时为务成子 - '尧' is the legendary Emperor Yao, and '务成子' is a title or epithet for a sage or deity.
    • 舜时为尹寿子 - '舜' is the legendary Emperor Shun, and '尹寿子' means 'Son of Longevity,' another title for a deity.
    • 夏禹时为真行子 - '夏禹' is the legendary Emperor Yu, and '真行子' means 'True Walker,' another title for a deity.
    • 殷汤时为锡则子 - '殷汤' is the legendary Emperor Tang of Shang, and '锡则子' means 'Son of Granting,' another title for a deity.
    • 文王时为文邑先生 - '文王' is the legendary King Wen of Zhou, and '文邑先生' means 'Mr. of the Literary Village,' another title for a sage or scholar.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 老子 - Laozi is a central figure in Daoism, traditionally credited with writing the *Tao Te Ching*. He is often depicted as a philosopher and sage who achieved immortality.
    • 木公 - '木公' is a title for a deity, possibly related to wood or trees. In Daoist mythology, '木公' is sometimes associated with the God of Wood or the God of Trees.
    • 广成子 - '广成子' is a legendary figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with Daoist teachings. He is said to have lived for thousands of years and taught Laozi.
    • 黄安 - Without further context, it is unclear who '黄安' is, but it could be a historical or mythological figure.
    • 孟岐 - Similarly, '孟岐' is a name, but its significance is unclear without additional information.
    • 三皇 - The 'Three Sovereigns' are mythical rulers in Chinese mythology, often associated with the early stages of Chinese civilization.
    • 伏羲 - '伏羲' is a legendary figure in Chinese mythology, often credited with the invention of writing and the creation of the *I Ching* (Book of Changes).
    • 神农 - '神农' is another legendary figure, often associated with agriculture and medicine.
    • 祝融 - '祝融' is a legendary fire deity, often associated with the south and the element of fire.
    • 黄帝 - '黄帝' is the legendary Yellow Emperor, a central figure in Chinese mythology and the founder of the Huaxia people.
    • 颛顼 - '颛顼' is a legendary ruler, often associated with the north and the element of water.
    • 帝喾 - '帝喾' is another legendary ruler, often associated with the west and the element of metal.
    • 尧 - '尧' is the legendary Emperor Yao, a central figure in Chinese mythology known for his virtue and wisdom.
    • 舜 - '舜' is the legendary Emperor Shun, another central figure in Chinese mythology known for his virtue and wisdom.
    • 夏禹 - '夏禹' is the legendary Emperor Yu, known for his efforts in flood control and the establishment of the Xia Dynasty.
    • 殷汤 - '殷汤' is the legendary Emperor Tang of Shang, known for overthrowing the Xia Dynasty and founding the Shang Dynasty.
    • 文王 - '文王' is the legendary King Wen of Zhou, known for his wisdom and the development of the *I Ching*.

    Proper Nouns

    • 总纂官 - Chief Compiler
    • 陆锡熊 - Lu Xixiong
    • 孙士毅 - Sun Shiyi
    • 总校官 - Chief Proofreader
    • 陆费墀 - Lu Feichi
    • 钦定四库全书 - Imperial Decree Four Treasuries
    • 神仙一 - Immortals Volume One

    Final Translation:

    Chief Compiler, Minister Ji Yun, Minister Lu Xixiong, Minister Sun Shiyi
    Chief Proofreader, Minister Lu Feichi

    Imperially Ordained Four Treasuries
    Taiping Guangji Volume One
    Immortals, Part One
    Laozi
    Wood God
    Guangchengzi
    Huang An
    Meng Qi

    Laozi
    Laozi, whose given name was Zhong'er and courtesy name was Boyang, was born in Qu Ren Village, Ku County, Chu State. His mother conceived him after being struck by a large meteor; though he was naturally endowed, he was born into the Li family and thus took the surname Li. Some say Laozi existed before the creation of heaven and earth, while others claim he is the essence of heaven, belonging to the category of divine beings. Some say his mother carried him for seventy-two years before giving birth, and he emerged from her left armpit, already with white hair, hence the name "Laozi." Others say his mother was unmarried, and Laozi took his mother's family name. Still others say his mother happened to be standing under a plum tree when he was born, and he was able to speak immediately, pointing to the plum tree and saying it would be his surname. Some say in the era of the Upper Three Sovereigns, he was the Master of the Mystic Center; in the era of the Lower Three Sovereigns, he was the Imperial Lord of the Golden Gate; during the reign of Fuxi, he was the Exalted One of Obscure Light; during the reign of Shen Nong, he was the Nine Spirits Laozi; during the reign of Zhurong, he was the Son of Longevity; during the reign of the Yellow Emperor, he was Guangchengzi; during the reign of Emperor Zuanxu, he was the Red-Blooded Son; during the reign of Emperor Ku, he was the Son of Fortune; during the reign of Emperor Yao, he was the True Walker; during the reign of Emperor Shun, he was the Son of Longevity; during the reign of Emperor Yu, he was the True Walker; during the reign of Emperor Tang of Shang, he was the Son of Granting; during the reign of King Wen of Zhou, he was the Mr. of the Literary Village. [2]

    守藏史或云在越为范蠡在齐为鸱夷子在吴为陶朱公皆见于羣书不出神仙正经未可据也稚川云洪以为老子若是天之精神当无世不出俯尊就卑委逸就劳背清澄而入臭浊弃天官而受人爵也夫有天地则有道术道术之士何时暂乏是以伏羲以来至于三代显名道术世世有之何必常是一老子也皆由晚学之徒好竒尚异茍欲推崇老子故有此説其实论之老子盖得道之尤精者非异也按史记云老子之子名宗事魏为将军有功封于段至宗之子注注之子宫宫之孙瑕仕于汉瑕子解为胶西王太傅家于齐则老子本神灵耳浅见道士欲以老子为神异使后代学 [3]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [3]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 守藏史 - A title referring to a historian or keeper of records, often associated with Laozi in Daoist tradition.
    • 未可据也 - Means 'cannot be relied upon' or 'is not credible.'
    • 伏羲以来 - Means 'since the time of Fuxi,' referring to the legendary first ruler of China.
    • 其实论之 - Means 'in fact, when discussing it.'
    • 得道之尤精者 - Means 'one who has attained the Dao with particular excellence.'
    • 非异类也 - Means 'not a different kind of being.'
    • 按史记 - Means 'according to the Records of the Grand Historian.'
    • 之子 - Means 'his son.'
    • 有功 - Means 'had merit or achieved success.'
    • 之孙 - Means 'his grandson.'
    • 仕于汉 - Means 'served the Han dynasty.'
    • 本神灵耳 - Means 'was originally a divine being.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 范蠡 - A historical figure known for his role in the state of Yue and his later life as a merchant.
    • 鸱夷子 - Another name for Fan Li, possibly referring to his life as a merchant.
    • 陶朱公 - A title given to Fan Li after his success as a merchant.
    • 羣书 - Refers to a variety of books or texts.
    • 神仙正经 - Canonical texts on immortals and the Daoist pursuit of immortality.
    • 稚川云 - Ge Hong, a famous Daoist alchemist and writer.
    • 洪以为 - Ge Hong's belief.
    • 天之精神 - The spiritual essence of heaven.
    • 三代 - The Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties.
    • 晚学之徒 - Later scholars or students.
    • 好竒尚异 - Love for the strange and unusual.
    • 茍欲推崇老子 - Desire to exalt Laozi.
    • 其实论之 - In fact, when discussing it.
    • 得道之尤精者 - One who has attained the Dao with particular excellence.
    • 非异类也 - Not a different kind of being.
    • 按史记 - According to the Records of the Grand Historian.
    • 之子 - His son.
    • 事魏 - Served the state of Wei.
    • 为将军 - Was a general.
    • 有功 - Had merit or achieved success.
    • 封于段 - Was enfeoffed in the region of Duan.
    • 之孙 - His grandson.
    • 仕于汉 - Served the Han dynasty.
    • 解为胶西王太傅 - Xie served as the Grand Tutor to the King of Jiaoxi.
    • 家于齐 - Settled in the state of Qi.
    • 本神灵耳 - Was originally a divine being.
    • 浅见道士 - Superficial or unenlightened Daoists.
    • 欲以老子为神异 - Wanted to make Laozi into a divine and extraordinary figure.
    • 使后代学 - To make later generations learn.

    Proper Nouns

    • 守藏史 - A title referring to a historian or keeper of records.
    • 范蠡 - A historical figure known for his role in the state of Yue and his later life as a merchant.
    • 鸱夷子 - Another name for Fan Li, possibly referring to his life as a merchant.
    • 陶朱公 - A title given to Fan Li after his success as a merchant.
    • 羣书 - A variety of books or texts.
    • 神仙正经 - Canonical texts on immortals and the Daoist pursuit of immortality.
    • 稚川 - Ge Hong, a famous Daoist alchemist and writer.
    • 洪 - Ge Hong.
    • 天之精神 - The spiritual essence of heaven.
    • 伏羲 - The legendary first ruler of China.
    • 三代 - The Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties.
    • 晚学之徒 - Later scholars or students.
    • 好竒尚异 - Love for the strange and unusual.
    • 茍欲推崇老子 - Desire to exalt Laozi.
    • 其实论之 - In fact, when discussing it.
    • 得道之尤精者 - One who has attained the Dao with particular excellence.
    • 非异类也 - Not a different kind of being.
    • 按史记 - According to the Records of the Grand Historian.
    • 之子 - His son.
    • 有功 - Had merit or achieved success.
    • 之孙 - His grandson.
    • 仕于汉 - Served the Han dynasty.
    • 本神灵耳 - Was originally a divine being.

    The Keeper of Records is sometimes said to have been Fan Li in Yue, Yi Zi in Qi, and Tao Zhu Gong in Wu, all of which are mentioned in various books but not in the canonical texts on immortals, so they cannot be relied upon. Zhi Chuan says that Hong believed that if Laozi were the spiritual essence of heaven, he would not have humbled himself to serve in lowly positions, given up ease for labor, turned away from purity and clarity to enter filth and turbidity, or abandoned his heavenly office to accept human rank. From the time of heaven and earth, there have always been practitioners of Daoist arts and techniques, and there have been such people generation after generation since the time of Fuxi, through the Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties, so why must it always be one Laozi? All of these names and titles for Laozi are due to later scholars' desire to exalt him, but in fact, when discussing it, Laozi is one who has attained the Dao with particular excellence and is not a different kind of being. According to the Records of the Grand Historian, Laozi's son was named Zong, who served the state of Wei as a general and was enfeoffed in the region of Duan. Zong's son was Zhu, who served the Han dynasty. Zhu's grandson was Xie, who served as the Grand Tutor to the King of Jiaoxi and settled in Qi. Therefore, Laozi was originally a divine being, but superficial Daoists wanted to make him into a divine and extraordinary figure for later generations to learn from. [3]

    者从之而不知此更使不信长生之可学也何者若谓老子是得道者则人必勉力竞慕若谓是神异则非可学也或云老子欲西度关关令尹喜知其非常人也从之问道老子惊怪故吐舌聃然遂有老聃之号亦不然也今按九变及元生十二化经老子未入关时固已名聃矣老子数易名字非但一聃而已所以尔者按九宫及三五经及元辰经云人生各有厄防到其时若易名字以随元气之变则可以延年度厄今世有道者亦多如此老子在周乃三百余年二百年之中必有厄防非一是以名稍多耳欲正定老子本末故当以史书实録为主并考仙经秘文以相叅审其他若俗説多 [4]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [4]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 者从之而不知此更使不信长生之可学也 - If one follows someone without understanding the deeper meaning, it leads to disbelief in the possibility of learning immortality.
    • 若谓老子是得道者则人必勉力竞慕 - If one says that Laozi is a person who has attained the Dao, then people will surely strive and compete to emulate him.
    • 若谓是神异则非可学也 - If one says that he is a divine being or something extraordinary, then it is not something that can be learned.
    • 或云老子欲西度关关令尹喜知其非常人也从之问道老子惊怪故吐舌聃然遂有老聃之号亦不然也 - Some say Laozi wanted to cross the western pass, and the magistrate of the pass, Yin Xi, knew that he was not an ordinary person and followed him to ask questions. Laozi was surprised and amazed, so he stuck out his tongue, which became the reason for the name 'Lao Dan.' However, this is not necessarily true.
    • 今按九变及元生十二化经老子未入关时固已名聃矣老子数易名字非但一聃而已所以尔者按九宫及三五经及元辰经云人生各有厄防到其时若易名字以随元气之变则可以延年度厄今世有道者亦多如此老子在周乃三百余年二百年之中必有厄防非一是以名稍多耳欲正定老子本末故当以史书实録为主并考仙经秘文以相叅审其他若俗説多 - According to the *Nine Transformations* and *Twelve Metamorphoses* texts, Laozi had already been named 'Dān' before entering the pass, and he changed his name multiple times, not just once. The reason for this is explained by the *Nine Palaces* and *Three and Five Cycles* texts, which suggest that people have different fates and must change their names to follow the changes in the primordial energy to extend their lifespan and overcome obstacles. Many contemporary sages also do this. Laozi lived in the Zhou dynasty for more than three hundred years, and within two hundred years, he must have faced many obstacles, hence the multiple name changes. To accurately determine the origin and end of Laozi, one should primarily rely on historical records and also examine esoteric texts to verify other popular sayings.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 老子 - Laozi, the legendary founder of Daoism.
    • 尹喜 - The magistrate of the western pass who is said to have encountered Laozi.
    • 九变及元生十二化经 - Esoteric Daoist texts that discuss transformation and metamorphosis.
    • 九宫及三五经及元辰经 - Other Daoist texts that discuss the nine palaces, the three and five cycles, and the annual celestial texts, which are part of Daoist cosmology and fate.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 者从之而不知此更使不信长生之可学也 - This phrase is elliptical and requires careful parsing to understand the relationship between following someone and disbelief in the possibility of learning immortality.
    • 或云老子欲西度关关令尹喜知其非常人也从之问道老子惊怪故吐舌聃然遂有老聃之号亦不然也 - This sentence is complex and requires breaking down into smaller, more manageable parts to understand the sequence of events and the reasons behind them.
    • 今按九变及元生十二化经老子未入关时固已名聃矣老子数易名字非但一聃而已所以尔者按九宫及三五经及元辰经云人生各有厄防到其时若易名字以随元气之变则可以延年度厄今世有道者亦多如此老子在周乃三百余年二百年之中必有厄防非一是以名稍多耳欲正定老子本末故当以史书实録为主并考仙经秘文以相叅审其他若俗説多 - This is a very long and complex sentence that requires careful parsing to understand the logical flow and the reasons given for Laozi's multiple name changes.

    Proper Nouns

    • 老子 - Laozi, the legendary founder of Daoism.
    • 尹喜 - The magistrate of the western pass.
    • 九变及元生十二化经 - Esoteric Daoist texts.
    • 九宫及三五经及元辰经 - Other Daoist texts.

    If one follows someone without understanding the deeper meaning, it leads to disbelief in the possibility of learning immortality. Why is this so? If one says that Laozi is a person who has attained the Dao, then people will surely strive and compete to emulate him. If one says that he is a divine being or something extraordinary, then it is not something that can be learned. Some say that Laozi wanted to cross the western pass, and the magistrate of the pass, Yin Xi, knew that he was not an ordinary person and followed him to ask questions. Laozi was surprised and amazed, so he stuck out his tongue, which became the reason for the name "Lao Dan." However, this is not necessarily true. According to the Nine Transformations and Twelve Metamorphoses texts, Laozi had already been named "Dān" before entering the pass, and he changed his name multiple times, not just once. The reason for this is explained by the Nine Palaces and Three and Five Cycles texts, which suggest that people have different fates and must change their names to follow the changes in the primordial energy to extend their lifespan and overcome obstacles. Many contemporary sages also do this. Laozi lived in the Zhou dynasty for more than three hundred years, and within two hundred years, he must have faced many obstacles, hence the multiple name changes. To accurately determine the origin and end of Laozi, one should primarily rely on historical records and also examine esoteric texts to verify other popular sayings. [4]

    虗妄洪按西升中胎及复命苞及珠韬玉机金篇内经皆云老子黄白色美眉广颡长耳大目疎齿方口厚唇额有三五达理日角月悬鼻纯骨防柱耳有三漏门足蹈二五手把十文以周文王时为守藏史至武王时为柱下史时俗见其乆夀故号之为老子夫人受命自有通神逺见者禀气与常人不同应为道主故能为天神所济众仙所从是以所出度世之法九丹八石金醴金液次存素守一思神歴藏行气錬形消灾辟恶治飬性絶谷变化厌胜教戒役使魅之法凡九百三十卷符书七十卷皆老子本起中篇所记者也自有目録其不在此数者皆后之道士私所増益非真文也老 [5]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [5]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 虗妄洪按 - Title or heading, possibly indicating the author or compiler of the text.
    • 西升中胎及复命苞及珠韬玉机金篇内经 - List of texts attributed to Laozi, including *Xisheng Zhongtai* (The Inner Chapters of the Westward Ascension), *Fuming Bao* (The Secret of Rebirth), *Zhutao Yujī* (The Jade Mechanism of the Pearl), and *Jinpiannèijing* (The Inner Canon of the Golden Chapter).
    • 黄白色美眉广颡长耳大目疎齿方口厚唇额有三五达理日角月悬鼻纯骨防柱耳有三漏门足蹈二五手把十文 - Detailed physical description of Laozi, including his appearance and physical features.
    • 周文王时为守藏史至武王时为柱下史 - Historical references to Laozi's roles during the reigns of King Wen and King Wu of Zhou.
    • 时俗见其乆夀故号之为老子 - Indicates that Laozi was given his name due to his long life.
    • 夫人受命自有通神逺见者禀气与常人不同应为道主故能为天神所济众仙所从 - Describes Laozi's spiritual qualities and his role as a spiritual leader.
    • 所出度世之法九丹八石金醴金液次存素守一思神歴藏行气錬形消灾辟恶治飬性絶谷变化厌胜教戒役使魅之法凡九百三十卷符书七十卷 - Lists various methods and texts attributed to Laozi, including alchemical practices, spiritual exercises, and magical rituals.
    • 皆老子本起中篇所记者也自有目録其不在此数者皆后之道士私所増益非真文也 - Indicates that these texts are part of a larger collection and that some may be later additions.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周文王 - King Wen of Zhou, a legendary ruler of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 武王 - King Wu of Zhou, the son of King Wen and a key figure in the Zhou conquest of the Shang dynasty.
    • 老子 - Laozi, the legendary founder of Daoism and the author of the *Daodejing*.
    • 九丹八石金醴金液 - Refers to alchemical practices and substances used in Daoist immortality techniques.
    • 素守一思神歴藏行气錬形消灾辟恶治飬性絶谷变化厌胜教戒役使魅之法 - Various Daoist practices and techniques, including meditation, alchemy, and magical rituals.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The long sentence providing a detailed description of Laozi and his roles during the Zhou dynasty needs to be broken down into more manageable segments in English.
    • The sentence describing Laozi's spiritual qualities and his role as a spiritual leader needs to be parsed to clarify the relationships and attributes mentioned.
    • The sentence listing various methods and texts attributed to Laozi needs to be broken down to ensure each method and text is clearly identified.

    Proper Nouns

    • 虗妄洪 - Likely the name of the author or compiler of the text.
    • 西升中胎 - *Xisheng Zhongtai* (The Inner Chapters of the Westward Ascension).
    • 复命苞 - *Fuming Bao* (The Secret of Rebirth).
    • 珠韬玉机金篇内经 - *Zhutao Yujī Jinpiannèijing* (The Jade Mechanism of the Pearl and the Inner Canon of the Golden Chapter).
    • 周文王 - King Wen of Zhou.
    • 武王 - King Wu of Zhou.
    • 老子 - Laozi.
    • 九丹八石金醴金液 - Alchemical substances and practices.
    • 素守一思神歴藏行气錬形消灾辟恶治飬性絶谷变化厌胜教戒役使魅之法 - Various Daoist practices and techniques.
    • 九百三十卷符书七十卷 - Numbers of texts and volumes attributed to Laozi.

    According to the Xisheng Zhongtai, Fuming Bao, Zhutao Yujī, and Jinpiannèijing, Laozi was described as having a yellowish-white complexion, beautiful eyebrows, a broad forehead, long ears, large eyes, sparse teeth, a square mouth, and thick lips. His forehead had three and five reaches of wisdom, his nose was pure and strong, his ears had three leaks, his feet touched the two and five, and his hands held the ten texts. He served as the Keeper of the Archives during the reign of King Wen of Zhou and as the Attendant in the Hall during the reign of King Wu of Zhou. People of his time admired his long life and thus called him Laozi. Those who receive their mandate inherently possess spiritual insight and foresight, with a different constitution from ordinary people, and should be the master of the Dao. Therefore, they can be aided by heavenly deities and followed by many immortals. Thus, the methods for transcending the world that he taught include the nine elixirs, eight stones, golden liquor, and golden fluid, followed by the preservation of the primordial essence, the unity of the One, the contemplation of the spirit, the traversal of the organs, the cultivation of qi, the refinement of the body, the elimination of disasters, the warding off of evil, the treatment of spirits, the cultivation of nature, the abstaining from food, transformation,厌胜, teaching, and the control of spirits. There are a total of 930 scrolls and 70 volumes of talismanic texts, all of which are recorded in the central chapters of the Laozi as the original texts. There is also a catalog of these texts. Any texts not included in this number are later additions by Daoist priests and are not genuine texts. [5]

    子恬淡无欲専以长生为务者故在周虽乆而名位不者盖欲和光同尘内实自然道成乃去盖仙人也孔子常往问礼先使子贡观焉子贡至老子告之曰子之师名丘相从三年而后可教焉孔子既见老子老子告之曰良贾深藏若虚君子盛徳若愚去子之骄气与多欲淫志是皆无益于子也孔子读书老子见而问之曰何书曰易也圣人亦读之老子曰圣人读之可也汝曷为读之其要何説孔子曰要在仁义老子曰蚊虻肤通夕不得眠今仁义惨然而汨人心乱莫大焉夫鹄不日浴而白乌不日染而黒天之自髙矣地之自厚矣日月自明矣星辰固自列矣草木固有区矣夫子修道 [6]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [6]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 子恬淡无欲专以长生为务者 - Describes a person who is calm, free from desires, and focuses solely on achieving immortality.
    • 和光同尘 - A metaphorical expression meaning to blend in with the world without standing out, often used to describe a person who lives in harmony with nature and society.
    • 内实自然 - Means 'internally, it is natural,' suggesting a state of being that aligns with the natural order.
    • 道成乃去 - Means 'once the Dao is achieved, one departs,' indicating that the person leaves the world after attaining spiritual perfection.
    • 君子盛德若愚 - A Confucian phrase meaning 'a noble person's great virtue appears as if they were foolish,' emphasizing humility and modesty.
    • 骄气与多欲淫志 - Refers to arrogance, excessive desires, and wild ambitions, which are seen as detrimental to one's spiritual development.
    • 蚊虻肤通夕不得眠 - A metaphorical expression comparing the discomfort caused by excessive moralism (仁义) to the irritation of insects biting the skin all night.
    • 夫鹄不日浴而白 - A metaphor comparing the inherent purity of a swan to the natural state of a person who follows the Dao.
    • 乌不日染而黑 - Another metaphor comparing the inherent darkness of a crow to the natural state of a person who follows the Dao.
    • 天之自高矣 - Means 'Heaven naturally rises high,' symbolizing the inherent order and structure of the universe.
    • 地之自厚矣 - Means 'Earth naturally becomes thick,' symbolizing the inherent stability and depth of the earth.
    • 日月自明矣 - Means 'the sun and moon naturally shine brightly,' symbolizing the inherent clarity and illumination of the cosmos.
    • 星辰固自列矣 - Means 'stars and constellations naturally align themselves,' symbolizing the inherent order and harmony of the celestial bodies.
    • 草木固有区矣 - Means 'plants and trees naturally have their own places,' symbolizing the inherent diversity and organization of nature.
    • 夫子修道 - Means 'Master, you cultivate the Dao,' referring to Confucius' pursuit of the Dao.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 子恬淡无欲专以长生为务者 - Refers to a person who practices Daoism and seeks immortality.
    • 和光同尘 - A Daoist concept emphasizing the importance of blending in with the world without standing out.
    • 内实自然 - A Daoist idea that true virtue is natural and spontaneous.
    • 道成乃去 - A Daoist belief that once one achieves the Dao, they leave the world to join the immortals.
    • 君子盛德若愚 - A Confucian concept emphasizing the importance of humility and modesty in a noble person.
    • 仁义 - A central concept in Confucianism, referring to benevolence and righteousness.
    • 蚊虻肤通夕不得眠 - A metaphorical expression comparing the discomfort of excessive moralism to the irritation of insects.
    • 夫鹄不日浴而白 - A metaphor comparing the inherent purity of a swan to the natural state of a person who follows the Dao.
    • 乌不日染而黑 - Another metaphor comparing the inherent darkness of a crow to the natural state of a person who follows the Dao.
    • 天之自高矣 - A metaphor for the inherent order and structure of the universe.
    • 地之自厚矣 - A metaphor for the inherent stability and depth of the earth.
    • 日月自明矣 - A metaphor for the inherent clarity and illumination of the cosmos.
    • 星辰固自列矣 - A metaphor for the inherent order and harmony of the celestial bodies.
    • 草木固有区矣 - A metaphor for the inherent diversity and organization of nature.
    • 夫子修道 - Refers to Confucius' pursuit of the Dao, a concept that bridges Confucian and Daoist thought.

    Proper Nouns

    • 子 - Refers to a person, likely a Daoist practitioner.
    • 孔子 - Confucius, a famous Chinese philosopher and teacher.
    • 子贡 - Zizhang, one of Confucius' disciples.
    • 老子 - Laozi, a famous Chinese philosopher and the founder of Daoism.
    • 丘 - The given name of Confucius.
    • 易 - The *Yijing* (Book of Changes), one of the Five Classics of Confucianism.

    A person who is calm, free from desires, and focuses solely on achieving immortality, thus in Zhou despite a long time without gaining fame or position, likely desires to blend in with the world and live in harmony with it, internally being natural, and once the Dao is achieved, departs, is an immortal. Confucius often went to ask about rituals, first sending his disciple Zizhang to observe. When Zizhang arrived, Laozi told him, "Your teacher's name is Qiu, and after three years of following him, you can be taught." After Confucius met Laozi, Laozi told him, "A good merchant hides their wealth, a noble person appears foolish, remove your arrogance, excessive desires, and wild ambitions, as all these are detrimental to you." Confucius was reading, and Laozi saw him and asked, "What are you reading?" Confucius replied, "The Yijing." Laozi said, "Even sages read it, but why do you read it? What is its essence?" Confucius said, "Its essence lies in benevolence and righteousness." Laozi replied, "The discomfort of insects biting the skin all night is unbearable. Now, benevolence and righteousness cause distress and confusion, which is the greatest disorder. The swan is naturally white, the crow is naturally black, Heaven naturally rises high, Earth naturally becomes thick, the sun and moon naturally shine brightly, stars and constellations naturally align themselves, plants and trees naturally have their own places. Master, you cultivate the Dao." [6]

    而趋则以至矣又何用仁义若击皷以求亡羊乎夫子乃乱人之性也老子问孔子曰亦得道乎孔子曰求二十七年而不得也老子曰使道可献人则人莫不献之其君使道而可进人则人莫不进之其亲矣使道可告人则人莫不告之兄弟矣使道可传人则人莫不传之其子矣然而不可者无他也中无主而道不可居也孔子曰丘治诗书礼乐易春秋诵先王之道明周召之迹以干七十余君而不见用甚矣人之难説也老子曰夫六艺先王之陈迹也岂其所陈哉今子所修者皆因陈迹也迹者履之出而迹岂异哉孔子归三日不谈子贡怪而问之孔子曰吾见人之用意如飞鸟者吾饰意 [7]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [7]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 而趋则以至矣 - Contrast with the following rhetorical question, indicating that rushing does not necessarily lead to success.
    • 若击皷以求亡羊乎 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'like striking drums to find a lost sheep,' criticizing the futility of using force or excessive effort to achieve a goal.
    • 夫子乃乱人之性也 - Criticism of imposing external rules or methods on human nature.
    • 使道可献人则人莫不献之其君 - Conditional statement emphasizing the universal appeal of the Way.
    • 使道可进人则人莫不进之其亲矣 - Conditional statement reinforcing the idea of the Way's natural and familial appeal.
    • 使道可告人则人莫不告之兄弟矣 - Conditional statement extending the idea to familial relationships.
    • 使道可传人则人莫不传之其子矣 - Conditional statement emphasizing the generational transmission of the Way.
    • 然而不可者无他也 - Explanation that the reason the Way cannot be imposed is not because of anything else.
    • 中无主而道不可居也 - Explanation that the Way cannot reside in people because there is no inner master.
    • 丘治诗书礼乐易春秋 - Confucius's study of the Six Classics: *Poetry*, *Book of Documents*, *Rites*, *Music*, *Changes*, and *Spring and Autumn Annals*.
    • 诵先王之道明周召之迹 - Reciting the ways of the ancient kings and clarifying the deeds of Duke Zhou and Duke Shao.
    • 以干七十余君而不见用 - Confucius's efforts to seek service from more than seventy rulers but not being employed.
    • 甚矣人之难说也 - Emphasis on the difficulty of persuading people.
    • 夫六艺先王之陈迹也 - The Six Arts are the traces left by the ancient kings.
    • 岂其所陈哉 - Questioning the value of these records.
    • 今子所修者皆因陈迹也 - Criticism of relying on historical texts.
    • 迹者履之出而迹岂异哉 - Questioning the distinction between the traces and the original actions.
    • 孔子归三日不谈 - Confucius returned home and did not speak for three days, indicating deep reflection.
    • 子贡怪而问之 - Zigong found this strange and asked him, showing curiosity and concern.
    • 吾饰意 - Confucius's effort to refine his intentions.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 夫子 - Refers to Confucius, the founder of Confucianism.
    • 老子 - Refers to Laozi, the founder of Daoism.
    • 诗书礼乐易春秋 - The Six Classics of Confucianism: *Poetry*, *Book of Documents*, *Rites*, *Music*, *Changes*, and *Spring and Autumn Annals*.
    • 先王之道 - Refers to the ways of the ancient kings, emphasizing moral and political wisdom.
    • 周召之迹 - Refers to the deeds of Duke Zhou and Duke Shao, two prominent figures in early Chinese history.
    • 六艺 - Refers to the Six Arts, which include the Six Classics and other skills such as archery and charioteering.
    • 子贡 - Refers to Zigong, one of Confucius's disciples.
    • 饰意 - Refers to refining one's intentions or thoughts, a concept important in both Confucian and Daoist thought.

    Proper Nouns

    • 夫子 - Confucius.
    • 老子 - Laozi.
    • 诗书礼乐易春秋 - The Six Classics: *Shijing*, *Shujing*, *Liji*, *Yuejing*, *Yijing*, *Chunqiu*.
    • 先王之道 - The ways of the ancient kings.
    • 周召之迹 - The deeds of Duke Zhou and Duke Shao.
    • 六艺 - The Six Arts.
    • 子贡 - Zigong.
    • 饰意 - Refining one's intentions.

    If one rushes, one can reach it, but what use is benevolence and righteousness? Is it like striking drums to find a lost sheep? Master, you are indeed disordering people's nature. Laozi asked Confucius, "Have you attained the Way?" Confucius replied, "I have sought it for twenty-seven years and have not attained it." Laozi said, "If the Way could be offered to people, no one would fail to offer it to their ruler; if the Way could be presented to people, no one would fail to present it to their parents; if the Way could be told to people, no one would fail to tell it to their brothers; if the Way could be passed on to people, no one would fail to pass it on to their children. But the reason it cannot be done is not because of anything else. Because there is no inner master, the Way cannot reside in them." Confucius said, "I have studied the Poetry, Book of Documents, Rites, Music, Changes, and Spring and Autumn Annals, reciting the ways of the ancient kings and clarifying the deeds of Duke Zhou and Duke Shao, seeking service from more than seventy rulers but have not been employed. How difficult it is to persuade people!" Laozi said, "The Six Arts are the traces left by the ancient kings. Are they what they have left behind? Now what you are studying are all based on these traces. The traces are the result of footsteps, and are they different?" Confucius returned home and did not speak for three days. Zigong found this strange and asked him. Confucius said, "I have refined my intentions." [7]

    以为弓弩射之未尝不及而加之也人之用意如麋鹿者吾饰意以为走狗而逐之未尝不衔而顿之也人之用意如渊鱼者吾饰意以为钩缗而投之未尝不钓而制之也至于龙乗云气游太清吾不能逐也今见老子其犹龙乎使吾口张而不能翕舌出而不能缩神错而不知其所居也阳子见于老子老子告之曰虎豹之文猿猱之防所以致射也阳子曰敢问明王之治老子曰明王之治功盖天下而以不自已化被万物而使民不恃其有徳而不称其名位乎不测而游乎无有者也老子将去而西出关以升昆仑关令尹喜占风气逆知当有神人来过乃扫道四十里见老子而知是也老子 [8]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [8]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 以為弓弩射之未嘗不及而加之也 - Treats someone's intentions as a target for an arrow or crossbow, emphasizing targeting and hitting.
    • 人之用意如麋鹿者 - Describes someone whose intentions are like deer, suggesting evasiveness or elusiveness.
    • 吾飾意以為走狗而逐之 - Crafts one's intent to be like a hunting dog, emphasizing pursuit and tracking.
    • 人之用意如渊鱼者 - Describes someone whose intentions are like fish in deep water, suggesting hidden or concealed intentions.
    • 吾飾意以為钩缗而投之 - Crafts one's intent to be like a fishing hook and line, emphasizing entrapment or capture.
    • 至於龙乗云气游太清 - Describes the dragon's ability to ride the clouds and ascend to the highest level of heaven, emphasizing transcendence and power.
    • 吾不能逐也 - Indicates the inability to pursue or catch the dragon, emphasizing the dragon's elusiveness.
    • 今见老子其犹龙乎 - Asks if Laozi is like a dragon, emphasizing his mysterious and powerful nature.
    • 使吾口张而不能翕舌出而不能缩神错而不知其所居也 - Describes a state of confusion and disorientation, emphasizing the impact of encountering Laozi.
    • 虎豹之文猿猱之防所以致射也 - Explains why one's appearance can attract harm, emphasizing the importance of caution and subtlety.
    • 功盖天下而以不自已 - Describes someone who has achieved great success but does not stop, emphasizing humility and continuous effort.
    • 化被万物而使民不恃其有德 - Describes the transformation of all things and the non-reliance on one's own virtue, emphasizing the natural and effortless nature of true virtue.
    • 不称其名位乎不测而游乎无有者也 - Describes someone whose actions are beyond their name and position, wandering without measure in the boundless, emphasizing the transcendent nature of true wisdom.
    • 老子将去而西出关以升昆仑 - Describes Laozi's departure and ascent to Mount Kunlun, emphasizing his transcendence and departure from the mundane world.
    • 关令尹喜占风气逆知当有神人来过乃扫道四十里见老子而知是也 - Describes the governor of the pass sensing the wind and foreseeing the arrival of a divine person, emphasizing the mystical and prophetic nature of the encounter.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 老子 - Refers to Laozi, the legendary founder of Daoism and author of the *Daodejing*.
    • 弓弩 - Refers to bows and crossbows, traditional weapons in ancient China.
    • 走狗 - Refers to hunting dogs, often used metaphorically to describe someone who is loyal and follows orders.
    • 钩缗 - Refers to fishing hooks and lines, symbolizing entrapment or capture.
    • 龙 - A mythical creature symbolizing power, transcendence, and the Dao.
    • 太清 - Refers to the highest level of heaven, a concept in Daoist cosmology.
    • 虎豹之文 - Refers to the patterns on tigers and leopards, symbolizing danger and beauty.
    • 猿猱之防 - Refers to the agility of apes, symbolizing quickness and cunning.
    • 昆仑 - Refers to Mount Kunlun, a mythical mountain in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortality and the Dao.
    • 关令尹喜 - Refers to the governor of the pass, a historical figure who is said to have met Laozi and recorded his teachings.

    Proper Nouns

    • 老子 - Laozi, the legendary founder of Daoism and author of the *Daodejing*.
    • 关令尹喜 - The governor of the pass, a historical figure who is said to have met Laozi and recorded his teachings.

    Treating someone's intentions as a target for an arrow or crossbow, one never fails to hit and add to them. Those whose intentions are like deer, I craft my intent to be like a hunting dog and pursue them, and never fail to catch and subdue them. Those whose intentions are like fish in deep water, I craft my intent to be like a fishing hook and line and cast it, and never fail to catch and control them. As for the dragon riding the clouds and ascending to the highest level of heaven, I cannot pursue it. Now, seeing Laozi, is he like a dragon? His appearance causes my mouth to open and not close, my tongue to protrude and not retract, my spirit to be confused and not know where it resides. Yangzi met Laozi, and Laozi told him, "The patterns on tigers and leopards, the agility of apes, these are what attract harm." Yangzi said, "I dare to ask about the governance of a sage king." Laozi replied, "The governance of a sage king surpasses the world in achievements but does not stop. It transforms all things and makes the people not rely on one's own virtue, not be commensurate with one's name and position, and wander without measure in the boundless." Laozi was about to leave and ascend west through the pass to Mount Kunlun. The governor of the pass, Yin Xi, sensed the wind and foresaw the arrival of a divine person, and swept the road for forty li. Seeing Laozi, he knew it was him. [8]

    在中国都未有所授知喜命应得道乃停关中老子有客徐甲少赁于老子约日雇百钱计欠甲七百二十万钱甲见老子出关游行速索偿不可得乃倩人作辞诣关令以言老子而为作辞者亦不知甲巳随老子二百余年矣唯计甲所应得之多许以女嫁甲甲见女美尤喜遂通辞于尹喜得辞大惊乃见老子老子问甲曰汝乆应死吾昔赁汝为官卑家贫无有使役故以太清生符与汝所以至今日汝何以言吾吾语汝到安息国固当以黄金计直还汝汝何以不能忍乃使甲张口向地其太真符立出于地丹书文字如新甲成一聚枯骨矣喜知老子神人能复使甲生乃为甲叩头请 [9]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [9]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 未有所授知 - Not yet taught or known, suggesting a state of ignorance or lack of instruction.
    • 喜命应得道 - Happy to receive the command and attain the Way, where '得道' means to attain enlightenment or the Dao.
    • 停关中 - Stay in the Guanzhong region, referring to a specific geographical area.
    • 太玄清生符 - The Great Mystery Clear Life Talisman, a mystical or spiritual object.
    • 倩人作辞 - To ask someone to write a petition, where '倩' means to entrust or ask for help.
    • 张口向地 - Open mouth to the ground, a metaphor for death or being reduced to nothing.
    • 叩头请 - Kneel and plead, a formal gesture of respect and request.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 老子 - Refers to Laozi, the legendary founder of Daoism and author of the *Tao Te Ching*.
    • 尹喜 - Refers to Yin Xi, a historical figure who is said to have encountered Laozi and recorded the *Tao Te Ching*.
    • 关令 - Refers to the official in charge of the pass, a common setting in Daoist legends.
    • 安息国 - Refers to the Parthian Empire, an ancient Iranian civilization.

    Proper Nouns

    • 老子 - Laozi
    • 尹喜 - Yin Xi
    • 关令 - Pass Official
    • 太玄清生符 - The Great Mystery Clear Life Talisman
    • 安息国 - Parthian Empire
    • 徐甲 - Xu Jia

    In China, Laozi had not yet been taught or known to attain the Way, and he was happy to receive the command and stay in the Guanzhong region. Laozi had a servant named Xu Jia, who had been leased to Laozi for a daily wage of one hundred coins. Laozi owed Jia seven hundred and twenty thousand coins. Seeing that Laozi was leaving the country, Jia quickly demanded repayment, but Laozi could not pay. Jia then asked someone to write a petition to the pass official to speak to Laozi, and the person who wrote the petition did not know that Jia had followed Laozi for over two hundred years. Upon receiving the petition, Yin Xi was greatly surprised and saw Laozi. Laozi asked Jia, "You should have died long ago. I once leased you as a lowly servant, and because you were poor and had no servants, I gave you the Great Mystery Clear Life Talisman, which is why you have lived until now. Why do you speak of me? I will tell you: when you reach the country of Anxi, you will be paid in gold according to the value. How could you not endure it?" Jia opened his mouth to the ground, and the Great Mystery Clear Life Talisman immediately appeared from the ground, with red inscriptions as new as ever. Jia became a pile of dry bones. Yin Xi knew that Laozi was a divine being who could revive Jia, so he knelt and pleaded. [9]

    命乞为老子出钱还之老子复以太符投之甲立更生喜即以钱二百万与甲遣之而去并执弟子之礼具以长生之事授喜喜又请教诫老子语之五千言喜退而书之名曰道徳经焉尹喜行其道亦得仙汉窦太后信老子之言孝文帝及外戚诸窦皆不得不读读之皆大得其益故文景之世天下谧然而窦氏三世保其荣宠太子太傅疏广父子深逹其意知功成身退之义同日弃官而归散金布惠保其清贵及诸隐士其遵老子之术者皆外损荣华内飬生夀无有颠沛于险世其洪源长流所润洋洋如此岂非乾坤所定万世之师表哉故庄周之徒莫不以老子为宗也【出神仙传】 [10]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [10]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 命乞为老子出钱还之 - Elliptical phrase meaning 'ordered to beg and pay money to the old man and return it.'
    • 太符 - Refers to a talisman or symbol associated with the 'Tài Xuán' (太玄), a work by Yang Xiong, a Han dynasty scholar.
    • 甲立更生 - Ambiguous phrase; '甲' could refer to a person named Jia or a general term for someone, and '立更生' means 'immediately revived' or 'instantly reborn.'
    • 执弟子之礼 - Means 'to follow the etiquette of a disciple,' indicating a formal relationship of respect and learning.
    • 长生之事 - Refers to the pursuit of longevity or immortality.
    • 五千言 - Refers to the 'Tao Te Ching' (道德经), which is traditionally said to have 5,000 characters.
    • 文景之世 - Refers to the reigns of Emperor Wen and Emperor Jing, known as the 'Wenjing Era,' a period of peace and prosperity in the Han dynasty.
    • 功成身退 - Phrase meaning 'to withdraw after achieving success,' often associated with the idea of stepping down from power or leaving a position of influence.
    • 洪源长流 - Metaphor for a great and enduring source, suggesting the lasting impact of something.
    • 乾坤所定 - Means 'determined by heaven and earth,' suggesting a cosmic or universal order.
    • 万世之师表 - Means 'a model for all generations,' indicating someone of great moral and intellectual authority.
    • 庄周之徒 - Refers to followers of Zhuangzi, a prominent Taoist philosopher.
    • 出神仙传 - '神仙传' (Shenxian Zhuan) is a collection of biographies of immortals and sages.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 老子 - Refers to Laozi, the legendary author of the 'Tao Te Ching' and a central figure in Taoism.
    • 尹喜 - A historical figure, a court official who is said to have met Laozi and received the 'Tao Te Ching' from him.
    • 窦太后 - Empress Dowager Dou, a powerful figure during the reign of Emperor Wen and Emperor Jing of the Han dynasty.
    • 孝文帝 - Emperor Wen of Han, known for his policies of frugality and moral governance.
    • 外戚诸窦 - Refers to the powerful family of Empress Dowager Dou and her relatives.
    • 疏广父子 - Refers to Shu Guang and his son, who were officials known for their wisdom and decision to retire early.
    • 文景之世 - The reigns of Emperor Wen and Emperor Jing, a period of peace and prosperity in the Han dynasty.
    • 庄周 - Zhuangzi, a prominent Taoist philosopher and author of the 'Zhuangzi' (庄子).
    • 神仙传 - A collection of biographies of immortals and sages, compiled by Ge Hong in the Jin dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 老子 - Laozi, the legendary author of the 'Tao Te Ching.'
    • 尹喜 - A historical figure who received the 'Tao Te Ching' from Laozi.
    • 窦太后 - Empress Dowager Dou.
    • 孝文帝 - Emperor Wen of Han.
    • 外戚诸窦 - The powerful family of Empress Dowager Dou.
    • 疏广父子 - Shu Guang and his son.
    • 文景之世 - The reigns of Emperor Wen and Emperor Jing.
    • 庄周 - Zhuangzi, a prominent Taoist philosopher.
    • 神仙传 - A collection of biographies of immortals and sages.

    A man was ordered to seek out money for the old man and return it to him. The old man then threw a talisman of the "Tài Xuán" at him, and he was immediately revived. The man named Jia was overjoyed and gave the old man two million coins, sending him away while following the etiquette of a disciple. He fully imparted to Jia the teachings of longevity, and Jia further sought instruction and admonition. The old man spoke to him the "Wǔ Qiān Yán" (五千言), and Jia wrote it down, naming it the "Dào Dé Jīng" (道德经). When Jia followed the teachings of the old man, he also attained immortality. Empress Dowager Dou believed in the words of Laozi, and Emperor Wen and the powerful family of Empress Dowager Dou all had no choice but to read the "Tao Te Ching." They all greatly benefited from it, so during the reigns of Emperors Wen and Jing, the world was peaceful, and the family of Dou enjoyed their honors and privileges for three generations. The Grand Tutor Shu Guang and his son deeply understood its meaning, knowing the principle of withdrawing after achieving success. They resigned from their posts on the same day, disbursed their wealth, and maintained their integrity and nobility, along with other recluses. Those who followed the teachings of Laozi all outwardly diminished their glory and inwardly cultivated their longevity, never suffering from adversity in dangerous times. Its great source and enduring influence spread so widely, how could it not be determined by heaven and earth as a model for all generations? Therefore, the followers of Zhuangzi all regarded Laozi as their master. [From the "Shenxian Zhuan" (神仙传)] [10]

    木公
    　　木公亦云东王父亦云东王公盖青阳之元气百物之先也冠三维之冠服九色云霞之服亦号玉皇君居于云房之间以紫云为盖青云为城仙童侍立玉女散香真僚仙官巨亿万计各有所职皆禀其命而朝奉翼衞故男女得道者名籍所焉昔汉初小儿于道歌曰着青裙入天门揖金母拜木公时人皆不识唯张子房知之乃再拜之曰此乃东王公之玉童也盖言世人登仙皆揖金母而拜木公焉或云居东极大芦中有山焉以青玉为室深
    广数里僚荐真仙时往谒九灵金丹一嵗再游其宫共校定男女真仙阶品功行以升降之总其行籍而上奏元始中开玉晨以禀命于老君也天地刼歴隂阳代谢世运兴废阳九百六举善黜恶靡不由之或与一玉女更投壶焉每投一投十二百枭设有入不出者天为【呼监切】嘘【嘘者言开口而笑也】枭而脱悮不接者天为之嗤儒者记而详焉所谓王者乃尊为贵上之称非其氏族也世人以王父王母为姓斯亦误矣【出仙传拾遗】 [11]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [11]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 木公 - Title or name for the 'Eastern Worthy Father' (东王父), a deity associated with the eastern direction and the element of wood.
    • 亦云 - Phrase meaning 'also called' or 'also known as,' used to introduce alternative names or titles.
    • 青阳之元气 - Refers to the primordial energy (元气) of the Qingyang (青阳), which is associated with the east and the element of wood.
    • 百物之先 - Means 'the first of all things,' emphasizing the primacy of this deity.
    • 冠三维之冠 - Describes a crown with three dimensions, likely a symbolic or elaborate headdress.
    • 服九色云霞之服 - Refers to clothing adorned with nine-colored clouds and mist, symbolizing divinity and transcendence.
    • 玉皇君 - Another title for the deity, meaning 'Lord of Jade,' associated with the highest heavens.
    • 居于云房之间 - Means 'dwelling in the cloud chambers,' referring to a heavenly abode.
    • 以紫云为盖，青云为城 - Describes a heavenly palace with a purple cloud canopy and green cloud walls.
    • 仙童侍立 - Means 'immortal children standing in attendance,' referring to attendants or servants of the deity.
    • 玉女散香 - Refers to 'jade maidens dispersing fragrance,' likely a metaphor for divine blessings or offerings.
    • 真僚仙官巨亿万计 - Means 'myriads of true officials and immortal bureaucrats,' referring to a vast number of divine servants.
    • 各有所职 - Means 'each having their own duties,' emphasizing the structured hierarchy of the divine realm.
    • 禀其命而朝奉翼衞 - Means 'receiving their commands and paying homage while protecting and serving,' describing the relationship between the deity and their servants.
    • 男女得道者名籍所隶焉 - Means 'the names of men and women who attain the Way are registered under their authority,' referring to the deity's role in overseeing spiritual achievements.
    • 揖金母 - Means 'bowing to the Queen Mother of the West,' a reference to another deity associated with the west and immortality.
    • 拜木公 - Means 'paying homage to the Eastern Worthy Father,' referring to the deity in the source text.
    • 开玉晨 - Means 'opening the Jade Morning,' likely referring to a divine realm or palace.
    • 禀命于老君 - Means 'receiving commands from the Lord Lao,' referring to the Daoist deity Laozi.
    • 天地刼歴 - Refers to the cycles of cosmic destruction and renewal.
    • 阴阳代谢 - Means 'the alternation of yin and yang,' referring to the natural cycles of change.
    • 世运兴废 - Means 'the rise and fall of the world's fortune,' referring to historical cycles.
    • 阳九百六 - Refers to a specific period of cosmic disaster in Chinese cosmology.
    • 举善黜恶 - Means 'promoting good and eliminating evil,' referring to moral governance.
    • 靡不由之 - Means 'nothing escapes this,' emphasizing the inevitability of these cycles.
    • 投壶 - A traditional Chinese game of skill involving throwing arrows into a pot.
    • 每投一投十二百枭 - Refers to a specific scoring system in the game, likely indicating a high score.
    • 设有入不出者 - Means 'if there is an entry but no exit,' referring to a failed move in the game.
    • 天为之噓 - Means 'the heavens will sigh,' a metaphor for divine disappointment or amusement.
    • 枭而脱悮 - Means 'the arrow will be released and the mistake will be corrected,' referring to the game's mechanics.
    • 天为之嗤 - Means 'the heavens will laugh,' a metaphor for divine amusement.
    • 王者乃尊为贵上之称 - Means 'the term 'king' is a title of respect and high status,' referring to the use of the term 'Wang.'
    • 世人以王父王母为姓 - Means 'the common people use 'Wangfu' and 'Wangmu' as surnames,' referring to the misuse of divine names.
    • 出仙传拾遗 - The source of the text, likely a collection of immortality legends.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 木公 - The Eastern Worthy Father, a deity associated with the east and the element of wood, often paired with the Queen Mother of the West (金母).
    • 东王父 - Another title for the Eastern Worthy Father, emphasizing his role as a father-like figure in the divine hierarchy.
    • 青阳 - The east, associated with the element of wood and the color green, symbolizing growth and renewal.
    • 元气 - Primordial energy or qi, the fundamental force of the universe.
    • 百物之先 - Emphasizes the deity's primacy in creation.
    • 三维之冠 - A symbolic crown, possibly representing the three dimensions of space or the three realms of heaven, earth, and humanity.
    • 九色云霞 - Nine-colored clouds and mist, symbolizing the divine and transcendent nature of the deity's attire.
    • 玉皇君 - The Lord of Jade, a title associated with the highest heavens in Daoist cosmology.
    • 云房 - Heavenly chambers or palaces, often described as floating in the clouds.
    • 紫云 - Purple clouds, symbolizing royalty and divinity.
    • 青云 - Green clouds, symbolizing nature and growth.
    • 仙童 - Immortal children, often depicted as attendants or servants of deities.
    • 玉女 - Jade maidens, likely referring to divine attendants or nymphs.
    • 真僚仙官 - True officials and immortal bureaucrats, referring to the divine hierarchy.
    • 巨亿万计 - Myriads, emphasizing the vast number of divine servants.
    • 男女得道者 - Men and women who attain the Way, referring to spiritual achievements.
    • 名籍所隶焉 - Names registered under the deity's authority, emphasizing their oversight.
    • 揖金母 - Bowing to the Queen Mother of the West, another deity associated with immortality.
    • 拜木公 - Paying homage to the Eastern Worthy Father, the deity in the source text.
    • 开玉晨 - Opening the Jade Morning, likely referring to a divine realm or palace.
    • 禀命于老君 - Receiving commands from the Lord Lao, the Daoist deity Laozi.
    • 天地刼歴 - Cycles of cosmic destruction and renewal, a concept in Chinese cosmology.
    • 阴阳代谢 - The alternation of yin and yang, the natural cycles of change.
    • 世运兴废 - The rise and fall of the world's fortune, referring to historical cycles.
    • 阳九百六 - A specific period of cosmic disaster in Chinese cosmology.
    • 举善黜恶 - Promoting good and eliminating evil, a principle of moral governance.
    • 靡不由之 - Nothing escapes this, emphasizing the inevitability of these cycles.
    • 投壶 - A traditional Chinese game of skill, often associated with courtship and social rituals.
    • 每投一投十二百枭 - A specific scoring system in the game, likely indicating a high score.
    • 设有入不出者 - If there is an entry but no exit, referring to a failed move in the game.
    • 天为之噓 - The heavens will sigh, a metaphor for divine disappointment or amusement.
    • 枭而脱悮 - The arrow will be released and the mistake will be corrected, referring to the game's mechanics.
    • 天为之嗤 - The heavens will laugh, a metaphor for divine amusement.
    • 王者乃尊为贵上之称 - The term 'king' is a title of respect and high status, referring to the use of the term 'Wang.'
    • 世人以王父王母为姓 - The common people use 'Wangfu' and 'Wangmu' as surnames, referring to the misuse of divine names.
    • 出仙传拾遗 - The source of the text, likely a collection of immortality legends.

    The Eastern Worthy Father, also known as the Eastern Worthy, is the primordial energy of Qingyang, the first of all things. He wears a crown with three dimensions and a robe adorned with nine-colored clouds and mist, and is also called the Lord of Jade. He dwells in the cloud chambers, with a purple cloud canopy and green cloud walls. Immortal children stand in attendance, and jade maidens disperse fragrance. There are myriads of true officials and immortal bureaucrats, each with their own duties, who receive his commands and pay homage while protecting and serving him. Thus, the names of men and women who attain the Way are registered under his authority.

    During the early Han dynasty, a child was singing on the road: "Wearing a green skirt, entering the heavenly gate, bowing to the Queen Mother, and paying homage to the Eastern Worthy Father." At that time, no one recognized him, but Zhang Liang knew and bowed to him twice, saying, "This is the jade child of the Eastern Worthy Father." It is said that when people ascend to immortality, they bow to the Queen Mother and pay homage to the Eastern Worthy Father. Some say he resides in the eastern extreme, in a vast reedland with a mountain made of green jade, deep and wide for several miles. True immortals often visit to pay homage to the Nine Spirits of the Golden Elixir. Twice a year, they tour his palace together to determine the rank and merit of male and female immortals, raising or lowering them accordingly. They compile their records and present them to the Lord Lao in the Jade Morning.

    The cycles of cosmic destruction and renewal, the alternation of yin and yang, the rise and fall of the world's fortune, and the specific period of cosmic disaster, all promote good and eliminate evil, and nothing escapes this. Sometimes he plays a game of skill with a jade maiden, where each throw scores twelve hundred points. If there is an entry but no exit, the heavens will sigh, and the arrow will be released and the mistake corrected. If there is no entry, the heavens will laugh. Scholars have recorded this in detail. The term "king" is a title of respect and high status, not a surname. The common people use "Wangfu" and "Wangmu" as surnames, which is also a mistake. [From The Collection of Immortality Legends] [11]

    广成子
    　　广成子者古之仙人也居崆峒之山石室之中黄帝闻而造焉曰敢问至道之要广成子曰尔治天下禽不待而飞草木不待黄而落何足以语至道黄帝退而闲居三月后往见之膝行而前再拜请问治身之道广成子答曰至道之精杳杳无视无听抱神以静形将自正必静必清无劳尔形无揺尔精乃可长生慎内闭外多知为败我守其一以处其和故千二百嵗而形未尝衰得我道者上为皇失吾道者下为土将去汝入无穷之门游无极之野与日月叅光与天地为常人其尽死而我独存矣【出神仙传】
    　　黄安
    　　黄安代郡人也为代郡卒云卑猥不获处人间执鞭推荆读书画地以记数一夕地成池时人谓安舌耕年可八十余彊视若童子常服硃砂举体皆赤冬不着衣坐一广长三尺时人问此有防年矣曰昔伏羲始造网罟得此以授吾其背已平矣此虫畏日月之光二千年则一出头我坐此虫已五出头矣行则负而趋世人谓安万嵗矣【出洞记】 [12]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [12]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 至道之要 - Literally 'the essentials of the ultimate way,' referring to the core principles of the highest truth or Dao.
    • 治天下 - Literally 'governing the world,' referring to the administration of the state or empire.
    • 禽不待而飞，草木不待黄而落 - A metaphorical expression indicating the natural order of things, suggesting that even without external forces, certain phenomena occur naturally.
    • 治身之道 - Literally 'the way of governing one's body,' referring to methods of self-cultivation and health.
    • 至道之精 - Literally 'the essence of the ultimate way,' referring to the most profound aspects of the Dao.
    • 杳杳 - Descriptive terms meaning 'deeply hidden' or 'obscure,' emphasizing the mysterious nature of the Dao.
    • 抱神以静 - Literally 'holding the spirit in stillness,' a practice of maintaining inner calm and focus.
    • 形将自正 - Literally 'the form will naturally become correct,' suggesting that by maintaining inner stillness, one's physical and mental state will improve.
    • 慎内闭外 - Literally 'being cautious internally and closing off externally,' a practice of protecting oneself from external distractions and influences.
    • 多知为败 - Literally 'too much knowledge leads to failure,' suggesting that excessive knowledge can be detrimental.
    • 我守其一以处其和 - Literally 'I hold onto one thing and maintain harmony,' referring to the practice of focusing on a single principle to achieve balance.
    • 无穷之门，无极之野 - Metaphorical expressions referring to boundless realms or dimensions.
    • 与日月叅光，与天地为常 - Literally 'sharing the light of the sun and moon, being constant with heaven and earth,' suggesting a state of eternal existence and harmony with nature.
    • 舌耕 - A metaphor for earning a living through teaching or writing, literally 'tilling with the tongue.'
    • 坐一 - Literally 'sitting on a turtle,' a symbolic act that may have spiritual or cultural significance.
    • 畏日月之光 - Literally 'fearing the light of the sun and moon,' suggesting a sensitivity to external forces.
    • 万嵗矣 - Literally 'ten thousand years old,' a hyperbolic expression indicating great age or longevity.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 广成子 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with Daoist teachings and immortality.
    • 黄帝 - The legendary first emperor of China, known for his contributions to Chinese culture and civilization.
    • 崆峒山 - A mountain in Gansu Province, often associated with Daoist and Buddhist traditions.
    • 伏羲 - The legendary first ruler of China, credited with creating the trigrams of the *Yijing* (Book of Changes).
    • 代郡 - An ancient commandery in northern China, now part of Hebei Province.
    • 洞记 - A collection of stories and legends, possibly a reference to a specific text or anthology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 广成子 - A legendary figure, often associated with immortality and Daoist teachings.
    • 黄帝 - The legendary first emperor of China.
    • 崆峒山 - A mountain in Gansu Province.
    • 伏羲 - The legendary first ruler of China.
    • 代郡 - An ancient commandery in northern China.
    • 洞记 - A collection of stories and legends.

    广成子

    Guangchengzi, an ancient immortal, resided in a stone chamber on Mount Kungtung. When Huangdi heard of him, he went to visit him and asked, "I dare to ask about the essentials of the ultimate way." Guangchengzi replied, "In governing the world, birds do not wait for the wind to fly, and grass and trees do not wait for autumn to fall. How can you speak of the ultimate way?" Huangdi then withdrew and lived in seclusion for three months before returning to see Guangchengzi. He knelt and bowed twice, asking, "What is the way of governing one's body?" Guangchengzi answered, "The essence of the ultimate way is deeply hidden and obscure. One must not see or hear, but hold the spirit in stillness. The form will naturally become correct. One must be calm and pure, not tire the body, nor disturb the spirit. Only then can one achieve immortality. Be cautious internally and close off externally; too much knowledge leads to failure. I hold onto one thing and maintain harmony, so I have lived for twelve hundred years without my form ever decaying. Those who attain my way become emperors, while those who lose it become commoners. I am about to leave you, entering the boundless gate and wandering in the boundless wilderness, sharing the light of the sun and moon, being constant with heaven and earth. When all others perish, I alone shall remain." (From The Book of Immortals)

    Huang An

    Huang An, a man from Dai Commandery, served as a soldier in Dai Commandery. He was lowly and unable to be in the human world, holding a whip and pushing thorns, reading books and drawing on the ground to count. One night, the ground turned into a pool, and people called Huang An a "tiller of the tongue." He was over eighty years old, yet he looked like a child. He often wore cinnabar, and his whole body was red. In winter, he did not wear clothes, sitting on a turtle that was three feet long. People asked if the turtle was very old. Huang An said, "In ancient times, Fuxi first created nets and traps and obtained this turtle to give to me. Its back has already become flat. This creature fears the light of the sun and moon, emerging once every two thousand years. I have sat on this creature for five such emergences. When I walk, I carry the turtle, and people say I am ten thousand years old." (From The Book of Grottoes) [12]

    孟岐
    　　孟岐清河之逸人年可七百嵗语及周初事了然如目前岐时侍周公升坛上岐以手摩成王足周公以玉笏与之岐常寳执每以衣裾拂拭笏厚七分今鋭欲折恒饵桂叶闻汉武帝好仙披草莱而来武帝厚待之后不知所之【出洞记】

    　　太平广记卷一
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　太平广记卷二
    　　神仙二
    　　周穆王　　　燕昭王　　　彭祖
    　　魏伯阳
    　　周穆王
    　　周穆王名满房后所生昭王子也昭王南廵不还穆王乃立时年五十矣立五十四年一百四嵗王少好神仙之道常欲使车辙马迹遍于天下以仿黄帝焉乃乗八骏之马奔戎使造父为御得白狐貉以祭于河宗导车渉弱水鱼鼈鼋鼍以为梁遂登于春山又觞西王母于瑶池之上王母谣曰白云在天道里悠逺山川间之将子无死尚能复来王答曰余归东土和洽诸夏万民平均吾顾见汝比及三年将复而野又至于雷首太 [13]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [13]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 了然如目前 - Clear and distinct, as if right before one's eyes.
    • 披草莱 - To come through the grass and weeds, symbolizing a journey through wild and untamed places.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周初 - Refers to the early Zhou period, specifically the reign of King Cheng of Zhou.
    • 周公 - Duke Zhou, a key figure in early Zhou history, known for his wisdom and contributions to the establishment of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 成王 - King Cheng of Zhou, the successor to King Wu and the son of Duke Zhou.
    • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a prominent emperor of the Han dynasty known for his military campaigns and patronage of the arts.
    • 玉笏 - A jade tablet, often used as a symbol of authority or status in ancient China.
    • 桂叶 - Leaves of the cassia tree, associated with longevity and immortality in Chinese culture.
    • 洞记 - A text that likely contains stories of immortals and supernatural beings, often found in Daoist literature.

    Proper Nouns

    • 孟岐 - Sheng Qi, the name of the recluse.
    • 清河 - Qinghe, the place where Sheng Qi lived.
    • 玉笏 - A jade tablet, a symbol of authority.
    • 桂叶 - Leaves of the cassia tree, associated with longevity and immortality.
    • 洞记 - The title of the text from which this excerpt is taken.

    Sheng Qi
    Sheng Qi, a recluse from Qinghe, was about seven hundred years old. When speaking of events from the early Zhou period, he clearly remembered them as if they were right before his eyes. At the time, he was serving Duke Zhou on the altar, and Sheng Qi used his hand to massage the feet of King Cheng. Duke Zhou gave him a jade tablet, which Sheng Qi treasured and often wiped with his sleeve. The tablet was seven fen thick and had a sharp edge that was nearly broken. He constantly ate the leaves of the cassia tree. Hearing that Emperor Wu of Han loved the Daoist practices, he came through the grass and weeds to the imperial court. Emperor Wu treated him well, but later it was unknown where he went. [From the Dongyuji (Record of the Eastern Cave)] [13]

    行遂入于宗周时尹喜既通流沙草栖于终南之隂王追其旧迹招隐士尹輙杜冲居于草栖之所因号楼观从诣焉祭公自郑圃来谒谏王以徐偃之乱王乃返国宗社复安王造昆仑时饮蜂山石髓食玉树之实又登羣玉山西王母所居皆得飞冲天之道而示迹托形者盖所以示民有终耳况其饮琬琰之膏进甜雪之味素莲黑枣碧藕白橘皆神仙之物得不延期长生乎又云西王母降穆王之宫相与升云而去【出仙传拾遗】
    　　燕昭王
    　　燕昭王者王哙之子也及即位好神仙之道仙人甘需臣事之为王述昆台登真之事去嗜欲撤声色无思无为可以致道王行之既乆谷将子乗虗而集告于王曰西王母将降观尔之所修示尔以灵之要后一年王母果至与王游燧林之下説炎皇钻火之术燃緑桂膏以照夜忽有飞蛾衔火集王之宫得圆丘硃砂结而为佩 [14]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [14]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 行遂入于宗周 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the road leads into the ancestral Zhou.'
    • 时尹喜既通流沙 - Means 'the time when Master Yin Xi had crossed the Gobi Desert.' '通流沙' refers to traversing the vast desert, symbolizing a journey of exploration or discovery.
    • 草栖于终南之阴 - Means 'dwelling in the shade of Mount Zhongnan.' '草栖' suggests a humble or rustic dwelling, while '终南之阴' refers to the north side of Mount Zhongnan, a mountain range associated with Daoist hermits.
    • 王追其旧迹 - Means 'the king followed his former footsteps.' '旧迹' refers to past traces or footsteps, symbolizing a pursuit of historical or spiritual legacy.
    • 招隐士尹辄杜冲 - Means 'recruiting the hermit Yin Zhe and Du Chong.' '隐士' refers to hermits or recluses, often associated with Daoist or Confucian ideals of withdrawal from the world.
    • 因号楼观 - Means 'built a pavilion for him.' '楼观' refers to a pavilion or observatory, often associated with Daoist or religious sites.
    • 从诣焉 - Means 'followed him there.' '从诣' suggests following someone to a place, often with a sense of reverence or devotion.
    • 祭公自郑圃来谒 - Means 'Ji Gong came from Zheng Garden to pay respects.' '郑圃' refers to a garden in Zheng, a place associated with Daoist or spiritual retreats.
    • 谏王以徐偃之乱 - Means 'advised the king about the chaos caused by Xu Yan.' '徐偃之乱' refers to a historical event or legend involving chaos or rebellion.
    • 王乃返国宗社复安 - Means 'the king then returned to his country, and the ancestral temple and the state were restored to peace.' '宗社' refers to the ancestral temple and the state, symbolizing the restoration of order.
    • 王造昆仑时饮蜂山石髓 - Means 'the king visited Kunlun Mountain and drank the essence of stones from Bee Mountain.' '石髓' refers to the essence or marrow of stones, often associated with immortality or longevity.
    • 食玉树之实 - Means 'ate the fruit of the jade tree.' '玉树' refers to a mythical tree associated with immortality or divine nourishment.
    • 又登羣玉山西王母所居 - Means 'he also climbed the Jade Mountain, where the Queen Mother of the West resides.' '羣玉山' refers to a mythical mountain associated with the Queen Mother of the West, a figure in Daoist mythology.
    • 皆得飞冲天之道 - Means 'all attained the way of flying and ascending to the heavens.' '飞冲天' refers to the ability to fly or ascend to heaven, often associated with immortality.
    • 而示迹托形者 - Means 'and those who showed signs and took forms.' '示迹托形' refers to the appearance or manifestation of divine beings in human form.
    • 盖所以示民有终耳 - Means 'this was to show the people that there is an end to all things.' '盖所以' means 'this is because,' and '示民有终' suggests the idea of finality or completion.
    • 况其饮琬琰之膏 - Means 'especially when they drink the essence of jade.' '琬琰' refers to precious jade, often associated with immortality or divine nourishment.
    • 进甜雪之味 - Means 'and taste the sweetness of snow.' '甜雪' refers to a mythical or divine form of snow, often associated with immortality or divine nourishment.
    • 素莲黑枣碧藕白橘 - Means 'white lotus, black jujube, green gourd, and white tangerine.' These are all mythical or divine fruits associated with immortality or divine nourishment.
    • 皆神仙之物 - Means 'all are things of the immortals.' '神仙之物' refers to objects or substances associated with immortality or divine nourishment.
    • 得不延期长生乎 - Means 'how could they not extend life and achieve immortality?' '延期长生' refers to the extension of life or achieving immortality.
    • 又云西王母降穆王之宫 - Means 'it is also said that the Queen Mother of the West descended to the palace of King Mu.' '西王母' refers to the Queen Mother of the West, a figure in Daoist mythology, and '穆王' refers to King Mu of Zhou, a historical figure associated with immortality.
    • 相与升云而去 - Means 'they ascended into the clouds together and departed.' '升云而去' refers to ascending into the clouds, often associated with immortality or divine departure.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宗周 - Refers to the former capital of the Zhou dynasty, symbolizing a historical or sacred site.
    • 尹喜 - A historical figure, a hermit and Daoist sage who is said to have met Laozi and recorded the *Tao Te Ching*.
    • 流沙 - Refers to the Gobi Desert, a vast and challenging terrain often associated with journeys of exploration or discovery.
    • 终南之阴 - Refers to the north side of Mount Zhongnan, a mountain range associated with Daoist hermits and spiritual retreats.
    • 祭公 - A historical figure, a high-ranking official in the Zhou dynasty, often associated with religious or spiritual matters.
    • 郑圃 - Refers to a garden in Zheng, a place associated with Daoist or spiritual retreats.
    • 徐偃之乱 - Refers to a historical event or legend involving chaos or rebellion, often associated with the fall of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 昆仑 - Refers to Kunlun Mountain, a mythical mountain associated with immortality, divine beings, and the Queen Mother of the West.
    • 蜂山 - Refers to a mythical mountain associated with the Queen Mother of the West and the production of divine substances.
    • 玉树 - Refers to a mythical tree associated with immortality or divine nourishment.
    • 羣玉山 - Refers to a mythical mountain associated with the Queen Mother of the West and the production of divine substances.
    • 西王母 - Refers to the Queen Mother of the West, a figure in Daoist mythology associated with immortality and divine nourishment.
    • 穆王 - Refers to King Mu of Zhou, a historical figure associated with immortality and the pursuit of the divine.
    • 燕昭王 - Refers to King Zhao of Yan, a historical figure associated with the pursuit of immortality and the cultivation of spiritual practices.
    • 甘需 - A historical figure, a hermit and Daoist sage who is said to have served King Zhao of Yan.
