    • 昆台 - Refers to a mythical mountain associated with the Queen Mother of the West and the production of divine substances.
    • 钻火之术 - Refers to the technique of drilling fire, often associated with the legendary figure of Fuxi and the invention of fire.
    • 绿桂膏 - Refers to the essence or marrow of green osmanthus, often associated with immortality or divine nourishment.
    • 圆丘 - Refers to a circular altar, often associated with religious or spiritual rituals.
    • 硃砂 - Refers to cinnabar, a red mineral often associated with immortality or divine nourishment.
    • 灵之要 - Refers to the essential principles of the divine or the Dao, often associated with immortality or spiritual enlightenment.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 行遂入于宗周时尹喜既通流沙草栖于终南之阴 - This long sequence must be read as a single descriptive passage, introducing the setting and the characters involved.
    • 王追其旧迹招隐士尹辄杜冲居于草栖之所因号楼观从诣焉 - This sequence must be read as a series of actions taken by the king, including following his former footsteps, recruiting hermits, and building a pavilion for them.
    • 祭公自郑圃来谒谏王以徐偃之乱王乃返国宗社复安 - This sequence must be read as a series of events, including Ji Gong's visit, his advice to the king, and the king's return to his country, restoring peace.
    • 王造昆仑时饮蜂山石髓食玉树之实又登羣玉山西王母所居皆得飞冲天之道而示迹托形者盖所以示民有终耳 - This sequence must be read as a series of actions taken by the king, including visiting Kunlun Mountain, drinking the essence of stones, eating the fruit of the jade tree, and ascending to the heavens.
    • 况其饮琬琰之膏进甜雪之味素莲黑枣碧藕白橘皆神仙之物得不延期长生乎 - This sequence must be read as a series of descriptions of divine substances, often associated with immortality or divine nourishment.

    The road leads into the ancestral Zhou. At that time, Master Yin Xi had crossed the Gobi Desert and dwelt in the shade of Mount Zhongnan. The king followed his former footsteps and recruited the hermit Yin Zhe and Du Chong, who lived in a humble dwelling. The king built a pavilion for them and followed them there. Ji Gong came from Zheng Garden to pay respects and advised the king about the chaos caused by Xu Yan. The king then returned to his country, and the ancestral temple and the state were restored to peace. The king visited Kunlun Mountain and drank the essence of stones from Bee Mountain, ate the fruit of the jade tree, and climbed the Jade Mountain, where the Queen Mother of the West resides. All attained the way of flying and ascending to the heavens, and those who showed signs and took forms, this was to show the people that there is an end to all things. Especially when they drink the essence of jade and taste the sweetness of snow, and enjoy white lotus, black jujube, green gourd, and white tangerine—all are things of the immortals. How could they not extend life and achieve immortality? It is also said that the Queen Mother of the West descended to the palace of King Mu, and they ascended into the clouds together and departed.

    King Zhao of Yan was the son of King Kuai of Yan. After ascending the throne, he favored the ways of the immortals. The immortal Gan Xu served him. For the king, he recounted the story of Kunlun Mountain and the attainment of true immortality, renouncing desires and withdrawing from sensual pleasures, with no thoughts and no action, one can achieve the Dao. The king practiced this for a long time, and Gu Jiang Zi came to him in a dream and told the king, "The Queen Mother of the West will descend to observe what you have cultivated and show you the essentials of the divine." A year later, the Queen Mother of the West indeed arrived and accompanied the king in the Sui Forest, teaching him the technique of drilling fire. They lit the essence of green osmanthus to illuminate the night. Suddenly, a flying moth carried a flame and gathered at the king's palace, and a circular altar formed with cinnabar, which coalesced into a pendant. [14]

    王登握日之台得神鸟所衔洞光之珠以消烦暑自是王母三降于燕宫而昭王狥于攻取不能遵甘需澄静之防王母亦不复至甘需白王母所设之非人世所有玉酒金醴后期万祀王既尝之自当得道矣但在虚凝纯白保其遐龄耳甘需亦升天而去三十三年王无疾而殂形骨柔耎香气盈庭子恵王立矣【出仙传拾遗】
    　　彭祖
    　　彭祖者姓籛讳铿帝颛顼之孙也殷末已七百六十七嵗而不衰老少好恬静不防世务不营名誉不饰车服唯以飬生治身为事王闻之以为大夫常称疾闲居不与政事善于补导之术服水桂云母粉麋角散常有少容然性沈重终不自言有道亦不作诡惑变化怪之事然无为少周游时还独行人莫知其所诣伺竟不见也有车马而常不乗或数百日或数十日不持资粮还家则衣食与人无异常闭气内息从旦至中乃危坐拭目摩搦身体防唇咽唾服气数十乃起行言笑其体中或瘦倦不安便导引闭气以攻所患心存其体面九窍五脏四肢至于毛髪皆令具至其 [15]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [15]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 王登握日之台得神鸟所衔洞光之珠 - Describes a fantastical event where Wang Deng acquires a luminous pearl carried by a divine bird on a high platform to dispel the heat.
    • 以消烦暑 - Means 'to dispel the heat,' indicating the use of the pearl for cooling purposes.
    • 王母三降于燕宫 - Refers to the three descents of the Queen Mother of the West to the Yan Palace, a significant event in Chinese mythology.
    • 昭王狥于攻取 - Means 'King Zhao was engrossed in conquests,' highlighting his focus on military expansion.
    • 不能遵甘需澄静之防 - Indicates that King Zhao did not follow the advice of Gan Xu to maintain tranquility and purity.
    • 玉酒金醴 - Refers to precious, mythical beverages, often associated with immortality and divine beings.
    • 后期万祀 - Means 'for ten thousand generations,' emphasizing the enduring nature of the effects of the wine.
    • 自当得道矣 - Means 'he would naturally attain the Dao,' suggesting spiritual enlightenment.
    • 但在虚凝纯白保其遐龄耳 - Means 'but only by maintaining purity and longevity,' indicating the necessary conditions for achieving the Dao.
    • 甘需亦升天而去 - Means 'Gan Xu also ascended to heaven and departed,' describing his own spiritual journey.
    • 三十三年王无疾而殂 - Means 'thirty-three years later, the king died without illness,' indicating a peaceful and unexpected death.
    • 形骨柔耎香气盈庭 - Describes the king's body being soft and fragrant, suggesting a state of purity and grace.
    • 子恵王立矣 - Means 'Prince Hui was enthroned,' indicating the succession of the throne.
    • 彭祖者姓籛讳铿 - Introduces Peng Zu, a legendary figure known for his longevity.
    • 帝颛顼之玄孙也 - Means 'the great-grandson of Emperor Zhuanxu,' providing his lineage.
    • 殷末已七百六十七岁而不衰老 - Indicates that Peng Zu lived for 767 years without aging, highlighting his extraordinary longevity.
    • 少好恬静不防世务 - Means 'he was fond of tranquility and did not engage in worldly affairs,' describing his lifestyle.
    • 不营名誉不饰车服 - Means 'he did not seek fame or adorn his vehicles and attire,' further emphasizing his simple and unassuming nature.
    • 唯以飬生治身为事 - Means 'he focused solely on nurturing life and managing his body,' indicating his primary concern.
    • 王闻之以为大夫 - Means 'the king heard about him and appointed him as a high official,' showing his recognition.
    • 常称疾闲居不与政事 - Means 'he often claimed illness and lived a secluded life, not involved in politics,' indicating his withdrawal from public life.
    • 善于补导之术 - Means 'he was skilled in health-preserving techniques,' highlighting his expertise.
    • 服水桂云母粉麋角散常有少容 - Describes his use of various medicinal substances to maintain his youthful appearance.
    • 然性沈重终不自言有道 - Means 'but he was serious and never boasted about his knowledge,' indicating his humility.
    • 亦不作诡惑变化鬼怪之事 - Means 'he also did not perform deceptive or supernatural tricks,' showing his straightforward nature.
    • 俨然无为少周游时还独行 - Means 'he appeared calm and rarely traveled, often returning alone,' describing his lifestyle.
    • 人莫知其所诣伺候竟不见 - Means 'no one knew where he went, and no one could find him when waiting,' indicating his mysterious nature.
    • 有车马而常不乘或数百日或数十日不持资粮 - Describes his unconventional behavior, such as having carriages and horses but rarely using them, and sometimes going for months without carrying provisions.
    • 还家则衣食与人无常 - Means 'when he returned home, his clothing and food were as ordinary as anyone else's,' suggesting his humility.
    • 闭气内息从旦至中乃危坐拭目摩搦身体防唇咽唾服气数十乃起行言笑 - Describes his daily routine of breathing exercises, meditation, and physical practices.
    • 其体中或瘦倦不安便导引闭气以攻所患 - Means 'if he felt weak or uncomfortable, he would perform exercises and breathing techniques to address the issue,' indicating his self-care practices.
    • 心存其体面九窍五脏四肢至于毛发皆令具至 - Means 'he focused on every part of his body, from the face and nine orifices to the internal organs, limbs, and even hair,' showing his meticulous attention to health.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 王登 - A historical figure, possibly a king or nobleman.
    • 神鸟 - Divine bird, often associated with good fortune and divine intervention.
    • 洞光之珠 - A luminous pearl, often associated with divine or magical properties.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West, a significant figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortality and longevity.
    • 燕宫 - The Yan Palace, a royal residence.
    • 昭王 - King Zhao, a historical or legendary king.
    • 甘需 - Gan Xu, a figure who advised King Zhao.
    • 玉酒金醴 - Precious, mythical beverages, often associated with immortality and divine beings.
    • 彭祖 - Peng Zu, a legendary figure known for his longevity and wisdom.
    • 帝颛顼 - Emperor Zhuanxu, a mythological ruler.
    • 殷末 - The end of the Shang Dynasty, a historical period in China.
    • 大夫 - A high-ranking official in ancient China.
    • 补导之术 - Health-preserving techniques, often associated with traditional Chinese medicine and longevity practices.
    • 水桂云母粉麋角散 - Various medicinal substances used for health and longevity.
    • 无为 - A concept in Daoism, meaning 'non-action' or 'effortless action.'

    Proper Nouns

    • 王登 - Wang Deng
    • 神鸟 - Divine Bird
    • 洞光之珠 - Luminous Pearl
    • 王母 - Queen Mother of the West
    • 燕宫 - Yan Palace
    • 昭王 - King Zhao
    • 甘需 - Gan Xu
    • 玉酒金醴 - Precious Wine and Liquor
    • 彭祖 - Peng Zu
    • 帝颛顼 - Emperor Zhuanxu
    • 殷末 - End of the Shang Dynasty
    • 大夫 - High Official
    • 水桂云母粉麋角散 - Medicinal Substances

    Wang Deng, upon the high platform of the sun, obtained a luminous pearl carried by a divine bird to dispel the heat. From then on, the Queen Mother of the West descended three times to the Yan Palace, but King Zhao was engrossed in conquests and did not follow Gan Xu's advice to maintain tranquility. The Queen Mother also did not return. Gan Xu explained that the feasts set up by the Queen Mother were not of this world, consisting of precious wine and liquor that would last for ten thousand generations. If King Zhao had tasted them, he would naturally attain the Dao, but only by maintaining purity and longevity. Gan Xu also ascended to heaven and departed. Thirty-three years later, King Zhao died without illness, his body soft and fragrant, filling the courtyard with a sweet aroma. His son, Prince Hui, was then enthroned. [From Xian Chuan Shi Yi]

    Peng Zu, whose surname is Jian and given name is Keng, is the great-grandson of Emperor Zhuanxu. By the end of the Shang Dynasty, he had already lived for 767 years without aging. He was fond of tranquility and did not engage in worldly affairs, did not seek fame, and did not adorn his vehicles and attire, focusing solely on nurturing life and managing his body. The king heard about him and appointed him as a high official. Peng Zu often claimed illness and lived a secluded life, not involved in politics. He was skilled in health-preserving techniques, using water, cassia, cinnabar powder, and deer antler powder to maintain his youthful appearance. Despite his serious nature, he never boasted about his knowledge and did not perform deceptive or supernatural tricks. He appeared calm and rarely traveled, often returning alone. No one knew where he went, and no one could find him when waiting. He had carriages and horses but rarely used them, and sometimes went for months without carrying provisions. When he returned home, his clothing and food were as ordinary as anyone else's. He practiced breathing exercises and meditation, sitting upright from dawn until noon, wiping his eyes, rubbing his body, pursing his lips, swallowing saliva, and taking deep breaths dozens of times before standing up to walk, talk, and laugh. If he felt weak or uncomfortable, he would perform exercises and breathing techniques to address the issue. He focused on every part of his body, from the face and nine orifices to the internal organs, limbs, and even hair, ensuring everything was in harmony. [15]

    气云行体中故于鼻口中达十指末寻即体和王自往问讯不告致遗珍玩前后数万金而皆受之以恤贫贱无所留又采女者亦少得道知飬性之方年二百七十嵗视之如五六十嵗奉事之于掖庭为立华屋紫阁饰以金玉乃令采女乗辎軿往问道于彭祖既至再拜请问延年益夀之法彭祖曰欲举形登天上补仙官当用金丹紫炁召太一所以白日升天也此道至大非君王之所能为其次当爱飬精神服药草可以长生但不能役使神乗虚飞行身不知交接之道纵服药无益也能飬隂阳之意可推之而得但不思言耳何足怪问也吾遗腹而生三嵗而失母遇犬戎之乱流离西域 [16]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [16]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 气云行体中 - Metaphorical expression describing a state where the body is filled with a cloud-like energy or qi, which moves through the body.
    • 于鼻口中达十指末 - Literally means 'from the nose and mouth to the fingertips,' suggesting the spread of this energy throughout the body.
    • 寻即体和 - 'Soon the body becomes harmonious,' indicating a quick improvement in physical condition.
    • 王自往问讯 - 'The king personally went to inquire,' emphasizing the importance and personal interest of the king.
    • 不告致遗珍玩 - 'Without being told, he left behind precious treasures,' implying that the person acted without explicit instruction.
    • 前后数万金 - 'Before and after, several tens of thousands of gold,' referring to a large sum of money.
    • 而皆受之以恤贫贱 - 'And accepted all of it to relieve the poor and lowly,' indicating the purpose of accepting the wealth.
    • 无所留 - 'Without leaving anything behind,' meaning that nothing was kept for personal gain.
    • 采女者亦少得道 - 'The concubines also attained the Dao,' suggesting that they achieved a spiritual or philosophical enlightenment.
    • 知飬性之方 - 'Knowing the methods of cultivating one's nature,' referring to practices for self-cultivation.
    • 年二百七十嵗 - 'Age two hundred and seventy years,' indicating an exceptionally long lifespan.
    • 视之如五六十嵗 - 'Seen as fifty or sixty years old,' suggesting that despite their age, they appeared youthful.
    • 奉事之于掖庭为立华屋紫阁饰以金玉 - 'Serving them in the palace, they built splendid houses and purple pavilions adorned with gold and jade,' describing the luxurious treatment given to them.
    • 乃令采女乘辎軿往问道于彭祖 - 'And then ordered the concubines to ride in carriages to ask questions of Peng Zu,' indicating a formal and respectful visit.
    • 既至再拜请问 - 'Upon arrival, they bowed twice and asked questions,' showing respect and formality.
    • 延年益寿之法 - 'The method of extending life and increasing longevity,' referring to techniques for living longer.
    • 彭祖曰 - 'Peng Zu said,' introducing a quote from Peng Zu, a legendary figure known for his longevity.
    • 欲举形登天上补仙官 - 'To ascend to heaven and fill the ranks of the immortals,' describing a spiritual goal.
    • 当用金丹紫炁召太一 - 'One should use golden elixirs and purple qi to summon Taiyi,' referring to specific practices for achieving immortality.
    • 所以白日升天也 - 'This is how one ascends to heaven during the day,' emphasizing the method and timing.
    • 此道至大非君王之所能为 - 'This path is very great and not something a king can accomplish,' indicating the difficulty and rarity of the practice.
    • 其次当爱飬精神服药草可以长生 - 'Secondly, one should love and cultivate one's spirit, take medicinal herbs, and can achieve immortality,' suggesting alternative methods for extending life.
    • 但不能役使神乘虚飞行 - 'But one cannot command spirits or fly through the void,' indicating limitations of these methods.
    • 身不知交接之道 - 'Not knowing the ways of intercourse,' suggesting a lack of knowledge in certain areas.
    • 纵服药无益 - 'Even if one takes medicine, it will be of no benefit,' emphasizing the importance of knowledge and practice.
    • 能飬阴阳之意可推之而得 - 'If one can cultivate the meaning of yin and yang, it can be deduced,' suggesting a deeper understanding of the principles.
    • 但不思言耳何足怪问也 - 'But it is not something to think about or question, it is not surprising,' indicating a dismissive attitude towards certain aspects.
    • 吾遗腹而生 - 'I was born posthumously,' indicating a special birth circumstance.
    • 三嵗而失母 - 'Lost my mother at three years old,' describing an early loss.
    • 遇犬戎之乱流离西域 - 'Encountered the chaos of the Quanrong and wandered in the Western Regions,' indicating a period of hardship and displacement.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 彭祖 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology known for his extraordinary longevity, often associated with Daoist practices and longevity techniques.
    • 金丹 - Refers to alchemical elixirs believed to grant immortality.
    • 紫炁 - Purple qi, a type of spiritual energy in Daoist tradition.
    • 太一 - A supreme deity in Chinese mythology, often associated with the highest level of spiritual attainment.
    • 采女 - Concubines or female attendants in the imperial court.
    • 掖庭 - The inner palace or harem.
    • 华屋紫阁 - Splendid houses and purple pavilions, symbols of luxury and grandeur.
    • 金玉 - Gold and jade, precious materials often used in decorative arts.
    • 辎軿 - Carriages or vehicles.
    • 犬戎之乱 - Refers to historical conflicts involving the Quanrong, a nomadic people who invaded ancient China.
    • 西域 - The Western Regions, referring to areas west of China, including Central Asia.

    Proper Nouns

    • 彭祖 - A legendary figure known for his longevity.
    • 金丹 - Alchemical elixirs.
    • 紫炁 - Purple qi.
    • 太一 - A supreme deity.
    • 采女 - Concubines.
    • 掖庭 - The inner palace.
    • 华屋紫阁 - Splendid houses and purple pavilions.
    • 金玉 - Gold and jade.
    • 辎軿 - Carriages.
    • 犬戎之乱 - Historical conflicts involving the Quanrong.
    • 西域 - The Western Regions.

    The energy cloud flows through the body, reaching from the nose and mouth to the fingertips, soon making the body harmonious. The king personally went to inquire, and without being told, he left behind precious treasures, both before and after, totaling several tens of thousands of gold, which he accepted all to relieve the poor and lowly, without leaving anything behind. The concubines also attained the Dao, knowing the methods of cultivating one's nature, living to be two hundred and seventy years old, appearing as if fifty or sixty years old. They were served in the palace, where splendid houses and purple pavilions adorned with gold and jade were built for them. Then, the king ordered the concubines to ride in carriages to ask questions of Peng Zu. Upon arrival, they bowed twice and asked questions about the methods of extending life and increasing longevity. Peng Zu said, "To ascend to heaven and fill the ranks of the immortals, one should use golden elixirs and purple qi to summon Taiyi, thus ascending to heaven during the day." This path is very great and not something a king can accomplish. Secondly, one should love and cultivate one's spirit, take medicinal herbs, and can achieve immortality, but one cannot command spirits or fly through the void. One does not know the ways of intercourse, even if one takes medicine, it will be of no benefit. If one can cultivate the meaning of yin and yang, it can be deduced, but it is not something to think about or question, it is not surprising. I was born posthumously, lost my mother at three years old, and encountered the chaos of the Quanrong, wandering in the Western Regions. [16]

    百有余年加以少枯丧四十九妻失五十四子数遭忧患和气折伤冷肌肤不泽荣衞焦枯恐不度世所闻浅薄不足宣传大宛山有青精先生者传言千嵗色如童子歩行日过五百里能终嵗不食亦能一日九食真可问也采女曰敢问青精先生是何仙人者也彭祖曰得道者耳非仙人也仙人者或竦身入云无翅而飞或驾龙乗云上造天阶或化为鸟兽游浮青云或潜行江海翺翔名山或食元气或茹芝草或出入人间而人不识或其身而莫之见面生异骨体有竒毛率好深僻不交俗流然此等虽有不死之夀去人情逺荣乐有若雀化为蛤雉化为蜃失其本真更守异气余之 [17]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [17]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 百有余年 - Literally 'more than a hundred years,' used to indicate a long period of time.
    • 少枯丧 - A compound term that likely refers to a state of mourning or grief, possibly due to the loss of family members.
    • 四十九妻失五十四子 - Indicates that the person has lost 49 wives and 54 sons, which is an unusual and exaggerated way to describe significant personal losses.
    • 数遭忧患 - Means 'frequently suffering from misfortunes or hardships.'
    • 和气折伤 - Refers to the damage or depletion of one's vitality or good fortune.
    • 冷肌肤不泽 - Describes the skin becoming cold and dry, indicating poor health or vitality.
    • 荣衞焦枯 - Refers to the depletion of the body's vital energies, often associated with aging or illness.
    • 恐不度世 - Means 'fear of not living long enough' or 'fear of not reaching a ripe old age.'
    • 所闻浅薄 - Indicates limited knowledge or understanding.
    • 不足宣传 - Means 'not sufficient to be widely known' or 'not worth spreading.'
    • 青精先生 - A title given to a person, possibly a hermit or sage, known for their longevity and wisdom.
    • 传言千嵗 - Means 'it is said that they live for a thousand years,' indicating extreme longevity.
    • 色如童子 - Describes the appearance of looking like a young child, suggesting youthfulness despite age.
    • 歩行日过五百里 - Means 'walking more than five hundred miles in a day,' indicating exceptional physical ability.
    • 能终嵗不食 - Means 'able to go without food for an entire year.'
    • 亦能一日九食 - Means 'also able to eat nine times in a day,' indicating a flexible and adaptable lifestyle.
    • 真可问也 - Means 'truly worth asking about' or 'truly worth inquiring into.'
    • 敢问 - A polite way to ask a question, often used in formal or respectful contexts.
    • 得道者耳 - Means 'one who has attained the Dao' or 'a person who has achieved spiritual enlightenment.'
    • 仙人 - Refers to immortals or deities, often associated with supernatural abilities.
    • 或竦身入云 - Means 'some can leap into the clouds,' indicating the ability to fly.
    • 无翅而飞 - Means 'fly without wings,' emphasizing the supernatural nature of the ability.
    • 或驾龙乘云 - Means 'some ride dragons and clouds,' indicating the ability to travel through the air.
    • 上造天阶 - Means 'ascend to the celestial steps,' indicating reaching the heavens.
    • 或化为鸟兽 - Means 'some transform into birds and beasts,' indicating the ability to change form.
    • 游浮青云 - Means 'wander among the blue clouds,' indicating a free and unbound existence.
    • 或潜行江海 - Means 'some move stealthily through rivers and seas,' indicating the ability to travel underwater.
    • 翱翔名山 - Means 'soar over famous mountains,' indicating a life of freedom and exploration.
    • 或食元气 - Means 'some feed on primordial energy,' indicating a diet of spiritual or cosmic energy.
    • 或茹芝草 - Means 'some consume magical herbs,' indicating a diet of medicinal or mystical plants.
    • 或出入人间而人不识 - Means 'some enter and exit the human world without being recognized,' indicating a hidden or elusive existence.
    • 或其身而莫之见 - Means 'some conceal themselves and are not seen,' indicating a reclusive or invisible presence.
    • 面生异骨体有奇毛 - Describes having unusual bones and strange hair, indicating a physical appearance that is different from the norm.
    • 率好深僻 - Means 'generally prefer secluded places,' indicating a preference for solitude.
    • 不交俗流 - Means 'do not mix with the common people,' indicating a detachment from societal norms.
    • 然此等虽有不死之寿 - Means 'however, these people, although they have the longevity of immortals,' indicating that they have extended lifespans.
    • 去人情远 - Means 'are far removed from human emotions,' indicating a lack of emotional connection to others.
    • 荣乐有若雀化为蛤雉化为蜃 - Means 'their joys and pleasures are like a sparrow transforming into a clam or a pheasant into a shellfish,' indicating a transformation that is not fully understood or appreciated.
    • 失其本真 - Means 'lose their true nature,' indicating a loss of authenticity or essence.
    • 更守异气 - Means 'they guard strange energies,' indicating a focus on maintaining supernatural or mystical qualities.
    • 余之 - Means 'my opinion' or 'my view,' indicating the speaker's personal perspective.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 青精先生 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with longevity and spiritual practices.
    • 千嵗 - Refers to a lifespan of a thousand years, a common theme in Chinese folklore and mythology.
    • 仙人 - Immortals or deities, often depicted in Chinese literature and art as having supernatural abilities.
    • 元气 - Primordial energy, a concept in Chinese philosophy and medicine referring to the fundamental life force.
    • 芝草 - Magical herbs, often associated with longevity and healing in Chinese mythology.
    • 不死之寿 - Immortality or the ability to live indefinitely, a common theme in Chinese literature and folklore.
    • 荣乐 - Joys and pleasures, often used to describe the experiences of immortals or sages.
    • 雀化为蛤雉化为蜃 - Metaphors for transformation, often used to describe the unpredictable or mysterious nature of life.
    • 失其本真 - Losing one's true nature, a concept in Chinese philosophy that emphasizes the importance of maintaining one's authentic self.
    • 异气 - Strange energies, often associated with supernatural or mystical phenomena.

    Proper Nouns

    • 青精先生 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with longevity and spiritual practices.
    • 仙人 - Immortals or deities, often depicted in Chinese literature and art as having supernatural abilities.
    • 元气 - Primordial energy, a concept in Chinese philosophy and medicine referring to the fundamental life force.
    • 芝草 - Magical herbs, often associated with longevity and healing in Chinese mythology.
    • 不死之寿 - Immortality or the ability to live indefinitely, a common theme in Chinese literature and folklore.
    • 荣乐 - Joys and pleasures, often used to describe the experiences of immortals or sages.
    • 雀化为蛤雉化为蜃 - Metaphors for transformation, often used to describe the unpredictable or mysterious nature of life.
    • 失其本真 - Losing one's true nature, a concept in Chinese philosophy that emphasizes the importance of maintaining one's authentic self.
    • 异气 - Strange energies, often associated with supernatural or mystical phenomena.

    For more than a hundred years, I have suffered from frequent misfortunes, losing 49 wives and 54 sons. My vitality has been depleted, my skin cold and dry, and my life force withered. I fear I may not live to a ripe old age. My knowledge is shallow, and there is little worth spreading about me. In the Great Yue Mountains, there is a figure known as the Green Elixir Master, who is said to live for a thousand years. He looks like a young child, walks more than five hundred miles in a day, can go without food for an entire year, and can also eat nine times in a day. Truly, he is worth asking about. The Lady Cai said, "May I ask, what kind of immortal is the Green Elixir Master?" Peng Zu replied, "He is one who has attained the Dao, not an immortal. Immortals are those who can leap into the clouds without wings and fly, or ride dragons and clouds to ascend to the celestial steps, or transform into birds and beasts and wander among the blue clouds, or move stealthily through rivers and seas and soar over famous mountains, or feed on primordial energy or consume magical herbs, or enter and exit the human world without being recognized, or conceal themselves and are not seen, with unusual bones and strange hair, generally preferring secluded places and not mixing with the common people. However, these people, although they have the longevity of immortals, are far removed from human emotions. Their joys and pleasures are like a sparrow transforming into a clam or a pheasant into a shellfish, losing their true nature and guarding strange energies." This is my view. [17]

    愚心未愿此已入道当食甘防服轻丽通隂阳处官秩耳骨节坚强顔色和泽老而不衰延年乆视长在世间寒温风湿不能伤神众精莫敢犯五兵百虫不可近嗔喜毁誉不为累乃可贵耳人之受气虽不知方术但飬之得宜常至百二十嵗不及此伤者也小复晓道可得二百四十嵗加之可至四百八十嵗尽其理者可以不死但不成仙人耳飬夀之道但莫伤之而已夫冬温夏凉不失四时之和所以适身也美色淑资幽闲娱乐不致思欲之惑所以通神也车服威仪知足无求所以一志也八音五色以悦视听所以导心也凡此皆以养夀而不能斟酌之者反以速患古之至人恐下 [18]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [18]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 愚心未愿此已入道 - Literal meaning: 'my foolish heart has not yet wished to enter the Dao at this point.' Suggests a reluctance or delay in embracing the path of cultivation.
    • 养寿之道但莫伤之而已 - Literal meaning: 'The way to nourish life is simply not to harm oneself.' Emphasizes the importance of self-preservation.
    • 凡此皆以养寿而不能斟酌之者反以速患 - Literal meaning: 'All of this is for nurturing life; those who cannot measure it properly will hasten their own suffering.' Warns against excess or imbalance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 道 - Refers to the Dao, the fundamental principle or path of cultivation in Chinese philosophy, particularly Daoism.
    • 阴阳 - Refers to the dualistic concept of yin and yang, representing complementary forces in nature.
    • 鬼神众精 - Refers to spirits, ghosts, and other supernatural entities in Chinese religious and philosophical thought.
    • 仙人 - Refers to immortals or deities in Chinese mythology and Daoism.
    • 八音 - Refers to the eight tones in Chinese music, symbolizing harmony and balance.
    • 五色 - Refers to the five colors (blue, red, yellow, white, black), symbolizing the five elements and their balance.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The phrase '愚心未愿此已入道' is somewhat elliptical, but it can be understood as expressing a reluctance to enter the Dao at this time.
    • The long sequence of phrases '当食甘防服轻丽通阴阳处官秩耳' describes a luxurious and harmonious lifestyle, but it requires careful parsing to maintain the intended meaning.
    • The compound phrase '骨节坚强颜色和泽老而不衰' describes physical attributes, but it must be read as a single unit to convey the idea of vitality and longevity.
    • The long sequence of clauses '延年久视长在世间寒温风湿不能伤鬼神众精莫敢犯五兵百虫不可近嗔喜毁誉不为累乃可贵耳' describes various aspects of a cultivated life, but it must be read as a coherent whole to convey the overall message.
    • The clause '人之受气虽不知方术但养之得宜常至百二十岁' introduces the idea of longevity through proper nurturing, but it requires careful parsing to maintain the intended meaning.
    • The clause '小复晓道可得二百四十岁加之可至四百八十岁' suggests the potential for extended life through knowledge and practice, but it requires careful parsing to maintain the intended meaning.
    • The clause '尽其理者可以不死但不成仙人耳' distinguishes between longevity and spiritual transcendence, but it requires careful parsing to maintain the intended meaning.
    • The clause '夫冬温夏凉不失四时之和所以适身也' emphasizes the importance of seasonal balance for health, but it requires careful parsing to maintain the intended meaning.
    • The clause '美色淑资幽闲娱乐不致思欲之惑所以通神也' emphasizes the importance of inner peace and detachment, but it requires careful parsing to maintain the intended meaning.
    • The clause '车服威仪知足无求所以一志也' emphasizes simplicity and mental clarity, but it requires careful parsing to maintain the intended meaning.
    • The clause '八音五色以悦视听所以导心也' emphasizes the importance of aesthetic harmony, but it requires careful parsing to maintain the intended meaning.
    • The clause '凡此皆以养寿而不能斟酌之者反以速患' warns against excess or imbalance, but it requires careful parsing to maintain the intended meaning.
    • The clause '古之至人恐下' expresses a concern with spiritual decline, but it requires careful parsing to maintain the intended meaning.

    My foolish heart has not yet wished to enter the Dao at this point. To eat sweet and fragrant foods, wear light and beautiful garments, understand the interplay of yin and yang, and hold official positions—these are what I desire. Strong bones, smooth and lustrous complexion, aging without decline, prolonging life, seeing far, and remaining in the world for a long time; cold, heat, dampness, and wind cannot harm, ghosts, spirits, and myriad insects dare not offend, weapons and creatures cannot approach, anger, joy, slander, and praise do not weigh one down—only then is it valuable. People, though ignorant of techniques, can live to 120 if they nurture themselves properly. With a little understanding of the Dao, one can reach 240 years; with further cultivation, 480 years. Those who fully understand the principles can achieve immortality, but they will not become immortals. The way to nourish life is simply not to harm oneself. In winter, warmth; in summer, coolness; not losing the harmony of the four seasons, thus adapting to the body. Beautiful appearance, refined qualities, seclusion, and entertainment, not leading to thoughts of desire, thus connecting with the divine. Clothing, carriage, dignity, contentment without desire, thus focusing the mind. The eight tones and five colors to please the ears and eyes, thus guiding the heart. All of this is for nurturing life; those who cannot measure it properly will hasten their own suffering. Ancient sages feared the lower realms. [18]

    才之子不识事宜流遯不还故絶其源故有上士别牀中士异被服药百裹不如独卧五音使人耳聋五味使人口爽茍能节宣其宜适抑扬其通塞者不以减年得其益也凡此之譬犹水火用之过当反为害也不知其经脉损伤血气不足内理空踈髄脑不实体已先病故为外物所犯因气寒酒色以发之耳若本充实岂有病也夫逺思彊记伤人忧喜悲哀伤人喜乐过差忿怒不解伤人汲汲所愿伤人隂阳不顺伤人有所伤者数种而独戒于房中岂不惑哉男女相成犹天地相生也所以神气导飬使人不失其和天地得交接之道故无终竟之限人失交接之道故有伤残之期能避 [19]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [19]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 才之子不识事宜流遯不还故絶其源 - Complex phrase meaning 'The descendants of the talented do not recognize propriety and drift away without returning, thus cutting off the source.'
    • 上士别牀中士异被 - Idiomatic phrase meaning 'The superior person uses separate beds, the middling person uses different quilts.'
    • 服药百裹不如独卧 - Idiomatic phrase meaning 'Taking a hundred doses of medicine is not as good as sleeping alone.'
    • 五音使人耳聋五味使人口爽 - Idiomatic phrase meaning 'The five tones deafen the ears, the five flavors dull the palate.'
    • 苟能节宣其宜适抑扬其通塞者不以减年得其益也 - Complex phrase meaning 'If one can regulate and moderate what is appropriate, and control what is open and blocked, they will not shorten their life but will gain benefits.'
    • 凡此之譬犹水火用之过当反为害也 - Idiomatic phrase meaning 'All of these things are like water and fire; if used excessively, they become harmful.'
    • 不知其经脉损伤血气不足内理空踈髓脑不实体已先病故为外物所犯因气寒酒色以发之耳 - Complex phrase meaning 'Not knowing the damage to the meridians and the insufficiency of blood and qi, the internal organs are hollow and the marrow and brain are not solid, the body is already diseased, so it is attacked by external things, and is triggered by cold, alcohol, and lust.'
    • 若本充实岂有病也 - Idiomatic phrase meaning 'If it were originally full, how could there be a disease?'
    • 夫逺思彊记伤人忧喜悲哀伤人喜乐过差忿怒不解伤人汲汲所愿伤人阴阳不顺伤人 - List of things that harm a person: 'Excessive thinking and strong memory harm a person, sorrow and joy and grief harm a person, excessive joy and anger harm a person, excessive desire harm a person, and imbalance of yin and yang harm a person.'
    • 有所伤者数种而独戒于房中岂不惑哉 - Idiomatic phrase meaning 'There are several things that cause harm, yet only sexual intercourse is warned against; is this not confusing?'
    • 男女相成犹天地相生也 - Idiomatic phrase meaning 'The union of men and women is like the interaction of heaven and earth.'
    • 所以神气导飬使人不失其和天地得交接之道故无终竟之限人失交接之道故有伤残之期能避 - Complex phrase meaning 'Therefore, the cultivation of spirit and qi guides people to maintain harmony. Heaven and earth gain the way of interaction, so there is no limit to their interaction. People lose the way of interaction, so there is a time for injury and disability. It can be avoided.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 才之子 - Refers to the descendants of talented individuals.
    • 上士 - Refers to the superior person in Confucian hierarchy.
    • 中士 - Refers to the middling person in Confucian hierarchy.
    • 五音 - Refers to the five tones in traditional Chinese music.
    • 五味 - Refers to the five flavors in traditional Chinese medicine and cuisine.
    • 经脉 - Refers to the meridians in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 血气 - Refers to blood and qi (vital energy) in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 阴阳 - Refers to the concept of yin and yang in traditional Chinese philosophy.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The phrase '才之子不识事宜流遯不还故絶其源' is elliptical and requires interpretation to clarify the relationship between the descendants of the talented and the source being cut off.
    • The phrase '上士别牀中士异被' is idiomatic and requires interpretation to clarify the meaning of 'separate beds' and 'different quilts.'
    • The phrase '服药百裹不如独卧' is idiomatic and requires interpretation to clarify the metaphorical meaning of 'sleeping alone.'
    • The phrase '五音使人耳聋五味使人口爽' is idiomatic and requires interpretation to clarify the metaphorical meaning of 'deafening the ears' and 'dulling the palate.'
    • The phrase '苟能节宣其宜适抑扬其通塞者不以减年得其益也' is complex and requires interpretation to clarify the relationship between regulation, moderation, and benefits.
    • The phrase '凡此之譬犹水火用之过当反为害也' is idiomatic and requires interpretation to clarify the metaphorical meaning of 'water and fire.'
    • The phrase '不知其经脉损伤血气不足内理空踈髓脑不实体已先病故为外物所犯因气寒酒色以发之耳' is complex and requires interpretation to clarify the relationship between internal damage and external factors.
    • The phrase '若本充实岂有病也' is idiomatic and requires interpretation to clarify the meaning of 'originally full.'
    • The phrase '夫逺思彊记伤人忧喜悲哀伤人喜乐过差忿怒不解伤人汲汲所愿伤人阴阳不顺伤人' is a list and requires interpretation to clarify the relationship between the listed items and harm.
    • The phrase '有所伤者数种而独戒于房中岂不惑哉' is idiomatic and requires interpretation to clarify the meaning of 'confusing.'
    • The phrase '男女相成犹天地相生也' is idiomatic and requires interpretation to clarify the metaphorical meaning of 'union of men and women.'
    • The phrase '所以神气导飬使人不失其和天地得交接之道故无终竟之限人失交接之道故有伤残之期能避' is complex and requires interpretation to clarify the relationship between spirit and qi, harmony, and interaction.

    The descendants of the talented do not recognize propriety and drift away without returning, thus cutting off the source. The superior person uses separate beds, the middling person uses different quilts. Taking a hundred doses of medicine is not as good as sleeping alone. The five tones deafen the ears, the five flavors dull the palate. If one can regulate and moderate what is appropriate, and control what is open and blocked, they will not shorten their life but will gain benefits. All of these things are like water and fire; if used excessively, they become harmful. Not knowing the damage to the meridians and the insufficiency of blood and qi, the internal organs are hollow and the marrow and brain are not solid, the body is already diseased, so it is attacked by external things, and is triggered by cold, alcohol, and lust. If it were originally full, how could there be a disease? Excessive thinking and strong memory harm a person, sorrow and joy and grief harm a person, excessive joy and anger harm a person, excessive desire harm a person, and imbalance of yin and yang harm a person. There are several things that cause harm, yet only sexual intercourse is warned against; is this not confusing? The union of men and women is like the interaction of heaven and earth. Therefore, the cultivation of spirit and qi guides people to maintain harmony. Heaven and earth gain the way of interaction, so there is no limit to their interaction. People lose the way of interaction, so there is a time for injury and disability. It can be avoided. [19]

    众伤之事得隂阳之术则不死之道也天地昼分而夜合一嵗三百六十交而精气和合故能生产万物而不穷人能则之可以长存次有服气得其道则邪气不得入治身之本要其余吐纳导引之术及念体中万神有舍影守形之事一千七百余条及四时首向责已谢过卧起早晏之法皆非真道可以教初学者以正其身人受精飬体服气炼形则万神自守其真不然者则荣衞枯悴万神自逝悲思所留者也人为道不负其本而逐其末告以至言而不能信见约要之书谓之轻浅而不尽服诵观夫太清北神中经之属以此自疲至死无益不亦悲哉又人苦多事少能弃世独往山居穴 [20]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [20]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 众伤之事 - Refers to serious injuries or harm to the body, particularly those that could be fatal.
    • 得阴阳之术 - Means 'to acquire the techniques of yin and yang,' referring to practices that balance the opposing forces of nature.
    • 不死之道也 - Means 'the way to immortality,' indicating the belief that these techniques can prevent death.
    • 天地昼分而夜合一 - Describes the natural cycle of day and night, with day and night merging into a single whole.
    • 岁三百六十交 - Refers to the 360 days of a year, emphasizing the cyclical nature of time.
    • 精气和合 - Means 'the harmonization of essence and qi (vital energy),' suggesting the balance of internal and external forces.
    • 生产万物 - Means 'to produce all things,' indicating the generative power of nature.
    • 穷人 - Refers to those who are poor or lacking in resources.
    • 则之 - Means 'to follow' or 'to adhere to,' indicating the adoption of these techniques.
    • 可以长存 - Means 'can live forever,' suggesting the potential for eternal life.
    • 次有服气 - Refers to the practice of 'breathing in qi,' a form of internal alchemy.
    • 得其道 - Means 'to attain the method,' indicating mastery of the technique.
    • 邪气不得入 - Means 'evil qi cannot enter,' suggesting protection from negative influences.
    • 治身之本要 - Means 'the fundamental principles of body cultivation,' indicating the core practices of health and longevity.
    • 其余吐纳导引之术 - Refers to additional techniques such as breathing exercises and physical guidance.
    • 念体中万神 - Means 'to think about the myriad spirits within the body,' indicating a focus on the spiritual aspects of the body.
    • 有舍影守形之事 - Means 'the practice of guarding the body's shadow,' suggesting a method of preserving the body's essence.
    • 一千七百余条 - Refers to over 1,700 different practices or methods.
    • 四时首向 - Means 'the direction of the four seasons,' indicating the alignment with natural cycles.
    • 责已谢过 - Means 'to take responsibility and apologize,' suggesting self-reflection and accountability.
    • 卧起早晏之法 - Refers to the practice of waking up early and going to bed late, indicating a disciplined lifestyle.
    • 真道 - Means 'the true path,' suggesting the genuine way to achieve immortality.
    • 初学者 - Means 'beginners,' indicating those new to the practice.
    • 以正其身 - Means 'to correct one's body,' suggesting the improvement of one's physical and spiritual state.
    • 人受精飬体 - Means 'a person receives essence and nourishes the body,' indicating the importance of internal cultivation.
    • 服气炼形 - Refers to the practice of breathing in qi and refining the body, suggesting a holistic approach to health and longevity.
    • 万神自守其真 - Means 'the myriad spirits will naturally protect their true nature,' suggesting the preservation of one's spiritual essence.
    • 荣衞枯悴 - Means 'the vital energies become withered and weak,' indicating a decline in health.
    • 万神自逝 - Means 'the myriad spirits will naturally depart,' suggesting a loss of spiritual vitality.
    • 悲思所留者也 - Means 'those who are left behind in sorrow and thought,' indicating the emotional impact of losing one's spiritual essence.
    • 人为道不负其本 - Means 'a person practicing the path does not abandon their roots,' suggesting the importance of staying true to one's origins.
    • 逐其末 - Means 'to pursue the trivial,' indicating a focus on superficial aspects rather than the core principles.
    • 告以至言 - Means 'to tell the highest truths,' suggesting the transmission of profound wisdom.
    • 不能信 - Means 'cannot believe,' indicating skepticism or disbelief.
    • 约要之书 - Means 'concise and essential books,' suggesting books that are brief but contain important information.
    • 谓之轻浅 - Means 'to consider them light and shallow,' indicating a lack of depth or seriousness.
    • 不亦悲哉 - Means 'is it not sad,' expressing a sense of pity or regret.
    • 又人苦多事少 - Means 'a person suffers from many troubles and few joys,' indicating a life filled with difficulties.
    • 能弃世独往山居穴 - Means 'to be able to abandon the world and live alone in a mountain cave,' suggesting a life of seclusion and simplicity.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 阴阳之术 - Refers to the practices of balancing yin and yang, a fundamental concept in Chinese philosophy and medicine.
    • 不死之道 - Refers to the pursuit of immortality, a common theme in Chinese alchemy and spiritual practices.
    • 天地昼分而夜合一 - Describes the natural cycle of day and night, reflecting the cyclical nature of time and the universe.
    • 岁三百六十交 - Refers to the 360 days of a year, emphasizing the cyclical nature of time and the importance of following natural rhythms.
    • 精气和合 - Refers to the balance of essence and qi, a key concept in Chinese medicine and alchemy.
    • 生产万物 - Refers to the generative power of nature, reflecting the belief in the interconnectedness of all things.
    • 穷人 - Refers to those who are poor or lacking in resources, suggesting a concern for the well-being of the less fortunate.
    • 服气 - Refers to the practice of breathing in qi, a form of internal alchemy that aims to enhance physical and spiritual health.
    • 治身之本要 - Refers to the fundamental principles of body cultivation, including diet, exercise, and mental discipline.
    • 吐纳导引之术 - Refers to techniques such as breathing exercises and physical guidance, which are used to enhance health and longevity.
    • 念体中万神 - Refers to the belief in the presence of numerous spirits within the body, reflecting the holistic view of health in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 有舍影守形之事 - Refers to the practice of guarding the body's shadow, a metaphor for preserving the body's essence and vitality.
    • 四时首向 - Refers to the direction of the four seasons, emphasizing the importance of aligning with natural cycles.
    • 责已谢过 - Refers to the practice of self-reflection and accountability, reflecting the importance of moral and ethical behavior.
    • 卧起早晏之法 - Refers to the practice of waking up early and going to bed late, suggesting a disciplined and healthy lifestyle.
    • 真道 - Refers to the genuine path to achieving immortality, emphasizing the importance of following authentic teachings.
    • 初学者 - Refers to beginners, suggesting the importance of starting with the basics and building a strong foundation.
    • 以正其身 - Refers to the improvement of one's physical and spiritual state, reflecting the holistic approach to health and well-being.
    • 人受精飬体 - Refers to the importance of internal cultivation, including the intake of essence and the nourishment of the body.
    • 服气炼形 - Refers to the practice of breathing in qi and refining the body, suggesting a holistic approach to health and longevity.
    • 万神自守其真 - Refers to the preservation of one's spiritual essence, reflecting the belief in the interconnectedness of the physical and spiritual realms.
    • 荣衞枯悴 - Refers to the decline in health, indicating the consequences of neglecting internal cultivation.
    • 万神自逝 - Refers to the loss of spiritual vitality, suggesting the importance of maintaining a balanced and healthy lifestyle.
    • 悲思所留者也 - Refers to the emotional impact of losing one's spiritual essence, reflecting the deep connection between physical and emotional well-being.
    • 人为道不负其本 - Refers to the importance of staying true to one's origins, reflecting the value placed on tradition and heritage.
    • 逐其末 - Refers to the pursuit of trivial matters, suggesting the importance of focusing on core principles rather than superficial aspects.
    • 告以至言 - Refers to the transmission of profound wisdom, reflecting the importance of passing down authentic teachings.
    • 不能信 - Refers to skepticism or disbelief, suggesting the challenges of accepting new ideas or teachings.
    • 约要之书 - Refers to concise and essential books, suggesting the importance of clear and direct communication.
    • 谓之轻浅 - Refers to the perception of something as light and shallow, indicating a lack of depth or seriousness.
    • 不亦悲哉 - Refers to a sense of pity or regret, expressing the emotional impact of certain situations.
    • 又人苦多事少 - Refers to a life filled with difficulties and few joys, suggesting the challenges of modern life.
    • 能弃世独往山居穴 - Refers to the practice of seclusion and simplicity, reflecting the value placed on inner peace and spiritual growth.

    The techniques of yin and yang can be used to treat serious injuries, and they are the way to immortality. The heavens divide into day and night, and they unite as one. In a year, there are 360 days of interaction, and the essence and qi harmonize, thus they can produce all things and not leave anyone in poverty. If one follows these techniques, they can live forever. Next, there is the practice of breathing in qi. If one attains the method, evil qi cannot enter. This is the fundamental principle of body cultivation, and there are also other techniques such as breathing exercises, physical guidance, and thinking about the myriad spirits within the body. There are over 1,700 different practices, and there are also the directions of the four seasons, taking responsibility and apologizing, and the practice of waking up early and going to bed late. These are not the true path and cannot be taught to beginners to correct their bodies. A person receives essence and nourishes their body, and breathes in qi and refines their body, then the myriad spirits will naturally protect their true nature. If not, then the vital energies become withered and weak, and the myriad spirits will naturally depart, leaving behind those who are left in sorrow and thought. A person practicing the path does not abandon their roots but pursues the trivial. They are told the highest truths but cannot believe, and they see concise and essential books, considering them light and shallow, and not fully understanding them. They read and recite books such as the Taiqing Beishen Zhongjing and tire themselves out, to no avail. Is it not sad? Moreover, a person suffers from many troubles and few joys, and can abandon the world and live alone in a mountain cave. [20]

    处者以道教之终不能行是非仁人之意也但知房中闭气节其思虑适饮食则得道也吾先师初着九节都解指韬形遯尤为开明四极九室诸经万三千首为以示始渉门庭者采女具受诸要以教王王试之有验殷王传彭祖之术屡欲秘之乃下令国中有传彭祖之道者诛之又欲害祖以絶之祖知之乃去不知所之其后七十余年闻人于流沙之国西见之王不常行彭祖之术得夀三百歳气力丁壮如五十时得郑女妖婬王失道而殂俗间言传彭祖之道杀人者由于王禁之故也后有黄山君者修彭祖之术数百年犹有少容彭祖既去乃追论其言以为彭祖经【出神仙传】 [21]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [21]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 處者以道教之終不能行 - The practitioner teaches Daoism but ultimately cannot put it into practice.
    • 是非仁人之意也 - This is not the intention of a benevolent person.
    • 但知房中閉氣節其思慮適飲食則得道也 - They only know that by practicing sexual techniques, controlling breath, and moderating thoughts and diet, one can attain the Dao.
    • 吾先師初著九節都解指韬形遯 - My master initially wrote *Nine Sections of Comprehensive Explanation*, which points to the art of hiding one's form and escaping.
    • 尤为开明四极九室诸经万三千首 - Especially elucidating the *Four Extremes*, *Nine Chambers*, and various scriptures with ten thousand and three thousand chapters.
    • 為以示始渉門庭者 - To show those who first enter the gate.
    • 采女具受諸要以教王王试之有驗 - The concubine received all the essentials and taught King Wang, who tested it and found it effective.
    • 殷王傳彭祖之術 - King of Yin transmitted the techniques of Pengzu.
    • 屢欲秘之 - He often wanted to keep it secret.
    • 乃下令國中有傳彭祖之道者誅之 - He then issued an order that anyone in the country who transmitted Pengzu's teachings would be executed.
    • 又欲害祖以絶之 - He also wanted to harm Pengzu to cut off the transmission.
    • 祖知之乃去不知所之 - Pengzu knew about it and left, his whereabouts unknown.
    • 其后七十余年聞人於流沙之國西見之 - After more than seventy years, someone in the Western Land of Flowing Sands saw him.
    • 王不常行彭祖之術得夀三百歳氣力丁壯如五十時 - King Wang did not often practice Pengzu's techniques, but he lived to be three hundred years old, with the strength and vigor of a fifty-year-old.
    • 得鄭女妖婬王失道而殂 - He obtained a Venerable Lady, who was licentious, and King Wang lost his way and died.
    • 俗間言傳彭祖之道殺人者由於王禁之故也 - People say that those who transmit Pengzu's teachings are killed because of King Wang's prohibition.
    • 後有黄山君者修彭祖之術數百年猶有少容 - Later, there was Huangshan Jun, who practiced Pengzu's techniques for several hundred years and still had a youthful appearance.
    • 彭祖既去乃追論其言以為彭祖經 - After Pengzu left, people compiled his teachings into the *Pengzu Classic*.
    • 【出神仙傳】 - [From the *Biographies of Immortals*].

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 處者 - Refers to a practitioner or scholar of Daoism.
    • 道教 - Refers to Daoism, a philosophical and religious tradition of Chinese origin.
    • 房中 - Refers to sexual techniques or practices.
    • 閉氣 - Refers to breath control or meditation techniques.
    • 九節都解 - Refers to a Daoist text or treatise.
    • 韬形遯 - Refers to the art of hiding one's form and escaping, a concept in Daoist alchemy and immortality practices.
    • 四极九室 - Refers to specific texts or chapters in Daoist literature.
    • 采女 - Refers to a concubine or female attendant.
    • 殷王 - Refers to King of Yin, a historical figure.
    • 彭祖 - Refers to Pengzu, a legendary figure in Chinese mythology known for his longevity and Daoist practices.
    • 流沙之國 - Refers to a mythical or distant land, often associated with the Western Regions.
    • 鄭女 - Refers to a Venerable Lady, likely a concubine or female attendant.
    • 黄山君 - Refers to Huangshan Jun, a historical or legendary figure.
    • 神仙傳 - Refers to the *Biographies of Immortals*, a collection of stories about immortals and Daoist practitioners.

    Proper Nouns

    • 處者 - Practitioner or scholar of Daoism.
    • 道教 - Daoism.
    • 房中 - Sexual techniques or practices.
    • 閉氣 - Breath control or meditation techniques.
    • 九節都解 - A Daoist text or treatise.
    • 韬形遯 - The art of hiding one's form and escaping.
    • 四极九室 - Specific texts or chapters in Daoist literature.
    • 采女 - A concubine or female attendant.
    • 殷王 - King of Yin.
    • 彭祖 - Pengzu.
    • 流沙之國 - A mythical or distant land.
    • 鄭女 - A Venerable Lady.
    • 黄山君 - Huangshan Jun.
    • 神仙傳 - The *Biographies of Immortals*.

    The practitioner teaches Daoism but ultimately cannot put it into practice, which is not the intention of a benevolent person. They only know that by practicing sexual techniques, controlling breath, and moderating thoughts and diet, one can attain the Dao. My master initially wrote Nine Sections of Comprehensive Explanation, which points to the art of hiding one's form and escaping, especially elucidating the Four Extremes, Nine Chambers, and various scriptures with ten thousand and three thousand chapters, to show those who first enter the gate. The concubine received all the essentials and taught King Wang, who tested it and found it effective. King of Yin transmitted the techniques of Pengzu. He often wanted to keep it secret and then issued an order that anyone in the country who transmitted Pengzu's teachings would be executed. He also wanted to harm Pengzu to cut off the transmission. Pengzu knew about it and left, his whereabouts unknown. After more than seventy years, someone in the Western Land of Flowing Sands saw him. King Wang did not often practice Pengzu's techniques, but he lived to be three hundred years old, with the strength and vigor of a fifty-year-old. He obtained a Venerable Lady, who was licentious, and King Wang lost his way and died. People say that those who transmit Pengzu's teachings are killed because of King Wang's prohibition. Later, there was Huangshan Jun, who practiced Pengzu's techniques for several hundred years and still had a youthful appearance. After Pengzu left, people compiled his teachings into the Pengzu Classic. [From the Biographies of Immortals] [21]

    魏伯阳
    　　魏伯阳者吴人也本髙门之子而性好道术后与弟子三人入山作神丹丹成知弟子心懐未尽乃试之曰丹虽成然先宜与犬试之若犬飞然后人可服耳若犬死即不可服乃与犬食犬即死伯阳谓诸弟子曰作丹唯恐不成今既成而犬食之死恐是未合神明之意服之恐复如犬为之奈何弟子曰先生当服之否伯阳曰吾背违世路委家入山不得道亦耻复还死之与生吾当服之乃服丹入口即死弟子顾视相谓曰作丹以求长生服之即死当奈此何独一弟子曰吾师非常人
    也服此而死得无意耶因乃取丹服之亦死余二弟子相谓曰所以得丹者欲求长生耳今服之即死焉用此为不服此药自可更得数十嵗在世间也遂不服乃共出山欲为伯阳及死弟子求棺木二子去后伯阳即起将所服丹内死弟子及白大口中皆起弟子姓虞遂皆仙去道逢入山伐木人乃作手书与乡里人寄谢二弟子乃始懊恨伯阳作参同契五行相凡三卷其説似周易其实假借爻象以论作丹之意而世之儒者不知神丹之事多作隂阳注之殊失其防矣【出神仙传】 [22]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [22]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 本髙門之子 - Literally 'originally the son of a high-ranking family,' indicating noble lineage.
    • 性好道術 - Means 'has a nature inclined towards the Daoist arts,' referring to a natural aptitude for Daoist practices.
    • 作神丹 - Means 'to create the elixir of immortality,' a central theme in Daoist alchemy.
    • 若犬飛 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'if the dog flies,' indicating success in the test.
    • 若犬死 - Means 'if the dog dies,' indicating failure in the test.
    • 唯恐不成 - Means 'fearing it would not be successful,' expressing concern about the outcome.
    • 殊失其防 - Means 'greatly misses the point,' indicating a misunderstanding of the intended meaning.
    • 假借爻象 - Means 'to borrow the symbols of the *Yi Jing*,' referring to the use of the *I Ching* (Book of Changes) symbols in alchemical discourse.
    • 隂陽注之 - Means 'to annotate it with yin and yang,' indicating an interpretation based on the yin-yang theory.
    • 無不利於事也 - Means 'nothing is unhelpful in matters,' suggesting that all efforts are beneficial.

    Hai Bo Yang
    Hai Bo Yang, a native of Wu, was originally from a high-ranking family and had a natural inclination towards Daoist practices. He entered the mountains with three disciples to create the elixir of immortality. After the elixir was completed, he knew that his disciples' minds were not fully resolved, so he tested it by saying, "Although the elixir is made, it should first be tried on a dog. If the dog flies, then it can be taken by humans. If the dog dies, it cannot be taken." He then fed the elixir to a dog, which immediately died. Hai Bo Yang said to his disciples, "In making the elixir, I feared it would not be successful. Now that it is made, yet the dog has died after taking it, it seems it does not align with the divine will. If I take it, I might also die like the dog. What should I do?" The disciples said, "Should you take it, Master?" Hai Bo Yang replied, "I have abandoned the world and left my family to enter the mountains. If I do not attain immortality, I will be ashamed to return. Death or life, I will take it." He then took the elixir, and upon consuming it, he died. The disciples looked at each other and said, "We made the elixir to seek immortality, but now that we have taken it and died, what good is this?" Only one disciple said, "My master is no ordinary person. If he dies after taking it, could there be no meaning in it?" He then took the elixir and also died. The other two disciples said to each other, "We obtained the elixir to seek immortality. Now that we have taken it and died, what use is it? If we do not take this medicine, we can still live for several more decades in the world." So they did not take it and left the mountain together, intending to find two coffins for Hai Bo Yang and the dead disciple. After the two disciples left, Hai Bo Yang rose and placed the elixir he had taken into the mouths of the dead disciple and the white dog, and they both came back to life. The disciple's surname was Yu, and they all ascended to immortality. On the way, they met a woodcutter entering the mountain and wrote a letter by hand to the villagers to thank the two disciples. The two disciples then felt regret. Hai Bo Yang wrote the Zhen Gong Qi (True and False Classic), which consists of three volumes discussing the transformation of the five elements. Its discourse resembles the I Ching (Book of Changes), but in reality, it borrows the symbols of the I Ching to discuss the meaning of making the elixir. The scholars of the world, not knowing about the elixir of immortality, often annotate it with yin and yang, greatly missing the point. [From the Shenxian Zhuan (Biographies of Immortals)] [22]

    太平广记卷二
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　太平广记卷三
    　　神仙三
    　　汉武帝
    　　汉武帝
    　　汉孝武皇帝景帝子也未生之时景帝梦一赤彘从云中下直入崇芳阁景帝而坐阁下果有赤龙如雾来蔽戸牖宫内嫔御望阁上有丹霞蓊蔚而起霞灭见赤龙盘廻栋间景帝召占者姚翁以问之翁曰吉祥也此阁必主命世之人攘夷狄而获嘉瑞为刘宗盛主也然亦大妖景帝使王夫人移居崇芳阁欲以顺姚翁之言也乃改崇芳阁为猗兰殿旬余景帝梦神女捧日以授王夫人夫人吞之十四月而生武帝景帝曰吾梦赤气化为赤龙占者以为吉可名之吉至三嵗景帝抱于膝上抚念之知其心藏洞彻试问児乐为天子否对曰由天不由児愿每日居宫垣在陛下前戏弄亦不敢逸豫以失子道景帝闻而愕然加敬而训之他日复抱之几前 [23]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [23]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 命世之人 - A person destined to rule or lead the world, often used to describe a great leader.
    • 攘夷狄 - To repel or drive away the barbarians, referring to the Han Dynasty's efforts to defend against nomadic tribes.
    • 嘉瑞 - A good omen or auspicious sign.
    • 大妖 - A great or significant omen, often with a dual meaning of both good and bad.
    • 神女捧日 - A divine woman holding the sun, a symbol of divine favor and blessing.
    • 吞之 - To swallow, often used metaphorically to describe conception.
    • 由天不由児 - "It is determined by heaven, not by the child," indicating that the child's fate is preordained.
    • 逸豫 - To indulge in pleasure or ease, often with a negative connotation.
    • 子道 - The proper conduct of a son, emphasizing filial piety and respect.
    • 愕然 - To be surprised or astonished.
    • 訓之 - To educate or instruct.
    • 戲弄 - To play or engage in playful activities.
    • 宮垣 - The walls or grounds of a palace, symbolizing the imperial domain.
    • 陛下 - An honorific term for the emperor, used by subjects to address him.
    • 膝上 - On the lap, indicating a close and intimate relationship.
    • 心藏洞徹 - A clear and profound mind, suggesting deep insight and understanding.
    • 命之 - To name or give a name.
    • 吉 - A name meaning "good" or "auspicious."

    Translation:

    The Taiping Guangji Volume Two
    　　The Qinding Siku Quanshu
    　　The Taiping Guangji Volume Three
    　　The Immortals Three
    　　Emperor Wu of Han
    　　Emperor Wu of Han

    Emperor Wu of Han was the son of Emperor Jing. Before his birth, Emperor Jing dreamed of a red pig descending from the clouds and entering directly into the Chongfang Pavilion. Emperor Jing sat beneath the pavilion, and indeed, a red dragon like mist came to obscure the doors and windows. The concubines and attendants inside the palace saw a crimson cloud rising from the pavilion, which then dissipated, revealing a red dragon circling the rafters. Emperor Jing summoned the diviner Yao Weng and asked him about the dream. Yao Weng said, "This is a sign of good fortune. This pavilion must be the home of a person destined to rule the world, repel the barbarians, and receive auspicious signs. It will be the greatest of the Liu clan, but it is also a great omen." Emperor Jing ordered Lady Wang to move into the Chongfang Pavilion to follow Yao Weng's words. He then renamed the Chongfang Pavilion as the Yilan Hall. After ten days, Emperor Jing dreamed of a divine woman holding the sun and presenting it to Lady Wang. Lady Wang swallowed it, and after fourteen months, Emperor Wu was born. Emperor Jing said, "I dreamed of a red vapor transforming into a red dragon. The diviners interpreted this as a good omen, so I named him Ji." By the age of three, Emperor Jing held him on his lap, caressed him, and thought deeply about him. He knew that the child had a clear and profound mind. He tested the child by asking if he would be happy to become emperor. The child replied, "It is determined by heaven, not by me. I wish to live daily within the palace grounds, playing before Your Majesty, but I will not indulge in pleasure to the point of neglecting my duties as a son." Emperor Jing was astonished and added his respect, then educated him. Another day, he held the child on the table again. [23]

    试问児悦习何书为朕言之乃诵伏羲以来羣圣所録隂阳诊及龙图防数万言无一字遗落至七嵗圣彻过人景帝令改名彻及即位好神仙之道常祷祈名山大川五岳以求神仙元封元年正月甲子登嵩山起道宫帝斋七日祠讫乃还至四月戊辰帝闲居承华殿东方朔董仲舒在侧忽见一女子着青衣美丽非常帝愕然问之女对曰我墉宫玉女王子登也向为王母所使从昆仑山来语帝曰闻子轻四海之禄寻道求生降帝王之位而屡祷山岳勤哉有似可教者也从今日清斋不闲人事至七月七日王母暂来也帝下席跪诺言讫玉女忽然不知所在帝问东方朔此何人朔曰是西王母紫兰宫玉女常传使命往来扶桑出入灵洲交关常阳传 [24]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [24]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 无一字遗落 - Means 'not a single word omitted,' emphasizing the child's thoroughness.
    • 从今日清斋不闲人事 - Means 'from today, fast and abstain from worldly affairs.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 伏羲 - The legendary first sage king of China, associated with the creation of Chinese civilization.
    • 景帝 - Refers to Emperor Jing of Han, the second emperor of the Western Han dynasty.
    • 元封元年 - The first year of the Yuánfēng era, a reign title of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 嵩山 - One of the Five Sacred Mountains in China, associated with Daoist practices.
    • 承华殿 - A palace within the imperial complex.
    • 东方朔 - A famous scholar and court jester during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 董仲舒 - A prominent Confucian scholar and philosopher during the Han dynasty.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West, a Daoist deity associated with immortality and longevity.
    • 昆仑山 - A mythical mountain in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortality and the divine.
    • 扶桑 - A mythical land in Chinese mythology, often associated with the sun and the east.
    • 灵洲 - A mythical island in Chinese mythology, often associated with the divine and the spiritual.
    • 常阳传 - A mythical figure or place in Chinese mythology, often associated with the sun and the divine.

    Proper Nouns

    • 伏羲 - The legendary first sage king of China.
    • 景帝 - Emperor Jing of Han.
    • 元封元年 - The first year of the Yuánfēng era.
    • 嵩山 - One of the Five Sacred Mountains.
    • 承华殿 - A palace within the imperial complex.
    • 东方朔 - A famous scholar and court jester.
    • 董仲舒 - A prominent Confucian scholar and philosopher.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West.
    • 昆仑山 - A mythical mountain.
    • 扶桑 - A mythical land.
    • 灵洲 - A mythical island.
    • 常阳传 - A mythical figure or place.

    The emperor asked the child what he had been studying and reciting. The child recited tens of thousands of words on topics recorded by sages since Fuxi, including yin-yang diagnosis, dragon charts, and turtle diagrams, not a single word omitted. By the age of seven, his wisdom surpassed that of ordinary people, and Emperor Jing ordered his name changed to "Chè." After ascending the throne, Emperor Chè became interested in Daoist practices for immortality, frequently praying to famous mountains, rivers, and the Five Sacred Mountains in search of immortality. In the first year of the Yuánfēng era, on the day Jiazi in the first month, he ascended Mount Song and built a Daoist temple. He fasted for seven days, completed the sacrifice, and then returned. By the day Wuchen in the fourth month, he was leisurely staying in the Chéng Huá Palace, with the scholars Dongfang Shuo and Dong Zhongshu by his side. Suddenly, he saw a beautiful woman dressed in green, and the emperor was startled and asked her. She replied, "I am the Jade Maiden Wang Ziteng from the Palace of Jade." She explained that she had been sent by the Queen Mother of the West from Mount Kunlun. She told the emperor that he valued the pursuit of immortality over the riches of the world and his imperial status, and that he had diligently prayed to mountains, making him worthy of instruction. She instructed the emperor to fast and abstain from worldly affairs until the Queen Mother of the West visited on the seventh day of the seventh month. The emperor bowed and agreed, after which the woman disappeared. The emperor asked Dongfang Shuo who the woman was. Dongfang Shuo identified her as a Jade Maiden from the Purple Orchid Palace of the Queen Mother of the West, who frequently conveys messages and travels between the Land of the Rising Sun and the Spirit Isle. [24]

    言都阿母昔出配北烛仙人近又召还使领命禄真灵官也帝于是登延灵之台盛斋存道其四方之事权委于冡宰焉到七月七日乃修除宫掖设坐大殿以紫罗荐地燔百和之香张云锦之帏燃九光之灯列玉门之酌蒲萄之醴宫监香果为天宫之帝乃盛服立于阶下敇端门之内不得有妄窥者内外寂谧以云驾到夜二更之后忽见西南如白云起郁然直来迳趋宫庭须臾转近闻云中箫鼓之声人马之响半食顷王母至也悬投殿前有似鸟集或驾龙虎或乗白麟或乗白或乗轩车或乗天马羣仙数千光耀庭宇既至从官不复知所在唯见王母乗紫云之辇驾九色斑龙别有五十天仙侧近鸾舆皆长丈余同执防旄之节佩金刚灵玺 [25]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [25]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 言都阿母 - Refers to the 'mother of Xuan Du,' a title or name of a figure in the text. The meaning is unclear, so it will be retained as is.
    • 出配北烛仙人 - Means 'was sent to be married to the Northern Candle Immortal.' '出配' is an archaic term for marriage, and '北烛仙人' is a specific figure.
    • 召还使领命禄真灵官也 - Means 'was recalled and appointed as the true immortal official in charge of destiny and fate.' '命禄' refers to destiny and fate, and '真灵官' is a title for an immortal official.
    • 帝于是登延灵之台 - Means 'the emperor then ascended the platform of extending immortality.' '延灵之台' is a specific platform.
    • 盛斋存道 - Means 'held a grand ceremony to preserve the Dao.' '盛斋' refers to a grand ceremony, and '存道' means to preserve the Dao.
    • 四方之事权委于冡宰 - Means 'the affairs of the four directions were entrusted to the Grand Steward.' '冡宰' is a title for the Grand Steward.
    • 到七月七日乃修除宫掖 - Means 'on the seventh day of the seventh month, the palace and its courtyards were prepared.' '修除' means to prepare or clean.
    • 设坐大殿以紫罗荐地 - Means 'a seat was set up in the great hall with purple silk spread on the ground.' '荐地' means to spread on the ground.
    • 燔百和之香 - Means 'burned the fragrance of a hundred herbs.' '百和之香' refers to a complex fragrance made from many herbs.
    • 张云锦之帏 - Means 'spread the cloud brocade curtains.' '云锦' refers to a type of brocade.
    • 燃九光之灯 - Means 'lit the lamps of nine lights.' '九光之灯' refers to lamps with nine lights.
    • 列玉门之 - Means 'arranged the jade gate's wine.' '玉门' refers to a jade gate, and '' refers to wine.
    • 酌蒲萄之醴 - Means 'poured the grape liquor.' '蒲萄之醴' refers to grape wine.
    • 宫监香果为天宫之 - Means 'the palace attendants and fragrant fruits were arranged as offerings for the Heavenly Palace.' '天宫之' refers to offerings for the Heavenly Palace.
    • 帝乃盛服立于阶下 - Means 'the emperor then dressed in his finest attire and stood at the foot of the stairs.' '盛服' means finest attire.
    • 敇端门之内不得有妄窥者 - Means 'ordered that no one inside the main gate should dare to peek.' '妄窥' means to peek without permission.
    • 内外寂谧以云驾到 - Means 'inside and outside were silent, waiting for the arrival of the cloud chariot.' '云驾' refers to a chariot drawn by clouds.
    • 夜二更之后 - Means 'after the second watch of the night.' '二更' refers to the second watch, which is from 9 PM to 11 PM.
    • 忽见西南如白云起郁然直来迳趋宫庭 - Means 'suddenly, a cloud like a white mist rose from the southwest and came directly to the palace.' '郁然' means rising in a dense manner.
    • 须臾转近闻云中箫鼓之声人马之响 - Means 'soon it turned closer, and the sound of flutes and drums and the noise of people and horses were heard from the cloud.' '箫鼓之声' refers to the sound of flutes and drums, and '人马之响' refers to the noise of people and horses.
    • 半食顷王母至也 - Means 'after half a meal's time, the Queen Mother arrived.' '半食顷' means half a meal's time, which is about 30 minutes.
    • 悬投殿前有似鸟集或驾龙虎或乗白麟或乗白或乗轩车或乗天马 - Means 'she suspended herself before the hall, resembling a flock of birds, sometimes riding dragons and tigers, sometimes riding white stags, sometimes riding white deer, sometimes riding chariots, and sometimes riding heavenly horses.' '悬投' means to suspend or descend, '鸟集' means a flock of birds, '龙虎' means dragons and tigers, '白麟' means white stags, '白' means white deer, '轩车' means chariots, and '天马' means heavenly horses.
    • 群仙数千光耀庭宇 - Means 'thousands of immortals shone brightly in the courtyard.' '光耀' means to shine brightly.
    • 既至从官不复知所在唯见王母乗紫云之辇驾九色斑龙 - Means 'once they arrived, the attendants no longer knew where they were, only seeing the Queen Mother riding a purple cloud chariot drawn by a nine-colored spotted dragon.' '从官' means attendants, '紫云之辇' means a purple cloud chariot, and '九色斑龙' means a nine-colored spotted dragon.
    • 别有五十天仙侧近鸾舆 - Means 'there were also fifty heavenly immortals near the phoenix carriage.' '鸾舆' means a phoenix carriage.
    • 皆长丈余同执防旄之节佩金刚灵玺 - Means 'all were over ten feet tall, holding the same ceremonial staff and wearing the diamond divine seal.' '防旄之节' means a ceremonial staff, and '金刚灵玺' means a diamond divine seal.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 都阿母 - Refers to a figure in Chinese mythology, possibly a goddess or a deity associated with the Xuan Du region.
    • 北烛仙人 - Refers to the Northern Candle Immortal, a figure in Chinese mythology associated with the north and light.
    • 真灵官 - Refers to a title for an immortal official in charge of destiny and fate.
    • 延灵之台 - Refers to a platform used for rituals and ceremonies to extend immortality.
    • 盛斋存道 - Refers to a grand ceremony to preserve the Dao, which is a central concept in Daoism.
    • 冡宰 - Refers to the Grand Steward, a high-ranking official in ancient China.
    • 七月七日 - Refers to the seventh day of the seventh month, a significant date in Chinese culture, often associated with the Qixi Festival.
    • 宫掖 - Refers to the palace and its courtyards.
    • 百和之香 - Refers to a complex fragrance made from many herbs, often used in religious and ceremonial contexts.
    • 云锦 - Refers to a type of brocade, often used in royal and ceremonial settings.
    • 九光之灯 - Refers to lamps with nine lights, often used in religious and ceremonial contexts.
    • 玉门 - Refers to a jade gate, a symbol of purity and nobility.
    • 蒲萄之醴 - Refers to grape wine, a luxury item in ancient China.
    • 天宫 - Refers to the Heavenly Palace, a place in Chinese mythology associated with the gods.
    • 云驾 - Refers to a chariot drawn by clouds, often used to describe the arrival of immortals or deities.
    • 王母 - Refers to the Queen Mother, a central figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortality and longevity.
    • 群仙 - Refers to a group of immortals or deities.
    • 鸾舆 - Refers to a phoenix carriage, a symbol of nobility and immortality.
    • 防旄之节 - Refers to a ceremonial staff, often used by officials and immortals.
    • 金刚灵玺 - Refers to a diamond divine seal, a symbol of authority and power.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 言都阿母昔出配北烛仙人近又召还使领命禄真灵官也 - This sentence is a complex statement about the mother of Xuan Du being sent to marry the Northern Candle Immortal and then recalled to be appointed as the true immortal official in charge of destiny and fate.
    • 帝于是登延灵之台盛斋存道其四方之事权委于冡宰焉 - This sentence describes the emperor ascending the platform of extending immortality, holding a grand ceremony to preserve the Dao, and entrusting the affairs of the four directions to the Grand Steward.
    • 到七月七日乃修除宫掖设坐大殿以紫罗荐地燔百和之香张云锦之帏燃九光之灯列玉门之酌蒲萄之醴宫监香果为天宫之 - This long sentence describes the preparations made on the seventh day of the seventh month, including cleaning the palace, setting up a seat, spreading purple silk, burning fragrant herbs, spreading cloud brocade curtains, lighting lamps with nine lights, arranging the jade gate's wine, pouring grape liquor, and arranging offerings for the Heavenly Palace.

    The mother of Xuan Du, known as the Northern Candle Immortal's wife, was once sent to marry the Northern Candle Immortal and was recently recalled to be appointed as the true immortal official in charge of destiny and fate. The emperor then ascended the platform of extending immortality, held a grand ceremony to preserve the Dao, and entrusted the affairs of the four directions to the Grand Steward. On the seventh day of the seventh month, the palace and its courtyards were prepared, and a seat was set up in the great hall with purple silk spread on the ground. The fragrance of a hundred herbs was burned, cloud brocade curtains were spread, and lamps with nine lights were lit. Wine from the jade gate was arranged, and grape liquor was poured. The palace attendants and fragrant fruits were arranged as offerings for the Heavenly Palace. The emperor then dressed in his finest attire and stood at the foot of the stairs, ordering that no one inside the main gate should dare to peek. Inside and outside were silent, waiting for the arrival of the cloud chariot. After the second watch of the night, suddenly a cloud like a white mist rose from the southwest and came directly to the palace. Soon it turned closer, and the sound of flutes and drums and the noise of people and horses were heard from the cloud. After half a meal's time, the Queen Mother arrived. She suspended herself before the hall, resembling a flock of birds, sometimes riding dragons and tigers, sometimes riding white stags, sometimes riding white deer, sometimes riding chariots, and sometimes riding heavenly horses. Thousands of immortals shone brightly in the courtyard. Once they arrived, the attendants no longer knew where they were, only seeing the Queen Mother riding a purple cloud chariot drawn by a nine-colored spotted dragon. There were also fifty heavenly immortals near the phoenix carriage, all over ten feet tall, holding the same ceremonial staff and wearing the diamond divine seal. [25]

    戴天真之冠咸住殿下王母唯扶二侍女上殿侍女年可十六七服青绫之袿容眸流盼神姿清发真美人也王母上殿东向坐着黄金褡防文采鲜明光仪淑穆带飞大绶腰佩分景之劒头上太华髻戴太真晨婴之冠履璚凤文之舄视之可年三十许修短得中天姿掩蔼容顔絶世真人也下车登牀帝跪拜问寒暄毕立因呼帝共坐帝面南王母自设天厨真妙非常丰珍上果芳华百味紫芝萎防芬芳填樏清香之酒非地上所有香气殊絶帝不能名也又命侍女更索桃果须臾以玉盘盛仙桃七颗大如鸭卵形圆青色以呈王母母以四颗与帝三颗自食桃味甘美口有盈味帝食輙其核王母问帝帝曰欲种之母曰此桃三千年一生实中夏地 [26]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [26]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 戴天真之冠 - A metaphor for adopting a pure and unadulterated state, often associated with immortals.
    • 咸住殿下 - Means 'all gathered beneath the hall,' indicating a gathering of people.
    • 唯扶二侍女上殿 - Means 'only supported by two maids up to the hall,' suggesting assistance in ascending.
    • 容眸流盼 - Describes eyes that move gracefully and expressively.
    • 神姿清发 - Means 'divine and clear appearance,' emphasizing an ethereal beauty.
    • 真美人也 - Means 'a true beauty,' affirming the maid's attractiveness.
    • 黄金褡防文采鲜明光仪淑穆 - Describes a golden belt with bright and clear patterns, indicating a dignified and elegant appearance.
    • 带飞大绶腰佩分景之劒 - Describes a belt with flying tassels and a great ribbon, with a sword that divides the light, symbolizing power and grace.
    • 头上太华髻戴太真晨婴之冠 - Describes a hairstyle and a crown, both named after mythical elements.
    • 履璚凤文之舄 - Describes shoes with patterns of jade and phoenix, indicating elegance and nobility.
    • 视之可年三十许修短得中天姿掩蔼容顔绝世 - Means 'looking to be around thirty years old, with a moderate height and an extraordinary beauty,' emphasizing her youthful and captivating appearance.
    • 真人也 - Means 'a true immortal,' affirming her divine status.
    • 下车登牀 - Means 'to get off the carriage and sit on the bed,' indicating a formal setting.
    • 帝跪拜问寒暄毕立 - Means 'the emperor knelt and bowed, asked about well-being, and then stood up,' indicating a ritual of respect.
    • 因呼帝共坐 - Means 'then invited the emperor to sit together,' indicating a gesture of hospitality.
    • 帝面南 - Means 'the emperor faced south,' indicating a position of authority.
    • 王母自设天厨真妙非常 - Means 'the Queen Mother herself set up a heavenly kitchen, truly wonderful and extraordinary,' indicating a feast of divine quality.
    • 丰珍上果芳华百味紫芝萎防芬芳填樏 - Describes a variety of precious fruits and herbs, emphasizing the richness and diversity of the feast.
    • 清香之酒非地上所有 - Means 'fragrant wine not found on earth,' indicating a divine beverage.
    • 香气殊絶帝不能名也 - Means 'the fragrance was so unique that the emperor could not name it,' emphasizing its otherworldly nature.
    • 又命侍女更索桃果 - Means 'then ordered the maids to fetch more peach fruits,' indicating a request for more food.
    • 须臾以玉盘盛仙桃七颗 - Means 'in a moment, they brought seven divine peaches on a jade plate,' indicating the speed and elegance of the service.
    • 大如鸭卵形圆青色 - Means 'as large as duck eggs, round and green in color,' describing the appearance of the peaches.
    • 以呈王母母以四颗与帝三颗自食 - Means 'to present to the Queen Mother, who gave four to the emperor and ate three herself,' indicating a gesture of generosity and equality.
    • 桃味甘美口有盈味 - Means 'the peach tasted sweet and left a lingering flavor,' emphasizing its deliciousness.
    • 帝食辄其核 - Means 'the emperor ate and spit out the pits,' indicating a casual act.
    • 王母问帝帝曰欲种之母曰此桃三千年一生实中夏地 - Means 'the Queen Mother asked the emperor, who said he wanted to plant them. The Queen Mother replied that these peaches take three thousand years to bear fruit in the Central Plains,' indicating the extraordinary nature of the peaches.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions
