    • 戴天真之冠 - A metaphor for adopting a pure and unadulterated state, often associated with immortals.
    • 殿下 - Refers to a hall or palace, indicating a formal setting.
    • 太华髻 - A hairstyle named after Mount Taihu, a mythical mountain.
    • 太真晨婴之冠 - A crown named after mythical elements, indicating divine status.
    • 璚凤文之舄 - Shoes with patterns of jade and phoenix, indicating elegance and nobility.
    • 天厨 - A heavenly kitchen, indicating a divine feast.
    • 仙桃 - Divine peaches, often associated with immortals and longevity.
    • 三千年一生实中夏地 - Indicates the extraordinary nature of the peaches, taking three thousand years to bear fruit in the Central Plains.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 戴天真之冠咸住殿下王母唯扶二侍女上殿 - This sentence is elliptical and grammatically compact, describing the gathering and the Queen Mother's ascent.
    • 黄金褡防文采鲜明光仪淑穆带飞大绶腰佩分景之劒 - This long sequence must be read as a single description of the Queen Mother's attire and accessories.
    • 头上太华髻戴太真晨婴之冠履璚凤文之舄 - This sequence describes her hairstyle and shoes, both named after mythical elements.
    • 视之可年三十许修短得中天姿掩蔼容顔绝世 - This sequence describes her appearance, emphasizing her youthful and captivating beauty.
    • 真人也 - This phrase affirms her divine status.
    • 下车登牀帝跪拜问寒暄毕立 - This sequence describes the emperor's actions, indicating a ritual of respect.
    • 因呼帝共坐帝面南 - This sequence describes the Queen Mother's invitation and the emperor's position, indicating a gesture of hospitality and authority.
    • 王母自设天厨真妙非常丰珍上果芳华百味紫芝萎防芬芳填樏 - This long sequence describes the feast, emphasizing its richness and diversity.
    • 清香之酒非地上所有 - This phrase emphasizes the otherworldly nature of the wine.
    • 香气殊絶帝不能名也 - This phrase emphasizes the unique fragrance.
    • 又命侍女更索桃果须臾以玉盘盛仙桃七颗 - This sequence describes the request and the service, indicating speed and elegance.
    • 大如鸭卵形圆青色以呈王母母以四颗与帝三颗自食 - This sequence describes the peaches and their distribution, indicating generosity and equality.
    • 桃味甘美口有盈味帝食辄其核 - This sequence describes the taste and the emperor's act, emphasizing the deliciousness.
    • 王母问帝帝曰欲种之母曰此桃三千年一生实中夏地 - This sequence describes the conversation, indicating the extraordinary nature of the peaches.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王母 - The Queen Mother, a figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 太华髻 - A hairstyle named after Mount Taihu.
    • 太真晨婴之冠 - A crown named after mythical elements.
    • 璚凤文之舄 - Shoes with patterns of jade and phoenix.
    • 天厨 - A heavenly kitchen.
    • 仙桃 - Divine peaches.
    • 中夏地 - The Central Plains, a region in China.

    Wearing the Crown of Naturalness, all gathered beneath the hall. The Queen Mother was only supported by two maids up to the hall. The maids were around sixteen or seventeen years old, dressed in green silk skirts, with eyes that moved gracefully and expressedively, and a divine and clear appearance, truly beautiful women. The Queen Mother ascended the hall and sat facing east, wearing a golden belt with bright and clear patterns, indicating a dignified and elegant appearance. She wore a belt with flying tassels and a great ribbon, with a sword that divides the light, symbolizing power and grace. Her hairstyle was a Taihu braid, and she wore a crown named after mythical elements. Her shoes had patterns of jade and phoenix, indicating elegance and nobility. Looking at her, she seemed to be around thirty years old, with a moderate height and an extraordinary beauty, truly an immortal. The emperor got off the carriage and sat on the bed, knelt and bowed, asked about well-being, and then stood up. Then he invited the emperor to sit together, with the emperor facing south. The Queen Mother herself set up a heavenly kitchen, truly wonderful and extraordinary, with a variety of precious fruits and herbs, emphasizing the richness and diversity of the feast. There was fragrant wine not found on earth, with a fragrance so unique that the emperor could not name it. Then she ordered the maids to fetch more peach fruits. In a moment, they brought seven divine peaches on a jade plate, as large as duck eggs, round and green in color, to present to the Queen Mother, who gave four to the emperor and ate three herself. The peach tasted sweet and left a lingering flavor, with the emperor eating and spitting out the pits. The Queen Mother asked the emperor, who said he wanted to plant them. The Queen Mother replied that these peaches take three thousand years to bear fruit in the Central Plains. [26]

    薄种之不生帝乃止于坐上酒觞数遍王母乃命诸侍女王子登弹八琅之璈又命侍女董双成吹云和之笙石公子击昆庭之金许飞琼鼓震灵之簧婉凌华拊五灵之石范成君击湘隂之磬叚安香作九天之钧于是众声澈朗灵音骇空又命法婴歌灵之曲歌毕王母曰夫欲修身当营其气太仙真经所谓行益易之道益者益精易者易形能益能易名上仙籍不益不易不离死厄行益易者谓常思灵宝也灵者神也寳者精也子但爱精握固闭气吞液气化为血血化为精精化为神神化为液液化为骨行之不倦神精充溢为之一年易气二年易血三年易精四年易五年易髓六年易骨七年易筋八年易发九年易形形易则变化变化则道成道成 [27]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [27]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 薄种之不生 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'the sparse sowing does not produce'; implies something not taking root or succeeding.
    • 帝乃止于坐上 - Literal translation: 'the emperor then stopped at the seat'; indicates a pause or moment of reflection.
    • 酒觞数遍 - Literal translation: 'the emperor drank wine several times'; suggests a ritual or formal occasion.
    • 王母乃命诸侍女 - Literal translation: 'the Queen Mother then commanded her attendants'; indicates a command or instruction given by a superior.
    • 王子登弹八琅之璈 - Literal translation: 'Prince Wang Zeng played the eight jade zither'; describes a specific musical performance.
    • 又命侍女董双成吹云和之笙 - Literal translation: 'and commanded the attendant Dong Shuangcheng to play the cloud harmonious flute'; another specific musical performance.
    • 石公子击昆庭之金 - Literal translation: 'Shi Gongzi struck the Kunting metal'; another musical performance.
    • 许飞琼鼓震灵之簧 - Literal translation: 'Xu Feiqiong struck the resonant spirit reed'; another musical performance.
    • 婉凌华拊五灵之石 - Literal translation: 'Wan Linghua struck the five spirits stone'; another musical performance.
    • 范成君击湘阴之磬 - Literal translation: 'Fan Chengjun struck the Xiangyin chime'; another musical performance.
    • 叚安香作九天之钧 - Literal translation: 'Jia Anxiang played the nine heavens lute'; another musical performance.
    • 于是众声澈朗 - Literal translation: 'thus, the various sounds were clear and bright'; describes the effect of the music.
    • 灵音骇空 - Literal translation: 'the divine sounds startled the void'; emphasizes the ethereal and powerful nature of the music.
    • 又命法婴歌玄灵之曲 - Literal translation: 'and commanded Fa Ying to sing the divine song'; another musical performance.
    • 歌毕王母曰 - Literal translation: 'after the song ended, the Queen Mother said'; indicates the conclusion of the musical performance and the beginning of a speech.
    • 夫欲修身当营其气 - Literal translation: 'those who wish to cultivate themselves should cultivate their qi (vital energy)'; introduces a philosophical concept.
    • 太仙真经所谓行益易之道 - Literal translation: 'as the True Scripture of the Great Immortals says, the path of increasing ease'; refers to a religious text.
    • 益者益精易者易形 - Literal translation: 'those who increase will increase their essence, and those who make things easy will make their form easy'; a metaphorical statement about spiritual and physical transformation.
    • 能益能易名上仙籍 - Literal translation: 'those who can increase and make things easy will be recorded in the register of the highest immortals'; indicates a reward for spiritual achievement.
    • 不益不易不离死厄 - Literal translation: 'those who do not increase and do not make things easy will not escape the fate of death'; emphasizes the importance of spiritual cultivation.
    • 行益易者谓常思灵宝也 - Literal translation: 'those who practice the path of increasing ease should always think of the spiritual treasure'; a metaphorical statement about the importance of spiritual focus.
    • 灵者神也寳者精也 - Literal translation: 'the spiritual is the divine, and the treasure is the essence'; explains the metaphors used in the previous statement.
    • 子但爱精握固闭气吞液 - Literal translation: 'you should love your essence, hold it firmly, close your breath, and swallow the fluid'; a set of spiritual practices.
    • 气化为血血化为精 - Literal translation: 'qi transforms into blood, and blood transforms into essence'; a description of the transformation process in spiritual cultivation.
    • 精化为神神化为液 - Literal translation: 'essence transforms into the divine, and the divine transforms into fluid'; continuing the transformation process.
    • 液化为骨行之不倦 - Literal translation: 'fluid transforms into bone, and if you do this without tiring'; emphasizing the importance of persistence in spiritual practice.
    • 神精充溢为之一年 - Literal translation: 'the divine and essence will be abundant, and this will be one year'; indicating a period of spiritual growth.
    • 易气二年易血三年 - Literal translation: 'transform qi in two years, transform blood in three years'; outlining a timeline for spiritual transformation.
    • 易精四年易髓五年 - Literal translation: 'transform essence in four years, transform marrow in five years'; continuing the timeline.
    • 易骨六年易筋七年 - Literal translation: 'transform bone in six years, transform sinew in seven years'; continuing the timeline.
    • 易发九年易形形易则变化 - Literal translation: 'transform hair in nine years, transform form, and when the form changes, there will be transformation'; indicating the final stages of spiritual transformation.
    • 变化则道成道成 - Literal translation: 'when there is transformation, the Dao is achieved, and the Dao is achieved'; emphasizing the ultimate goal of spiritual practice.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 王母 - Refers to the Queen Mother of the West, a prominent figure in Chinese mythology and Taoism.
    • 太仙真经 - Refers to the True Scripture of the Great Immortals, a religious text associated with Taoist beliefs.
    • 灵宝 - Refers to spiritual treasures or divine objects in Taoist tradition.
    • 上仙籍 - Refers to the register of the highest immortals, a concept in Taoist cosmology.
    • 玄灵之曲 - Refers to a divine or mystical song, often associated with spiritual practices.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West.
    • 王子登 - A name, likely a musician or attendant.
    • 董双成 - A name, likely a musician or attendant.
    • 石公子 - A name, likely a musician or attendant.
    • 许飞琼 - A name, likely a musician or attendant.
    • 婉凌华 - A name, likely a musician or attendant.
    • 范成君 - A name, likely a musician or attendant.
    • 叚安香 - A name, likely a musician or attendant.
    • 法婴 - A name, likely a musician or attendant.
    • 太仙真经 - The True Scripture of the Great Immortals.
    • 灵宝 - Spiritual treasures or divine objects.
    • 上仙籍 - The register of the highest immortals.
    • 玄灵之曲 - A divine or mystical song.

    Translation

    The sparse planting does not grow, so the emperor paused at his seat and drank wine several times. The Queen Mother then commanded her attendants: Prince Wang Zeng played the eight-lute instrument, and Attendant Dong Shuangcheng played the cloud-harmonious flute. Prince Shi Gong struck the Kunting metal, Xu Feiqiong struck the resonant spirit reed, and Wan Linghua struck the five-spirit stones. Fan Chengjun struck the Xiangyin chime, and Anxiang made the nine-heavenly bell. Thus, the various sounds were clear and bright, and the divine sounds startled the void. She then commanded Fa Ying to sing the divine song. After the song ended, the Queen Mother said, "Those who wish to cultivate themselves should cultivate their qi. As the True Scripture of the Great Immortals says, the path of increasing ease: those who increase will increase their essence, and those who make things easy will make their form easy. Those who can increase and make things easy will be recorded in the register of the highest immortals. Those who do not increase and do not make things easy will not escape the fate of death. Those who practice the path of increasing ease should always think of the spiritual treasure. The spiritual is the divine, and the treasure is the essence. You should love your essence, hold it firmly, close your breath, and swallow the fluid. Qi transforms into blood, and blood transforms into essence. Essence transforms into the divine, and the divine transforms into fluid. Fluid transforms into bone, and if you do this without tiring, the divine and essence will be abundant, and this will be one year. Transform qi in two years, transform blood in three years, transform essence in four years, transform marrow in five years, transform bone in six years, transform sinew in seven years, transform hair in nine years, transform form, and when the form changes, there will be transformation. When there is transformation, the Dao is achieved, and the Dao is achieved."

    [27]

    则为仙人吐纳六气口中甘香欲食芝存得其味防息揖吞从心所适气者水也无所不成至柔之物通致神精矣此元始天王在丹房之中所説防言今敇侍笈玉女李庆孙书録之以相付子善録而修焉于是王母语言既毕啸命官使驾龙严车欲去帝下席叩头请留殷勤王母乃止王母乃遣侍女郭密香与上元夫人相问云王九光之母敬谢但不相见四千余年矣天事劳我致以愆面汉主好道适来视之见某了了似可成进然形慢神秽脑血淫漏五脏不淳关胄彭孛骨无精液脉浮反升肉多精少瞳子不夷三尸狡乱白失时虽当语之以至道殆恐非仙才也吾乆在人间实为臭浊然时复可游望以写细念庸主对坐悒悒不乐夫人可暂来 [28]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [28]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 則為仙人吐納六氣 - Refers to the practice of breathing in and out the six qi (氣), which are fundamental energies in Chinese cosmology. '吐納' means 'breathing in and out,' and '六氣' refers to the six fundamental energies.
    • 口中甘香欲食芝存得其味 - '口中甘香' means 'sweet and fragrant in the mouth,' and '欲食芝' means 'desiring to eat the lingzhi mushroom.' '存得其味' means 'to retain its flavor.'
    • 防息揖吞从心所适 - '防息' means 'to control one's breath,' '揖吞' means 'to bow and swallow,' and '从心所适' means 'to follow one's heart's desire.'
    • 此元始天王在丹房之中所説防言 - '元始天王' refers to the Primordial Heavenly Emperor, a deity in Taoist cosmology. '丹房' means 'elixir room,' and '所説防言' means 'the words spoken.'
    • 今敇侍笈玉女李庆孙书録之以相付子善録而修焉 - '敇' means 'to command,' '侍笈玉女' refers to a jade maiden who serves as an attendant, and '书録之以相付子善録而修焉' means 'to write and record it to hand it over to you, so you may record and cultivate it well.'
    • 王母语言既毕啸命官使驾龙严车欲去 - '王母' refers to the Queen Mother of the West, a figure in Chinese mythology. '语言既毕' means 'having finished speaking,' '啸命' means 'to summon with a whistle,' '官' refers to officials, and '驾龙严车' means 'to ride on a dragon-drawn chariot.'
    • 帝下席叩头请留殷勤王母乃止 - '帝下席' means 'the emperor got off his seat,' '叩头' means 'to bow his head,' and '殷勤' means 'sincerely.'
    • 王母乃遣侍女郭密香与上元夫人相问云 - '遣' means 'to send,' '侍女' refers to a maid, '郭密香' is the name of the maid, and '相问' means 'to ask.'
    • 王九光之母敬谢但不相见四千余年矣 - '王九光' is a name, '母' means 'mother,' '敬谢' means 'to respectfully thank,' and '四千余年矣' means 'for over four thousand years.'
    • 天事劳我致以愆面汉主好道适来视之见某了了似可成进然形慢神秽脑血淫漏五脏不淳关胄彭孛骨无精液脉浮反升肉多精少瞳子不夷三尸狡乱白失时虽当语之以至道殆恐非仙才也 - This is a complex sentence describing the condition of the person being observed. '天事劳我致以愆面' means 'the heavenly affairs have tired me, causing me to look guilty,' '汉主好道' means 'the Han ruler is fond of the Dao,' '见某了了似可成进' means 'I see that he seems to be able to advance,' '形慢神秽' means 'his form is sluggish and his spirit is impure,' '脑血淫漏' means 'his brain and blood are leaking,' '五脏不淳' means 'his five organs are not pure,' '关胄彭孛' means 'his bones are swollen,' '骨无精液' means 'his bones lack essence and fluid,' '脉浮反升' means 'his pulse is floating and rising,' '肉多精少' means 'his flesh is abundant but his essence is scarce,' '瞳子不夷' means 'his pupils are not level,' '三尸狡乱' means 'the three ghosts are cunning and chaotic,' '白失时' means 'the black and white are out of time,' '虽当语之以至道殆恐非仙才也' means 'even if I were to tell him the highest Dao, I fear he is not worthy of becoming a sage.'
    • 吾乆在人间实为臭浊然时复可游望以写细念庸主对坐悒悒不乐夫人可暂来 - '吾乆在人间' means 'I have been in the human world for a long time,' '实为臭浊' means 'it is indeed foul and turbid,' '时复可游望' means 'I can occasionally wander and look around,' '以写细念' means 'to express my fine thoughts,' '庸主对坐悒悒不乐' means 'the ordinary ruler sitting opposite me is unhappy,' '夫人可暂来' means 'you can come for a short while.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元始天王 - The Primordial Heavenly Emperor, a deity in Taoist cosmology.
    • 丹房 - An elixir room, a place for alchemical practices in Taoism.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West, a figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 汉主 - Refers to the ruler of the Han dynasty.
    • 上元夫人 - A goddess in Chinese mythology.
    • 三尸 - The three ghosts, a concept in Chinese alchemy and Taoism, representing the lower self.

    In the practice of breathing in and out the six qi, one desires to eat the lingzhi mushroom, retain its flavor, and control one's breath while following one's heart's desire. Qi is water, and it can accomplish anything, being the most flexible of substances, capable of conveying the essence of the divine. This is what the Primordial Heavenly Emperor said in the elixir room, and now I command the jade maiden Li Qingsun to write and record it, so you may record and cultivate it well. Then, the Queen Mother finished her speech, summoned officials with a whistle, and ordered them to ride on a dragon-drawn chariot to depart. The emperor got off his seat, bowed his head, and sincerely asked her to stay, and she did. The Queen Mother then sent the maid Guo M密香 to ask the Lady of the Upper Yuan, saying: "The mother of Wang Jiuguang respectfully thanks you, but we have not seen each other for over four thousand years." The heavenly affairs have tired me, causing me to look guilty. The Han ruler is fond of the Dao, and I have come to see him. I see that he seems to be able to advance, but his form is sluggish and his spirit is impure. His brain and blood are leaking, his five organs are not pure, his bones are swollen, and his bones lack essence and fluid. His pulse is floating and rising, his flesh is abundant but his essence is scarce, his pupils are not level, the three ghosts are cunning and chaotic, the black and white are out of time. Even if I were to tell him the highest Dao, I fear he is not worthy of becoming a sage. I have been in the human world for a long time, and it is indeed foul and turbid, but I can occasionally wander and look around to express my fine thoughts. The ordinary ruler sitting opposite me is unhappy, and you can come for a short while. [28]

    否若能屈驾当停相须帝见侍女下殿俄失所在须臾郭侍女返上元夫人又遣一侍女答问云阿环再拜上问起居逺隔绛河扰以官事遂替顔色近五千年仰恋光润情系无违密香至奉信承降尊于汉主处闻命之际登当命驾先被太帝君敕使诣洲挍定天元正尔暂住如是当还还便束带愿暂少留帝因问王母不审上元何真也王母曰是三天上元之官统领十万玉女名箓者也俄而夫人至亦闻云中箫鼓之声既至从官文武千余人并是女子年皆十八九许形容明逸多服青衣光彩耀目真灵官也夫人年可二十余天姿精耀灵眸絶朗服青霜之袍云彩乱色非锦非绣不可名字头作三角髻余发散垂至腰戴九云夜光之冠曳六出火玉之 [29]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [29]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 否若能屈驾当停相须 - Formal expression meaning 'if you can deign to stay, I will wait for you.'
    • 帝见侍女下殿俄失所在 - Literary expression describing the sudden disappearance of a servant girl after she leaves the throne room.
    • 须臾郭侍女返 - Means 'soon after, the servant girl named Guo returned.'
    • 上元夫人又遣一侍女答问 - Means 'the Lady of the Upper Yuan sent another servant girl to answer the question.'
    • 阿环再拜上问起居 - Means 'Ahuan bowed twice and inquired about his well-being.'
    • 远隔绛河扰以官事 - Means 'far apart by the River of Crimson, troubled by official duties.'
    • 遂替颜色近五千年 - Means 'his complexion changed as if it had been five thousand years.'
    • 仰恋光润情系无违 - Means 'yearning for the luster, emotions are bound and without contradiction.'
    • 密香至奉信承降尊于汉主处 - Means 'secret fragrance arrived, bearing a message of submission to the Han ruler.'
    • 闻命之际登当命驾 - Means 'upon hearing the command, I will immediately prepare to travel.'
    • 先被太帝君敕使诣玄洲挍定天元正尔暂住 - Means 'first, I was ordered by the Great Emperor to go to the Immortal Isle to verify the heavenly origin and temporarily reside there.'
    • 如是当还还便束带愿暂少留 - Means 'if so, I will return and prepare to depart, wishing to stay a little longer.'
    • 帝因问王母不审上元何真也 - Means 'the emperor then asked the Queen Mother, not knowing what the Upper Yuan really is.'
    • 王母曰是三天上元之官统领十万玉女名箓者也 - Means 'the Queen Mother replied, 'It is the official of the Upper Yuan of the Three Heavens, overseeing ten thousand jade maidens and the list of names.''
    • 俄而夫人至亦闻云中箫鼓之声 - Means 'soon after, the lady arrived, and the sound of flutes and drums in the clouds was heard.'
    • 从官文武千余人并是女子年皆十八九许形容明逸多服青衣光彩耀目真灵官也 - Means 'the attendants, numbering over a thousand, were all women, around eighteen or nineteen years old, with bright and graceful appearances, mostly wearing blue robes, their radiance dazzling, truly divine officials.'
    • 夫人年可二十余天姿精耀灵眸绝朗服青霜之袍云彩乱色非锦非绣不可名字头作三角髻余发散垂至腰戴九云夜光之冠曳六出火玉之 - Means 'the lady was about twenty years old, with a refined and radiant appearance, her eyes exceptionally bright, wearing a robe of frosty blue, the clouds swirling in colors that are neither silk nor embroidery, impossible to name, with a triangular bun on her head and long hair cascading down to her waist, wearing a nine-cloud night-glow crown and a six-petal fire jade robe.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 帝 - Refers to the emperor, likely the Han emperor.
    • 侍女 - Refers to female servants or maids.
    • 上元夫人 - Refers to the Lady of the Upper Yuan, a deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 阿环 - A name, possibly a servant or attendant.
    • 绛河 - Refers to the River of Crimson, a mythical river in Chinese mythology.
    • 密香 - Refers to a secret fragrance, possibly a messenger or a symbol of divine communication.
    • 太帝君 - Refers to the Great Emperor, a deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 玄洲 - Refers to the Immortal Isle, a mythical place in Chinese mythology.
    • 天元 - Refers to the heavenly origin or the origin of the universe.
    • 王母 - Refers to the Queen Mother of the West, a deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 三天上元之官 - Refers to the official of the Upper Yuan of the Three Heavens, a deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 玉女 - Refers to jade maidens, divine attendants in Chinese mythology.
    • 名箓 - Refers to the list of names, possibly a registry of divine beings.
    • 云中箫鼓之声 - Refers to the sound of flutes and drums in the clouds, a mythical phenomenon.
    • 青衣 - Refers to blue robes, often worn by divine beings in Chinese mythology.
    • 九云夜光之冠 - Refers to a nine-cloud night-glow crown, a mythical headdress.
    • 六出火玉之 - Refers to a six-petal fire jade robe, a mythical garment.

    Proper Nouns

    • 帝 - The emperor.
    • 侍女 - Female servants or maids.
    • 上元夫人 - The Lady of the Upper Yuan.
    • 阿环 - A name, possibly a servant or attendant.
    • 绛河 - The River of Crimson.
    • 密香 - A secret fragrance.
    • 太帝君 - The Great Emperor.
    • 玄洲 - The Immortal Isle.
    • 天元 - The heavenly origin.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West.
    • 三天上元之官 - The official of the Upper Yuan of the Three Heavens.
    • 玉女 - Jade maidens.
    • 名箓 - The list of names.
    • 云中箫鼓之声 - The sound of flutes and drums in the clouds.
    • 青衣 - Blue robes.
    • 九云夜光之冠 - A nine-cloud night-glow crown.
    • 六出火玉之 - A six-petal fire jade robe.

    If you can deign to stay, I will wait for you. The emperor saw the servant girl descend from the throne room, but she soon disappeared. Soon after, the servant girl named Guo returned. The Lady of the Upper Yuan then sent another servant girl to answer the question, who said, "Ahuan bowed twice and inquired about your well-being. Far apart by the River of Crimson, troubled by official duties, his complexion changed as if it had been five thousand years. Yearning for the luster, emotions are bound and without contradiction." Secret fragrance arrived, bearing a message of submission to the Han ruler. Upon hearing the command, I will immediately prepare to travel. First, I was ordered by the Great Emperor to go to the Immortal Isle to verify the heavenly origin and temporarily reside there. If so, I will return and prepare to depart, wishing to stay a little longer. The emperor then asked the Queen Mother, not knowing what the Upper Yuan really is. The Queen Mother replied, "It is the official of the Upper Yuan of the Three Heavens, overseeing ten thousand jade maidens and the list of names." Soon after, the lady arrived, and the sound of flutes and drums in the clouds was heard. The attendants, numbering over a thousand, were all women, around eighteen or nineteen years old, with bright and graceful appearances, mostly wearing blue robes, their radiance dazzling, truly divine officials. The lady was about twenty years old, with a refined and radiant appearance, her eyes exceptionally bright, wearing a robe of frosty blue, the clouds swirling in colors that are neither silk nor embroidery, impossible to name, with a triangular bun on her head and long hair cascading down to her waist, wearing a nine-cloud night-glow crown and a six-petal fire jade robe. [29]

    佩垂鳯文林华之绶腰流黄挥精之劒上殿向王母拜王母坐而止之呼同坐北向夫人设厨厨亦精珍与王母所设者相似王母敕帝曰此真元之母尊贵之神汝当起拜帝拜问寒温还坐夫人笑曰五浊之人耽酒营利嗜味淫色固其常也且帝以天子之贵其乱目者倍于凡焉而复于华丽之墟拔嗜欲之根愿无为之事良有志焉王母曰所谓有心哉夫人谓帝曰汝好道乎闻数招方术祭山岳祠神祷河川亦为勤矣勤而不获实有由也汝胎性胎性滛胎性奢胎性酷胎性贼五者恒舍于荣卫之中五脏之内虽获良针固难愈也则使气奔而攻神是故神扰而气竭滛则使精漏而魂疲是故精竭而魂消奢则使真离而魄秽是故命逝而失酷 [30]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [30]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 佩垂鳯文林華之绶 - A decorative belt or sash with phoenix motifs, symbolizing high rank or distinction.
    • 腰流黄挥精之劒 - A sword with yellow flowing energy, likely referring to a ceremonial or symbolic weapon.
    • 上殿向王母拜 - To ascend the hall and pay homage to the Queen Mother, indicating a ritual act of respect.
    • 王母坐而止之 - The Queen Mother sits and halts the proceedings, showing her authority.
    • 呼同坐北向夫人设厨 - Calling the lady facing north to set up the kitchen, suggesting a specific arrangement for the event.
    • 厨亦精珍与王母所设者相似 - The kitchen preparations are also exquisite and similar to those set by the Queen Mother.
    • 此真元之母尊贵之神 - This refers to the true and noble deity, emphasizing the Queen Mother's divine status.
    • 汝当起拜 - You should rise and pay respects, indicating a formal command.
    • 五浊之人耽酒营利嗜味淫色固其常也 - People of the five defilements are addicted to wine, profit, flavors, and lust, which is their usual behavior.
    • 且帝以天子之贵其乱目者倍于凡焉 - And the emperor, being the Son of Heaven, has even more distractions than ordinary people.
    • 而复于华丽之墟拔嗜欲之根 - Yet he must remove the roots of desire in the midst of luxury.
    • 愿无为之事良有志焉 - He wishes to engage in non-action and has good intentions.
    • 所谓有心哉 - This means 'it is said that there is intention,' indicating a recognition of the emperor's efforts.
    • 夫人谓帝曰 - The lady says to the emperor, indicating a direct address.
    • 汝好道乎 - Do you like the Dao? Asking about the emperor's interest in the Way.
    • 闻数招方术祭山岳祠神祷河川亦为勤矣 - Hearing that you frequently invite experts to perform rituals for mountains, rivers, and deities, showing diligence.
    • 勤而不获实有由也 - Despite diligence, not achieving results has reasons.
    • 汝胎性胎性滛胎性奢胎性酷胎性贼五者恒舍于荣卫之中五脏之内 - Your innate nature includes five traits: licentiousness, indulgence, extravagance, harshness, and theft, always present in your body and organs.
    • 虽获良针固难愈也 - Even with good medical treatment, it is difficult to cure, indicating the severity of the problem.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West, a prominent figure in Chinese mythology and Daoism, often associated with longevity and immortality.
    • 北向夫人 - A lady facing north, possibly a high-ranking official or attendant.
    • 五浊之人 - Refers to people of the five defilements, a concept in Buddhist and Daoist thought.
    • 天子之贵 - The Son of Heaven, the emperor, indicating his supreme status.
    • 无为之事 - Non-action or wu wei, a key concept in Daoism, referring to acting in harmony with the natural order.
    • 方术 - Various esoteric practices and techniques, often associated with Daoist and alchemical traditions.
    • 山岳祠神祷河川 - Rituals and prayers to mountains, rivers, and deities, common in ancient Chinese religious practices.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West.
    • 北向夫人 - A lady facing north.
    • 五浊之人 - People of the five defilements.
    • 天子 - The emperor, the Son of Heaven.
    • 方术 - Various esoteric practices and techniques.
    • 山岳祠神祷河川 - Rituals and prayers to mountains, rivers, and deities.

    The emperor, adorned with a phoenix-decorated sash and a sword of flowing golden energy, ascended the hall to pay homage to the Queen Mother. She sat and halted the proceedings, calling the lady facing north to set up the kitchen, which was also exquisite and similar to the preparations made by the Queen Mother. The Queen Mother commanded the emperor, saying, "This is the true and noble deity, a revered and exalted spirit. You should rise and pay respects." The emperor bowed and inquired about her well-being before resuming his seat. The lady smiled and said, "People of the five defilements are addicted to wine, profit, flavors, and lust, which is their usual behavior. Moreover, as the Son of Heaven, the emperor faces even more distractions than ordinary people. Yet, he must remove the roots of desire in the midst of luxury and wish to engage in non-action, which shows good intentions." The Queen Mother replied, "It is said that there is intention." The lady addressed the emperor, asking, "Do you like the Dao?" She heard that he frequently invited experts to perform rituals for mountains, rivers, and deities, showing diligence. Despite this diligence, not achieving results has reasons. Your innate nature includes five traits: licentiousness, indulgence, extravagance, harshness, and theft, always present in your body and organs. Even with good medical treatment, it is difficult to cure. Licentiousness causes the vital energy to rush and attack the spirit, leading to a disturbed spirit and exhausted energy. Indulgence causes the essence to leak and the soul to grow weary, leading to the depletion of essence and the fading of the soul. Extravagance causes the true self to separate and the soul to become impure, leading to the loss of life and the fading of the soul. Harshness and theft further exacerbate these issues." [30]

    则使丧仁而自攻是故失仁而眼乱贼则使心鬭而口干是故内战而外絶此五事者皆是截身之刀锯刳命之斧斤矣虽复志好长生不能遣兹五难亦何为损性而自劳乎然由是得此小益以自知往尔若从今已舍尔五性反诸柔善明务察下慈务矜寃恵务济贫赈务施劳念务存孤惜务及爱身恒为隂徳救济死厄旦夕孜孜不泄精液于是闭诸滛飬汝神放诸奢从至俭勤斋戒节饮食絶五谷去羶腥鸣天鼔饮玉浆荡华池叩金梁按而行之当有异耳今阿母迂天尊之重下降于蟪蛄之窟霄虗之而诣狐鸟之爼且阿母至诫妙唱音验其敬朂节度明修所奉比及百年阿母必能致汝于都之墟迎汝于昆阆之中位以仙官游于十方信吾言 [31]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [31]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 喪仁而自攻 - Losing benevolence (仁) leads to internal conflict and self-harm.
    • 失仁而眼亂 - Losing benevolence (仁) leads to confusion and chaos.
    • 心鬭而口干 - Mental struggle and verbal dryness, likely due to stress or conflict.
    • 内战而外絶 - Internal strife leading to external isolation.
    • 截身之刀锯刳命之斧斤 - Metaphorical expressions describing destructive forces that threaten one's life and well-being.
    • 志好长生不能遣兹五难 - Even a desire for longevity cannot overcome these five difficulties.
    • 反诸柔善明务察下慈务矜寃恵务济贫赈务施劳念务存孤惜务及爱身恒为隂徳救济死厄 - Series of commands or exhortations emphasizing kindness, compassion, and moral actions.
    • 鸣天鼓饮玉浆荡华池叩金梁 - Describes various spiritual practices or rituals.
    • 闭诸淫飬汝神放诸奢从至俭勤斋戒节饮食絶五谷去羶腥 - Commands to abstain from certain behaviors and adopt a disciplined lifestyle.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 仁 - A central concept in Confucianism, referring to benevolence or humaneness.
    • 天尊 - Refers to a high-ranking deity or divine figure.
    • 蟪蛄之窟霄虗之 - Metaphorical references to humble and remote places.
    • 狐鸟之爼 - Refers to a lowly or humble place of worship.
    • 阿母 - Likely a reference to a divine or maternal figure.
    • 都之墟 - Refers to a mythical or spiritual realm.
    • 昆阆之中 - Refers to a celestial or divine place.
    • 仙官 - Refers to a divine or immortal official.
    • 十方 - Refers to the ten directions, often used in Buddhist and Taoist contexts to denote the universe.

    Proper Nouns

    • 阿母 - A divine or maternal figure.
    • 天尊 - A high-ranking deity.
    • 都之墟 - A mythical or spiritual realm.
    • 昆阆之中 - A celestial or divine place.
    • 仙官 - A divine or immortal official.
    • 十方 - The ten directions, often used in Buddhist and Taoist contexts.

    If one loses benevolence (仁) and attacks oneself, then as a result, losing benevolence leads to confusion and chaos. Thus, the heart struggles and the mouth becomes dry, leading to internal strife and external isolation. These five things are like the knife and saw that cut the body and the axe that carves the life. Even if one has the will to live long, these five difficulties cannot be overcome, so why harm one's nature and weary oneself?

    However, by following these small benefits, one can gain self-awareness. If one follows these teachings from now on, abandon the five evils, return to gentleness and goodness, be clear and discerning, be compassionate and just, be merciful and help the poor, be diligent and remember the orphaned, cherish and love oneself, always perform hidden virtues, and rescue those in danger. Day and night, strive diligently without wasting one's essence. Thus, close all lust, cultivate your spirit, discard all luxury, follow extreme frugality, practice fasting and abstinence, moderate diet, abstain from grains, avoid fatty and pungent foods, sound the heavenly drum, drink the jade liquor, cleanse the splendid pool, strike the golden beam, and follow these practices, and there will be something extraordinary.

    Now, the divine mother (阿母) has descended from the heavy weight of the celestial lord (天尊) to the humble cave of the cicada (蟪蛄之窟) and the remote place of the heavens (霄虗之), and has come to the humble altar of the fox and birds (狐鸟之爼). Moreover, the divine mother has given the utmost caution and sung the profound sounds (妙唱音), verifying the respect and encouragement, the clear and measured guidance, the bright and pure offerings. By the time one reaches a hundred years, the divine mother will certainly bring you to the realm of the spirit (都之墟) and welcome you to the celestial place (昆阆之中), appointing you as a divine official (仙官) to roam the ten directions (十方). Believe my words. [31]

    矣子励之哉若不能尔无所言矣帝下席跪谢曰臣受性凶顽生长乱浊面墙不啓无由开达然贪生畏死奉敬神今日受教此乃天也某戢圣命以为身范是小丑之臣当获生活唯垂哀防愿赐上元夫人使帝还坐王母谓夫人曰卿之为戒言甚急切更使未解之人畏于志意夫人曰若其志道将以身投饿虎忘躯破灭蹈火履水固于一志必无忧也其志道则心凝真性嫌惑之徒不畏急言急言之发欲成其志耳阿母既有念必当赐以尸之方耳王母曰此子勤心已乆而不遇良师遂欲毁其正志当疑天下必无仙人是故我发阆宫暂舎尘浊既欲坚其仙志又欲令向化不惑也今日相见令人念之至于尸下方吾甚不惜后三年吾必欲赐 [32]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [32]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 矣子励之哉 - Formal exhortation, meaning 'O son, encourage yourself!'
    • 若不能尔无所言矣 - Conditional statement, meaning 'If you cannot do this, then there is nothing more to say.'
    • 帝下席跪谢曰 - Formal expression of submission and gratitude, meaning 'The emperor got up from his seat, knelt down, and said,'
    • 臣受性凶顽 - Self-description, meaning 'I have a nature that is fierce and stubborn.'
    • 生长乱浊 - Description of one's upbringing, meaning 'grown up in chaos and filth.'
    • 面墙不啓 - Metaphor for being ignorant, meaning 'facing a wall without enlightenment.'
    • 无由开达 - Phrase indicating a lack of means to achieve understanding, meaning 'no way to open and reach enlightenment.'
    • 奉敬神 - Phrase indicating reverence and worship, meaning 'revering and worshipping the gods.'
    • 今日受教 - Phrase indicating receiving instruction, meaning 'today I receive teaching.'
    • 此乃天也 - Phrase indicating divine will, meaning 'this is the will of heaven.'
    • 某戢圣命以为身范 - Phrase indicating obedience to a divine command, meaning 'I will obey the divine command and take it as a model for my life.'
    • 唯垂哀防 - Phrase indicating a request for mercy, meaning 'only show compassion.'
    • 愿赐上元夫人 - Request for assistance, meaning 'I wish to be granted the help of the Lady of the Upper Yuan.'
    • 使帝还坐 - Command, meaning 'to make the emperor sit again.'
    • 王母谓夫人曰 - Formal address, meaning 'The Queen Mother said to the Lady,'
    • 卿之为戒言甚急切 - Phrase indicating a stern warning, meaning 'Your words of warning are very urgent and severe.'
    • 更使未解之人畏于志意 - Phrase indicating a fear of those who do not understand, meaning 'further making those who do not understand afraid of their intentions.'
    • 若其志道将以身投饿虎忘躯破灭蹈火履水固于一志必无忧也 - Complex sentence indicating a commitment to the path, meaning 'If one is committed to the path, they will throw themselves into the mouth of a hungry tiger, forget their body, face destruction, walk through fire and water, and remain steadfast in their resolve, there will be no worries.'
    • 阿母既有念必当赐以尸之方耳 - Phrase indicating a promise, meaning 'Since the Mother has such thoughts, she will surely grant the method of spiritual transformation.'
    • 此子勤心已乆而不遇良师遂欲毁其正志 - Phrase indicating a concern, meaning 'This child has been diligent for a long time but has not met a good teacher, so they are about to destroy their true aspirations.'
    • 当疑天下必无仙人 - Phrase indicating doubt, meaning 'they should doubt that there are no immortals in the world.'
    • 是故我发阆宫暂舎尘浊 - Phrase indicating a reason, meaning 'Therefore, I have left the Palace of Láng to temporarily stay away from the world's impurities.'
    • 既欲坚其仙志又欲令向化不惑也 - Phrase indicating a purpose, meaning 'both to strengthen their aspiration for immortality and to make them follow the path without confusion.'
    • 今日相见令人念之 - Phrase indicating a reflection, meaning 'Today's meeting makes one reflect on this.'
    • 至于尸下方吾甚不惜 - Phrase indicating a sentiment, meaning 'As for the lower realm of spiritual transformation, I do not regret it at all.'
    • 后三年吾必欲赐 - Phrase indicating a promise, meaning 'In three years, I will certainly grant.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 帝 - Refers to an emperor, likely a figure in a mythological or historical context.
    • 王母 - Refers to the Queen Mother, a figure in Chinese mythology associated with immortality and spiritual transformation.
    • 上元夫人 - Refers to the Lady of the Upper Yuan, another figure in Chinese mythology associated with immortality and spiritual transformation.
    • 阆宫 - Refers to the Palace of Láng, a mythical place associated with immortality and spiritual transformation.
    • 尘浊 - Refers to the world's impurities, often used in a spiritual context to describe the mundane world.

    Proper Nouns

    • 帝 - The emperor.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother.
    • 上元夫人 - The Lady of the Upper Yuan.
    • 阆宫 - The Palace of Láng.
    • 尘浊 - The world's impurities.

    O son, encourage yourself! If you cannot do this, then there is nothing more to say. The emperor got up from his seat, knelt down, and said, "I have a nature that is fierce and stubborn, grown up in chaos and filth, facing a wall without enlightenment, no way to open and reach enlightenment. Yet I fear death and revere the gods. Today I receive teaching; this is the will of heaven. I will obey the divine command and take it as a model for my life. I am a lowly minister who should be granted life. Only show compassion and grant me the help of the Lady of the Upper Yuan, so that the emperor may sit again." The Queen Mother said to the Lady, "Your words of warning are very urgent and severe, further making those who do not understand afraid of their intentions." The Lady said, "If one is committed to the path, they will throw themselves into the mouth of a hungry tiger, forget their body, face destruction, walk through fire and water, and remain steadfast in their resolve, there will be no worries. If one is not committed to the path, their heart will be focused on true nature, and those who are suspicious and confused will not fear harsh words. Harsh words are spoken to strengthen their resolve. Since the Mother has such thoughts, she will surely grant the method of spiritual transformation." The Queen Mother said, "This child has been diligent for a long time but has not met a good teacher, so they are about to destroy their true aspirations. They should doubt that there are no immortals in the world. Therefore, I have left the Palace of Láng to temporarily stay away from the world's impurities, both to strengthen their aspiration for immortality and to make them follow the path without confusion. Today's meeting makes one reflect on this. As for the lower realm of spiritual transformation, I do not regret it at all. In three years, I will certainly grant." [32]

    以成丹半剂石象散一具与之则帝不得复停当今匈奴未弥边陲有事何必令其仓卒舎天下之尊而便入林岫但当问笃向之志何如如其改吾方数来王母因拊帝背曰汝用上元夫人至言必得长生可不朂勉帝跪曰臣书之金简以身佩之焉帝又见王母巾笈中有一卷书盛以紫锦之囊帝问此书是仙方不审其目可得瞻盻否王母出以示之曰此五岳真形图也昨青城诸仙就吾请求今当过以付之乃三天太上所出文秘禁重岂汝秽质所宜佩乎今且与汝光生经可以通神劝心也帝下地叩头固请不已王母曰昔上皇清虚元年三天太上道君下观六合瞻河海之长短察丘山之髙卑立天柱而安于地理植五岳而拟诸镇辅 [33]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [33]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 以成丹半剂石象散一具与之 - Give him half a dose of the perfected elixir and one unit of the stone imitation powder.
    • 不得复停 - Cannot stop again, meaning he cannot continue to refrain or delay.
    • 弥边陲有事 - There are matters at the borders, indicating ongoing issues or conflicts at the frontier.
    • 仓卒舎天下之尊 - Suddenly abandon the dignity of the world, suggesting a hasty decision to leave his high position.
    • 入林岫 - Enter the forest and mountains, referring to a retreat into nature.
    • 问笃向之志 - Ask about his steadfast aspirations, inquiring about his true intentions or goals.
    • 如其改 - If he changes, indicating a potential shift in his resolve.
    • 吾方数来 - I will come frequently, suggesting repeated visits.
    • 王母 - Queen Mother, a title often used for the Queen Mother of the West in Taoist mythology.
    • 拊帝背 - Pat the emperor's back, a gesture of comfort or encouragement.
    • 汝用上元夫人至言 - You use the profound words of the Lady of the Upper Yuan, referring to wise counsel.
    • 必得长生 - Will certainly achieve immortality, indicating the promise of eternal life.
    • 可不朂勉 - Can you not strive? urging the emperor to make an effort.
    • 帝跪曰 - The emperor knelt and said, indicating a formal and respectful response.
    • 臣书之金简以身佩之 - I will write it on a golden tablet and wear it on my body, a symbolic act of commitment.
    • 又见王母巾笈中有一卷书 - Also saw a scroll in the Queen Mother's bag, indicating a hidden or secret document.
    • 盛以紫锦之囊 - Stored in a purple silk bag, describing the container of the scroll.
    • 五岳真形图 - True form diagram of the Five Peaks, a sacred map of the five sacred mountains.
    • 昨青城诸仙就吾请求 - Yesterday, the immortals of Qingcheng came to me and requested it, indicating a recent request.
    • 乃三天太上所出 - It was created by the Supreme Lord of the Three Heavens, attributing divine origin.
    • 文秘禁重 - Secret and sacred text, emphasizing the importance and confidentiality of the document.
    • 岂汝秽质所宜佩乎 - How could it be suitable for your impure body? questioning the emperor's worthiness.
    • 今且与汝光生经 - Now, I will give you the *Jingguang Shengjing*, a text that can illuminate the mind and heart.
    • 可以通神劝心也 - It can communicate with the gods and encourage the heart, indicating spiritual and moral benefits.
    • 帝下地叩头固请不已 - The emperor knelt and repeatedly begged, showing persistence and reverence.
    • 昔上皇清虚元年 - In the first year of the Qingxu era of the Upper Emperor, specifying a historical date.
    • 三天太上道君下观六合 - The Supreme Lord of the Three Heavens descended to observe the six directions, indicating a divine inspection.
    • 瞻河海之长短 - Observed the length and breadth of rivers and seas, suggesting a comprehensive view.
    • 察丘山之高卑 - Examined the heights and depths of hills and mountains, indicating a detailed observation.
    • 立天柱而安于地理 - Established the pillars of heaven to stabilize the land, suggesting a cosmic order.
    • 植五岳而拟诸镇辅 - Planted the Five Peaks to serve as stabilizers, indicating the symbolic and functional role of the mountains.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 成丹 - Perfected elixir, a key concept in Taoist alchemy, representing the ultimate goal of achieving immortality.
    • 石象散 - Stone imitation powder, a lesser form of the elixir, used to mimic its effects.
    • 王母 - Queen Mother of the West, a prominent figure in Taoist mythology, often associated with immortality and wisdom.
    • 上元夫人 - Lady of the Upper Yuan, a goddess in Taoist mythology known for her wisdom and guidance.
    • 五岳真形图 - True form diagram of the Five Peaks, a sacred map that is believed to have mystical powers.
    • 青城诸仙 - Immortals of Qingcheng, referring to the legendary immortals associated with the Qingcheng Mountains in Sichuan.
    • 三天太上道君 - Supreme Lord of the Three Heavens, a high-ranking deity in Taoism.
    • 六合 - Six directions, referring to the four cardinal points plus up and down, symbolizing the entire universe.
    • 上皇清虚元年 - The first year of the Qingxu era of the Upper Emperor, a specific historical period in Taoist cosmology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 成丹 - Perfected elixir.
    • 石象散 - Stone imitation powder.
    • 王母 - Queen Mother of the West.
    • 上元夫人 - Lady of the Upper Yuan.
    • 五岳真形图 - True form diagram of the Five Peaks.
    • 青城诸仙 - Immortals of Qingcheng.
    • 三天太上道君 - Supreme Lord of the Three Heavens.
    • 六合 - Six directions.
    • 上皇清虚元年 - The first year of the Qingxu era of the Upper Emperor.
    • 五岳 - Five Peaks (Mount Tai, Mount Hua, Mount Heng, Mount Heng, and Mount Song).

    The emperor was given half a dose of the perfected elixir and one unit of the stone imitation powder. He could no longer refrain from taking them. Currently, the Xiongnu were not yet settled at the borders, and there were ongoing matters there. Why should he suddenly abandon the dignity of the world and retreat into the forest and mountains? Instead, he should be asked about his steadfast aspirations. If he changes, I will come frequently. The Queen Mother patted the emperor's back and said, "You use the profound words of the Lady of the Upper Yuan, and you will certainly achieve immortality. Can you not strive?" The emperor knelt and said, "I will write it on a golden tablet and wear it on my body." The emperor also saw a scroll in the Queen Mother's bag, stored in a purple silk bag. He asked if the book was a divine prescription and whether he could look at it. The Queen Mother showed it to him and said, "This is the True Form Diagram of the Five Peaks. Yesterday, the immortals of Qingcheng came to me and requested it. Now, I will pass it on to them. It was created by the Supreme Lord of the Three Heavens, a secret and sacred text. How could it be suitable for your impure body? Now, I will give you the Jingguang Shengjing, which can communicate with the gods and encourage the heart." The emperor knelt and repeatedly begged. The Queen Mother said, "In the first year of the Qingxu era of the Upper Emperor, the Supreme Lord of the Three Heavens descended to observe the six directions, examining the length and breadth of rivers and seas, the heights and depths of hills and mountains, and established the pillars of heaven to stabilize the land. He planted the Five Peaks to serve as stabilizers." [33]

    贵昆陵以舍仙尊蓬丘以馆真人安水神于极隂之源栖太帝于扶桑之墟于是方丈之阜为理命之室沧浪海岛飬九老之堂祖瀛炎长元流光生凤麟聚窟各为洲名并在沧流大海津之中水则碧黑俱流波则震荡羣精诸仙玉女聚居沧溟其名难测其实分明乃因山源之规矩覩河岳之盘曲陵回阜转山髙陇长周旋逶迤形似书字是故因象制名定实之号书形秘于台而出为真之信诸仙佩之皆如传章道士执之经行山川百神羣尊奉亲近汝虽不正然数访仙泽扣求不忘于道欣子有心今以相与当深奉慎如事君父泄示凡夫必祸及也上元夫人语帝曰阿母今以琼笈妙韫发紫台之文赐汝八防之书五岳真形可谓至珍 [34]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [34]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 貴昆陵 - Refers to the high esteem given to tombs or burial sites, often associated with reverence for ancestors or deities.
    • 舍仙尊蓬丘 - A metaphorical expression where '舍' (to dwell) and '仙尊' (immortal sage) are used to describe a place of residence for immortals, likely referring to a sacred or mythical location.
    • 館真人 - Refers to a place where immortals or enlightened beings reside.
    • 安水神於极隂之源 - Places the water deity in the source of the utmost yin (the most profound or hidden part of the underworld or deep waters).
    • 栖太帝於扶桑之墟 - Sets the Great Emperor (太帝) in the ruins of the mythical tree *Fusang*, which is often associated with the eastern horizon and the rising sun in Chinese mythology.
    • 方丈之阜 - Refers to a high mound or hill, often used in mythological contexts.
    • 理命之室 - A room or place where fate or destiny is managed or determined.
    • 沧浪海岛 - A mythical island in the vast ocean, often associated with immortality and spiritual realms.
    • 飬九老之堂 - A hall where the nine elderly sages are nurtured or honored.
    • 祖瀛炎 - Refers to the primordial ocean and the ancient fire, symbolizing the origins of the universe.
    • 长元流光 - Describes a flow of primordial light or energy.
    • 生凤麟 - Refers to the birth of mythical creatures, symbolizing auspiciousness and harmony.
    • 聚窟 - Refers to a gathering place or a hidden cave, often associated with immortals or treasures.
    • 各为洲名 - Each of these places has its own name, indicating a variety of distinct locations.
    • 并在沧流大海津之中 - All these places are located in the vast, mystical ocean of the *Xuanjin* (a mythical sea).
    • 水则碧黑俱流 - The water flows in both green and black colors, symbolizing the duality of nature.
    • 波则震荡群精 - The waves cause the spirits or energies to vibrate or resonate.
    • 诸仙玉女聚居沧溟 - Various immortals and jade maidens gather in the vast ocean, indicating a realm of divine beings.
    • 其名难测 - Their names are difficult to discern or understand, suggesting their mysterious and transcendent nature.
    • 其实分明 - Their true essence is clear and distinct, despite the difficulty in naming them.
    • 乃因山源之规矩 - The forms of these places are shaped by the natural laws of mountains and rivers.
    • 覩河岳之盘曲 - Observing the winding and twisting forms of rivers and mountains.
    • 陵回阜转 - The hills and mounds turn and twist, creating a complex landscape.
    • 山高陇长 - The mountains are tall and the ridges are long, emphasizing their grandeur.
    • 周旋逶迤 - The landscape twists and turns, creating a winding path.
    • 形似书字 - The shapes resemble written characters, suggesting a connection between nature and written language.
    • 是故因象制名 - Therefore, names are given based on the resemblance to certain forms or symbols.
    • 定实之号 - The names are fixed and definitive.
    • 书形秘于台 - The written forms are kept secret in the *Xuantai* (a mythical place of knowledge).
    • 而出为真之信 - They are revealed as signs or symbols of the immortals.
    • 诸仙佩之 - The immortals wear these symbols, indicating their importance.
    • 皆如传章 - They are all like transmitted texts or scriptures.
    • 道士执之 - The Taoist priests hold these symbols.
    • 经行山川 - They travel through mountains and rivers, indicating a journey or pilgrimage.
    • 百神羣 - Various gods and spirits, indicating a diverse pantheon.
    • 尊奉亲近 - They are revered and approached closely, indicating a deep respect.
    • 汝虽不正 - You, though not perfect, are still valued.
    • 然数访仙泽 - Nevertheless, you frequently seek the grace of the immortals.
    • 扣求不忘于道 - You persistently seek the Dao without forgetting it.
    • 欣子有心 - I am pleased that you have a sincere heart.
    • 今以相与 - I give this to you now.
    • 当深奉慎 - You should deeply respect and be cautious with it.
    • 如事君父 - Treat it as you would treat your ruler or father.
    • 泄示凡夫 - Do not reveal it to ordinary people.
    • 必祸及也 - It will bring misfortune if revealed.
    • 上元夫人语帝曰 - The Lady of the Upper Yuan speaks to the emperor.
    • 阿母今以琼笈妙韫 - Mother is now giving the precious *Qiongji* (a mythical book or scroll).
    • 发紫台之文 - Revealing the writings from the *Zitai* (a mythical place of wisdom).
    • 赐汝八防之书 - Granting you the *Eight Protective Books*.
    • 五岳真形 - The true forms of the Five Sacred Mountains, which are central to Taoist cosmology.
    • 可谓至珍 - They can be considered the utmost treasures.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 贵昆陵 - Refers to the high esteem given to tombs or burial sites, often associated with reverence for ancestors or deities.
    • 蓬丘 - A mythical place often associated with immortals and the afterlife.
    • 太帝 - The Great Emperor, a deity in Chinese mythology, often associated with the sky or the universe.
    • 扶桑 - A mythical tree in Chinese mythology, often associated with the eastern horizon and the rising sun.
    • 方丈 - A high mound or hill, often used in mythological contexts.
    • 理命之室 - A place where fate or destiny is managed or determined, often associated with the Daoist concept of predestination.
    • 沧浪海岛 - A mythical island in the vast ocean, often associated with immortality and spiritual realms.
    • 九老 - The nine elderly sages, often associated with wisdom and longevity in Chinese mythology.
    • 祖瀛炎 - Refers to the primordial ocean and the ancient fire, symbolizing the origins of the universe.
    • 长元流光 - Describes a flow of primordial light or energy, often associated with the creation of the universe.
    • 凤麟 - Mythical creatures, the phoenix and the unicorn, symbolizing auspiciousness and harmony.
    • 聚窟 - A gathering place or a hidden cave, often associated with immortals or treasures.
    • 沧流大海津 - The vast, mystical ocean of the *Xuanjin*, a mythical sea often associated with the underworld or the realm of the immortals.
    • 碧黑俱流 - The water flows in both green and black colors, symbolizing the duality of nature.
    • 震荡群精 - The waves cause the spirits or energies to vibrate or resonate, indicating a dynamic and interconnected universe.
    • 诸仙玉女 - Various immortals and jade maidens, indicating a realm of divine beings.
    • 其名难测 - Their names are difficult to discern or understand, suggesting their mysterious and transcendent nature.
    • 其实分明 - Their true essence is clear and distinct, despite the difficulty in naming them.
    • 山源之规矩 - The natural laws of mountains and rivers, indicating the order and structure of the natural world.
    • 河岳之盘曲 - The winding and twisting forms of rivers and mountains, indicating the complexity and beauty of the natural landscape.
    • 陵回阜转 - The hills and mounds turn and twist, creating a complex landscape.
    • 山高陇长 - The mountains are tall and the ridges are long, emphasizing their grandeur.
    • 周旋逶迤 - The landscape twists and turns, creating a winding path.
    • 形似书字 - The shapes resemble written characters, suggesting a connection between nature and written language.
    • 因象制名 - Names are given based on the resemblance to certain forms or symbols, indicating a symbolic and metaphorical approach to naming.
    • 定实之号 - The names are fixed and definitive, indicating a stable and enduring tradition.
    • 书形秘于台 - The written forms are kept secret in the *Xuantai* (a mythical place of knowledge), indicating a sacred and protected tradition.
    • 真之信 - Signs or symbols of the immortals, indicating a connection to the divine.
    • 道士执之 - The Taoist priests hold these symbols, indicating their importance in religious practice.
    • 经行山川 - They travel through mountains and rivers, indicating a journey or pilgrimage.
    • 百神羣 - Various gods and spirits, indicating a diverse pantheon.

    The high esteem given to tombs and burial sites is to dwell in the sacred places of the immortals, where the immortal sages reside, and the water deity is placed in the source of the utmost yin, while the Great Emperor dwells in the ruins of the mythical tree Fusang. Thus, the high mound becomes the room where fate is managed, and the mythical island in the vast ocean is the hall where the nine elderly sages are nurtured. The primordial ocean and ancient fire give rise to the flow of primordial light, and the mythical creatures, the phoenix and the unicorn, gather in the hidden caves, each of which has its own name, all located in the vast, mystical ocean of the Xuanjin. The water flows in both green and black colors, and the waves cause the spirits to vibrate, as various immortals and jade maidens gather in the vast ocean. Their names are difficult to discern, but their true essence is clear and distinct. The forms of these places are shaped by the natural laws of mountains and rivers, and the winding and twisting forms of rivers and mountains are observed. The hills and mounds turn and twist, the mountains are tall and the ridges are long, the landscape twists and turns, and the shapes resemble written characters. Therefore, names are given based on the resemblance to certain forms or symbols, and the names are fixed and definitive. The written forms are kept secret in the Xuantai and revealed as signs of the immortals, which the immortals wear, all like transmitted texts or scriptures. The Taoist priests hold these symbols and travel through mountains and rivers, as various gods and spirits are revered and approached closely. Though not perfect, you frequently seek the grace of the immortals and persistently seek the Dao without forgetting it. I am pleased that you have a sincere heart and give this to you now. You should deeply respect and be cautious with it, treating it as you would treat your ruler or father. Do not reveal it to ordinary people, for it will bring misfortune if revealed. The Lady of the Upper Yuan spoke to the emperor, saying: "Mother is now giving the precious Qiongji, revealing the writings from the Zitai, granting you the Eight Protective Books and the true forms of the Five Sacred Mountains, which can be considered the utmost treasures." [34]

    且贵上帝之观矣子自非受命合神弗见此文矣今虽得其真形观其妙理而无五帝六甲左右飞之符太隂六丁通真逐玉女之箓太阳六戊招神天光策精之书左乙混沌东之文右庚素収摄杀之律壬癸六遯地八术丙丁入火赤班符六辛入金致黄水月华之法六巳石精金光藏景化形之方子午卯酉八禀十诀六咸仪丑辰未戌地真素诀长生紫书三五顺行寅申巳亥紫度炎光内视中方凡此十二事者当何以召山朝地神摄总万精驱策百束虎豹役蛟龙乎子所谓适知其一未见其他也帝下席叩头曰某下土浊民不识清真今日闻道是生命防遇圣母今当赐以真形修以度世夫人云今告帝应须五帝六甲六丁六 [35]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [35]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 且贵上帝之观矣 - Formal expression meaning 'Moreover, the profound view of the Supreme Deity is esteemed.'
    • 子自非受命合神弗见此文矣 - Means 'You would not see this text unless you were divinely chosen and united with the gods.'
    • 今虽得其真形观其妙理 - Means 'Now, even if you obtain its true form and observe its subtle principles.'
    • 而无五帝六甲左右飞之符 - Means 'but without the talismans of the Five Emperors, Six Jia, and the left and right flying spirits.'
    • 太隂六丁通真逐玉女之箓 - Refers to the talisman of the six Ding stars of the Great Yin, which connects to the true and pursues the jade maidens.
    • 太阳六戊招神天光策精之书 - Refers to the book of the six Wu stars of the Sun, which summons divine light and controls essence.
    • 左乙混沌东之文 - Refers to the text of the left Yi, which is chaotic and eastern.
    • 右庚素収摄杀之律 - Refers to the law of the right Geng, which is pure and controls killing.
    • 壬癸六遯地八术 - Refers to the eight techniques of the six遁 (dun) of Ren and Gui, which are hidden and earthly.
    • 丙丁入火赤班符 - Refers to the red banner of fire entering the Bing and Ding.
    • 六辛入金致黄水月华之法 - Refers to the method of the six Xin entering metal to produce yellow water and lunar light.
    • 六巳石精金光藏景化形之方 - Refers to the method of the six Si, which is the essence of stone, golden light, and transformation into form.
    • 子午卯酉八禀十诀 - Refers to the eight endowments and ten rules of the Zi, Wu, Mao, and You.
    • 六咸仪丑辰未戌地真素诀 - Refers to the pure and true rules of the six Yi, which are the earth's true and pure rules.
    • 长生紫书三五顺行 - Refers to the purple book of longevity, which follows the three and five in sequence.
    • 寅申巳亥紫度炎光内视中方 - Refers to the purple degree of the fiery light, which is internally viewed in the center of the four directions.
    • 凡此十二事者 - Means 'All of these twelve matters.'
    • 当何以召山朝地神摄总万精驱策百束虎豹役蛟龙乎 - Means 'How can one summon the mountain spirits, face the earth gods, control all spirits, drive the hundred ghosts, bind tigers and leopards, and command dragons and snakes?'
    • 子所谓适知其一未见其他也 - Means 'You only know one aspect and have not seen the others.'
    • 帝下席叩头曰 - Means 'The emperor got off his seat and bowed his head, saying...'
    • 某下土浊民不识清真 - Means 'I am a lowly and impure person who does not recognize purity and truth.'
    • 今日闻道 - Means 'Today, I have heard the Dao, which is a matter of life and death, and met the Holy Mother.'
    • 今当赐以真形修以度世 - Means 'Now, I will be granted the true form to cultivate and transcend the world.'
    • 夫人云 - Means 'The lady said...'
    • 今告帝应须五帝六甲六丁六 - Means 'Now, I tell the emperor that he should have the Five Emperors, Six Jia, and Six Ding.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 上帝 - Refers to the Supreme Deity, often associated with the highest deity in Taoist cosmology.
    • 五帝 - Refers to the Five Emperors, often associated with the Five Elements and directions.
    • 六甲 - Refers to the six Jia, a concept in Chinese astrology and divination.
    • 太隂 - Refers to the Great Yin, often associated with the moon and the feminine principle.
    • 太阳 - Refers to the Sun, often associated with the masculine principle.
    • 六丁 - Refers to the six Ding, a concept in Chinese astrology and divination.
    • 六戊 - Refers to the six Wu, a concept in Chinese astrology and divination.
    • 左乙 - Refers to the left Yi, a concept in Chinese astrology and divination.
    • 右庚 - Refers to the right Geng, a concept in Chinese astrology and divination.
    • 壬癸 - Refers to the Ren and Gui, a concept in Chinese astrology and divination.
    • 六遯 - Refers to the six遁 (dun), a concept in Chinese astrology and divination.
    • 丙丁 - Refers to the Bing and Ding, a concept in Chinese astrology and divination.
    • 六辛 - Refers to the six Xin, a concept in Chinese astrology and divination.
    • 六巳 - Refers to the six Si, a concept in Chinese astrology and divination.
    • 子午卯酉 - Refers to the four cardinal directions and their associated hours.
    • 八禀十诀 - Refers to the eight endowments and ten rules, a concept in Chinese astrology and divination.
    • 六咸仪丑辰未戌 - Refers to the six Yi, a concept in Chinese astrology and divination.
    • 长生紫书 - Refers to the purple book of longevity, a concept in Chinese Taoist texts.
    • 三五顺行 - Refers to the three and five in sequence, a concept in Chinese astrology and divination.
    • 寅申巳亥 - Refers to the four cardinal directions and their associated hours.
    • 紫度炎光 - Refers to the purple degree of the fiery light, a concept in Chinese Taoist texts.
    • 内视中方 - Refers to the internal viewing in the center, a concept in Chinese Taoist texts.
    • 山 - Refers to mountain spirits.
    • 地神 - Refers to earth gods.
    • 万精 - Refers to all spirits.
    • 百 - Refers to the hundred ghosts.
    • 虎豹 - Refers to tigers and leopards.
    • 蛟龙 - Refers to dragons and snakes.
    • 圣母 - Refers to the Holy Mother, often associated with the goddess of longevity and immortality in Taoism.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 且贵上帝之观矣 - This phrase introduces a high-level statement about the Supreme Deity.
    • 子自非受命合神弗见此文矣 - This clause sets a condition for seeing the text.
    • 今虽得其真形观其妙理 - This clause describes the current state of obtaining the true form and observing the subtle principles.
    • 而无五帝六甲左右飞之符 - This clause lists the missing elements.
    • 太隂六丁通真逐玉女之箓 - This clause describes a specific talisman.
    • 太阳六戊招神天光策精之书 - This clause describes another specific talisman.
    • 左乙混沌东之文 - This clause describes another specific text.
    • 右庚素収摄杀之律 - This clause describes another specific law.
    • 壬癸六遯地八术 - This clause describes another specific technique.
    • 丙丁入火赤班符 - This clause describes another specific talisman.
    • 六辛入金致黄水月华之法 - This clause describes another specific method.
    • 六巳石精金光藏景化形之方 - This clause describes another specific method.
    • 子午卯酉八禀十诀 - This clause describes another specific rule.
    • 六咸仪丑辰未戌地真素诀 - This clause describes another specific rule.
    • 长生紫书三五顺行 - This clause describes another specific book.
    • 寅申巳亥紫度炎光内视中方 - This clause describes another specific method.
    • 凡此十二事者 - This clause summarizes the twelve matters.
    • 当何以召山朝地神摄总万精驱策百束虎豹役蛟龙乎 - This clause asks a series of questions about the use of these elements.
    • 子所谓适知其一未见其他也 - This clause comments on the limited knowledge.
    • 帝下席叩头曰 - This clause describes the emperor's action and speech.
    • 某下土浊民不识清真 - This clause describes the emperor's self-deprecation.
    • 今日闻道 - This clause means 'Today, I have heard the Dao, which is a matter of life and death, and met the Holy Mother.'
    • 今当赐以真形修以度世 - This clause means 'Now, I will be granted the true form to cultivate and transcend the world.'
    • 夫人云 - This clause means 'The lady said...'
    • 今告帝应须五帝六甲六丁六 - This clause means 'Now, I tell the emperor that he should have the Five Emperors, Six Jia, and Six Ding.'

    Moreover, the profound view of the Supreme Deity is esteemed. You would not see this text unless you were divinely chosen and united with the gods. Now, even if you obtain its true form and observe its subtle principles, but without the talismans of the Five Emperors, Six Jia, and the left and right flying spirits, the talisman of the six Ding stars of the Great Yin, which connects to the true and pursues the jade maidens, the book of the six Wu stars of the Sun, which summons divine light and controls essence, the text of the left Yi, which is chaotic and eastern, the law of the right Geng, which is pure and controls killing, the eight techniques of the six遁 (dun) of Ren and Gui, which are hidden and earthly, the red banner of fire entering the Bing and Ding, the method of the six Xin entering metal to produce yellow water and lunar light, the method of the six Si, which is the essence of stone, golden light, and transformation into form, the eight endowments and ten rules of the Zi, Wu, Mao, and You, the pure and true rules of the six Yi, which are the earth's true and pure rules, the purple book of longevity, which follows the three and five in sequence, the purple degree of the fiery light, which is internally viewed in the center of the four directions, all of these twelve matters, how can one summon the mountain spirits, face the earth gods, control all spirits, drive the hundred ghosts, bind tigers and leopards, and command dragons and snakes? You only know one aspect and have not seen the others. The emperor got off his seat and bowed his head, saying, "I am a lowly and impure person who does not recognize purity and truth. Today, I have heard the Dao, which is a matter of life and death, and met the Holy Mother. Now, I will be granted the true form to cultivate and transcend the world." The lady said, "Now, I tell the emperor that he should have the Five Emperors, Six Jia, and Six Ding." [35]

    符致之术既蒙啓发益无量唯愿告诲济臣饥渇使已枯之木蒙阳之润焦炎之草幸甘雨之溉不敢多陈帝啓叩不已王母又告夫人曰夫真形寳文宫所贵此子守求不已誓以必得故亏科禁特以与之然五帝六甲通真招神此术邈必须清洁至诚殆非流浊所宜施行吾今既赐彼以真形夫人当授之以致之途矣吾尝忆与夫人共登陇朔野及曜真之山视王子童王子立就吾求请太上隐书吾以三元秘言不可传泄于中仙夫人时亦有言见助于子童之言志矣吾既难违来意不独执惜至于今日之事有以相似后造朱火丹陵食味甚好忆此未乆而已七千嵗矣夫人既以告帝篇目十二事毕必当匠而成之縁何令人主 [36]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [36]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 符致之术 - A specific technique or method, possibly related to alchemy or spiritual cultivation.
    • 啓发益无量 - Enlightenment and immense benefit.
    • 告诲济臣饥渇 - To instruct and satisfy my thirst for knowledge.
    • 已枯之木蒙阳之润 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'a dried-up tree receiving the nourishment of the sun.'
    • 焦炎之草幸甘雨之溉 - Another metaphorical expression meaning 'scorched grass receiving the nourishment of sweet rain.'
    • 真形寳文宫所贵 - Valuable texts and techniques highly esteemed in the realm of the divine.
    • 守求不已誓以必得 - To persistently seek and vow to attain.
    • 亏科禁特以与之 - To violate rules and regulations specifically to grant it.
    • 五帝六甲通真招神 - A method involving the Five Emperors and the Six Jia (a system of divination) to communicate with the divine.
    • 清洁至诚 - Purity and sincerity.
    • 流浊所宜施行 - Suitable for those who are impure and insincere.
    • 真形夫人当授之以致之途 - The True Form Lady should instruct him on the path to the divine.
    • 忆与夫人共登陇朔野 - Remembering climbing the mystical mountains and northern wilderness with the Lady.
    • 曜真之山 - A sacred mountain.
    • 太上隐书 - A secret book of the highest level.
    • 三元秘言 - Secret words of the Three Yuan (a concept in Taoism).
    • 中仙 - Intermediate immortals.
    • 匠而成之 - To craft and complete it.
    • 缘何令人主 - Why should this be given to the ruler?

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 符致之术 - Likely refers to a specific alchemical or spiritual technique.
    • 五帝六甲 - Refers to the Five Emperors and the Six Jia, a system of divination in Chinese culture.
    • 真形 - Refers to the true form or essence, often associated with immortals or deities.
    • 宫 - Refers to the divine realm or palace.
    • 中仙 - Refers to intermediate immortals, a concept in Taoist cosmology.
    • 三元 - Refers to the Three Yuan, a concept in Taoism that includes the Three Officials (天官, 地官, 水官).

    Proper Nouns

    • 符致之术 - A specific technique or method.
    • 五帝六甲 - The Five Emperors and the Six Jia.
    • 真形 - The true form or essence.
    • 宫 - The divine realm or palace.
    • 中仙 - Intermediate immortals.
    • 三元 - The Three Yuan.
    • 太上隐书 - A secret book of the highest level.
    • 三元秘言 - Secret words of the Three Yuan.

    The technique of 符致 has been enlightened and has brought immense benefit. I only wish to be instructed and satisfy my thirst for knowledge, so that a dried-up tree may receive the nourishment of the sun, and scorched grass may be blessed by the sweet rain. I dare not speak at length, but the Emperor has repeatedly sought my guidance. The Queen Mother then told the Lady: "The True Form and Precious Texts are highly esteemed in the divine realm. This person has persisted in seeking and vowed to attain it, so I have violated the rules and regulations to grant it to him. However, the method of 五帝六甲通真招神 is profound and requires purity and sincerity; it is not suitable for those who are impure and insincere. I have now granted him the True Form, and you should instruct him on the path to the divine. I recall with the Lady climbing the mystical mountains and northern wilderness, and visiting the sacred mountain of 曜真. We saw 王子童 and 王子立, who immediately sought me out to request the 太上隐书. I believed that the secret words of the Three Yuan should not be divulged to intermediate immortals, and the Lady also spoke, supporting 子童's request. I found it difficult to go against his wishes and did not want to hold back. Today's matter is similar to that. After creating the 朱火丹陵 and tasting the divine fruits, the flavor was excellent. I remember this not long ago, but it has already been seven thousand years. The Lady has already informed the Emperor of the twelve chapters of the text, and after they are completed, they must be crafted into a whole. Why should this be given to the ruler?" [36]

    稽首请乞叩头流血上元夫人曰阿环不茍惜向不持来耳此是太虚羣文真人赤童所出传之既自有男女之限禁又宜授得道者恐彼下才未应得此耳王母色不平乃曰天禁漏泄犯违明科传必其人授必知真者夫人何向下才而説其飞之篇目乎妄説则泄泄而不传是衒天道此禁岂轻于传别敇三官司直推夫人之轻泄也吾之五岳真形太寳乃太上天皇所出其文寳妙而为天仙之信岂复应下授于汉主直以某孜孜之心数请川岳勤修斋戒以求神仙之应志在度世不明师故吾等有以下眄之耳至于教仙之术不复限惜而弗传夫人且有致之方能独执之乎吾今所以授某真形文者非谓其必能得道欲使其精诚有验 [37]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [37]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 稽首请乞叩头流血 - Formal expression of deep reverence and supplication, literally 'bowing with clasped hands and begging until blood flows from the forehead.'
    • 上元夫人曰阿环不茍惜 - 'The Lady of the Upper Yuan said, 'Ahuan is not stingy.' Here, '茍惜' means 'stingy' or 'grudging,' but it carries a tone of reproach.
    • 此是太虚羣文真人赤童所出传之 - 'This is from the *Tai Xu Qun Wen Zhen Ren Chi Tong* (a text or tradition).' The phrase '所出传之' means 'originates from and is transmitted by.'
    • 男女之限禁 - Refers to restrictions based on gender, possibly indicating that the text is meant for male practitioners.
    • 授得道者 - Means 'given to those who have attained the Dao,' suggesting that the text is for advanced practitioners.
    • 恐彼下才未应得此耳 - 'I fear that those of lesser talent are not yet worthy of it.' The phrase '未应得此' means 'not yet worthy of it.'
    • 王母色不平乃曰 - 'The Queen Mother's expression was displeased, and she said.' The phrase '色不平' means 'expression was displeased' or 'looked displeased.'
    • 天禁漏泄犯违明科 - 'The heavenly prohibition has been leaked and violated, defying the clear regulations.' The phrase '天禁' refers to heavenly laws or prohibitions.
    • 传必其人授必知真者 - 'It must be transmitted to the right person, and given to those who know the truth.' The phrase '知真者' means 'those who know the truth.'
    • 夫人何向下才而说其飞之篇目乎 - 'Why, Lady, are you speaking of its flying chapters to those of lesser talent?' The phrase '向下才' means 'to those of lesser talent.'
    • 妄说则泄泄而不传是衒天道 - 'If you speak falsely, it will leak and not be transmitted, thus flaunting the heavenly way.' The phrase '衒天道' means 'flaunting the heavenly way' or 'showing off the Dao.'
    • 别敇三官司直推夫人之轻泄也 - 'I will issue a separate edict to the Three Offices to investigate Lady's light-hearted leakage.' The phrase '轻泄' means 'light-hearted leakage' or 'casual disclosure.'
    • 吾之五岳真形太宝乃太上天皇所出其文寳妙而为天仙之信 - 'My *Wu Yue Zhen Xing Tai Bao* (Five Peaks True Form Great Treasure) was created by the Supreme Heavenly Emperor. Its text is a treasure and a symbol of trust for the Heavenly Immortals.' The phrase '天仙之信' means 'symbol of trust for the Heavenly Immortals.'
    • 岂复应下授于汉主 - 'How could it be passed down to a Han ruler?' The phrase '岂复应' means 'how could it be passed down.'
    • 直以某孜孜之心数请川岳勤修斋戒以求神仙之应志在度世不覩明师 - 'It is only because I have been persistently asking the rivers and mountains, diligently practicing the rituals, and seeking the response of the Immortals, with the intention of transcending the world, and not having seen a true master.' The phrase '孜孜之心' means 'persistent heart' or 'diligent heart.'
    • 故吾等有以下眄之耳 - 'Therefore, we have looked down upon you.' The phrase '以下眄之' means 'looked down upon.'
    • 至于教仙之术不复限惜而弗传 - 'As for the techniques of teaching Immortals, there are no more restrictions or regrets, and they are not transmitted.' The phrase '限惜' means 'restrictions or regrets.'
    • 夫人且有致飞之方能独执之乎 - 'Lady, do you have the method to achieve flying, and can you hold it alone?' The phrase '致飞之方' means 'method to achieve flying.'
    • 吾今所以授某真形文者非谓其必能得道欲使其精诚有验 - 'I am giving you the *Zhen Xing Wen* (True Form Text) today, not because I believe you will certainly attain the Dao, but to test your sincerity and see if it works.' The phrase '精诚有验' means 'sincerity and see if it works.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 上元夫人 - A deity in Chinese mythology, often associated with the moon and the Daoist pantheon.
    • 阿环 - A character in Daoist texts, often depicted as a female deity or immortal.
    • 太虚羣文真人赤童 - A figure or text in Daoist tradition, possibly referring to a specific immortal or a collection of texts.
    • 五岳真形太宝 - A Daoist text or talisman believed to have the power to protect and guide practitioners.
    • 太上天皇 - Refers to the Supreme Heavenly Emperor, a high-ranking deity in Daoism.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West, a prominent figure in Chinese mythology and Daoism.
    • 汉主 - Refers to the rulers of the Han dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 川岳 - Refers to rivers and mountains, often used in Daoist texts to symbolize natural forces and spiritual guidance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 上元夫人 - The Lady of the Upper Yuan.
    • 阿环 - Ahuan.
    • 太虚羣文真人赤童 - The *Tai Xu Qun Wen Zhen Ren Chi Tong*.
    • 五岳真形太宝 - The *Wu Yue Zhen Xing Tai Bao*.
    • 太上天皇 - The Supreme Heavenly Emperor.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West.
    • 汉主 - The Han rulers.
    • 川岳 - Rivers and mountains.

    Bowing with clasped hands and begging until blood flows from the forehead, the Lady of the Upper Yuan said, "Ahuan is not stingy. I did not bring it earlier. This is from the Tai Xu Qun Wen Zhen Ren Chi Tong, and it is transmitted by the Tai Xu Qun Wen Zhen Ren Chi Tong. It has its own restrictions based on gender and should be given to those who have attained the Dao. I fear that those of lesser talent are not yet worthy of it." The Queen Mother's expression was displeased, and she said, "The heavenly prohibition has been leaked and violated, defying the clear regulations. It must be transmitted to the right person, and given to those who know the truth. Why, Lady, are you speaking of its flying chapters to those of lesser talent? If you speak falsely, it will leak and not be transmitted, thus flaunting the heavenly way. I will issue a separate edict to the Three Offices to investigate Lady's light-hearted leakage. My Wu Yue Zhen Xing Tai Bao was created by the Supreme Heavenly Emperor. Its text is a treasure and a symbol of trust for the Heavenly Immortals. How could it be passed down to a Han ruler? It is only because I have been persistently asking the rivers and mountains, diligently practicing the rituals, and seeking the response of the Immortals, with the intention of transcending the world, and not having seen a true master. Therefore, we have looked down upon you. As for the techniques of teaching Immortals, there are no more restrictions or regrets, and they are not transmitted. Lady, do you have the method to achieve flying, and can you hold it alone? I am giving you the Zhen Xing Wen today, not because I believe you will certainly attain the Dao, but to test your sincerity and see if it works." [37]

    求仙之不惑可以诱进向化之徒又欲令悠悠者知天地间有此灵真之事足以却不信之狂夫耳吾意在此也此子性气滛暴服精不纯何能得成真仙浮空参差十方乎勤而行之适可度于不死耳明科所云非长生难闻道难也行之难非行之难也终之难良匠能与人规矩不能使人必巧也何足之夫人谢曰谨受命矣但环畴昔倒景君无常先生二君传灵飞之约以四千年一传女授女不授男太上科禁已表于昭生之符矣环受书以来并贤大女即抱兰凡传六十八女子固不可授男也伏见扶广山青真小童受六甲飞于太甲中元凡十二事与环所受者同青真是环入火弟子所受六甲未闻别授于人彼男官也今止敇取之将以授 [38]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [38]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 求仙之不惑 - Unwavering pursuit of immortality or transcendence.
    • 可以诱进向化之徒 - Can be used to guide and inspire those who seek transformation.
    • 足以却不信之狂夫耳 - Sufficient to refute the skepticism of the ignorant.
    • 吾意在此也 - My intention lies in this.
    • 此子性气淫暴 - This person's temperament is licentious and violent.
    • 服精不纯 - Their practice of cultivating essence is not pure.
    • 何能得成真仙浮空参差十方乎 - How could they achieve true immortality and float in the void, transcending all directions?
    • 勤而行之适可度于不死耳 - If they diligently practice, they may attain a state of immortality.
    • 明科所云非长生难闻道难也 - As stated in the clear regulations, it is not that immortality is difficult or the Way is hard to hear.
    • 行之难非行之难也终之难 - The difficulty lies not in the act itself, but in seeing it through.
    • 良匠能与人规矩不能使人必巧也 - A skilled craftsman can teach the rules, but cannot guarantee that the student will become skilled.
    • 何足之 - How can this be considered significant?
    • 夫人谢曰谨受命矣 - The woman replied, 'I humbly accept your instructions.'
    • 但环畴昔倒景君无常先生二君传灵飞之约 - But the ring, which I received from Lord Daojing and Master Wuchang, was entrusted to me by two gentlemen.
    • 以四千年一传女授女不授男 - It is passed down every four thousand years from woman to woman, not to men.
    • 太上科禁已表于昭生之符矣 - The supreme regulations have already been inscribed on the talisman of eternal life.
    • 环受书以来并贤大女即抱兰 - Since I received the book, along with the virtuous daughter, I have embraced the orchid.
    • 凡传六十八女子固不可授男也 - It has been passed down to sixty-eight women, and it is indeed not to be given to men.
    • 伏见扶广山青真小童受六甲飞于太甲中元 - I have observed the young boy of Qingzhen in Fuguang Mountain, who received the Six Jia talisman in the middle of the Taijia period.
    • 凡十二事与环所受者同 - All twelve items are the same as those I received.
    • 青真是环入火弟子所受六甲未闻别授于人 - Qingzhen is the disciple who received the Six Jia in the fire, and I have not heard of it being passed to anyone else.
    • 彼男官也今止敇取之将以授 - He is a male official, and now I am ordered to take it and pass it on.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 求仙 - The pursuit of immortality or transcendence, a common theme in Chinese alchemical and Taoist texts.
    • 不惑 - Derived from the Confucian concept of 'thirty without confusion,' referring to unwavering resolve.
    • 向化之徒 - Those who seek spiritual transformation or enlightenment.
