    • 太上科禁 - Refers to the supreme regulations or decrees of the Taoist canon, often associated with immortality and spiritual practices.
    • 昭生之符 - A talisman or amulet symbolizing eternal life or enlightenment.
    • 六甲 - A term in Taoist and alchemical texts, often associated with talismans or magical formulas.
    • 扶广山 - A mythical or sacred mountain in Taoist lore, often associated with immortality and spiritual practices.
    • 青真小童 - A reference to a young boy associated with the Taoist deity Qingzhen, often linked to immortality and spiritual cultivation.

    Proper Nouns

    • 太上科禁 - The supreme regulations or decrees of the Taoist canon.
    • 昭生之符 - A talisman or amulet symbolizing eternal life or enlightenment.
    • 六甲 - A term in Taoist and alchemical texts, often associated with talismans or magical formulas.
    • 扶广山 - A mythical or sacred mountain in Taoist lore, often associated with immortality and spiritual practices.
    • 青真小童 - A reference to a young boy associated with the Taoist deity Qingzhen, often linked to immortality and spiritual cultivation.
    • 倒景君 - A title or name associated with a Taoist deity or sage.
    • 无常先生 - A title or name associated with a Taoist deity or sage.

    The unwavering pursuit of immortality can guide and inspire those who seek transformation, and it is also intended to make the ignorant believe in the existence of such miraculous truths, sufficient to refute the skepticism of the ignorant. This is my intention. This person's temperament is licentious and violent, and their practice of cultivating essence is not pure. How could they achieve true immortality and float in the void, transcending all directions? If they diligently practice, they may attain a state of immortality. As stated in the clear regulations, it is not that immortality is difficult or the Way is hard to hear. The difficulty lies not in the act itself, but in seeing it through. A skilled craftsman can teach the rules, but cannot guarantee that the student will become skilled. How can this be considered significant? The woman replied, "I humbly accept your instructions." But the ring, which I received from Lord Daojing and Master Wuchang, was entrusted to me by two gentlemen. It is passed down every four thousand years from woman to woman, not to men. The supreme regulations have already been inscribed on the talisman of eternal life. Since I received the book, along with the virtuous daughter, I have embraced the orchid. It has been passed down to sixty-eight women, and it is indeed not to be given to men. I have observed the young boy of Qingzhen in Fuguang Mountain, who received the Six Jia talisman in the middle of the Taijia period. All twelve items are the same as those I received. Qingzhen is the disciple who received the Six Jia in the fire, and I have not heard of it being passed to anyone else. He is a male official, and now I am ordered to take it and pass it on. [38]

    帝也先所以告篇目者意是愍其有心将欲坚其専气令且广求他日与之亦欲以男授男承科而行使勤而方获令知天真之珍贵耳非徒茍执衒泄天道阿环主臣愿不罪焉阿母真形之贵愍于勤志亦已授之可谓大不宜矣王母笑曰亦可恕乎上元夫人即命侍女纪离容径到扶广山敇青真小童出六甲左右飞致神之方十二事当以授汉主也须臾侍女还捧五色玉笈凤文之蕴以出六甲之文曰弟子何昌言向奉使绛河摄南真七元君检校羣龙猛兽之数事毕授教承阿母相诣汉主家不意天灵至尊乃复下降于臭浊中也不审起居比来何如侍女纪离容至云尊母欲得金书秘字六甲飞左右防精之文十二事欲授汉主辄封一通付 [39]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [39]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 帝也先所以告篇目者意是愍其有心 - The emperor Da Xian's intention in revealing the titles is to pity those who have good intentions.
    • 将欲坚其専气 - To strengthen their resolve.
    • 令且广求他日与之 - To encourage them to seek widely in the future and share with others.
    • 亦欲以男授男承科而行使勤而方获 - Also, to pass on the knowledge from one man to another, making them diligent and thus achieving success.
    • 令知天真之珍贵耳 - So they may understand the preciousness of natural talent.
    • 非徒茍执衒泄天道阿环主臣愿不罪焉 - Not just to casually flaunt or reveal the heavenly way, but to respect the emperor and his ministers, hoping to avoid blame.
    • 阿母真形之贵愍于勤志亦已授之 - The true form of the mother is valuable; pitying their diligence, she has already bestowed it upon them.
    • 王母笑曰亦可恕乎 - The Queen Mother laughed and said, 'Can it be forgiven?'
    • 上元夫人即命侍女纪离容径到扶广山敇青真小童出六甲左右飞致神之方十二事当以授汉主也 - The Lady of the Upper Yuan immediately ordered the maid Jili Rong to go directly to Fuguang Mountain, where she instructed the young boy Qingzhen to bring out twelve methods of summoning spirits and divine techniques, which were to be given to the Han ruler.
    • 须臾侍女还捧五色玉笈凤文之蕴以出六甲之文曰 - Soon the maid returned, holding a five-colored jade box containing the phoenix-texted scriptures, and brought out the Six Jia texts, saying...
    • 弟子何昌言向奉使绛河摄南真七元君检校羣龙猛兽之数事毕授教承阿母相诣汉主家不意天灵至尊乃复下降于臭浊中也不审起居比来何如 - The disciple He Changyan, who was previously sent to the Red River to capture the Southern True Seven Yuan Lord, has completed the inspection of the number of dragons and fierce beasts. After receiving instruction, he was sent by the mother to visit the Han ruler's home. He did not expect the divine and supreme one to descend again into the impure world. He asked about the well-being of the ruler recently.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 帝也先 - Refers to Emperor Da Xian, a figure in Chinese mythology or historical literature.
    • 阿母 - Refers to a female deity or a revered figure, often associated with the Queen Mother.
    • 王母 - Refers to the Queen Mother, a prominent figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortality and divine wisdom.
    • 上元夫人 - Refers to the Lady of the Upper Yuan, a deity or a high-ranking figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 六甲 - Refers to a set of texts or techniques related to divination and spiritual practices.
    • 汉主 - Refers to the ruler of the Han dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 绛河 - Refers to the Red River, a mythical or symbolic location.
    • 南真七元君 - Refers to the Southern True Seven Yuan Lord, a deity or a group of deities in Chinese mythology.
    • 羣龙猛兽 - Refers to dragons and fierce beasts, often symbolic of power and danger in Chinese mythology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 帝也先 - Emperor Da Xian.
    • 阿母 - The Mother (a deity or revered figure).
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother.
    • 上元夫人 - The Lady of the Upper Yuan.
    • 汉主 - The Han ruler.
    • 绛河 - The Red River.
    • 南真七元君 - The Southern True Seven Yuan Lord.
    • 六甲 - The Six Jia texts or techniques.
    • 扶广山 - Fuguang Mountain.
    • 青真小童 - The young boy Qingzhen.
    • 纪离容 - The maid Jili Rong.
    • 何昌言 - The disciple He Changyan.

    The emperor Da Xian revealed the titles with the intention of pitying those who had good intentions, wishing to strengthen their resolve and encourage them to seek widely in the future and share with others. He also wanted to pass on the knowledge from one man to another, making them diligent and thus achieving success, so they may understand the preciousness of natural talent. Not just to casually flaunt or reveal the heavenly way, but to respect the emperor and his ministers, hoping to avoid blame. The true form of the mother is valuable; pitying their diligence, she has already bestowed it upon them. This could be considered a great misstep. The Queen Mother laughed and said, "Can it be forgiven?" The Lady of the Upper Yuan immediately ordered the maid Jili Rong to go directly to Fuguang Mountain, where she instructed the young boy Qingzhen to bring out twelve methods of summoning spirits and divine techniques, which were to be given to the Han ruler. Soon the maid returned, holding a five-colored jade box containing the phoenix-texted scriptures, and brought out the Six Jia texts, saying: "The disciple He Changyan, who was previously sent to the Red River to capture the Southern True Seven Yuan Lord, has completed the inspection of the number of dragons and fierce beasts. After receiving instruction, he was sent by the mother to visit the Han ruler's home. He did not expect the divine and supreme one to descend again into the impure world. He asked about the well-being of the ruler recently." The maid Jili Rong replied, "The honored mother wishes to receive the golden script and secret characters of the Six Jia, the methods of summoning spirits and divine techniques, twelve in total, which are to be given to the Han ruler. She has already sealed one copy and sent it." [39]

    信曰帝虽有心实非仙才讵宜以此传泄于行尸乎昌近在帝处见有上言者甚众云山哭于丛林孤魂号于絶域兴师旅而族有功忘赏劳而刑士卒纵横白骨烦扰黔首滛酷自恣罪已彰于太上怨已见于天气嚣言互闻必不得度世也奉尊见敇不敢违耳王母叹曰言此子者诚多然帝亦不必推也夫好道慕仙者精诚志念斋戒思愆辄除过一月克已反善奉敬真神存真守一行此一月辄除过一年帝念道累年斋亦勤矣累祷名山愿求度脱校计功过殆已相掩但今以去勤修至诚奉上元夫人之言不宜复奢淫暴虐使万兆劳残寃魂穷有被屈之诉流血之尸忘功赏之辞耳夫人乃下席起立手执八色玉笈凤文之蕴仰帝而祝曰九天浩 [40]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [40]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 帝雖有心實非仙才 - The emperor may have the intention, but he is not truly a talent for the way of the immortals.
    • 讵宜以此传泄于行尸乎 - How appropriate is it to reveal this to one who is like a living corpse?
    • 昌近在帝处见有上言者甚众 - Chang, who is near the emperor, has seen many who have spoken out.
    • 山鬼哭于丛林孤魂号于绝域 - The spirits of the mountains weep in the forests, and the lonely souls cry in remote regions.
    • 兴师旅而族有功忘赏劳而刑士卒 - He raises armies and rewards those who have achieved success, but forgets to reward those who have labored and punishes the soldiers.
    • 纵横白骨烦扰黔首 - The land is filled with the bones of the fallen, and the common people are troubled.
    • 淫酷自恣罪已彰于太上 - His cruelty and tyranny are evident to the highest heavens.
    • 怨已见于天气嚣言互闻必不得度世也 - The resentment is visible in the atmosphere, and conflicting voices are heard, indicating that he will not achieve immortality.
    • 奉尊见敇不敢违耳 - I am bound by your command and dare not disobey.
    • 王母叹曰 - The Queen Mother sighed and said.
    • 言此子者诚多然帝亦不必推也 - Those who speak of this child are many, but the emperor does not need to push further.
    • 夫好道慕仙者精诚志念斋戒思愆辄除过一月克已反善奉敬真神存真守一行此一月辄除过一年 - Those who love the Dao and aspire to immortality, with sincere devotion and meditation, fasting and reflecting on their faults, can eliminate their sins in one month. By practicing self-restraint and returning to goodness, they honor the true gods and maintain their purity.
    • 帝念道累年斋亦勤矣累祷名山愿求度脱校计功过殆已相掩 - The emperor has thought about the Dao for many years and has been diligent in his fasting. He has repeatedly prayed at famous mountains, wishing to be freed from his sins. The balance of his good and bad deeds is nearly equal.
    • 但今以去勤修至诚奉上元夫人之言不宜复奢淫暴虐使万兆劳残冤魂穷鬼有被屈之诉流血之尸忘功赏之辞耳 - But now, he should diligently practice sincerity and follow the words of the Upper Prime Lady. He should not indulge in luxury, cruelty, or tyranny, which cause the common people to suffer and the spirits of the dead to complain. The bodies of the fallen and the forgotten rewards of the soldiers should be remembered.
    • 夫人乃下席起立手执八色玉笈凤文之蕴仰帝而祝曰 - The lady then stood up, holding the eight-colored jade scroll with the phoenix-textured contents, and looked up to the emperor, saying.
    • 九天浩 - The vastness of the nine heavens.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 帝 - Refers to the emperor, likely a specific ruler in the context of the story.
    • 仙才 - Refers to the talent or aptitude for immortality, a concept deeply rooted in Chinese Daoist and Buddhist traditions.
    • 行尸 - Refers to a person who is alive but lacks spiritual or moral life, akin to a walking corpse.
    • 上言者 - Refers to those who speak out or make formal complaints, often to higher authorities.
    • 山鬼 - Refers to mountain spirits, a common figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 孤魂 - Refers to lonely or wandering souls, often associated with the dead who have not found peace.
    • 绝域 - Refers to remote or distant regions, often used to describe places far from civilization.
    • 师旅 - Refers to armies or military forces.
    • 黔首 - Refers to the common people, a term often used in ancient Chinese texts.
    • 淫酷 - Refers to excessive cruelty or tyranny.
    • 太上 - Refers to the highest heaven or the supreme deity, often used in Daoist contexts.
    • 嚣言 - Refers to conflicting or noisy voices, often used to describe public unrest.
    • 度世 - Refers to achieving immortality or transcending the mortal world.
    • 王母 - Refers to the Queen Mother of the West, a significant figure in Chinese mythology and Daoism.
    • 好道慕仙 - Refers to those who love the Dao (Way) and aspire to immortality, a common theme in Chinese spiritual literature.
    • 精诚志念斋戒思愆 - Refers to sincere devotion, meditation, fasting, and reflection on one's faults, practices common in spiritual cultivation.
    • 真神 - Refers to true gods or deities, often associated with the Daoist pantheon.
    • 上元夫人 - Refers to a high-ranking deity or spiritual figure, often associated with the Daoist pantheon.
    • 八色玉笈凤文之蕴 - Refers to an eight-colored jade scroll with phoenix-textured contents, a symbol of high spiritual authority and wisdom.
    • 九天 - Refers to the nine heavens, a concept in Chinese cosmology representing the highest levels of heaven.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This sentence combines a concessive clause with a rhetorical question, emphasizing the emperor's lack of spiritual worth.
    • This long, complex sentence describes multiple grievances and their consequences, requiring careful segmentation in translation.
    • This sentence transitions from obedience to the Queen Mother's response, requiring clear separation in English.
    • This sentence contrasts the many who speak of the emperor with the advice not to act on their words.
    • This sentence describes the path to spiritual purification, requiring careful translation to maintain the flow and meaning.
    • This sentence summarizes the emperor's efforts and the current state of his spiritual account.
    • This sentence is a call for the emperor to change his ways, requiring careful translation to convey the urgency and moral imperative.
    • This sentence describes the lady's respectful and formal gesture, requiring careful translation to maintain the formality and respect.
    • This poetic phrase requires a translation that captures its grandeur and spiritual significance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 帝 - The emperor.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West.
    • 上元夫人 - A high-ranking deity or spiritual figure.
    • 八色玉笈凤文之蕴 - An eight-colored jade scroll with phoenix-textured contents.
    • 九天 - The nine heavens.

    Believing the emperor may have the intention, but he is not truly a talent for the way of the immortals, how appropriate is it to reveal this to one who is like a living corpse? Chang, who is near the emperor, has seen many who have spoken out, saying that the spirits of the mountains weep in the forests, and the lonely souls cry in remote regions. He raises armies and rewards those who have achieved success, but forgets to reward those who have labored and punishes the soldiers. The land is filled with the bones of the fallen, and the common people are troubled. His cruelty and tyranny are evident to the highest heavens, and the resentment is visible in the atmosphere. Conflicting voices are heard, indicating that he will not achieve immortality. I am bound by your command and dare not disobey. The Queen Mother sighed and said, "Those who speak of this child are many, but the emperor does not need to push further." Those who love the Dao and aspire to immortality, with sincere devotion and meditation, fasting and reflecting on their faults, can eliminate their sins in one month. By practicing self-restraint and returning to goodness, they honor the true gods and maintain their purity. In one month, they can eliminate their sins for a year. The emperor has thought about the Dao for many years and has been diligent in his fasting. He has repeatedly prayed at famous mountains, wishing to be freed from his sins. The balance of his good and bad deeds is nearly equal. But now, he should diligently practice sincerity and follow the words of the Upper Prime Lady. He should not indulge in luxury, cruelty, or tyranny, which cause the common people to suffer and the spirits of the dead to complain. The bodies of the fallen and the forgotten rewards of the soldiers should be remembered. The lady then stood up, holding the eight-colored jade scroll with the phoenix-textured contents, and looked up to the emperor, saying, "The vastness of the nine heavens." [40]

    洞太上耀灵神照寂清虚朗明登虚者妙守气者生至念道臻寂感真诚役神形辱安精年荣授帝飞及此六丁左右招神天光策精可以歩虚可以形长生乆视还白留青我传有四万之纪授帝传在四十之龄违犯泄漏祸必族倾反是天真必沉幽尔其慎祸敢告刘生尔师主是真青童小君太上中黄道君之师真元始十天王入室弟子也姓延陵名阳字庇华形有婴孩之貎故仙宫以青真小童为号其为器也玉朗洞照圣周万变镜幽览才为真俊游于扶广权此始运馆圃治仙职分子在师居从尔所愿不存所授命必倾沦言毕夫人一一手指所施用节度以示帝焉凡十二事都毕又告帝曰夫五帝者方面之天精六甲六位之通 [41]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [41]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 洞太上耀灵神照 - Refers to a divine illumination or enlightenment from the highest spiritual realm.
    • 玄寂清虚朗明 - Describes a state of profound tranquility, clarity, and enlightenment, often associated with Daoist or Buddhist spiritual practices.
    • 登虚者妙守气者生 - Refers to the idea that those who ascend to the spiritual realm (登虚) and those who cultivate their vital energy (守气) are both essential for life and spiritual attainment.
    • 至念道臻寂感真诚役神形 - Suggests reaching a state of pure, sincere thought and spiritual attainment, where the body and spirit are harmonized.
    • 授帝飞及此六丁左右招神天光策精 - Refers to the bestowal of divine power or knowledge, possibly involving the six celestial spirits (六丁) and the manipulation of divine light and energy.
    • 可以步虚可以隐形 - Means the ability to walk in the spiritual realm (步虚) and to become invisible or intangible (隐形).
    • 长生久视还白留青 - Refers to the goals of longevity, eternal vision, and the preservation of youth and vitality.
    • 我传有四万之纪授帝传在四十之龄 - Indicates the transmission of knowledge or power over a long period (四万之纪) and the age at which the recipient attains this knowledge (四十之龄).
    • 违犯泄漏祸必族倾 - Warns that violating or leaking this knowledge will result in the destruction of one's family or lineage.
    • 反是天真必沉幽冥 - Suggests that those who betray this truth will descend into darkness or oblivion.
    • 尔其慎祸敢告刘生 - A warning to the listener (刘生) to be cautious of the consequences.
    • 尔师主是真青童小君太上中黄道君之师真元始十天王入室弟子也 - A complex title or description of the teacher, indicating a high spiritual rank and connection to various deities and spiritual masters.
    • 姓延陵名阳字庇华形有婴孩之貌故仙宫以青真小童为号 - Describes the teacher's name, character, and appearance, suggesting a youthful and pure spiritual essence.
    • 其为器也玉朗洞照圣周万变玄镜幽览才为真俊 - Describes the teacher's spiritual qualities, emphasizing clarity, wisdom, and adaptability.
    • 游于扶广权此始运馆玄圃治仙职分子在师居从尔所愿不存所授命必倾沦 - Refers to the teacher's role in the spiritual realm, including the management of celestial gardens and the fulfillment of wishes, but warns that failing to fulfill the teachings will lead to ruin.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 洞太上 - Refers to the highest spiritual realm or deity in Daoist or Buddhist cosmology.
    • 六丁 - Refers to the six celestial spirits in Daoist tradition, often associated with divination and spiritual power.
    • 天光策精 - Refers to the manipulation of divine light and energy, a concept often found in Daoist and Buddhist spiritual practices.
    • 步虚 - A Daoist practice of walking in the spiritual realm, often associated with meditation and spiritual ascent.
    • 隐形 - Refers to the ability to become invisible or intangible, a common theme in Daoist and Buddhist spiritual texts.
    • 长生久视 - Refers to the Daoist ideal of longevity and eternal vision, often associated with immortality.
    • 还白留青 - Refers to the preservation of youth and vitality, a common theme in Daoist and Buddhist texts.
    • 四万之纪 - Refers to a long period of time, often used in Daoist texts to describe the duration of spiritual knowledge or power.
    • 四十之龄 - Refers to the age of forty, often associated with spiritual attainment in Daoist and Confucian traditions.
    • 族倾 - Refers to the destruction of one's family or lineage, a common warning in Daoist and Buddhist texts.
    • 幽冥 - Refers to darkness or oblivion, often used to describe the consequences of spiritual failure.
    • 延陵 - A surname, possibly referring to a historical figure or a spiritual master.
    • 扶广 - Refers to a celestial garden or paradise in Daoist cosmology.
    • 玄圃 - Refers to a celestial garden or paradise, often associated with the dwelling place of immortals in Daoist texts.

    Proper Nouns

    • 洞太上 - The highest spiritual realm or deity in Daoist or Buddhist cosmology.
    • 六丁 - The six celestial spirits in Daoist tradition.
    • 天光策精 - A concept related to the manipulation of divine light and energy.
    • 步虚 - A Daoist practice of walking in the spiritual realm.
    • 隐形 - The ability to become invisible or intangible.
    • 长生久视 - The Daoist ideal of longevity and eternal vision.
    • 还白留青 - The preservation of youth and vitality.
    • 四万之纪 - A long period of time.
    • 四十之龄 - The age of forty.
    • 族倾 - The destruction of one's family or lineage.
    • 幽冥 - Darkness or oblivion.
    • 延陵 - A surname, possibly referring to a historical figure or a spiritual master.
    • 扶广 - A celestial garden or paradise in Daoist cosmology.
    • 玄圃 - A celestial garden or paradise, often associated with the dwelling place of immortals in Daoist texts.

    From the highest spiritual realm, the divine illumination of the Supreme Deity shines with profound tranquility, clarity, and enlightenment. Those who ascend to the spiritual realm and those who cultivate their vital energy are both essential for life and spiritual attainment. Reaching a state of pure, sincere thought and spiritual attainment, one harmonizes the body and spirit, ensuring the safety and prosperity of the essence, and bestowing upon the emperor the power to fly and summon the six celestial spirits. With this divine light and energy, one can walk in the spiritual realm and become invisible. The goals of longevity, eternal vision, and the preservation of youth and vitality are within reach. The knowledge I transmit has been passed down over a long period, and the emperor attains this knowledge at the age of forty. Violating or leaking this knowledge will result in the destruction of one's family, and betraying this truth will lead to descent into darkness. Be cautious, for I warn you, Liu Sheng. Your teacher is the true Blue Child Immortal, a disciple of the Supreme Middle Yellow Deity, and a follower of the True Primordial Ten Heavenly Kings. His surname is Yanling, his name is Yang, and his courtesy name is Bi Hua. He possesses the appearance of an infant, hence the Heavenly Palace refers to him as the Blue Child Immortal. His spiritual qualities are clear and wise, able to adapt to all changes, like a mirror that sees deeply into the mysteries of the universe. He cultivates in the celestial garden, managing the celestial gardens and fulfilling wishes, but if you fail to fulfill the teachings, you will surely be ruined. After speaking, the woman pointed out the twelve things to be used and the measures to be taken, showing them to the emperor. After all twelve things were completed, she further warned the emperor: "The Five Deities are the heavenly essences of the five directions, and the six celestial spirits are the intermediaries of the six positions." [41]

    佩而尊之可致长生此书上帝封于景之台子其寳秘焉王母曰此三天太上之所撰藏于紫陵之台以坛之房封以华琳之函韫以兰茧之帛约以紫罗之素印以太帝之玺受之者四十年传一人无其人八十年可顿授二人得道者四百年一传得仙者四千年一传得真者四万年一传升太上者四十万年一传非其人谓之泄天道得其人不传是谓蔽天寳非限妄传是谓轻天老受而不敬是谓慢天藻泄蔽轻慢四者取死之刀斧延祸之车乗也泄者身死于道路受上刑而骸裂蔽者盲聋于来世命凋枉而卒殁轻则钟祸于父母诣都而考罚慢则终而堕恶道弃疾于后世此皆道之科禁故以相戒不可不慎也王母因授以五岳真形图 [42]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [42]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 佩而尊之可致长生 - Wearing and respecting it can lead to longevity.
    • 此书上帝封于玄景之台 - This book was sealed by God on the platform of Xuanjing.
    • 子其宝秘焉 - You should treasure and keep it secret.
    • 韫以兰茧之帛约以紫罗之素印以太帝之玺 - Wrapped in silk of lily-white color, tied with purple silk, and sealed with the seal of the Great Emperor.
    • 受之者四十年传一人无其人八十年可顿授二人 - The recipient can pass it on to one person every forty years, and if there is no suitable person, it can be passed on to two people after eighty years.
    • 得道者四百年一传得仙者四千年一传得真者四万年一传升太上者四十万年一传 - Those who attain the Way can pass it on once every four hundred years; those who attain immortality can pass it on once every four thousand years; those who attain truth can pass it on once every forty thousand years; those who ascend to the Highest can pass it on once every four hundred thousand years.
    • 非其人谓之泄天道得其人不传是谓蔽天宝非限妄传是谓轻天老受而不敬是谓慢天藻泄蔽轻慢四者取死之刀斧延祸之车乘也 - If it is not passed on to the right person, it is called leaking the heavenly way; if it is passed on to the right person but not transmitted, it is called concealing the heavenly treasure; if it is passed on without limit, it is called being light with the heavenly elders; if it is received without respect, it is called being slow to the heavenly elders. These four are like the knife and axe of death and the carriage of calamity.
    • 泄者身死于道路受上刑而骸裂蔽者盲聋于来世命凋枉而卒殁轻则钟祸于父母诣玄都而考罚慢则终而堕恶道弃疾于后世 - Those who leak it will die on the road and suffer the upper punishment with their bones shattered; those who conceal it will be blind and deaf in the next life and die prematurely; those who are light with it will bring disaster to their parents and be punished in the Xuan Capital; those who are slow to it will fall into evil paths and be abandoned by virtue in the afterlife.
    • 此皆道之科禁故以相戒不可不慎也 - These are all the rules and prohibitions of the Way, so they are used to warn each other and one must be careful.
    • 王母因授以五岳真形图 - The Queen Mother then bestowed upon him the True Forms Diagram of the Five Peaks.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 佩而尊之可致长生 - Reflects the belief in the power of talismans and sacred texts to grant longevity, a common theme in Taoist and folk religious practices.
    • 此书上帝封于玄景之台 - The Xuanjing Platform is a celestial location often associated with divine texts and sacred knowledge in Chinese mythology.
    • 子其宝秘焉 - The act of treasuring and keeping something secret is a common theme in texts dealing with sacred knowledge and esoteric practices.
    • 王母曰 - The Queen Mother, also known as the Queen Mother of the West, is a prominent figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortality and the bestowal of sacred knowledge.
    • 此三天太上之所撰藏于紫陵之台 - The Three Highest Ones are a group of deities in Taoist cosmology, and the Purple Peak Platform is another celestial location associated with divine texts.
    • 韫以兰茧之帛约以紫罗之素印以太帝之玺 - The use of specific materials and seals in the wrapping and sealing process reflects the importance of ritual and symbolism in the transmission of sacred knowledge.
    • 受之者四十年传一人无其人八十年可顿授二人 - This passage outlines the strict rules for the transmission of sacred knowledge, reflecting the belief in the importance of proper lineage and the potential consequences of improper transmission.
    • 得道者四百年一传得仙者四千年一传得真者四万年一传升太上者四十万年一传 - This passage outlines the different levels of attainment and the corresponding frequency of transmission, reflecting the hierarchical structure of Taoist and esoteric practices.
    • 非其人谓之泄天道得其人不传是谓蔽天宝非限妄传是谓轻天老受而不敬是谓慢天藻泄蔽轻慢四者取死之刀斧延祸之车乘也 - This passage outlines the four major sins in the transmission of sacred knowledge and their consequences, reflecting the seriousness with which these practices were viewed.
    • 泄者身死于道路受上刑而骸裂蔽者盲聋于来世命凋枉而卒殁轻则钟祸于父母诣玄都而考罚慢则终而堕恶道弃疾于后世 - This passage describes the specific punishments for each of the four sins, reflecting the belief in the importance of proper conduct in esoteric practices.
    • 此皆道之科禁故以相戒不可不慎也 - This passage emphasizes the importance of following the rules and prohibitions of the Way, reflecting the belief in the importance of discipline and caution in esoteric practices.
    • 王母因授以五岳真形图 - The True Forms Diagram of the Five Peaks is a sacred text in Taoist and esoteric practices, often associated with the Five Peaks of China and the Five Elements.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 佩而尊之可致长生 - A simple conditional statement, but the result clause '可致长生' (can lead to longevity) is somewhat ambiguous in its exact meaning and implications.
    • 此书上帝封于玄景之台子其宝秘焉 - A compound sentence with a subject and predicate, but the exact relationship between the two clauses is somewhat ambiguous.
    • 王母曰此三天太上之所撰藏于紫陵之台 - A compound sentence with a subject and predicate, but the exact relationship between the two clauses is somewhat ambiguous.
    • 韫以兰茧之帛约以紫罗之素印以太帝之玺 - A compound sentence with a subject and predicate, but the exact relationship between the three clauses is somewhat ambiguous.
    • 受之者四十年传一人无其人八十年可顿授二人 - A compound sentence with a subject and predicate, but the exact relationship between the two clauses is somewhat ambiguous.
    • 得道者四百年一传得仙者四千年一传得真者四万年一传升太上者四十万年一传 - A compound sentence with a subject and predicate, but the exact relationship between the four clauses is somewhat ambiguous.

    If one wears it and respects it, it can lead to longevity. This book was sealed by God on the platform of Xuanjing. You should treasure and keep it secret. The Queen Mother said, "This was written by the Three Highest Ones and is stored on the Purple Peak Platform, in the hall of the sacred altar, wrapped in silk of lily-white color, tied with purple silk, and sealed with the seal of the Great Emperor." The recipient can pass it on to one person every forty years, and if there is no suitable person, it can be passed on to two people after eighty years. Those who attain the Way can pass it on once every four hundred years; those who attain immortality can pass it on once every four thousand years; those who attain truth can pass it on once every forty thousand years; those who ascend to the Highest can pass it on once every four hundred thousand years. If it is not passed on to the right person, it is called leaking the heavenly way; if it is passed on to the right person but not transmitted, it is called concealing the heavenly treasure; if it is passed on without limit, it is called being light with the heavenly elders; if it is received without respect, it is called being slow to the heavenly elders. These four are like the knife and axe of death and the carriage of calamity. Those who leak it will die on the road and suffer the upper punishment with their bones shattered; those who conceal it will be blind and deaf in the next life and die prematurely; those who are light with it will bring disaster to their parents and be punished in the Xuan Capital; those who are slow to it will fall into evil paths and be abandoned by virtue in the afterlife. These are all the rules and prohibitions of the Way, so they are used to warn each other and one must be careful. The Queen Mother then bestowed upon him the True Forms Diagram of the Five Peaks. [42]

    帝拜受俱毕夫人自弹云林之璈歌歩之曲王母命侍女曰四非答哥哥毕乃告帝从者姓名及冠带执佩物名所以得知而纪焉至明旦王母与上元夫人同乗而去人马龙虎导从音乐如初而时云彩郁勃尽为香气极望西南良乆乃絶帝既见王母及上元夫人乃信天下有神仙之事其后帝以王母所授五真图光经及上元夫人所授六甲飞十二事自撰集为一卷及诸经图皆奉以黄金之箱封以白玉之函以瑚为轴紫锦为囊安着柏梁台上数自斋防朝拜烧香洒扫然后乃执省焉帝自受法出入六年意防清畅髙韵自许为神真见降必当度世恃此不修至徳更兴起台馆劳万民坑降杀服逺征夷狄路盈怒叹流血膏城每事不从 [43]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [43]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 帝拜受俱畢 - Formal expression of obeisance, indicating the emperor prostrated himself and received something, then completed the act.
    • 夫人自彈雲林之璈 - Lady playing a zither associated with the clouds and forests, symbolizing a mystical and ethereal quality.
    • 歌步玄之曲 - Singing a song of the Tao, reflecting spiritual and philosophical themes.
    • 王母命侍女曰四非答哥哥畢 - Queen Mother commanding a servant to give four non-answers to brother, likely a cryptic phrase with deeper meaning.
    • 畢乃告帝從者姓名及冠帶執佩物名 - After completing the task, informing the emperor of the names and details of the attendants.
    • 至明旦王母與上元夫人同乘而去 - Next morning, the Queen Mother and the Lady of the Upper Yuan left together.
    • 人馬龍虎導從音樂如初 - Human and horse attendants with dragon and tiger escorts, and music as before, describing a grand procession.
    • 時雲彩郁勃盡為香氣 - Clouds dense and heavy, all turning into fragrance, creating a mystical atmosphere.
    • 極望西南良久乃絶 - Looking far away to the southwest, after a long time, it disappeared, describing the visual experience.
    • 帝既見王母及上元夫人乃信天下有神仙之事 - After seeing the Queen Mother and the Lady of the Upper Yuan, the emperor believed in the existence of immortals.
    • 其後帝以王母所授五真圖靈光經及上元夫人所授六甲靈飛十二事 - Later, the emperor compiled the Five True Figures' Spiritual Light Scripture and the Twelve Matters of Sixty Jia's Spiritual Flight.
    • 自撰集為一卷 - Compiled himself into one volume.
    • 及諸經圖皆奉以黄金之箱封以白玉之函以珊瑚為軸紫錦為囊 - Placed in a golden box, sealed in a jade casket, with a coral axis, and a purple silk pouch, describing the elaborate storage.
    • 安着柏梁台上 - Stored on the Pine Beam Terrace.
    • 數自齋戒朝拜燒香洒掃然後乃執省焉 - Often fasted and purified, worshiped, burned incense and swept, then examined the texts.
    • 帝自受法出入六年意防清暢髙韻自許為神真見降必當度世恃此不修至徳更興起台館勞役萬民坑降殺服遠征夷狄路盈怒嘆流血膏城每事不從 - Received the teachings for six years, mind clear and smooth, highly esteemed himself, immortals would descend, transcend to the other world, relied on this and did not cultivate virtue, built more palaces and pavilions, tired the people, pitfalls and punishments, military expeditions against barbarians, filled with anger and lamentation, blood flowed like grease on the city walls, did not follow each matter.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 帝 - Refers to the emperor, likely Emperor Huan of Han.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West, a figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 上元夫人 - The Lady of the Upper Yuan, another mythological figure.
    • 雲林之璈 - A zither associated with the clouds and forests, symbolizing a mystical and ethereal quality.
    • 步玄之曲 - A song of the Tao, reflecting spiritual and philosophical themes.
    • 四非答哥哥畢 - A cryptic phrase that likely has a deeper meaning, possibly related to the Four Non-Answers in Buddhist philosophy.
    • 五真圖靈光經 - A text attributed to the Five True Figures, likely a collection of spiritual teachings.
    • 六甲靈飛十二事 - A set of teachings related to the Sixty Jia, a system of divination and astrology.
    • 柏梁台 - A famous palace in ancient China, often associated with cultural and religious activities.
    • 齋戒 - Ritual fasting and purification, common in religious and spiritual practices.
    • 度世 - To transcend the mortal world and achieve immortality.
    • 至徳 - Highest virtue, often associated with moral and ethical conduct.
    • 夷狄 - Barbarians or non-Han Chinese peoples, often used in a derogatory sense.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence '帝拜受俱畢夫人自彈雲林之璈歌步玄之曲' describes the emperor's actions and the lady's performance in a continuous sequence.
    • The sequence of commands and information in '王母命侍女曰四非答哥哥畢乃告帝從者姓名及冠帶執佩物名' is compact and requires careful parsing.
    • The description of the departure in '至明旦王母與上元夫人同乘而去人馬龍虎導從音樂如初' is detailed and must be read as a single event.
    • The sequence of sensory experiences in '時雲彩郁勃盡為香氣極望西南良久乃絶' is vivid and must be read as a continuous scene.
    • The cause-and-effect relationship between the emperor's experience and his belief in '帝既見王母及上元夫人乃信天下有神仙之事' is clear.
    • The sequence of receiving, compiling, and storing the texts in '其後帝以王母所授五真圖靈光經及上元夫人所授六甲靈飛十二事自撰集為一卷' is detailed.
    • The description of the elaborate storage in '及諸經圖皆奉以黄金之箱封以白玉之函以珊瑚為軸紫錦為囊安着柏梁台上' is detailed and must be read as a single action.
    • The sequence of ritual practices in '數自齋戒朝拜燒香洒掃然後乃執省焉' is clear and must be read as a continuous process.
    • The sequence of the emperor's actions and their consequences in '帝自受法出入六年意防清暢髙韻自許為神真見降必當度世恃此不修至徳更興起台館勞役萬民坑降殺服遠征夷狄路盈怒嘆流血膏城每事不從' is detailed and must be read as a continuous narrative.

    Proper Nouns

    • 帝 - Emperor Huan of Han.
    • 王母 - Queen Mother of the West.
    • 上元夫人 - Lady of the Upper Yuan.
    • 雲林之璈 - A zither associated with the clouds and forests.
    • 步玄之曲 - A song of the Tao.
    • 五真圖靈光經 - Five True Figures' Spiritual Light Scripture.
    • 六甲靈飛十二事 - Twelve Matters of Sixty Jia's Spiritual Flight.
    • 柏梁台 - Pine Beam Terrace.

    The emperor prostrated himself and received everything, and then the lady played the zither of the cloud forest and sang the Taoist song. The Queen Mother commanded her attendants to say, "Four Non-Answers to Brother," and upon completion, she informed the emperor of the names of her followers, their attire, and the names of the objects they held, thus making it possible to record and remember them. By the next morning, the Queen Mother and the Lady of the Upper Yuan left together, accompanied by human and horse attendants with dragon and tiger escorts, and the music was as before. At that time, the dense clouds turned into fragrant aromas, and they looked far into the southwest, and after a long time, it disappeared. The emperor, having seen the Queen Mother and the Lady of the Upper Yuan, then believed that there were indeed immortals in the world. Afterward, the emperor compiled the Five True Figures' Spiritual Light Scripture and the Twelve Matters of Sixty Jia's Spiritual Flight, which he had received from the Queen Mother and the Lady of the Upper Yuan, into one volume. All the scriptures and diagrams were placed in a golden box, sealed in a jade casket, with a coral axis and a purple silk pouch, and stored on the Pine Beam Terrace. He often fasted and purified himself, worshiped, burned incense, and swept, and then examined the texts. The emperor, having received the teachings, practiced them for six years, and his mind was clear and smooth, highly esteemed by himself, believing that immortals would descend and he would surely transcend the mortal world. Relying on this, he did not cultivate the highest virtue, instead building more palaces and pavilions, tiring the people, and imposing pitfall and punishment. He launched military expeditions against barbarians, filling the roads with anger and lamentation, and blood flowed like grease on the city walls. He did not follow each matter. [43]

    至太初元年十一月乙酉天火烧柏梁台真形图灵飞经録十二事灵光经及自撰所受凡十四卷并函并失王母当知武帝既不从训故火灾耳其后东方朔一旦乗龙飞去同时众人见从西北上冉冉仰望良乆大雾覆之不知所适至元狩二年二月帝病行盩厔西憇五柞宫丁卯帝崩入殡未央宫前殿三月葬茂陵是夕帝棺自动而有声闻宫外如此数遍又有芳香异常陵毕坟埏间大雾门柱壊雾经一月许日帝塜中先有一玉箱一玉杖此是西胡康渠王所献帝甚爱之故入梓宫中其后四年有人于扶风市中买得此二物帝时左右侍人有识此物是先帝所珍玩者因认以告有司诘之买者乃商人也从关外来宿鄽市其日见一人于北车巷中 [44]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [44]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 至太初元年十一月乙酉 - Specific date in the Chinese lunar calendar; requires careful translation to maintain historical accuracy.
    • 天火烧柏梁台 - Literal description of a fire destroying the *Bailiang* Palace; may carry symbolic or historical significance.
    • 真形图灵飞经録十二事 - Refers to a specific text or collection of texts, possibly a religious or esoteric work; exact nature unclear and may require further research.
    • 灵光经 - Another esoteric or religious text, likely related to Daoist or Buddhist practices.
    • 王母当知 - Refers to the Queen Mother of the West; suggests she is aware of or involved in the events described.
    • 武帝既不从训 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Han; indicates he did not follow a certain instruction or teaching, leading to the fire.
    • 东方朔一旦乘龙飞去 - Refers to Dongfang Shuo, a legendary figure known for his wit and supernatural abilities; describes his sudden departure, likely a mythological event.
    • 冉冉仰望良久 - Vivid description of people looking up for a long time, possibly in awe or anticipation.
    • 大雾覆之 - Describes a thick fog that covers the area, obscuring visibility.
    • 不知所适 - Means 'not knowing where to go'; indicates confusion or uncertainty.
    • 元狩二年二月 - Specific date in the Chinese lunar calendar.
    • 帝病行盩厔西憇五柞宫 - Describes Emperor Wu's illness and his stay at the *Wuzuo* Palace in *Zhouzhi*.
    • 丁卯帝崩 - Indicates the day of Emperor Wu's death.
    • 入殡未央宫前殿 - Describes the process of moving his body to the *Weiyang* Palace's main hall for burial preparations.
    • 陵毕坟埏间大雾 - Describes a thick fog that appears in the tomb area.
    • 门柱壊雾经一月许日 - Indicates that the fog lasted for about a month.
    • 帝塜中先有一玉箱一玉杖 - Describes the discovery of a jade box and a jade staff in the tomb.
    • 西胡康渠王所献 - Indicates that the jade items were gifts from *Kangqu* King of the Western Hu, a historical figure or tribe.
    • 其后四年有人于扶风市中买得此二物 - Describes the discovery of the jade items in the market in *Fufeng* four years later.
    • 有识此物是先帝所珍玩者 - Indicates that someone recognized the jade items as treasures of the late emperor.
    • 从关外来宿鄽市 - Describes the person's origin and where they stayed before selling the items.
    • 北车巷中 - Specifies the location where the person was seen.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太初元年 - Refers to the first year of the *Taichu* era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 柏梁台 - A famous palace built by Emperor Wu of Han, known for its grandeur and significance.
    • 真形图灵飞经録十二事 - Likely refers to a Daoist or esoteric text, possibly related to immortality or spiritual practices.
    • 灵光经 - Another esoteric text, possibly related to Daoist or Buddhist teachings.
    • 武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Han, one of the most famous emperors of the Han Dynasty.
    • 东方朔 - A legendary figure known for his wit and supernatural abilities, often associated with Daoist mythology.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West, a figure in Chinese mythology often associated with immortality and the heavens.
    • 元狩二年 - Refers to the second year of the *Yuanshou* era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 盩厔 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location in the Han Dynasty.
    • 五柞宫 - A palace where Emperor Wu stayed during his illness.
    • 未央宫 - The main imperial palace of the Han Dynasty, located in Chang'an.
    • 茂陵 - The mausoleum of Emperor Wu of Han, located in modern-day Shaanxi Province.
    • 扶风 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location in the Han Dynasty.
    • 西胡康渠王 - Refers to a king of the Western Hu, a historical tribe or region.
    • 关外 - Refers to the area outside the Great Wall, often associated with frontier regions.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence '至太初元年十一月乙酉天火烧柏梁台' is elliptical and requires careful parsing to understand the sequence of events.
    • The sentence '真形图灵飞经録十二事灵光经及自撰所受凡十四卷并函并失' lists multiple texts and their status, requiring careful translation to maintain clarity.
    • The sentence '王母当知武帝既不从训故火灾耳' connects the Queen Mother's awareness with the fire, requiring careful interpretation of the causal relationship.
    • The sentence '其后东方朔一旦乘龙飞去' describes a sudden and dramatic event, requiring careful translation to maintain the sense of wonder.
    • The sentence '冉冉仰望良久大雾覆之不知所适' describes a sequence of events in a poetic manner, requiring careful translation to maintain the vivid imagery.
    • The sentence '元狩二年二月帝病行盩厔西憇五柞宫丁卯帝崩入殡未央宫前殿' describes a series of events in a compact manner, requiring careful parsing to understand the sequence.
    • The sentence '陵毕坟埏间大雾门柱壊雾经一月许日' describes a series of events in a compact manner, requiring careful parsing to understand the sequence.
    • The sentence '帝塜中先有一玉箱一玉杖' describes the discovery of specific items in the tomb, requiring careful translation to maintain clarity.
    • The sentence '西胡康渠王所献' indicates the origin of the jade items, requiring careful translation to maintain clarity.
    • The sentence '其后四年有人于扶风市中买得此二物' describes the discovery of the jade items in the market, requiring careful translation to maintain clarity.
    • The sentence '有识此物是先帝所珍玩者' indicates that someone recognized the jade items as treasures of the late emperor, requiring careful translation to maintain clarity.
    • The sentence '从关外来宿鄽市' describes the person's origin and where they stayed before selling the items, requiring careful translation to maintain clarity.
    • The sentence '北车巷中' specifies the location where the person was seen, requiring careful translation to maintain clarity.

    Proper Nouns

    • 太初元年 - The first year of the *Taichu* era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 柏梁台 - A famous palace built by Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 真形图灵飞经録十二事 - Likely refers to a Daoist or esoteric text.
    • 灵光经 - Another esoteric text.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 东方朔 - A legendary figure known for his wit and supernatural abilities.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West.
    • 元狩二年 - The second year of the *Yuanshou* era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 盩厔 - A place name.
    • 五柞宫 - A palace where Emperor Wu stayed during his illness.
    • 未央宫 - The main imperial palace of the Han Dynasty.
    • 茂陵 - The mausoleum of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 扶风 - A place name.
    • 西胡康渠王 - A king of the Western Hu.
    • 关外 - The area outside the Great Wall.

    In the first year of the Taichu era, on the day of the Yi You in the eleventh month, a fire broke out at the Bailiang Palace, destroying the True Form Diagram, the Lingfei Scripture recording twelve matters, the Lingguang Scripture, and all fourteen volumes of works he had personally compiled, along with their enclosures, all of which were lost. The Queen Mother of the West must have known that Emperor Wu did not follow the teachings, which led to the fire. Afterward, Dongfang Shuo suddenly rode off on a dragon and flew away. At the same time, the crowd saw him ascending from the northwest, looking up for a long time as he slowly rose, covered by a thick fog, and they did not know where he went. In the second month of the Yuanshou era of the second year, Emperor Wu fell ill and traveled west to stay at the Wuzuo Palace in Zhouzhi. On the day of Ding Mao, he died. His body was moved to the Weiyang Palace's main hall for burial preparations. In the third month, he was buried at the Maoling Mausoleum. That evening, his coffin moved on its own, making sounds heard outside the palace, repeating this several times. There was also an unusual fragrance. After the burial, a thick fog appeared in the tomb area, and the door pillars were damaged. The fog lasted for about a month. In the tomb, there was first a jade box and a jade staff. These were gifts from the King of the Western Hu, Kangqu, whom Emperor Wu greatly cherished, so they were placed in the coffin. Four years later, someone bought these two items in the market of Fufeng. A servant of Emperor Wu recognized the items as treasures of the late emperor and reported them to the authorities. Upon questioning, the buyer was revealed to be a merchant who had come from outside the Great Wall and stayed at a market in the suburbs. That day, he was seen in the North Vehicle Lane. [44]

    卖此二物青布三十疋钱九万即售之度实不知卖箱杖主姓名事实如此有司以闻商人放还诏以二物付太庙又帝崩时遗诏以杂经三十余卷常读玩之使随身敛到元康二年河东功曹李友入上党抱犊山采药于岩室中得此经盛以金箱卷后题东观臣姓名记日月武帝时也河东太守张纯以经箱奏进帝问武帝时左右侍臣有典书中冉登见经及箱流涕对曰此孝武皇帝殡殓时物也臣当时以着梓宫中不知何縁得出宣帝大怆然惊愕以经付孝武帝庙中按九都龙真经云得仙之下者皆先死过太隂中链尸骸度地戸然后乃得尸去耳且先敛经杖乃忽显出货于市中经见山室自非神变幽妙孰能如此者乎【出汉武帝内传】 [45]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [45]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 賣此二物 - Sell these two items, where '此' refers to the items mentioned earlier in the text.
    • 青布三十疋 - Thirty bolts of blue cloth, where '疋' is an ancient unit of cloth measurement.
    • 錢九萬 - Nine thousand coins, indicating the price.
    • 即售之 - Sell it immediately, a direct imperative.
    • 度實不知 - In fact, I do not know, where '度' means 'estimate' or 'think'.
    • 賣箱杖主姓名 - The name of the owner of the box and staff, where '箱杖' refers to the box and staff mentioned in the text.
    • 事實如此 - The facts are as such, indicating that the situation is as described.
    • 有司以聞 - The officials reported it, where '有司' refers to government officials.
    • 商人放還 - The merchant was released, where '放還' means 'released' or 'allowed to return'.
    • 詔以二物付太廟 - The imperial decree ordered the two items to be given to the Imperial Ancestral Temple, where '太廟' refers to the imperial ancestral temple.
    • 帝崩時遺詔 - At the time of the emperor's death, the will, where '崩' means 'death' of an emperor.
    • 雜經三十余卷 - Over thirty scrolls of miscellaneous scriptures, where '雜經' refers to a collection of religious texts.
    • 常讀玩之 - Frequently read and cherished, where '玩' means 'cherish' or 'treasure'.
    • 使隨身斂 - To be kept with the body, where '斂' means 'to bury' or 'to place in a coffin'.
    • 元康二年 - In the second year of the Yuan Kang era, referring to a specific period in Chinese history.
    • 河東功曹李友 - Li You, an official in Hedong, where '河東' is a historical region and '功曹' is a local official.
    • 入上黨抱犊山 - Entered Shangdang's Baodushan, where '上黨' and '抱犊山' are geographical locations.
    • 采藥於岩室中 - Gathered herbs in a cave, where '岩室' refers to a cave or cavern.
    • 得此經 - Found this scripture, where '此經' refers to the scripture mentioned earlier.
    • 盛以金箱 - Stored in a golden box, where '金箱' refers to a box made of gold.
    • 卷后題 - The scroll is inscribed with, where '題' means 'inscribed' or 'written'.
    • 東觀臣姓名 - The name of the Eastern Observatory official, where '東觀' refers to an ancient observatory or office.
    • 記日月 - Recorded the date and month, where '日月' refers to the date and month.
    • 武帝時也 - During the reign of Emperor Wu, where '武帝' refers to Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 河東太守張純 - Zhang Chun, the governor of Hedong, where '河東' is a historical region and '太守' is a governor.
    • 以經箱奏進 - Presented the scripture and box to the emperor, where '奏進' means 'to present' or 'to submit'.
    • 帝問 - The emperor asked, where '帝' refers to the emperor.
    • 左右侍臣 - Officials around the emperor, where '左右' refers to those close to the emperor.
    • 典書中冉登 - Official in charge of books, Rande, where '典書' refers to an official in charge of books.
    • 見經及箱 - Saw the scripture and the box, where '經及箱' refers to the scripture and the box.
    • 流涕對曰 - Cried and replied, where '流涕' means 'to cry' and '對曰' means 'replied'.
    • 此孝武皇帝殡殓时物也 - These are the items used in the burial of Emperor Wu, where '孝武皇帝' refers to Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 臣當時以着梓宫中 - I placed them in the coffin at that time, where '梓宫' refers to the coffin.
    • 不知何縁得出 - I do not know how they could have come out, where '何縁' means 'how' or 'why'.
    • 宣帝大怆然驚愕 - The emperor was greatly saddened and astonished, where '宣帝' refers to the current emperor.
    • 以經付孝武帝廟中 - Ordered the scripture to be placed in the temple of Emperor Wu, where '孝武帝廟' refers to the temple of Emperor Wu.
    • 按九都龙真经云 - According to the *Nine Capitals Dragon True Scripture*, where '九都龙真经' refers to a specific scripture.
    • 得仙之下者皆先死 - Those who attain immortality first die, where '得仙' means 'attain immortality'.
    • 過太隂中链尸骸 - Pass through the realm of the Great Yin to refine the body, where '太隂' refers to the realm of the dead.
    • 度地戸 - Cross the threshold of the earth, where '地戸' refers to the threshold of the earth.
    • 然后乃得尸去耳 - Then they can leave their bodies, where '尸' means 'leave the body'.
    • 且先敛经杖 - Moreover, the scripture and staff were first buried, where '敛' means 'to bury'.
    • 乃忽显出货于市中 - Then suddenly appeared and were sold in the market, where '货于市中' means 'sold in the market'.
    • 经见山室 - The scripture was seen in a mountain cave, where '山室' refers to a mountain cave.
    • 自非神变幽妙 - Unless it is a divine transformation of the mysterious, where '神变幽妙' refers to a divine and mysterious transformation.
    • 孰能如此者乎 - Who could do such a thing? where '孰能' means 'who could' and '如此' means 'such a thing'.
    • 出汉武帝内传 - From the *Inner Transmission of Emperor Wu of Han*, where '汉武帝内传' refers to a specific historical text.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 青布三十疋 - Refers to a specific type of cloth and its measurement, which is an ancient unit of cloth.
    • 錢九萬 - Refers to the monetary value in ancient Chinese currency.
    • 太廟 - Refers to the imperial ancestral temple, a significant religious and political institution in ancient China.
    • 帝崩時遺詔 - Refers to the will of the emperor at the time of his death, a significant legal and political document.
    • 雜經三十余卷 - Refers to a collection of religious texts, which were important in ancient Chinese religious practices.
    • 河東 - Refers to a historical region in China, which was significant in ancient Chinese history.
    • 上黨抱犊山 - Refers to specific geographical locations in ancient China.
    • 金箱 - Refers to a box made of gold, which was a valuable and significant item in ancient Chinese culture.
    • 東觀 - Refers to an ancient observatory or office, which was significant in ancient Chinese administration.
    • 孝武皇帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Han, a significant historical figure in ancient China.
    • 河東太守 - Refers to a local official in ancient China, which was significant in ancient Chinese administration.
    • 宣帝 - Refers to the current emperor, which was significant in ancient Chinese politics.
    • 九都龙真经 - Refers to a specific scripture, which was significant in ancient Chinese religious practices.
    • 太隂 - Refers to the realm of the dead, which was significant in ancient Chinese religious beliefs.
    • 地戸 - Refers to the threshold of the earth, which was significant in ancient Chinese religious beliefs.
    • 出汉武帝内传 - Refers to a specific historical text, which was significant in ancient Chinese historical records.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 賣此二物青布三十疋錢九萬即售之 - This sentence is a compact description of selling two items: thirty bolts of blue cloth for nine thousand coins and selling them immediately.
    • 度實不知賣箱杖主姓名事實如此 - This sentence is a statement of uncertainty about the identity of the owner of the box and staff, followed by a statement of fact.
    • 有司以聞商人放還 - This sentence is a statement of the officials reporting the situation and the merchant being released.
    • 詔以二物付太廟 - This sentence is a statement of the imperial decree ordering the two items to be given to the Imperial Ancestral Temple.

    The merchant sold two items: thirty bolts of blue cloth for nine thousand coins, and sold them immediately. In fact, he did not know the name of the owner of the box and staff. The facts are as such, and the officials reported it. The merchant was released, and the imperial decree ordered the two items to be given to the Imperial Ancestral Temple. At the time of the emperor's death, the will ordered that over thirty scrolls of miscellaneous scriptures, which were frequently read and cherished, be kept with the body. In the second year of the Yuan Kang era, Li You, an official in Hedong, entered Shangdang's Baodushan and gathered herbs in a cave. He found this scripture, stored in a golden box, inscribed with the name of the Eastern Observatory official and the date and month, during the reign of Emperor Wu. Zhang Chun, the governor of Hedong, presented the scripture and box to the emperor. The emperor asked the officials around him, and Rande, the official in charge of books, saw the scripture and the box. He cried and replied, "These are the items used in the burial of Emperor Wu. I placed them in the coffin at that time. I do not know how they could have come out." The current emperor was greatly saddened and astonished, and ordered the scripture to be placed in the temple of Emperor Wu. According to the Nine Capitals Dragon True Scripture, those who attain immortality first die, pass through the realm of the Great Yin to refine the body, cross the threshold of the earth, and then can leave their bodies. Moreover, the scripture and staff were first buried, then suddenly appeared and were sold in the market. The scripture was seen in a mountain cave. Unless it is a divine transformation of the mysterious, who could do such a thing? (From the Inner Transmission of Emperor Wu of Han) [45]

    太平广记卷三
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　太平广记卷四
    　　神仙四
    　　王子乔　　　凤纲　　　　琴髙
    　　谷先生　　萧史　　　　徐福
    　　王母使者　　月支使者　　衞叔卿
    　　张楷　　　　阳翁伯
    　　王子乔
    　　王子乔者周灵王太子也好吹笙作凤凰鸣游伊洛之间道士浮丘公接以上嵩山三十余年后求之于山见桓良曰告我家七月七日待我于缑氏山头果乗白驻山岭望之不到举手谢时人数日而去后立祠于缑氏及嵩山【出列仙传】
    　　凤纲
    　　凤纲者渔阳人也常采百草花以水渍封泥之自正月始尽九月末止埋之百日煎九火卒死者以药内口中皆立活纲常服此药至数百嵗不老后入地肺山中仙去【出神仙传】 [46]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [46]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 太平广记 - A collection of strange tales and supernatural stories compiled during the Song Dynasty.
    • 钦定四库全书 - The Complete Library of the Four Treasuries, a massive imperial compilation of Chinese texts.
    • 神仙 - Refers to immortals or deities in Chinese mythology.
    • 王子乔 - A legendary figure often associated with immortality.
    • 凤纲 - Another legendary figure, known for his longevity and ascension to immortality.
    • 缑氏山头 - A specific mountain where the story of Wang Ziqiao takes place.
    • 驻山岭 - To stop or halt on a mountain ridge.
    • 举手谢 - To wave goodbye or take leave with a gesture.
    • 博学求之 - To seek knowledge broadly.
    • 无不利于事也 - Nothing is unhelpful in matters, suggesting that all roles or pursuits are valuable in their own right.
    • 地肺山 - A mythical mountain where Feng Gang ascended to immortality.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周灵王 - King Ling of Zhou, a ruler during the Eastern Zhou period.
    • 浮丘公 - A legendary figure who helped Wang Ziqiao ascend to immortality.
    • 嵩山 - One of the Five Great Mountains of China, often associated with immortality and Taoist practices.
    • 渔阳 - An ancient county in present-day Beijing, known for its cultural and historical significance.
    • 神仙传 - A collection of biographies of immortals, compiled by Ge Hong during the Jin Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 太平广记 - The Complete Library of the Four Treasuries.
    • 钦定四库全书 - The Complete Library of the Four Treasuries.
    • 王子乔 - Wang Ziqiao.
    • 凤纲 - Feng Gang.
    • 缑氏山 - Gou Mountain.
    • 嵩山 - Songshan Mountain.
    • 渔阳 - Yuyang County.
    • 地肺山 - Difei Mountain.
    • 神仙传 - Biographies of Immortals.

    The Complete Library of the Four Treasuries, Volume Three
    The Complete Library of the Four Treasuries, Volume Four
    Immortals Four
    Wang Ziqiao, Feng Gang, Qinhao
    Guigu Mr., Xiao Shi, Xu Fu
    Queen Mother's Messenger, Yuechi Messenger, Weishuqing
    Zhang Kai, Yang Wengbo

    Wang Ziqiao
    Wang Ziqiao was the crown prince of King Ling of Zhou. He loved to play the bamboo flute and imitate the sound of a phoenix. He roamed between the rivers Yi and Luo. The Taoist master Float Hill Gong brought him up to Songshan Mountain. After more than thirty years, when someone sought him on the mountain, he met Huan Liang and said, "Tell my family that I will wait for them on Gou Mountain on the seventh day of the seventh month." Indeed, he rode on a white crane and stopped on the mountain ridge. People could not see him, and he waved goodbye with his hand, taking leave from the world for several days. Later, shrines were built in honor of him on Gou Mountain and Songshan Mountain. (From Biographies of Immortals)

    Feng Gang
    Feng Gang was from Yuyang. He often collected flowers from various grasses, soaked them in water, and sealed them with clay. From the beginning of January to the end of September, he buried them for a hundred days, then boiled them nine times. Those who died suddenly could be revived by putting the medicine in their mouths. Feng Gang often took this medicine and lived for hundreds of years without aging. Later, he ascended to Difei Mountain and became an immortal. (From Biographies of Immortals) [46]

    萧史
    　　萧史不知得道年代貌如二十许人善吹箫作鸾鳯之响而琼姿炜烁风神超迈真天人也混迹于世时莫能知之秦穆公有女弄玉善吹箫公以弄玉妻之遂教弄玉作凤鸣居十数年吹箫似鳯声鳯凰来止其屋公为作鳯台夫妇止其上不饮不食不下数年一旦弄玉乗鳯萧史乗龙升天而去秦为作鳯女祠时闻箫声今洪州西山絶顶有萧史石仙坛石室及岩屋真像存焉莫知年代【出神仙传拾遗】
    　　徐福
    　　徐福字君房不知何许人也秦始皇时大宛中多枉死者横道数有鸟衔草覆死人面皆登时活有司奏闻始皇始皇使使者赍此草以问北郭谷先生云是东海中祖洲上不死之草生琼田中一名飬神芝其叶似菰不丛生一株可活千人始皇于是谓可索得因遣福及童男童女各三千人乗楼船入海寻祖洲不返后不知所之逮沈羲得道黄老遣福为使者乗白虎车度世君司马生乗龙车侍郎薄延之乗白鹿车俱来迎羲而去由是后人知 [48]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [48]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 蕭史不知得道年代貌如二十許人善吹簫作鸞鳳之響瓊姿煒燁風神超邁真天人也 - Describes Xiao Shi's appearance and abilities, emphasizing his divine nature and musical talent.
    • 秦穆公有女弄玉善吹簫公以弄玉妻之遂教弄玉作鳳鳴居十數年吹簫似鳳聲鳳凰來止其屋公為作鳳台夫婦止其上不飲不食不下數年 - Describes the union between Xiao Shi and Nongyu, their musical training, and their ascension to heaven.
    • 一旦弄玉乘鳳蕭史乘龍升天而去秦為作鳳女祠時聞簫聲今洪州西山絕頂有蕭史石仙壇石室及岩屋真像存焉莫知年代 - Describes the final departure of Xiao Shi and Nongyu and the lasting legacy of their story.
    • 徐福字君房不知何許人也秦始皇時大宛中多枉死者橫道數有鳥銜草覆死人面皆登時活有司奏聞始皇始皇使使者赍此草以問北郭鬼谷先生云是東海中祖洲上不死之草生瓊田中一名養神芝其葉似菰不叢生一株可活千人 - Describes the legend of Xu Fu, the immortal grass, and the events surrounding it.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 蕭史 - A legendary figure from the Warring States period, known for his musical skills and his union with Nongyu.
    • 弄玉 - A legendary figure from the Warring States period, known for her musical talents and her union with Xiao Shi.
    • 秦穆公 - Prince Mu of Qin, a historical figure from the Warring States period, known for his wisdom and generosity.
    • 鳳凰 - A mythical bird symbolizing nobility, grace, and immortality in Chinese culture.
    • 徐福 - A legendary figure from the Qin dynasty, known for his expedition to find the immortal grass and his association with the legend of the elixir of life.
    • 秦始皇 - Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of a unified China, known for his ambitious projects and his quest for immortality.
    • 大宛 - An ancient kingdom in Central Asia, known for its horses and its role in the Silk Road.
    • 北郭鬼谷先生 - The Master of North De Ghost Valley, a legendary figure known for his wisdom and his role in the legend of the immortal grass.
    • 祖洲 - A mythical island in the East Sea, known for its immortal grass and its association with the legend of Xu Fu.
    • 黃老 - A philosophical school combining elements of Daoism and Legalism, emphasizing the Way of the Yellow Emperor and the Laozi.
    • 沈羲 - A legendary figure from the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his attainment of the Way and his association with the legend of Xu Fu.
    • 司馬生 - The Master of the World, a legendary figure associated with the legend of Xu Fu.
    • 薄延之 - The Attendant, a legendary figure associated with the legend of Xu Fu.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The long sentences describing Xiao Shi, Nongyu, and Xu Fu must be read as comprehensive descriptions of their divine nature, linked events, and the legend of the immortal grass.

    Final Translation

    Xiao Shi

    Xiao Shi, whose age at achieving immortality is unknown, appears to be around twenty years old. He is skilled in playing the flute to produce the sound of phoenixes, and his noble appearance shines brightly, his spirit surpassing the ordinary, truly making him a celestial being. He blends into the world, and no one can recognize him. Prince Mu of Qin had a daughter named Nongyu who was skilled in playing the flute. Prince Mu married her to Xiao Shi, and he then taught Nongyu to produce the sound of a phoenix. After living together for several years, her flute playing sounded like the call of a phoenix, and a phoenix came to rest in their house. Prince Mu built a phoenix platform for them, and they lived there without eating or drinking for several years. One day, Nongyu rode a phoenix and Xiao Shi rode a dragon, ascending to heaven and departing. The Qin state built a shrine for the Phoenix Maiden, and people could still hear the sound of the flute. Today, at the summit of Xi Mountain in Hongzhou, there are stone altars, stone chambers, and rock houses with true images of Xiao Shi, and no one knows the age. [From Suiyuan Zhuan (Tales of Immortals)]

    Xu Fu

    Xu Fu, also known as Junfang, whose origin is unknown. During the reign of Qin Shi Huang, in Dawan, many innocent people died, and birds would fly over them, covering their faces with grass, and they would come back to life immediately. The officials reported this to Qin Shi Huang, who sent envoys to ask the Master of North De Ghost Valley, who said it was the 'immortal grass' from Zuzhou Island in the East Sea, growing in the Jade Fields, also known as 'Nourishing Spirit Grass,' with leaves similar to gusi (a type of grass), not growing in clusters, and one plant can revive a thousand people. Qin Shi Huang believed it could be found and sent Xu Fu along with 3,000 boys and girls in a palace ship to search for Zuzhou Island in the sea, but they never returned, and no one knows where they went. Later, when Shen Xi attained the Way of Huang-Lao, he sent Xu Fu as an envoy, riding in a white tiger chariot, to usher him into the world. The Master of the World, Simasheng, rode in a dragon chariot, and the Attendant, Bao Yanzhi, rode in a white deer chariot, all coming to welcome Shen Xi and leaving. From then on, people knew. [48]

    福得道矣又唐开元中有士人患半身枯黑御医张尚容等不能知其人聚族言曰形体如是寜可乆闻大海中有神仙正当求仙方可愈此疾宗族留之不可因与侍者赍粮至登州大海侧遇空舟乃赍所携挂帆随风可行十余日近一孤岛岛上有数百人如朝谒状须臾至岸岸侧有妇人洗药因问彼皆何者妇人指云中心牀坐须鬂白者徐君也又问徐君是谁妇人云君知秦始皇时徐福曰知之此则是也顷之众各散去某遂登岸致谒具
    语始末求其医理徐君曰汝之疾遇我即生初以美饭哺之器物皆竒小某嫌其薄君云能尽此为再餐也但恐不尽尔某连啖之如数瓯物致饱而饮亦以一小器盛酒饮之致醉翌日以黑药数丸令食食讫痢黑汁数升其疾乃愈某求住奉事徐君云尔有禄位未宜即留当以东风相送无愁归路遥也复与黄药一袋云此药善治一切病还遇病者可以刀圭饮之某还数日至登州以药奏闻时宗令有疾者服之皆愈【出仙传拾遗及广异记】 [49]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [49]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 福得道矣 - Indicates that the person has attained the Dao or achieved enlightenment.
    • 又唐开元中有士人患半身枯黑 - Narrative introduction describing a scholar during the Tang Dynasty who suffered from a condition where half of his body turned black.
    • 御医张尚容等不能知其人 - Indicates that the imperial physician Zhang Shangrong and others could not diagnose the condition.
    • 聚族言曰 - A phrase indicating that the family gathered to discuss the matter.
    • 形体如是宁可久存 - A question expressing concern about whether someone with such a condition can survive for long.
    • 闻大海中有神仙正当求仙方可愈此疾 - A statement suggesting that there are immortals in the sea, and only by seeking them can the disease be cured.
    • 宗族留之不可因与侍者赍粮至登州大海侧 - Indicates that the family tried to prevent him from leaving but failed, so he went with his attendants to the coast near Dengzhou.
    • 遇空舟乃赍所携挂帆随风可行十余日 - Describes how they found an empty boat, loaded their supplies, and sailed for over ten days.
    • 近一孤岛岛上有数百人如朝谒状 - Describes approaching a solitary island where hundreds of people were gathered as if for an audience.
    • 须臾至岸岸侧有妇人洗药 - Indicates that soon they reached the shore, and a woman was washing medicine there.
    • 具语始末求其医理 - A phrase indicating that they explained the entire situation and sought medical advice.
    • 汝之疾遇我即生初以美饭哺之器物皆奇小 - A statement from the woman indicating that the person's disease would be cured by her, starting with feeding them delicious food in small containers.
    • 能尽此为再餐也但恐不尽尔 - A statement indicating that they should finish the food, which would be considered a second meal, but it might not be enough.
    • 连啖之如数瓯物致饱而饮亦以一小器盛酒饮之致醉 - Describes how they continued eating until full and then drank small amounts of wine until drunk.
    • 翌日以黑药数丸令食食讫痢黑汁数升其疾乃愈 - Describes the next day when the woman gave them several pills, which caused them to expel black liquid and ultimately cured the disease.
    • 某求住奉事徐君云尔有禄位未宜即留当以东风相送无愁归路遥也 - A statement indicating that the person wanted to stay and serve the woman, but she said that they had official duties and should not stay, promising to send them back with the east wind.
    • 复与黄药一袋云此药善治一切病还遇病者可以刀圭饮之 - Describes how the woman gave them a bag of yellow medicine, saying it could cure all diseases and could be used to treat others.
    • 某还数日至登州以药奏闻时玄宗令有疾者服之皆愈 - Describes how the person returned after several days to Dengzhou and reported the medicine to Emperor Xuanzong, who ordered it to be used by those who were ill, and they all recovered.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 唐开元 - Refers to the Kaiyuan era of the Tang Dynasty, a period of prosperity and cultural flourishing.
    • 徐福 - Refers to Xu Fu, a famous figure from the Qin Dynasty who is said to have sailed east in search of immortality.
    • 玄宗 - Refers to Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a renowned emperor known for his cultural patronage and military campaigns.
    • 大海中有神仙 - A common theme in Chinese mythology and folklore, suggesting the existence of immortals in the sea.
    • 黄药 - Refers to a type of medicinal herb, often associated with healing and longevity.

    Proper Nouns

    • 唐开元 - The Kaiyuan era of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 徐福 - Xu Fu, a famous figure from the Qin Dynasty.
    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 黄药 - A type of medicinal herb.

    A Man Who Attained the Dao and Sought Immortality

    In the Kaiyuan era of the Tang Dynasty, there was a scholar who suffered from a condition where half of his body turned black. The imperial physician Zhang Shangrong and others could not diagnose his ailment. The family gathered and said, "With such a condition, can one survive for long?" They heard that there were immortals in the sea, and only by seeking them could the disease be cured. The family tried to prevent him from leaving, but they failed, so he went with his attendants to the coast near Dengzhou. They found an empty boat, loaded their supplies, and sailed for over ten days. They approached a solitary island where hundreds of people were gathered as if for an audience. Soon they reached the shore, and a woman was washing medicine there. They explained the entire situation and sought medical advice. The woman indicated that the person's disease would be cured by her, starting with feeding them delicious food in small containers. She said, "If you can finish this, it will be considered a second meal, but it might not be enough." They continued eating until full and then drank small amounts of wine until drunk. The next day, the woman gave them several pills, which caused them to expel black liquid and ultimately cured the disease. The person wanted to stay and serve the woman, but she said, "You have official duties and should not stay here. I will send you back with the east wind; do not worry about the long journey." She also gave them a bag of yellow medicine, saying it could cure all diseases and could be used to treat others. After several days, they returned to Dengzhou and reported the medicine to Emperor Xuanzong, who ordered it to be used by those who were ill, and they all recovered. [49]

    王母使者
    　　汉武帝天汉三年帝廵东海祠恒山王母遣使献灵胶四两吉光毛裘武帝以付外库不知胶裘二物之妙也以为西国虽逺而贡者不竒使者未遣之帝幸华林苑射虎兕弩防使者时随驾因上言请以胶一分以口濡其胶以续弩帝惊曰此异物也乃使武士数人对牵引之终日不脱胜未续时也胶青色如碧玉吉光毛裘黄白盖神马之裘入水终日不沈入火不焦帝悟厚赂使者而遣去集胶出自凤麟洲洲在西海中地靣正方皆一千五百里四靣皆弱水绕之上多凤麟数万为羣煮凤喙及麟角合煎作胶名之集胶一名连金泥弓弩已断之刀劒已断之鐡以胶连续终不脱也【出仙传拾遗】 [50]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [50]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 王母遣使獻靈膠四兩吉光毛裘 - The Queen Mother sent an envoy to present four ounces of magical glue and the fur coat of a divine beast.
    • 武帝以付外庫不知膠裘二物之妙也 - Emperor Wu placed it in the outer treasury; did not know the wonders of the glue and the fur coat.
    • 以為西國雖逺而貢者不竒 - Thought that although the Western country was far away, what it offered was not strange.
    • 使者時隨駕請以膠一分以口濡其膠以續弩 - The envoy happened to be accompanying the emperor; requested to use one part of the glue, moisten it with his mouth, and continue the bowstring.
    • 此異物也乃使武士數人對牽引之終日不脫勝未續時也 - This is a strange object; then ordered several warriors to pull it; all day without coming apart; better than when it was not continued.
    • 膠青色如碧玉吉光毛裘黃白蓋神馬之 - The glue is green like emerald jade; the fur coat of the divine beast is yellow and white, like the fur of a divine horse.
    • 裘入水終日不沈入火不焦 - The fur coat does not sink in water all day; does not burn in fire.
    • 帝悟厚賂使者而遣去 - The emperor understood and gave the envoy a generous gift before sending him away.
    • 集膠出自鳳麟洲 - The glue is produced on the Phoenix and Unicorn Island.
    • 洲在西海中 - The island is in the Western Sea.
    • 地面正方皆一千五百里 - The island is square, with each side being one thousand five hundred li.
    • 四面皆弱水繞之 - Surrounded by weak water on all sides.
    • 上多鳳麟數萬為羣 - There are tens of thousands of phoenixes and unicorns living in groups.
    • 煮鳳喙及麟角合煎作膠名之集膠一名連金泥 - Cooking the beaks of phoenixes and the horns of unicorns together, they make the glue called 'Set Bowstring Glue' or 'Gold Link Clay.'
    • 弓弩已斷之刀劒已斷之鐵以膠連續終不脫也 - Bowstrings and arrows, swords and iron, once broken, can be reconnected with the glue and will never come apart.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 王母 - The Queen Mother, a figure in Chinese mythology often associated with immortality and longevity.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu, a prominent ruler of the Han dynasty.
    • 天漢三年 - The third year of Emperor Wu's Tianhan era.
    • 鳳麟洲 - Phoenix and Unicorn Island, a mythical place in Chinese folklore.
    • 弱水 - Weak water, a mythical substance that is difficult to cross.
    • 集膠 - Set Bowstring Glue, a magical substance with extraordinary properties.
    • 連金泥 - Gold Link Clay, another name for the same magical substance.
    • 鳳喙及麟角 - Beaks of phoenixes and horns of unicorns, ingredients used to make the magical glue.
    • 弓弩已斷之刀劒已斷之鐵 - Broken bowstrings, arrows, swords, and iron, which can be reconnected with the glue.

    The Envoy of the Queen Mother

    In the third year of Emperor Wu's Tianhan era, he toured the Eastern Sea and paid homage to Mount Heng, where the Queen Mother sent an envoy to present four ounces of magical glue and the fur coat of a divine beast. Emperor Wu placed these items in the outer treasury but did not know the wonders of the glue and the fur coat. He thought that although the Western country was far away, what it offered was not strange. The envoy, who happened to be accompanying the emperor, requested to use one part of the glue, moisten it with his mouth, and continue the bowstring of a crossbow. Emperor Wu was amazed and said, "This is a strange object." He then ordered several warriors to pull on the bowstring, and it remained unbroken all day, even better than when it was not continued. The glue was green like emerald jade, and the fur coat of the divine beast was yellow and white, like the fur of a divine horse. The fur coat did not sink in water all day and did not burn in fire. Understanding the power of the glue, Emperor Wu gave the envoy a generous gift and sent him away.

    The glue, known as "Set Bowstring Glue" or "Gold Link Clay," is produced on the Phoenix and Unicorn Island, which is located in the Western Sea. The island is square, with each side measuring one thousand five hundred li, and is surrounded by weak water on all sides. There are tens of thousands of phoenixes and unicorns living in groups on the island. The glue is made by cooking the beaks of phoenixes and the horns of unicorns together, resulting in a substance that can reconnect broken bowstrings, arrows, swords, and iron, ensuring they never come apart. [50]

    月支使者
    　　汉延和三年春武帝幸安定西胡月支国王遣使献香四两大如雀卵黑如桑椹帝以香非中国所乏以付外库又献猛兽一头形如五六十日犬子大如貍其毛黄色国使将以呈帝帝见使者抱之以入其气秃悴尤怪其所贡之非问使者曰此小物何谓猛兽使者对曰夫威加于百禽者不必计其大小是以神驎为巨象之王凤凰为大之宗亦不在巨细也臣国去此三十万里常占东风入律百旬不休青云干吕连月不散者中国将有好道之君矣我国王将仰中土而慕道风薄金玉而厚灵物故搜竒蕴而索神香歩天林而请猛兽 [51]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [51]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 延和三年 - Specific year in the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, indicating the time period.
    • 幸安定 - ‘幸’ means ‘to visit’ or ‘to grace with a visit,’ and ‘安定’ is a place name. Together, it means ‘visited An Ding.’
    • 月支国王 - ‘月支’ is a historical kingdom, and ‘国王’ means ‘king.’ Together, it refers to the king of the Yuezhi kingdom.
    • 献香 - ‘献’ means ‘to present’ or ‘to offer,’ and ‘香’ means ‘incense.’ Together, it means ‘presented incense.’
    • 猛兽 - ‘猛’ means ‘fierce’ or ‘wild,’ and ‘兽’ means ‘beast.’ Together, it means ‘fierce beast.’
    • 形如五六十日犬子 - ‘形如’ means ‘shaped like,’ ‘五六十日’ means ‘about fifty to sixty days old,’ and ‘犬子’ means ‘puppy.’ Together, it means ‘shaped like a puppy about fifty to sixty days old.’
    • 大如狸 - ‘大如’ means ‘as big as,’ and ‘狸’ means ‘weasel.’ Together, it means ‘as big as a weasel.’
    • 其毛黄色 - ‘其毛’ means ‘its fur,’ and ‘黄色’ means ‘yellow.’ Together, it means ‘its fur is yellow.’
    • 气秃悴 - ‘气’ means ‘appearance’ or ‘look,’ ‘秃’ means ‘bald’ or ‘withered,’ and ‘悴’ means ‘wasted’ or ‘dejected.’ Together, it means ‘withered and dejected appearance.’
    • 夫威加于百禽者不必计其大小 - ‘夫’ is an introductory particle, ‘威加于’ means ‘exerts authority over,’ ‘百禽’ means ‘many birds,’ ‘不必’ means ‘does not necessarily,’ and ‘计其大小’ means ‘consider its size.’ Together, it means ‘One who exerts authority over many birds does not necessarily consider its size.’
    • 神驎为巨象之王 - ‘神驎’ means ‘divine unicorn,’ ‘为’ means ‘is,’ ‘巨象’ means ‘giant elephant,’ and ‘之王’ means ‘the king.’ Together, it means ‘the divine unicorn is the king of giant elephants.’
    • 凤凰为大之宗 - ‘凤凰’ means ‘phoenix,’ ‘为’ means ‘is,’ ‘大’ means ‘great bird,’ and ‘之宗’ means ‘the leader.’ Together, it means ‘the phoenix is the leader of great birds.’
    • 仰中土而慕道风 - ‘仰’ means ‘look up to,’ ‘中土’ means ‘central lands’ (China), ‘而’ is a conjunction, ‘慕’ means ‘admire,’ and ‘道风’ means ‘way of the Dao.’ Together, it means ‘look up to the central lands and admire the way of the Dao.’
    • 薄金玉而厚灵物 - ‘薄’ means ‘light on,’ ‘金玉’ means ‘gold and jade,’ ‘而’ is a conjunction, ‘厚’ means ‘value highly,’ and ‘灵物’ means ‘spiritual objects.’ Together, it means ‘light on gold and jade but value highly spiritual objects.’
    • 搜竒蕴而索神香 - ‘搜’ means ‘search for,’ ‘竒蕴’ means ‘rare treasures,’ ‘而’ is a conjunction, ‘索’ means ‘seek,’ and ‘神香’ means ‘divine incense.’ Together, it means ‘search for rare treasures and seek divine incense.’
    • 步天林而请猛兽 - ‘步’ means ‘walk through,’ ‘天林’ means ‘heavenly forest,’ ‘而’ is a conjunction, ‘请’ means ‘request,’ and ‘猛兽’ means ‘fierce beast.’ Together, it means ‘walk through the heavenly forest and request a fierce beast.’

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 汉延和三年 - Specific year in the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, indicating the time period.
    • 月支 - Refers to the Yuezhi kingdom, a historical Central Asian kingdom.
    • 香 - Incense was highly valued in ancient China, often used in religious and ceremonial contexts.
    • 猛兽 - Fierce beasts were often presented as tributes or symbols of power and strength.
    • 神驎 - The divine unicorn is a mythical creature symbolizing purity and power.
    • 凤凰 - The phoenix is a mythical bird symbolizing immortality and rebirth.
    • 中土 - Refers to the central lands, traditionally associated with China.
    • 道风 - The way of the Dao, referring to the philosophical and spiritual path of Daoism.
    • 金玉 - Gold and jade were highly valued materials in ancient China, often used in rituals and as symbols of wealth and status.
    • 灵物 - Spiritual objects were considered sacred and often used in religious practices.
    • 天林 - The heavenly forest is a metaphorical term, possibly referring to a sacred or mystical place.

    Proper Nouns

    • 汉 - The Han dynasty, a major historical period in Chinese history.
    • 延和三年 - A specific year in the reign of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a significant ruler of the Han dynasty.
    • 安定 - A place name, possibly referring to a region or city in ancient China.
    • 月支国王 - The king of the Yuezhi kingdom.
    • 神驎 - The divine unicorn.
    • 凤凰 - The phoenix.
    • 中土 - The central lands, traditionally associated with China.
    • 金玉 - Gold and jade.
    • 灵物 - Spiritual objects.
    • 天林 - The heavenly forest.

    In the third year of the Yanyuan era of the Han dynasty, Emperor Wu visited An Ding. The king of the Yuezhi kingdom sent an envoy to present four large pieces of incense, which were as big as sparrow eggs and as black as mulberry fruits. The emperor, considering that incense was not a rare commodity in China, had it stored in the imperial treasury. The envoy also presented a fierce beast, which was shaped like a puppy about fifty to sixty days old and as big as a weasel, with yellow fur. The envoy carried the beast into the emperor's presence.
