The beast had a withered and dejected appearance. The emperor was puzzled by the nature of the beast and asked the envoy, "What do you mean by calling this a fierce beast?" The envoy replied, "One who exerts authority over many birds does not necessarily consider its size. The divine unicorn is the king of giant elephants, and the phoenix is the leader of great birds. It does not matter if it is large or small. Our king looks up to the central lands and admires the way of the Dao. He values spiritual objects more than gold and jade. Therefore, he searches for rare treasures and seeks divine incense, and walks through the heavenly forest to request a fierce beast." [51]

    乗肥车而济弱水策足以度飞沙契濶途径艰苦蹊路于今十三年矣神香辟天残之死疾猛兽却百邪之魍魉夫此二物者实济众生之至要助至化之升平岂图陛下乃不知贵乎是臣国占风之谬也今日仰鉴天姿乃非有道之君也眼多视则贪恣口多言则犯难身多动则注贼心多节则奢侈未有用此四多而天下成治者也帝黙然不平帝乃使使者勑猛兽发声试听之使者乃指兽令发一声兽防唇良乆忽如天雷霹之响又作两目如防磹之炎光乆乃止帝登时颠蹷掩耳振动不能自止侍者乃武士皆失仗帝忌之因以此 [52]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [52]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 乗肥车而济弱水 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'riding a fat carriage to cross the weak water,' symbolizing a difficult journey or challenge.
    • 神香辟天残之死疾 - Means 'divine fragrance repels the deadly disease of heaven's remnants,' suggesting a supernatural remedy.
    • 猛兽却百邪之魍魉 - Means 'the fierce beast repels the myriad demons,' implying protection from evil.
    • 实济众生之至要 - Means 'it is truly essential for the salvation of all beings,' emphasizing the importance of the two items mentioned.
    • 助至化之升平 - Means 'it aids in the great transformation and prosperity,' suggesting a role in achieving peace and harmony.
    • 岂图陛下乃不知贵乎 - Means 'how could it be that Your Majesty does not know its value,' expressing surprise at the emperor's ignorance.
    • 臣国占风之谬也 - Means 'it is a mistake in my country's divination of the wind,' suggesting a misinterpretation or error.
    • 今日仰鉴天姿乃非有道之君也 - Means 'today, upon observing Your Majesty's appearance, it is clear that you are not a virtuous ruler,' criticizing the emperor's lack of virtue.
    • 眼多视则贪恣 - Means 'having many eyes leads to greed and indulgence,' suggesting that excessive observation leads to negative traits.
    • 口多言则犯难 - Means 'having many words leads to difficulties,' implying that excessive speech can cause problems.
    • 身多动则注贼 - Means 'having many actions leads to attracting thieves,' suggesting that excessive activity can invite trouble.
    • 心多节则奢侈 - Means 'having many desires leads to extravagance,' implying that excessive desires result in wastefulness.
    • 未有用此四多而天下成治者也 - Means 'there has never been a case where using these four excesses has led to the governance of the world,' criticizing the emperor's behavior.
    • 帝黙然不平 - Means 'the emperor was silent but discontented,' indicating a reaction to the criticism.
    • 帝乃使使者勑猛兽发声试听之 - Means 'the emperor then sent an envoy to command the fierce beast to make a sound for testing,' suggesting a test of the beast's power.
    • 使者乃指兽令发一声 - Means 'the envoy pointed to the beast and ordered it to make a sound,' describing the action taken.
    • 兽防唇良乆忽如天雷霹之响 - Means 'the beast's mouth remained closed for a long time, then suddenly made a sound like the thunder of a bolt of lightning,' describing the beast's powerful roar.
    • 又作两目如防磹之炎光 - Means 'it also made its two eyes like the fiery light of a lantern,' describing the beast's intense gaze.
    • 帝登时颠蹷掩耳振动不能自止 - Means 'the emperor immediately fell to the ground, covering his ears, and could not stop shaking,' describing the emperor's reaction to the sound.
    • 侍者乃武士皆失仗 - Means 'the attendants, who were all warriors, lost their weapons,' indicating the impact of the sound on the attendants.
    • 帝忌之因以此 - Means 'the emperor feared it and therefore acted accordingly,' suggesting that the emperor took action due to his fear.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 陛下 - Refers to the emperor, a term of respect used in addressing the ruler.
    • 臣国 - Refers to the speaker's country, indicating the speaker's position as a subject of the emperor.
    • 占风 - Refers to divination or fortune-telling, a practice used to predict future events.
    • 有道之君 - Refers to a virtuous ruler, a concept from Confucian philosophy.
    • 四多 - Refers to the four excesses: many eyes, many words, many actions, and many desires, which are criticized in the text.
    • 天雷霹 - Refers to the sound of thunder, a powerful and awe-inspiring natural phenomenon.
    • 防磹 - Refers to a type of lantern, used to describe the intense light of the beast's eyes.

    Proper Nouns

    • 陛下 - The emperor.
    • 臣国 - The speaker's country.
    • 有道之君 - A virtuous ruler.
    • 四多 - The four excesses: many eyes, many words, many actions, and many desires.
    • 天雷霹 - The sound of thunder.
    • 防磹 - A type of lantern.

    Riding a fat carriage to cross the weak water, using a whip to measure the flying sand, traversing difficult and arduous paths—this journey has been ongoing for thirteen years. Divine fragrance repels the deadly disease of heaven's remnants, and the fierce beast repels the myriad demons. These two things are truly essential for the salvation of all beings and aid in the great transformation and prosperity. How could it be that Your Majesty does not know their value? It is a mistake in my country's divination of the wind. Today, upon observing Your Majesty's appearance, it is clear that you are not a virtuous ruler. Having many eyes leads to greed and indulgence, having many words leads to difficulties, having many actions leads to attracting thieves, and having many desires leads to extravagance. There has never been a case where using these four excesses has led to the governance of the world. The emperor was silent but discontented. The emperor then sent an envoy to command the fierce beast to make a sound for testing. The envoy pointed to the beast and ordered it to make a sound. The beast's mouth remained closed for a long time, then suddenly made a sound like the thunder of a bolt of lightning, and it also made its two eyes like the fiery light of a lantern. The emperor immediately fell to the ground, covering his ears, and could not stop shaking. The attendants, who were all warriors, lost their weapons. The emperor feared it and therefore acted accordingly. [52]

    兽付上林苑令虎食之虎见兽皆相聚屈迹如也帝恨使者言不逊欲罪之明日失使者及猛兽所在至始元元年京城大疫死者大半帝取月支神香烧之于城内其死未三日者皆活香气经三月不歇帝信神香乃秘録余香一旦函检如故而失神香也此香出于聚窟洲人鸟山山上多与枫相似而香闻数里名为返魂亦能自作声如羣牛吼闻之者心振神骇伐其木根于玉釡中煮取汁更以防火熟煎之如黑状令可丸名为惊精香或名振灵丸或名返生香或名振檀香或名却死香一种六名斯实灵物也【出仙传拾遗】 [53]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [53]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 獸付上林苑令虎食之 - Ambiguous; could mean 'the beast was sent to the Upper Forest Garden and eaten by a tiger' or 'the beast was given to the Upper Forest Garden and eaten by a tiger.'
    • 虎見獸皆相聚屈迹如也 - Ambiguous; could mean 'when tigers see beasts, they all gather and bow as if in reverence' or 'when tigers see beasts, they all gather and bow as if they are.'
    • 帝恨使者言不逊欲罪之 - The emperor was angry at the messenger's impolite words and wanted to punish him.
    • 明日失使者及猛兽所在 - The next day, the location of the messenger and the fierce beasts was lost.
    • 至始元元年京城大疫死者大半 - In the first year of Shiyuan, the capital suffered a great epidemic, and more than half of the people died.
    • 帝取月支神香燒之於城內其死未三日者皆活 - The emperor took the divine incense from Yuezhi and burned it inside the city, and those who had died within three days all came back to life.
    • 香氣經三月不歇 - The fragrance lasted for three months without fading.
    • 帝信神香乃秘錄余香一旦函檢如故而失神香也 - Ambiguous; could mean 'the emperor believed in the divine incense and secretly recorded the remaining fragrance, but one day the seal was broken and the divine incense was lost' or 'the emperor believed in the divine incense and secretly recorded the remaining fragrance, but one day the seal was broken and the divine incense was no longer there.'
    • 此香出於聚窟洲人鳥山山上多與楓相似而香聞數里名為返魂香亦能自作聲如群牛吼聞之者心振神駭伐其木根於玉釜中煮取汁更以火熟煎之如黑膏狀令可丸名為驚精香或名振靈丸或名返生香或名振檀香或名卻死香一種六名斯實靈物也【出仙傳拾遺】 - This incense comes from the mountain of People and Birds on the island of Juku Zhou. The mountain is covered with trees similar to maple trees, and the fragrance can be smelled for several miles. It is called the Return Soul Incense and can make sounds like a herd of cows bellowing. Those who hear it are shaken to their core. The roots of the trees are cut and boiled in a jade cauldron, and the juice is further cooked with fire until it becomes a black paste, which can be made into pills. It is also known as the Shock Spirit Pill, the Revive Spirit Pill, the Return to Life Incense, the Vibrate Sandalwood Incense, or the Turn Death Incense. One incense has six names, and it is indeed a divine substance. [From *Xian Chuan Shi Yi*]

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 上林苑 - The imperial garden in the capital, where exotic animals were kept.
    • 始元元年 - The first year of the Shiyuan era, a period in the reign of Emperor Zhao of Han.
    • 月支神香 - The divine incense from Yuezhi, a kingdom in Central Asia known for its medicinal and magical substances.
    • 聚窟洲 - The island of Juku Zhou, a mythical place in Chinese folklore.
    • 人鳥山 - The mountain of People and Birds, a mythical location on the island of Juku Zhou.
    • 仙傳拾遺 - *Xian Chuan Shi Yi*, a collection of stories about immortals and supernatural beings.

    Proper Nouns

    • 上林苑 - The Upper Forest Garden, an imperial garden.
    • 始元元年 - The first year of the Shiyuan era.
    • 月支神香 - The divine incense from Yuezhi.
    • 聚窟洲 - The island of Juku Zhou.
    • 人鳥山 - The mountain of People and Birds.
    • 仙傳拾遺 - *Xian Chuan Shi Yi*, a collection of stories about immortals and supernatural beings.

    A beast was sent to the Upper Forest Garden and eaten by a tiger. When tigers saw beasts, they all gathered and bowed as if in reverence. The emperor was angry at the messenger's impolite words and wanted to punish him. The next day, the location of the messenger and the fierce beasts was lost. In the first year of Shiyuan, the capital suffered a great epidemic, and more than half of the people died. The emperor took the divine incense from Yuezhi and burned it inside the city, and those who had died within three days all came back to life. The fragrance lasted for three months without fading. The emperor believed in the divine incense and secretly recorded the remaining fragrance, but one day the seal was broken and the divine incense was lost. This incense comes from the mountain of People and Birds on the island of Juku Zhou. The mountain is covered with trees similar to maple trees, and the fragrance can be smelled for several miles. It is called the Return Soul Incense and can make sounds like a herd of cows bellowing. Those who hear it are shaken to their core. The roots of the trees are cut and boiled in a jade cauldron, and the juice is further cooked with fire until it becomes a black paste, which can be made into pills. It is also known as the Shock Spirit Pill, the Revive Spirit Pill, the Return to Life Incense, the Vibrate Sandalwood Incense, or the Turn Death Incense. One incense has six names, and it is indeed a divine substance. [From Xian Chuan Shi Yi] [53]

    衞叔卿
    　　卫叔卿者中山人也服云母得仙汉仪凤二年八月壬辰孝武皇帝闲居殿上忽有一人乗云车驾白鹿从天而下来集殿前其人年可三十许色如童子羽衣星冠帝乃惊问曰为谁答曰吾中山衞叔卿也帝曰子若是中山人乃朕臣也可前共语叔卿本意谒帝谓帝好道见之必加优礼而帝今云是朕臣也于是大失望黙然不应忽然不知所在帝甚悔恨即遣使者梁伯至中山推求叔卿不得见但见其子名度世即将还见帝问云汝父今在何所对曰臣父少好仙道尝服药导引不交世事委家而去已四十余年云当入太华山也帝即遣使者与 [54]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [54]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 衞叔卿者中山人也 - Formal introduction stating that 'Wei Shuqing is a man from Zhongshan.'
    • 服云母得仙 - Means 'by consuming cloud mother (a mineral) he attained immortality.'
    • 漢儀鳳二年八月壬辰 - Refers to the specific date in the Yifeng era of the Han dynasty.
    • 孝武皇帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Han, one of the most famous emperors in Chinese history.
    • 閒居殿上 - Means 'sitting alone in the palace hall.'
    • 忽有一人乘云車驾白鹿从天而下来集殿前 - Describes a person suddenly arriving in a cloud vehicle and white deer, descending from heaven to the palace hall.
    • 色如童子 - Means 'his appearance was like that of a child.'
    • 羽衣星冠 - Describes a celestial being with feathered robes and star-crowned headgear.
    • 帝乃惊问曰 - Means 'the emperor was startled and asked.'
    • 子若是中山人乃朕臣也 - Means 'if you are from Zhongshan, then you are my subject.'
    • 可前共语 - Means 'please come forward and speak.'
    • 叔卿本意谒帝 - Means 'Wei Shuqing's original intention was to meet the emperor.'
    • 谓帝好道见之必加优礼 - Means 'he thought that since the emperor valued the Dao, he would surely show him great respect.'
    • 于是大失望 - Means 'thus, he was greatly disappointed.'
    • 默然不应 - Means 'he remained silent and did not respond.'
    • 忽然不知所在 - Means 'suddenly, he disappeared without a trace.'
    • 帝甚悔恨 - Means 'the emperor was very regretful.'
    • 即遣使者梁伯至中山推求叔卿不得见 - Means 'he immediately sent an envoy named Liang Bo to Zhongshan to seek out Wei Shuqing, but he could not find him.'
    • 但见其子名度世即将还见帝 - Means 'he only saw his son, named Duoshi, who was then brought back to see the emperor.'
    • 问云汝父今在何所 - Means 'he asked, 'Where is your father now?''
    • 对曰臣父少好仙道尝服药导引不交世事委家而去已四十余年云当入太华山也 - Means 'the son replied, 'My father, when young, loved the Dao and practiced alchemy and qigong. He did not engage in worldly affairs, left home, and has been gone for over forty years. He said he would enter Mount Taihua.''
    • 帝即遣使者与 - Means 'the emperor then sent an envoy with...' (elliptical clause)

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 衞叔卿 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology known for his immortality.
    • 中山 - A region in ancient China, often associated with the state of Zhongshan.
    • 云母 - A mineral believed to have magical properties in traditional Chinese medicine and alchemy.
    • 漢儀鳳二年 - The year 72 AD during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 孝武皇帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, known for his military conquests and cultural patronage.
    • 太华山 - Mount Taihua, a sacred mountain in Chinese mythology associated with immortals.
    • 仙道 - The pursuit of immortality and spiritual enlightenment, a central theme in Chinese Taoism.
    • 服药导引 - Practices of alchemy and qigong, traditional methods of achieving longevity and spiritual cultivation.

    Proper Nouns

    • 衞叔卿 - Wei Shuqing, a legendary figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 中山 - Zhongshan, a region in ancient China.
    • 云母 - Cloud mother, a mineral believed to have magical properties.
    • 漢儀鳳二年 - The year 72 AD during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 孝武皇帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 太华山 - Mount Taihua, a sacred mountain in Chinese mythology.
    • 仙道 - The pursuit of immortality and spiritual enlightenment.
    • 服药导引 - Practices of alchemy and qigong.
    • 梁伯 - Liang Bo, an envoy sent by the emperor.
    • 度世 - Duoshi, the son of Wei Shuqing.

    Wei Shuqing was a man from Zhongshan who attained immortality by consuming cloud mother. In the year 72 AD during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, the emperor was sitting alone in the palace hall when suddenly a person appeared in a cloud vehicle and white deer, descending from heaven to the palace hall. The person was about thirty years old, with the appearance of a child, wearing feathered robes and a star-crowned headgear. The emperor was startled and asked, "Who are you?" The person replied, "I am Wei Shuqing from Zhongshan." The emperor said, "If you are from Zhongshan, then you are my subject. Please come forward and speak." Wei Shuqing's original intention was to meet the emperor, thinking that since the emperor valued the Dao, he would surely show him great respect. Thus, he was greatly disappointed and remained silent and did not respond. Suddenly, he disappeared without a trace. The emperor was very regretful and immediately sent an envoy named Liang Bo to Zhongshan to seek out Wei Shuqing, but he could not find him. He only saw his son, named Duoshi, who was then brought back to see the emperor. The emperor asked, "Where is your father now?" The son replied, "My father, when young, loved the Dao and practiced alchemy and qigong. He did not engage in worldly affairs, left home, and has been gone for over forty years. He said he would enter Mount Taihua." The emperor then sent an envoy with... [54]

    度世共之华山求寻其父到山下欲上辄火不能上也积数十日度世谓使者曰岂不欲令吾与他人俱往乎乃斋戒独上未到其岭于絶岩之下望见其父与数人博戱于石上紫云欝欝于其上白玉为牀又有数仙童执幢节立其后度世望而载拜叔卿曰汝来何为度世曰帝甚恨前日仓卒不得与父言语今故遣使者梁伯与度世共来愿更得见父也叔卿曰前为太上所遣欲诫帝以大灾之期及救危厄之法国祚可延而彊梁自贵不识真道而反欲臣我不足告语是以去耳今当与中黄太乙共定天元吾终不复往耳度世曰不审向与父并坐是 [55]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [55]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 斋戒独上 - Fasting and praying before ascending alone, indicating a ritualistic or spiritual preparation.
    • 度世望而载拜 - Bowing deeply upon seeing the father, showing respect and reverence.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 华山 - One of the Five Great Mountains of China, known for its mystical and religious significance.
    • 太上 - Refers to the highest deity in Taoism, often associated with the Jade Emperor.
    • 梁伯 - A messenger, possibly a high-ranking official or a divine envoy.
    • 叔卿 - The father's name, likely a respected figure in a spiritual or mythological context.
    • 博戱 - Refers to a board game, possibly Go or another traditional Chinese game.
    • 紫云欝欝 - Purple clouds are often associated with divine or supernatural beings in Chinese mythology.
    • 白玉为牀 - Symbolizes purity and divinity.
    • 仙童执幢节 - Young immortals holding ceremonial staffs, indicating a divine or mystical presence.
    • 大灾之期 - Refers to a period of great disaster or calamity.
    • 救危厄之法国祚可延 - Refers to the idea of saving the nation from peril and extending its fortune.
    • 彊梁自贵 - Refers to the emperor's arrogance and self-importance.
    • 不识真道 - Indicates a lack of understanding of the true path or way.
    • 臣我 - Refers to the act of submitting or obeying, implying a hierarchical relationship.

    Proper Nouns

    • 度世 - The son's name.
    • 梁伯 - The messenger's name.
    • 叔卿 - The father's name.

    度世 accompanied the messenger to Mount Hua to search for his father. Upon reaching the base of the mountain, they found it difficult to ascend, as if a fire blocked the way. After many days, the messenger asked, "Do you not wish for someone else to accompany us?" The messenger then fasted and prayed before ascending alone. Before reaching the summit, at the foot of a steep cliff, he saw his father playing a game with several others on a stone surface. Above them, purple clouds gathered, and a bed made of white jade was visible. Several young immortals holding ceremonial staffs stood behind them. Upon seeing this, the messenger bowed deeply. The father said, "Why have you come?" The messenger replied, "The emperor deeply regrets not being able to speak with you due to a sudden event. He has now sent me and the messenger Liang Bo to see you again." The father said, "I was sent by the highest deity to warn the emperor about the impending disaster and to advise on saving the nation from peril. However, the emperor's arrogance and ignorance made it futile to communicate further, so I left. Now, I am to work with Zhong Huang Taiyi to determine the heavenly mandate, and I will not return. Is it true that you saw me playing a game with others?" [55]

    谁也叔卿曰洪崖先生许由巢父火低公飞黄子王子晋薛容耳今世向大乱天下无聊后数百年间土灭金亡汝归当取吾斋室西北隅大柱下玉函函中有神素书取而按方合服之一年可能乗云而行道成来就吾于此勿得为汉臣也亦不复为语帝也度世于是拜辞而去下山见梁伯不告所以梁伯意度世必有所得乃叩头于度世求乞道术先是度世与之共行见伯情行温实乃以语之梁伯但不见柱下之神方耳后掘得玉函封以飞仙之香取而饵服乃五色云母遂合药服之与梁伯俱仙去留其方与子而世人多有得之者【出神仙传】 [56]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [56]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 誰也叔卿曰 - Ambiguous phrase meaning 'Who also said to Shu Qing' or 'Who also told Shu Qing.' The exact relationship between the speaker and Shu Qing is unclear.
    • 按方合服之 - Literal meaning 'to follow the formula and take it.' Suggests following instructions in a book to achieve a specific outcome.
    • 勿得為漢臣 - Literal meaning 'do not become a Han official.' Implies a moral or spiritual choice to avoid worldly power.
    • 亦不復為語帝 - Literal meaning 'also do not speak to the emperor.' Suggests avoiding communication with the emperor after achieving a spiritual goal.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 洪崖先生 - A legendary figure often associated with immortality and the Daoist tradition.
    • 許由 - A hermit sage who refused the throne of the Xia Dynasty, symbolizing the ideal of reclusiveness and virtue.
    • 巢父 - Another hermit sage who lived in seclusion and rejected worldly power.
    • 火低公 - Less well-known, but could be a deity or sage in the Daoist pantheon.
    • 飛黃子 - A legendary figure, possibly a deity or sage.
    • 王子晉 - A legendary prince known for his ascension to heaven.
    • 薛容耳 - Less well-known, but could be a deity or sage.
    • 度世 - A historical figure, possibly a Daoist practitioner or immortal.
    • 梁伯 - A historical figure, possibly a Daoist practitioner or immortal.

    Proper Nouns

    • 洪崖先生 - A legendary figure.
    • 許由 - A hermit sage.
    • 巢父 - A hermit sage.
    • 火低公 - A less well-known figure.
    • 飛黃子 - A legendary figure.
    • 王子晉 - A legendary prince.
    • 薛容耳 - A less well-known figure.
    • 度世 - A historical figure.
    • 梁伯 - A historical figure.

    Who also said to Shu Qing: "Hóngyá Master, Xǔ Yóu, Cháofù, Huǒdīgōng, Fēihuáng Zǐ, Wángzǐ Jìn, and Xuē Róng Ěr. The current world is heading towards chaos and is full of troubles. After several hundred years, the earth will be destroyed and the metal will perish. When you return, you should fetch my study room in the northwest corner under the large pillar. There is a jade box, and inside it is a divine white book. Take it and follow the formula to take it. In one year, you may be able to ride on clouds. Once the way is accomplished, come to me here. Do not become a Han official, nor speak to the emperor. Du Shi then bowed and bid farewell, and left the mountain to meet Liang Bo without explaining the reason. Liang Bo thought Du Shi must have gained something and knelt before Du Shi to beg for the Dao art. Previously, Du Shi traveled with him and saw that Liang Bo was kind and sincere, so he told him. Liang Bo only saw the divine formula under the pillar, and later dug up the jade box, sealed with flying immortal incense, and took it as a pill. It was five-colored cloud mica, and he prepared and took the medicine. Both became immortals and left, leaving the formula for his son. Many people in the world obtained it." [56]

    张楷
    　　张楷字公超有道术居华山谷中能为五里雾有玉诀金匮之学坐在立亡之道人学其术者填门如市故云雾市金华山有张超谷焉【出仙传拾遗】
    　　阳翁伯
    　　阳翁伯者卢龙人也事亲以孝葬父母于无终山山髙八十里其上无水翁伯庐于墓侧昼夜号恸神明感之出泉于其墓侧因引水就官道以济行人尝有饮马者以白玉一升与之令翁伯种之当生美玉果生白璧长二尺者数防一日忽有青童乗虗而至引翁伯至海上仙山谒羣仙曰此种玉阳翁伯也一仙人曰汝以孝于亲神真所感昔以玉种与
    之汝果能种之汝当夫妇俱仙今此宫即汝他日所居也天帝将廵省于此开礼玉十珏汝可致之言讫使仙童与俱还翁伯以礼玉十珏以授仙童北平徐氏有女翁伯欲求婚徐氏谓媒者曰得白璧一防可矣翁伯以白璧五防遂壻徐氏数年云龙下迎夫妇俱升天今谓其所居为玉田坊翁伯仙去后子孙立大石柱于田中以纪其事【出仙传拾遗】 [57]

    Zhang Kai
    Zhang Kai, also known as Gongchao, possessed magical arts and resided in the valleys of Huashan. He could create a fog that extended for five miles and had knowledge of jade secrets and gold vaults, as well as the technique of disappearing at will. People who came to learn his arts filled his door like a market, hence the name "Cloudy Mist Market" for Jinheshan, which also has a valley named after Zhang Kai.

    Yang Wengbo
    Yang Wengbo was a man from Lulong who was filial to his parents. He buried them on Wuzhong Mountain, which was eighty li high and had no water at its peak. Yang Wengbo built a hut beside the graves and wept day and night. His filial piety moved the gods, who caused a spring to appear beside his parents' graves. He then led the water to the official road to help travelers. Once, a man who was drinking water from a horse offered him a catty of white jade, telling him to plant it, which would produce beautiful jade. Indeed, several pieces of white jade, each two feet long, grew. One day, a child riding on the wind appeared and led Yang Wengbo to an island in the sea where the immortals lived. They paid homage to the immortals, and one said, "This jade is from Yang Wengbo." Another immortal said, "Your filial piety moved the gods, and they gave you the jade seeds. You have successfully planted them, so you and your wife will both become immortals. This palace is where you will reside in the future." The Heavenly Emperor was about to inspect there and ordered ten pieces of jade to be presented. Yang Wengbo presented ten pieces of jade to the immortal child, who returned with him. The Xu family of Beiping had a daughter, and Yang Wengbo wanted to marry her. The Xu family told the matchmaker, "If he can give us a piece of white jade, we will agree." Yang Wengbo gave them five pieces of white jade and thus married the Xu family's daughter. After several years, a cloud dragon descended to welcome them, and they both ascended to heaven. Their place of residence is now called Yutianfang. After Yang Wengbo became an immortal, his descendants erected a large stone pillar in the field to commemorate the event. [57]

    太平广记卷四
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　太平广记卷五
    　　神仙五
    　　王次仲　　　墨子　　　　刘政
    　　孙博　　　　天门子　　　玉子
    　　茅蒙　　　　沈羲　　　　陈安世
    　　王次仲
    　　王次仲者古之神仙也当周末战国之时合纵连横之际居大夏小夏山以为世之篆文功多而用寡难以速就四海多事笔札所先乃变篆籕之体为书始皇既定天下以其功利于人征之入秦不至复命使召之勑使者曰吾削平六合一统天下孰敢不賔者次仲一书生而逆天子之命若不起当杀之持其首来以正风俗无肆其悍慢也诏使至山致命次仲化为大鸟振翼而飞使者惊拜曰无以复命亦恐见杀惟神人悯之鸟徘徊空中故堕三翮使者得之以进始皇素好神仙之道闻其变化颇有悔恨今谓之落翮山在幽州界乡里祠之不絶【出仙传拾遗】 [58]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [58]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 太平广记 - A vast collection of Chinese legends, stories, and anecdotes compiled during the Song Dynasty.
    • 钦定四库全书 - Refers to the official compilation of the Siku Quanshu, one of the largest encyclopedias in Chinese history.
    • 神仙 - Literally 'immortals' or 'deities,' often referring to beings who have achieved a state of spiritual transcendence.
    • 王次仲 - A legendary figure known for his contributions to the development of Chinese calligraphy.
    • 合纵连横 - Refers to the diplomatic strategies used by states during the Warring States period.
    • 大夏小夏山 - The name of a mountain where Wang Cizhong is said to have lived.
    • 篆文 - Refers to the ancient Chinese script known as seal script.
    • 四海多事 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'many troubles and upheavals in the world.'
    • 笔札 - Refers to writing materials, such as brushes and paper.
    • 诏使 - An official messenger sent by the emperor.
    • 落翮山 - The name of a mountain where Wang Cizhong is said to have transformed into a bird.
    • 乡里祠之不绝 - Refers to the ongoing worship of Wang Cizhong in the local community.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • Refers to the Warring States period, a time of great turmoil and conflict in Chinese history.
    • Refers to Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China, known for his unification of China.
    • Refers to Qin Shi Huang's conquest and unification of the six warring states.
    • A collection of legends and stories about immortals and their deeds.
    • Refers to a region in northern China, historically significant for its strategic location.
    • A historical administrative region in northern China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王次仲 - A legendary figure known for his contributions to the development of Chinese calligraphy.
    • 墨子 - A philosopher and founder of the Mohist school of thought.
    • 刘政 - A historical figure or literary character.
    • 孙博 - A historical figure or literary character.
    • 天门子 - A legendary figure or deity.
    • 玉子 - A legendary figure or deity.
    • 茅蒙 - A legendary figure or deity.
    • 沈羲 - A legendary figure or deity.
    • 陈安世 - A legendary figure or deity.
    • 始皇帝 - Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China.
    • 幽州界 - A historical administrative region in northern China.
    • 落翮山 - The name of a mountain where Wang Cizhong is said to have transformed into a bird.
    • 仙传拾遗 - A collection of legends and stories about immortals and their deeds.

    Volume 4 of the Taiping Guangji
    　　The Qinding Siku Quanshu
    　　Volume 5 of the Taiping Guangji
    　　The Five Immortals
    　　Wang Cizhong　　　Mencius　　　Liu Zheng
    　　Sun Bo　　　　Tianmenzi　　　Yuzi
    　　Mao Meng　　　Shen Xi　　　　Chen Anshi
    　　Wang Cizhong
    　　Wang Cizhong was an ancient immortal who lived during the Warring States period when the states were engaged in the strategies of vertical and horizontal alliances. He resided in the Great and Small Xia Mountains, believing that the ancient seal script, though effective, was cumbersome and difficult to use quickly. Given the many troubles and upheavals in the world, he prioritized writing materials and thus transformed the seal script into a more efficient form of writing. After Emperor Qin Shi Huang unified the six states, he recognized the practical benefits of Wang Cizhong's script and ordered him to come to the capital. Wang Cizhong refused to comply, and the emperor sent messengers to summon him again. The emperor instructed the messengers to say, "I have unified the world; who dares not to come and pay homage?" Wang Cizhong, a mere scholar, defied the emperor's command. If he did not come, he was to be killed, and his head brought back to correct the customs and prevent arrogance and disrespect. When the messengers reached the mountain and delivered the message, Wang Cizhong transformed into a large bird and flew away. The messengers were shocked and bowed, saying, "We have nothing to report, and we fear for our lives. We beg the immortal to have mercy." The bird circled in the air and dropped three feathers, which the messengers collected and presented to the emperor. Emperor Qin Shi Huang, who had always been fond of the ways of immortals, heard of the transformation and felt a great deal of regret. This mountain is now called the Feathered Wing Mountain, located in the borders of Youzhou, where the locals continue to worship him without interruption. [From Xianchuan Shiyi] [58]

    墨子
    　　墨子者名翟宋人也仕宋为大夫外治经典内修道术著书十篇号为墨子世多学者与儒家分途务尚俭约颇毁孔子有公输般者为楚造云梯之械以攻宋墨子闻之往诣楚脚壊裂裳裹足七日七夜到见公输般而説之曰子为云梯以攻宋宋何罪之有余于地而不足于民杀所不足而争所有余不可谓智宋无罪而攻之不可谓仁知而不争不可谓忠争而不得不可谓强公输般曰吾不可以巳言于王矣墨子见王曰于今有人舍其文轩隣有一弊舆而欲窃之舍其锦绣隣有短褐而欲窃之舍其粱肉隣有糟糠而欲窃之此为何若人也王曰若然者必有狂疾翟曰楚有云梦之麋鹿江汉 [59]

    Mencius
    Mencius, whose given name was Zilu, was from the state of Song and served as a high-ranking official there. Externally, he managed classical texts; internally, he cultivated methods and wrote ten books, and was known as Mencius. Many scholars followed Mencius, diverging from the path of Confucianism and emphasizing frugality and simplicity, and quite criticizing Confucius. There was a craftsman named Gongshu Ban who, for the state of Chu, constructed a cloud ladder to attack the state of Song. Mencius heard of this and went to Chu, where he tore his clothes to wrap his feet, traveling seven days and seven nights to meet Gongshu Ban and persuade him. He said, "You are making the cloud ladder to attack Song. What crime does Song have? Chu has more than enough land but lacks people. Killing those who lack and fighting for what is already abundant cannot be called wise. Attacking an innocent state cannot be called benevolent. Knowing something is wrong but not speaking out cannot be called loyal. Speaking out but failing to achieve the desired result cannot be called strong." Gongshu Ban replied, "I cannot stop speaking to the king." Mencius then met the king and said, "Imagine if there were a person who gave up a fine carriage but wanted to steal a broken cart, gave up fine brocades but wanted to steal a coarse robe, gave up fine food but wanted to steal coarse food. What kind of person would do such things?" The king said, "If so, such a person must be mad." Mencius replied, "Chu has the abundant deer of Yunmeng and the rivers and lakes of Jianghan." [59]

    之鱼为天下富宋无雉鲋鱼犹梁肉与糟糠也楚有杞梓豫章宋无数丈之木此犹锦绣之与短褐也臣闻大王更议攻宋有与此同王曰善哉然公输般已为云梯谓必取宋于是见公输般墨子解带为城以幞为械公输般乃设攻城之机九变而墨子九拒之公输之攻城械尽而墨子之守有余也公输般曰吾知所以攻子矣吾不言墨子曰吾知子所以攻我我亦不言王问其故墨子曰公输之意不过杀臣谓宋莫能守耳然臣之弟子禽滑厘等三百人早已操臣守御之器在宋城上而待楚防矣虽杀臣不能絶也楚乃止不复攻宋墨子年八十有二乃叹曰世事已可知荣位非常保将委流俗 [60]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [60]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 之魚為天下富宋無雉鲋鱼 - Ambiguous phrase comparing the wealth of Cui to that of the State of Song; possible interpretation: 'Cui's fish are as wealthy as the State of Song, without wild birds and small fish.'
    • 猶梁肉與糟糠也 - Simile comparing richness to simplicity; possible interpretation: 'like fine food and coarse grain.'
    • 此猶锦绣之与短褐也 - Simile comparing luxury to simplicity; possible interpretation: 'this is like silk brocade compared to coarse clothing.'
    • 公输之攻城械尽而墨子之守有余也 - Describes Gongshu Ban's siege machines being exhausted while Mozi's defenses remain strong.
    • 世事已可知荣位非常保将委流俗 - Reflects Confucian and Daoist philosophies; possible interpretation: 'The world's affairs are already known; honor and rank are not always secure; I will entrust myself to the common people.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 墨子 - Mozi, a philosopher and strategist from the Warring States period.
    • 公输般 - Gongshu Ban, a master craftsman and engineer from the Warring States period.
    • 宋 - The State of Song, one of the smaller states during the Warring States period.
    • 楚 - The State of Chu, a major power during the Warring States period.
    • 禽滑厘 - Gengshu Li, a disciple of Mozi.

    Proper Nouns

    • 墨子 - Mozi
    • 公输般 - Gongshu Ban
    • 宋 - The State of Song
    • 楚 - The State of Chu
    • 禽滑厘 - Gengshu Li

    Cui's fish are as wealthy as the State of Song, without wild birds and small fish, like fine food and coarse grain. Chu has valuable trees, while Song has no trees as tall as several meters, this is like silk brocade compared to coarse clothing. I have heard that the great king is reconsidering the attack on Song, and it is the same as this. The king said, "Good." However, Gongshu Ban has already built a cloud ladder, saying that they will definitely take Song. They then met Gongshu Ban. Mozi used his belt to represent a city and a scarf to represent weapons. Gongshu Ban set up nine changes of siege machines, but Mozi countered each one. Gongshu Ban's siege machines were exhausted, but Mozi's defenses still had plenty. Gongshu Ban said, "I know how to attack you, but I won't tell you." Mozi said, "I know how you plan to attack me, but I won't tell you either." The king asked why. Mozi said, "Gongshu Ban's intention was only to kill me, thinking that Song could not be defended. However, my disciples, including Gengshu Li and others, more than three hundred of them, have already prepared my defensive tools and are waiting on the walls of Song to defend against Chu. Even if you kill me, you cannot stop them." Chu then stopped and did not attack Song again. Mozi, at the age of eighty-two, sighed and said, "The world's affairs are already known; honor and rank are not always secure; I will entrust myself to the common people." [60]

    以从赤松子游耳乃入周狄山精思道法想像神仙于是数闻左右山间有诵书声者墨子卧后又有人来以衣覆足墨子乃伺之忽见一人乃起问之曰君岂非山岳之气乎将度世之神仙乎愿且少留诲以道要神人曰知子有志好道故来相子欲何求墨子曰愿得长生与天地相毕耳于是神人授以素书朱英九方道教戒五行变化凡二十五篇告墨子曰子有仙骨又聪明得此便成不复须师墨子拜受合作遂得其騐乃撰集其要以为五行记乃得地仙居以避战国至汉武帝时遣使者杨违束帛加璧以聘墨子墨子不出视其顔色常如五十许人周游五岳不止一处【出神仙传】 [61]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [61]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 以从赤松子游耳 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'to go with Cishi (a legendary immortal) to wander,' indicating the pursuit of immortality or joining the ranks of immortals.
    • 精思道法 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'to deeply contemplate the Daoist teachings,' referring to intense meditation and study of Daoist principles.
    • 想像神仙 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'to imagine or visualize immortals,' a practice in Daoist cultivation.
    • 数闻左右山间有诵书声者 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'frequently hearing the sound of reciting books in the mountains around,' indicating the presence of spiritual or scholarly activities.
    • 墨子卧后又有人来以衣覆足 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'after Mozi lay down, someone came to cover his feet with a cloth,' suggesting a supernatural visitation or a sign of divine favor.
    • 神人曰知子有志好道故来相子欲何求 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'the divine being said that he knew you had a strong will and interest in the Dao, so he came to wait and see what you desire.' Note: '相' might be a typo or corruption; it should be '相待' (to wait).
    • 愿且少留诲以道要 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'I wish to stay for a short while and teach you the essentials of the Dao.'
    • 子有仙骨又聪明得此便成不复须师 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'you have the bone structure of a celestial being and are intelligent; if you obtain this, you will become one without needing a teacher.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 赤松子 - Legendary immortal in Chinese mythology, known for his longevity and magical abilities.
    • 墨子 - Historical figure, the founder of Mohism, a philosophical school in ancient China.
    • 五行 - Five Elements (Wuxing), which are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. They are fundamental concepts in Chinese philosophy and Daoism.
    • 五岳 - Five Great Mountains of China, which are Mount Tai, Mount Heng (Shanxi), Mount Heng (Hunan), Mount Hua, and Mount Song. They are considered sacred mountains in Chinese culture.
    • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a significant ruler of the Han Dynasty, known for his military campaigns and patronage of the arts.
    • 使者杨违 - Messenger named Yang Wei, sent by Emperor Wu to invite Mozi to court.
    • 束帛加璧 - Gift consisting of silk and jade, often used in formal invitations or as a sign of respect.

    Proper Nouns

    • 赤松子 - Legendary immortal.
    • 墨子 - Founder of Mohism.
    • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 使者杨违 - Messenger named Yang Wei.
    • 五行记 - Text on the Five Elements.
    • 五岳 - Five Great Mountains of China.

    Mozi, wishing to join the ranks of immortals, went to Zhou Di Mountain to deeply contemplate Daoist teachings and imagine immortals. There, he frequently heard the sound of reciting books in the mountains around. After laying down, someone came to cover his feet with a cloth. Mozi waited for the person and suddenly saw someone. He stood up and asked, "Are you not the spirit of the mountains and hills? Or are you a celestial being about to transcend the mortal world? I wish you would stay for a short while and teach me the essentials of the Dao." The divine being replied, "I know you have a strong will and interest in the Dao, so I came to wait and see what you desire." Mozi said, "I wish to attain eternal life and live on forever." The divine being then gave Mozi a white book containing 25 chapters on the Dao, the Five Elements, and their transformations. He told Mozi, "You have the bone structure of a celestial being and are intelligent. If you obtain this, you will become one without needing a teacher." Mozi bowed and received the book, and after practicing its teachings, he achieved the desired results. He then compiled the essential parts into a text called the Wuxing Ji and became an earthly immortal, living in seclusion to avoid the Warring States period. When Emperor Wu of Han sent a messenger named Yang Wei with silk and jade to invite Mozi to court, Mozi did not go. Despite this, his appearance remained youthful, and he continued to travel extensively among the Five Great Mountains. [61]

    刘政
    　　刘政者沛人也髙才博物学无不览以为世之荣贵乃须臾耳不如学道可得长生乃絶进趋之路求飬生之术勤寻异闻不逺千里茍有胜已虽奴客必师事之复治墨子五行记兼服朱英丸年百八十余嵗色如童子能变化形以一人分作百人百人作千人千人作万人又能三军之众使成一丛林木亦能使成鸟兽试取他人器物易置其处人不知觉又能种五果立使华实 [62]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [62]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 高才博物 - Describes a person of great talent and extensive knowledge.
    • 学无不览 - Means 'learned in everything,' indicating a broad range of knowledge.
    • 世之荣贵 - Refers to worldly honor and wealth.
    • 须臾耳 - Means 'momentary' or 'transient,' emphasizing the fleeting nature of worldly success.
    • 学道可得长生 - Means 'studying the Dao can lead to immortality.'
    • 绝进趋之路 - Means 'cut off the path of advancement,' referring to abandoning worldly pursuits.
    • 求飬生之术 - Means 'seeking the methods of nourishing life,' referring to practices for longevity.
    • 勤寻异闻 - Means 'diligently seeking unusual knowledge.'
    • 不远千里 - Means 'not hesitating to travel thousands of miles,' emphasizing dedication.
    • 苟有胜已 - Means 'if anyone is better than oneself,' indicating humility and openness to learning.
    • 虽奴客必师事之 - Means 'even if they are servants or guests, one must treat them as teachers,' emphasizing respect for knowledge.
    • 复治墨子五行记 - Means 'also studied the *Mozi* and the *Wuxing Ji*,' referring to specific texts.
    • 服朱英丸 - Means 'took the *Zhuying Dan*,' referring to a specific elixir.
    • 年百八十余岁 - Means 'over one hundred and eighty years old,' emphasizing longevity.
    • 色如童子 - Means 'looks like a child,' describing youthful appearance.
    • 能变化形 - Means 'can change and transform forms,' referring to supernatural abilities.
    • 以一人分作百人 - Means 'can divide oneself into a hundred people,' describing the ability to multiply.
    • 百人作千人 - Means 'a hundred people can become a thousand,' continuing the theme of multiplication.
    • 千人作万人 - Means 'a thousand people can become ten thousand,' further emphasizing the ability to multiply.
    • 能使成三军之众 - Means 'can make an army of three thousand,' referring to the ability to create large groups.
    • 使成一丛林木 - Means 'can turn into a grove of trees,' describing the ability to transform into nature.
    • 亦能使成鸟兽 - Means 'can also turn into birds and beasts,' continuing the theme of transformation.
    • 试取他人器物易置其处 - Means 'can take other people's objects and place them elsewhere,' describing the ability to manipulate objects.
    • 人不知觉 - Means 'people do not notice,' emphasizing the subtlety of the ability.
    • 又能种五果立使华实 - Means 'can also plant five fruits and make them bear flowers and fruit immediately,' describing the ability to cultivate plants instantly.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 刘政 - A historical figure whose name is preserved as it is.
    • 沛人 - Refers to a person from Pei (沛), a place in ancient China.
    • 墨子五行记 - Refers to the *Mozi* (墨子) and the *Wuxing Ji* (五行记), specific texts studied by Liu Zheng.
    • 朱英丸 - Refers to a specific elixir known as *Zhuying Dan* (朱英丸), associated with longevity practices.
    • 墨子 - Refers to the *Mozi* (墨子), a classical text attributed to Mozi (墨子), a philosopher from the Warring States period.
    • 五行记 - Refers to the *Wuxing Ji* (五行记), a text related to the Five Elements theory.
    • 长生 - Refers to the concept of immortality, a common theme in Daoist and alchemical traditions.
    • 变化 - Refers to the ability to transform or change, a concept found in Daoist and alchemical practices.
    • 五果 - Refers to five types of fruits, often associated with cultivation and longevity in Chinese culture.
    • 华实 - Refers to flowers and fruit, symbolizing growth and abundance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 刘政 - The name of the historical figure.
    • 沛 - The place of Liu Zheng's origin.
    • 墨子 - The title of the *Mozi* text.
    • 五行记 - The title of the *Wuxing Ji* text.
    • 朱英丸 - The name of the elixir.
    • 五果 - The term for five types of fruits.

    Liu Zheng, a man from Pei, was of great talent and extensive knowledge, learned in everything. He believed that worldly honor and wealth were transient, and that studying the Dao could lead to immortality. Therefore, he cut off the path of advancement and sought the methods of nourishing life. He diligently sought unusual knowledge, not hesitating to travel thousands of miles. If anyone was better than himself, even if they were servants or guests, he would treat them as teachers. He also studied the Mozi and the Wuxing Ji. He took the Zhuying Dan and lived to be over one hundred and eighty years old, looking like a child. He could change and transform forms, dividing himself into a hundred people, a hundred people becoming a thousand, and a thousand people becoming ten thousand. He could also make an army of three thousand, turn into a grove of trees, and turn into birds and beasts. He could take other people's objects and place them elsewhere, without people noticing. He could also plant five fruits and make them bear flowers and fruit immediately. [62]

    可食坐致行厨饭膳供数百人又能吹气为风飞砂扬石以手指屋宇山陵壶器便欲頽壊复指之即还如故又能化生美女之形及作水火又能一日之中行数千里能水兴云奋手起雾聚土成山刺地成渊能忽老忽少乍大乍小入水不沾歩行水上召江海中鱼鼈蛟龙鼋鼍即皆登岸又口吐五色之气方广十里直上连天又能跃上下去地数百丈后去不知所在【出神仙传】
    　　孙博
    　　孙博者河东人也有清才能属文著书百余篇诵经数十万言晚乃好道治墨子之术能令草木金石皆为火光照数里亦能使身成火口中吐火指大树生草则焦枯更指还如故又有人亡奴藏匿军中者捕之不得博语奴主曰吾为卿烧其营舎奴必走出卿但谛伺捉之于是博以一赤丸子掷军门须臾火起烛天奴果走出乃得之博乃复以一青丸子掷之火即灭屋舎百物如 [63]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [63]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 可食坐致行厨饭膳供数百人 - Describes a person's ability to provide food for hundreds of people, emphasizing their extraordinary culinary skills or magical abilities.
    • 又能吹气为风飞砂扬石 - Describes the ability to create wind and make sand and stones fly, suggesting a supernatural or magical power.
    • 以手指屋宇山陵壶器便欲頽壊 - Indicates the ability to cause buildings, mountains, and vessels to collapse with a finger, highlighting a destructive power.
    • 复指之即还如故 - Suggests that the person can reverse the damage by pointing again, indicating control over the effects of their power.
    • 又能化生美女之形 - Describes the ability to transform into the form of a beautiful woman, indicating shapeshifting or transformation.
    • 及作水火 - Refers to the ability to create water and fire, suggesting control over natural elements.
    • 又能一日之中行数千里 - Describes the ability to travel thousands of miles in a single day, indicating superhuman speed or teleportation.
    • 能水兴云奋手起雾 - Describes the ability to summon water, clouds, and fog with a gesture, suggesting control over atmospheric phenomena.
    • 聚土成山刺地成渊 - Indicates the ability to create mountains and deep pits with the movement of the earth, highlighting earth manipulation.
    • 能忽老忽少乍大乍小 - Describes the ability to change age and size at will, indicating shapeshifting or transformation.
    • 入水不沾歩行水上 - Suggests the ability to walk on water without getting wet, indicating waterwalking or hydrokinesis.
    • 召江海中鱼鼈蛟龙鼋鼍即皆登岸 - Describes the ability to call aquatic creatures onto the shore, indicating control over marine life.
    • 口吐五色之气方广十里直上连天 - Describes the ability to exhale a five-colored gas that spreads for ten miles and reaches the sky, suggesting a powerful and visible display of energy.
    • 又能跃上下去地数百丈 - Indicates the ability to leap hundreds of feet into the air and fall back down, highlighting superhuman strength and agility.
    • 后去不知所在 - Suggests that the person disappears and is no longer known to be in a particular place, indicating invisibility or teleportation.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 神仙传 - Refers to the *Xian Sheng Zhuan* (Biographies of Immortals), a collection of stories about immortals and supernatural beings.
    • 墨子之术 - Refers to the teachings of Mozi, a philosopher known for his advocacy of universal love and non-aggression.
    • 河东 - Refers to Hedong, a region in ancient China, known for its fertile land and strategic importance.
    • 草木金石 - Refers to plants, trees, and stones, indicating a broad range of natural elements that the person can manipulate.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The phrase '可食坐致行厨饭膳供数百人' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can provide food for hundreds of people by sitting and summoning a traveling kitchen' or 'can summon a traveling kitchen to provide food for hundreds of people.'
    • The phrase '又能吹气为风飞砂扬石' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can blow air to create wind and make sand and stones fly' or 'can create wind and make sand and stones fly with a breath.'
    • The phrase '以手指屋宇山陵壶器便欲頽壊' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'with a finger, can cause buildings, mountains, and vessels to collapse' or 'can cause buildings, mountains, and vessels to collapse with a finger.'
    • The phrase '复指之即还如故' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'by pointing again, can restore things to their original state' or 'can restore things to their original state by pointing again.'
    • The phrase '又能化生美女之形' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can transform into the form of a beautiful woman' or 'can create a beautiful woman from nothing.'
    • The phrase '及作水火' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can create water and fire' or 'can manipulate water and fire.'
    • The phrase '又能一日之中行数千里' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can travel thousands of miles in a single day' or 'can cover thousands of miles in a single day.'
    • The phrase '能水兴云奋手起雾' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can summon water, clouds, and fog with a gesture' or 'can create water, clouds, and fog with a gesture.'
    • The phrase '聚土成山刺地成渊' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can create mountains and deep pits with the movement of the earth' or 'can create mountains and deep pits by manipulating the earth.'
    • The phrase '能忽老忽少乍大乍小' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can change age and size at will' or 'can change age and size suddenly.'
    • The phrase '入水不沾歩行水上' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can walk on water without getting wet' or 'can walk on water and not get wet.'
    • The phrase '召江海中鱼鼈蛟龙鼋鼍即皆登岸' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can call aquatic creatures onto the shore' or 'can summon aquatic creatures to the shore.'
    • The phrase '口吐五色之气方广十里直上连天' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can exhale a five-colored gas that spreads for ten miles and reaches the sky' or 'can create a five-colored gas that spreads for ten miles and reaches the sky.'
    • The phrase '又能跃上下去地数百丈' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'can leap hundreds of feet into the air and fall back down' or 'can jump hundreds of feet into the air and fall back down.'
    • The phrase '后去不知所在' is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'disappears and is no longer known to be in a particular place' or 'disappears and is no longer seen.'

    Proper Nouns

    • 孙博 - The name of the individual described in the text.
    • 河东 - The place name Hedong.
    • 墨子 - The philosopher Mozi.
    • 神仙传 - The title of the collection of stories about immortals and supernatural beings.

    A person who could summon a traveling kitchen to provide food for hundreds of people, blow air to create wind and make sand and stones fly, and cause buildings, mountains, and vessels to collapse with a finger. By pointing again, they could restore things to their original state. They could also transform into the form of a beautiful woman, create water and fire, travel thousands of miles in a single day, summon water, clouds, and fog with a gesture, create mountains and deep pits with the movement of the earth, change age and size at will, walk on water without getting wet, call aquatic creatures onto the shore, exhale a five-colored gas that spreads for ten miles and reaches the sky, leap hundreds of feet into the air and fall back down, and disappear without a trace. [From the Xian Sheng Zhuan (Biographies of Immortals)]

    Sun Bo, a man from Hedong, had clear talent, writing skills, and had authored over a hundred articles or books. He had memorized tens of thousands of words of scriptures and later became interested in Daoism and studied the teachings of Mozi. He could make plants, trees, and stones emit light like fire, illuminating several miles, and transform his body into fire and exhale fire from his mouth. By pointing at a large tree, he could cause grass to wither and then restore it to its original state. There was a runaway slave hiding in the army who could not be captured. Sun Bo told the slave owner that he would burn the camp to force the slave to flee, allowing the owner to catch the slave. He threw a red ball at the camp gate, causing a fire to start and the slave to flee, which allowed the owner to catch the slave. He then threw a blue ball to extinguish the fire and restore the camp to its original state. [63]

    故不损博每作火有所烧他人以水灌之终不可灭须臾自止之方止行水火中不沾灼亦能使千百人从已蹈之俱不沾灼又与人往水上布席而坐饮食作乐使众人舞于水上又山间石壁地上盘石博入其中渐见背及两耳良乆都没又能吞刀剑数十枚及壁中出入如有孔穴也能引镜为刀屈刀为镜可积时不改须博指之乃复如故后入林滤山服神丹而仙去【出神仙传】
    　　天门子
    　　天门子者姓王名刚尤明补飬之要故其经曰阳生立于寅纯木之精隂生立于申纯金之精夫以木投金无往不伤故隂能疲阳也隂人所以着脂粉者法金之白也是以真人道士莫不留心注意精其微妙审其盛衰我行青龙彼行白虎取彼朱雀前我武不死之道也又隂人之情也毎急于求阳然而外自收抑不肯请阳者明金不为木屈也阳性气刚燥志节踈畧至于游宴言和气柔辞语卑下明木之畏于金也天门子既行此道年二百八十嵗犹有童子之色乃服珠醴得仙入洲山去也【出神仙传】 [64]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [64]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 故不损博每作火有所烧他人以水灌之终不可灭须臾自止之方止行水火中不沾灼亦能使千百人从已蹈之俱不沾灼 - Describes a magical ability to control fire and water, where the fire cannot be extinguished by water and the person can walk through water and fire without being harmed.
    • 又与人往水上布席而坐饮食作乐使众人舞于水上 - Describes a magical ability to create a floating platform on water and entertain people with music and dance.
    • 又山间石壁地上盘石博入其中渐见背及两耳良久都没 - Describes a magical ability to enter a stone wall and disappear, leaving only the back and ears visible for a while.
    • 又能吞刀剑数十枚及壁中出入如有孔穴 - Describes a magical ability to swallow multiple swords and knives and move through walls as if they were holes.
    • 也能引镜为刀屈刀为镜可积时不改须博指之乃复如故 - Describes a magical ability to transform a mirror into a knife and vice versa, and then return them to their original form.
    • 后入林虑山服神丹而仙去 - Describes the final act of taking a divine pill and ascending to immortality.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 神仙传 - A collection of stories about immortals and their magical abilities.
    • 林虑山 - A mountain in Henan province, China, known for its historical and cultural significance.
    • 玄洲山 - A mythical mountain in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortals.
    • 神丹 - A divine pill or elixir of immortality.

    Proper Nouns

    • 天门子 - The title or name of the legendary figure.
    • 王刚 - The name of the legendary figure.
    • 林虑山 - The name of a mountain in Henan province.
    • 玄洲山 - The name of a mythical mountain in Chinese mythology.
    • 神丹 - The name of a divine pill or elixir of immortality.

    Translation

    Hence, he did not suffer damage. Whenever he made fire, it would burn something, and others would try to extinguish it with water, but it could not be put out. In a moment, it would stop on its own, and he could walk through water and fire without being harmed. He could also make thousands of people follow him onto the water, and they would not be harmed either. Moreover, he would go to the water with others, lay out a mat, and sit, eat, and enjoy music, making people dance on the water. In the mountains, he could enter a stone wall and disappear, leaving only his back and ears visible for a long time. He could also swallow dozens of swords and knives and move through walls as if they were holes. He could transform a mirror into a knife and a knife into a mirror, and it would remain unchanged for a while until he pointed at it, and it would return to its original form. Later, he entered the mountains of Linlu, took a divine pill, and ascended to immortality, entering the mythical mountain Xuanzhou.

    Tianmenzi

    Tianmenzi, whose name was Wang Gang, was particularly skilled in the art of nourishment and cultivation. Therefore, his teachings said: Yang is born in the hour of Yin, the pure essence of wood; Yin is born in the hour of Shen, the pure essence of metal. When wood is thrown against metal, it cannot prevail, so Yin can weaken Yang. Women often apply makeup to imitate the whiteness of metal, which is why true people and Daoists pay close attention to the subtle and intricate details, examining their strength and decline. I move like the Azure Dragon, and he moves like the White Tiger. I take the Vermilion Bird in front, and I move like the Black Warrior, following the path of immortality. Moreover, the nature of women is often eager for Yang, but they restrain themselves from seeking it, clearly showing that metal (Yin) does not yield to wood (Yang). Men, on the other hand, have a strong and dry temperament, with loose and scattered will and character. They speak softly and humbly during feasts, clearly showing that wood (Yang) is afraid of metal (Yin). Tianmenzi practiced this path and lived for two hundred and eighty years, still looking like a child. He then took a divine pill, attained immortality, and entered the mythical mountain Xuanzhou. [64]

    玉子
    　　玉子者姓章名震南郡人也少好学众经周幽王征之不出乃叹曰人生世间日失一日去生转逺去死转近而但贪富贵不知飬性命命尽气絶则死位为王侯金玉如山何益于灰土乎独有神仙度世可以无穷耳乃师长桑子具受众术乃别造一家之法着道书百余篇其术以务魁为主而精于五行之意演其微妙以飬性治病消灾散祸能起飘风发屋折木作雷雨云雾能以木瓦石为六畜龙虎立成能分形为百千人能渉江海含水喷之
    皆成珠玉亦不变或时闭气不息举之不起推之不动屈之不曲伸之不直或百日数十日乃起每与弟子行各丸泥为马与之皆令闭目须臾成大马乗之日行千里又能吐气五色起数丈见飞鸟过指之即堕临渊投符召鱼鼈之属悉来上岸能令弟子举眼见千里外物亦不能乆也其务魁时以器盛水着两肘之间之水上立有赤光辉辉起一丈以此水治病病在内饮之在外者洗之皆立愈后入崆峒山合丹白日升天而去【出神仙传】 [65]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [65]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 玉子者姓章名震南郡人也 - Classical biographical formula meaning 'Yuzi, surnamed Zhang, named Zhen, from the state of Nanyang.'
    • 少好学 - Means 'young and fond of learning,' indicating early intellectual inclination.
    • 众经周幽王征之不出 - '众经周' likely refers to the Zhou Dynasty, and '幽王' is the posthumous name of King You of Zhou. '征之不出' means 'he was summoned but did not appear.'
    • 人生世间日失一日 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'each day spent in the world is a day lost.'
    • 去生转远去死转近 - Means 'the distance to life grows further, while the distance to death grows closer.'
    • 贪富贵不知飬性命 - '贪富贵' means 'greedily pursuing wealth and status,' and '不知飬性命' means 'unaware of nurturing one's life.'
    • 命尽气绝则死 - Means 'when life ends and breath ceases, death follows.'
    • 位为王侯金玉如山何益于灰土乎 - '位为王侯' means 'holding the position of king or marquis,' and '金玉如山' means 'mountains of gold and jade.' The phrase '何益于灰土乎' is a rhetorical question meaning 'what benefit do they bring to the dust?'
    • 独有神仙度世可以无穷耳 - '独有神仙度世' means 'only immortals can transcend the world,' and '可以无穷耳' means 'and can thus be eternal.'
    • 乃师长桑子具受众术 - '师长桑子' means 'he became a disciple of Changsang Zi,' and '具受众术' means 'and learned all his techniques.'
    • 乃别造一家之法 - Means 'and then created his own unique system.'
    • 着道书百余篇 - Means 'wrote over a hundred books on Daoism.'
    • 其术以务魁为主 - '务魁' is likely a specific technique or practice, possibly related to alchemy or spiritual cultivation.
    • 精于五行之意 - Means 'he was skilled in the principles of the Five Elements.'
    • 演其微妙以飬性治病消灾散祸 - '演其微妙' means 'expounded its subtleties,' and '以飬性治病消灾散祸' means 'to cultivate the self, heal diseases, eliminate disasters, and disperse calamities.'
    • 能起飘风发屋折木作雷雨云雾 - Means 'he could summon fierce winds, lift roofs, break trees, and create thunder, rain, clouds, and mists.'
    • 能以木瓦石为六畜龙虎立成 - Means 'he could transform wood, tiles, and stones into cattle, horses, tigers, and dragons instantly.'
    • 能分形为百千人 - Means 'he could divide his form into hundreds or thousands of people.'
    • 能渉江海含水喷之皆成珠玉 - Means 'he could cross rivers and seas, hold water in his mouth, and spit it out as pearls and jade.'
    • 或时闭气不息举之不起推之不动屈之不曲伸之不直 - Means 'sometimes he could hold his breath, and when lifted, he would not rise; when pushed, he would not move; when bent, he would not curve; when stretched, he would not straighten.'
    • 或百日数十日乃起 - Means 'sometimes he would remain like this for hundreds or tens of days before returning to normal.'
    • 每与弟子行各丸泥为马与之皆令闭目须臾成大马乗之日行千里 - Means 'whenever he traveled with his disciples, he would each roll mud into horses, and when they closed their eyes, the horses would instantly become large ones, allowing them to travel a thousand miles in a day.'
    • 又能吐气五色起数丈见飞鸟过指之即堕 - Means 'he could also exhale five-colored breath that rose several zhang high, and when he saw a flying bird, he could point at it and make it fall.'
    • 临渊投符召鱼鼈之属悉来上岸 - Means 'when he stood by a deep pool and threw down a talisman, all kinds of fish and turtles would come ashore.'
    • 能令弟子举眼见千里外物亦不能乆也 - Means 'he could make his disciples see things a thousand miles away, but they could not do so for long.'
    • 其务魁时以器盛水着两肘之间之水上立有赤光辉辉起一丈以此水治病病在内饮之在外者洗之皆立愈 - '务魁时' refers to a specific time or state of practice. '以器盛水着两肘之间' means 'he would hold water in a vessel between his elbows,' and '之水上立有赤光辉辉起一丈' means 'when he poured it onto the water, a red glow would rise a zhang high.' '以此水治病病在内饮之在外者洗之皆立愈' means 'using this water to treat diseases, those inside would drink it, and those outside would wash with it, and all would be instantly cured.'
    • 后入崆峒山合丹白日升天而去 - '合丹' means 'to concoct elixirs,' and '白日升天而去' means 'ascended to heaven in broad daylight and departed.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 玉子 - The name 'Yuzi' (玉子) is likely a pen name or title, possibly referring to a historical figure or a legendary Daoist.
    • 长桑子 - 'Changsang Zi' is a legendary Daoist figure often associated with alchemy and immortality.
    • 周幽王 - King You of Zhou, the last king of the Western Zhou Dynasty, known for his corruption and the eventual fall of the dynasty.
    • 五行 - The Five Elements (Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, Water) are a fundamental concept in Chinese philosophy and Daoism.
    • 崆峒山 - Mount Kungtung, a mythical mountain in Chinese folklore often associated with Daoist immortals and alchemy.

    Proper Nouns

    • 玉子 - A name or title, possibly fictional or legendary.
    • 长桑子 - A legendary Daoist figure.
    • 周幽王 - King You of Zhou, a historical figure.
    • 五行 - The Five Elements, a philosophical concept.
    • 崆峒山 - Mount Kungtung, a mythical mountain.

    Yuzi, surnamed Zhang, named Zhen, was from the state of Nanyang. As a youth, he was fond of learning. When the king of Zhou, King You, summoned him, he did not appear. He then sighed and said, "Each day spent in the world is a day lost. The distance to life grows further, while the distance to death grows closer. Yet people greedily pursue wealth and status, unaware of nurturing their lives. When life ends and breath ceases, death follows. Even if one holds the position of king or marquis, mountains of gold and jade bring no benefit to the dust. Only immortals can transcend the world and thus be eternal."

    He then became a disciple of Changsang Zi and learned all his techniques. He then created his own unique system and wrote over a hundred books on Daoism. His practices were primarily based on the technique of "wu kui," and he was skilled in the principles of the Five Elements. He expounded its subtleties to cultivate the self, heal diseases, eliminate disasters, and disperse calamities. He could summon fierce winds, lift roofs, break trees, and create thunder, rain, clouds, and mists. He could transform wood, tiles, and stones into cattle, horses, tigers, and dragons instantly. He could divide his form into hundreds or thousands of people. He could cross rivers and seas, hold water in his mouth, and spit it out as pearls and jade. Sometimes he could hold his breath, and when lifted, he would not rise; when pushed, he would not move; when bent, he would not curve; when stretched, he would not straighten. Sometimes he would remain like this for hundreds or tens of days before returning to normal.

    Whenever he traveled with his disciples, he would each roll mud into horses, and when they closed their eyes, the horses would instantly become large ones, allowing them to travel a thousand miles in a day. He could also exhale five-colored breath that rose several zhang high, and when he saw a flying bird, he could point at it and make it fall. When he stood by a deep pool and threw down a talisman, all kinds of fish and turtles would come ashore. He could make his disciples see things a thousand miles away, but they could not do so for long.

    During his practice of "wu kui," he would hold water in a vessel between his elbows, and when he poured it onto the water, a red glow would rise a zhang high. Using this water to treat diseases, those inside would drink it, and those outside would wash with it, and all would be instantly cured. Later, he entered Mount Kungtung to concoct elixirs and ascended to heaven in broad daylight, departing from the mortal world. [From the Biographies of Immortals] [65]

    茅蒙
    　　茅蒙字初成咸阳南闗人也即东卿司命君盈之髙祖也蒙性慈悯好行隂徳防静博学逆覩周室之衰不求进于诸侯常叹人生若电流出处宜及其时于是师北郭谷先生受长生之术神丹之方后入华山静斋絶尘修道合药乗龙驾云白日升天先是其邑歌謡曰神仙得者茅初成驾龙上升入太清时下洲戏赤城继世而往在我盈帝若学之腊嘉平秦始皇闻之因改腊为嘉平【出洞仙传】
    　　沈羲
    　　沈羲者吴郡人学道于蜀中但能消灾治病济救百姓不知服药物功徳感天神识之羲与妻贾氏共载诣子妇卓孔宁家还逢白鹿车一乗青龙车一乗白虎车一乗从者皆数十骑皆朱衣仗矛带剑辉赫满道问羲曰君是沈羲否羲愕然不知何等答曰是也何为问之骑人曰羲有功于民心不忘道自少小以来履行无过夀命不长年夀将尽黄老今遣仙官来下迎之侍郎薄延之乗白鹿车是也度世君司马生青龙车是也迎使者徐福白虎车是也须臾有三仙 [66]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [66]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 茅蒙字初成 - Standard biographical formula meaning 'Mao Meng, courtesy name Chucheng.'
    • 性慈悯好行隂徳 - Means 'of a compassionate nature and fond of practicing hidden virtues.'
    • 防静博学 - Means 'studious and learned,' combining the qualities of being attentive and knowledgeable.
    • 逆覩周室之衰 - Means 'foreseeing the decline of the Zhou dynasty,' indicating a prophetic or insightful nature.
    • 不求进于诸侯 - Means 'not seeking advancement among the feudal lords,' suggesting a disinterest in worldly power.
    • 人生若电流出处宜及其时 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'life is like an electric current, and one should take advantage of the right moment to act.'
    • 师北郭谷先生 - Means 'studying under Master Beigu Gu,' indicating a specific teacher or mentor.
    • 受长生之术神丹之方 - Means 'learning the techniques for longevity and the formula for divine pills.'
    • 入华山静斋绝尘修道合药 - Means 'entering Mount Hua to practice in a quiet retreat, free from worldly distractions, to cultivate the Dao and prepare medicines.'
    • 乘龙驾云白日升天 - Common motif in Daoist and mythological texts, meaning 'riding a dragon and cloud to ascend to heaven during the day.'
    • 其邑歌謡曰 - Means 'a song or rhyme in his hometown says,' introducing a folk tradition or local lore.
    • 神仙得者茅初成驾龙上升入太清 - Means 'the immortal who achieved enlightenment, Mao Chucheng, rode a dragon to ascend to the Great Purity,' referring to a Daoist ideal.
    • 时下洲戏赤城 - Means 'sometimes descending to Xuanzhou to play in Chicheng,' referring to mythical places in Daoist cosmology.
    • 继世而往在我盈帝若学之腊嘉平 - Means 'those who follow should learn from me, Emperor Ying, if they wish to study in the month of JiaPing,' referring to a specific historical or mythological figure.
    • 秦始皇闻之因改腊为嘉平 - Means 'Emperor Qin Shi Huang heard this and thus changed the month of La to JiaPing,' indicating a historical event or legend.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 茅蒙 - A historical or mythological figure, possibly associated with Daoist practices.
    • 初成 - A courtesy name, indicating a formal or scholarly identity.
    • 咸阳南闗 - Refers to the southern gate of Xianyang, an ancient city in China.
    • 东卿司命君盈 - A title or name, possibly referring to a high-ranking official or deity in Daoist or historical contexts.
    • 周室之衰 - Refers to the decline of the Zhou dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 诸侯 - Refers to feudal lords, indicating a hierarchical political structure.
    • 华山 - A famous mountain in China, often associated with Daoist practices and immortality.
    • 龙 - A mythical creature in Chinese culture, often associated with power and transformation.
    • 云 - Clouds are often associated with spiritual ascension in Chinese mythology.
    • 太清 - Refers to the highest level of heaven in Daoist cosmology.
    • 洲 - A mythical place in Daoist cosmology, often associated with immortality.
    • 赤城 - Another mythical place in Daoist cosmology.
    • 秦始皇 - Emperor Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of a unified China.
    • 腊嘉平 - A specific month or period, possibly a historical or mythological reference.

    Proper Nouns

    • 茅蒙 - A historical or mythological figure.
    • 初成 - A courtesy name.
    • 咸阳南闗 - The southern gate of Xianyang.
    • 东卿司命君盈 - A title or name.
    • 周室 - The Zhou dynasty.
    • 诸侯 - Feudal lords.
    • 华山 - A famous mountain.
    • 龙 - A mythical creature.
    • 云 - Clouds.
    • 太清 - The highest level of heaven.
    • 洲 - A mythical place.
    • 赤城 - Another mythical place.
    • 秦始皇 - Emperor Qin Shi Huang.
    • 腊嘉平 - A specific month or period.

    Mao Meng
    Mao Meng, courtesy name Chucheng, was from the southern gate of Xianyang. He was the great-grandfather of East Minister of Fate, Jun Ying. Mao Meng was of a compassionate nature and fond of practicing hidden virtues. He was studious and learned, foreseeing the decline of the Zhou dynasty, and did not seek advancement among the feudal lords. He often lamented that life is like an electric current, and one should take advantage of the right moment to act. Therefore, he studied under Master Beigu Gu, learning the techniques for longevity and the formula for divine pills. Later, he entered Mount Hua to practice in a quiet retreat, free from worldly distractions, to cultivate the Dao and prepare medicines. He rode a dragon and cloud to ascend to heaven during the day. Prior to this, a song or rhyme in his hometown said, "The immortal who achieved enlightenment, Mao Chucheng, rode a dragon to ascend to the Great Purity, sometimes descending to Xuanzhou to play in Chicheng. Those who follow should learn from me, Emperor Ying, if they wish to study in the month of JiaPing." Emperor Qin Shi Huang heard this and thus changed the month of La to JiaPing. [From Dong Xian Zhuan]

    Shen Xi
    Shen Xi was from Wu County and studied Daoism in Shu. He could only eliminate disasters, treat illnesses, and save the people, but did not know the merits of taking medicine. Heaven and spirits recognized his deeds. Shen Xi and his wife, Jia Shi, were traveling to visit his daughter-in-law, Zhuo Kongning, when they encountered a white deer carriage, an azure dragon carriage, and a white tiger carriage. The attendants were all dozens of riders in red clothes, holding spears and swords, shining brightly along the road. They asked Shen Xi, "Are you Shen Xi?" Shen Xi was startled and did not know who they were. He replied, "Yes, why do you ask?" The riders said, "Shen Xi has done good for the people and has not forgotten the Dao. Since his youth, he has lived without fault. His lifespan is not long, and his years will soon come to an end. Huanglao has sent divine officials to come down and welcome him. The Attendant, Bo Yan, is on the white deer carriage, the Lord of the World, Sima Sheng, is on the azure dragon carriage, and the welcoming envoy, Xu Fu, is on the white tiger carriage." Soon, three immortals appeared. [66]

    人羽衣持节以白玉简青玉界丹玉字授羲羲不能识遂载羲升天升天之时道间鉏耘人皆共见不知何等斯须大雾雾觧失其所在但见羲所乗车牛在田食苗或有识是羲车牛以语羲家子弟恐是邪魅将羲藏山谷间乃分布于百里之内求之不得四百余年忽还鄊里推求得数十世孙名懐喜懐喜告曰闻先人説家有先人仙去久不归也留数十日説初上天时云不得见帝但见老君东向而坐左右勑羲不得谢但黙坐而已宫殿郁郁如云气五色黄不可
    名状侍者数百人多女少男庭中有珠玉之树众芝丛生龙虎成羣游戏其间闻琅琅如铜铁之声不知何等四壁熠熠有符书着之老君身形畧长一丈被发文衣身体有光辉须臾数玉女持金案玉杯来赐羲曰此是神丹饮者不死夫妻各一杯夀万歳乃吿言饮服毕拜而勿谢服药后赐二枚大如鸡子脯五寸遗羲曰暂还人间治百姓疾病如欲上来书此符悬之竿杪吾当迎汝乃以一符及仙方一首赐羲羲奄忽如寐已在地上多得其符验也【出神仙传】 [67]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [67]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 人羽衣持节以白玉简青玉界丹玉字授羲 - Describes a celestial being presenting a white jade tablet, green jade boundary, and red jade inscription to Xu.
    • 遂载羲升天 - Means 'then he carried Xu up to heaven.'
    • 道间鉏耘人皆共见不知何等 - Describes people working in the fields who all saw something but did not know what it was.
    • 斯须大雾雾觧失其所在 - Means 'soon after, a dense fog appeared and then cleared, making it impossible to see where they had gone.'
    • 或有识是羲车牛以语羲家子弟 - Some recognized the oxen as Xu's and told his family members.
    • 恐是邪魅将羲藏山谷间 - Fearing it might be an evil spirit taking Xu away, they hid him in a mountain valley.
    • 乃分布于百里之内求之不得 - They searched for him within a hundred miles but could not find him.
    • 四百余年忽还鄊里推求得数十世孙名懐喜 - After over four hundred years, he suddenly returned to his hometown, and they found his descendant named Huai Xi.
    • 闻先人説家有先人仙去久不归也 - Huai Xi heard his ancestors say that a relative had become a celestial being and had not returned for a long time.
    • 留数十日説初上天时云不得见帝但见老君东向而坐左右勑羲不得谢但黙坐而已 - He stayed for several days and described his initial ascent to heaven, saying he did not see the Emperor but only Laozi sitting facing east, with attendants instructing him not to bow but to sit silently.
    • 宫殿郁郁如云气五色黄不可名状 - The palace was grand, like clouds with five colors of purple and yellow, indescribable in appearance.
    • 侍者数百人多女少男庭中有珠玉之树众芝丛生龙虎成群游戏其间 - There were hundreds of attendants, mostly women, and in the courtyard, there were trees of jade and precious stones, clusters of various types of mushrooms, and dragons and tigers playing together.
    • 闻琅琅如铜铁之声不知何等 - He heard a sound like metal ringing, but did not know what it was.
    • 四壁熠熠有符书着之 - The walls were brightly lit, with magical inscriptions written on them.
    • 老君身形略长一丈被发文衣身体有光辉 - Laozi's body was about ten feet tall, with a beard and wearing a robe, radiating light.
    • 须臾数玉女持金案玉杯来赐羲曰此是神丹饮者不死夫妻各一杯夀万歳 - Soon, several jade maidens brought golden trays and jade cups, offering him a potion called Shen Dan, which grants immortality. They gave him and his wife a cup each, promising them a lifespan of ten thousand years.
    • 乃吿言饮服毕拜而勿谢服药后赐二枚大如鸡子脯五寸遗羲曰暂还人间治百姓疾病如欲上来书此符悬之竿杪吾当迎汝 - They instructed him to drink the potion and then bow without thanking them. After taking the medicine, they gave him two pills as large as eggs and five inches thick, telling him to return to the human world temporarily to treat the common people's illnesses. If he wanted to ascend again, he should write this talisman and hang it at the top of a pole, and they would come to meet him.
    • 羲奄忽如寐已在地上多得其符验也 - Xu suddenly fell asleep and woke up on the ground, having received many of these talismans, which proved to be effective.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 老君 - Refers to Laozi, a central figure in Daoism, often depicted as a wise and powerful sage.
    • 神丹 - A term for an elixir of immortality in Daoist tradition.
    • 符书 - Magical inscriptions or talismans used in Daoist rituals.
    • 仙去 - Refers to becoming a celestial being or achieving immortality.
    • 百里之内 - A traditional unit of distance, often used to indicate a large area.
    • 数十世孙 - Refers to a descendant many generations removed.
    • 仙方一首 - A formula or method for achieving immortality.

    Proper Nouns

    • 老君 - Laozi, a central figure in Daoism.
    • 神丹 - An elixir of immortality.
    • 符书 - Magical inscriptions or talismans.
    • 仙去 - Becoming a celestial being or achieving immortality.
    • 百里之内 - A traditional unit of distance.
    • 数十世孙 - A descendant many generations removed.
    • 仙方一首 - A formula or method for achieving immortality.

    A Celestial Being in Feathers and Holding a Jade Tablet

    A celestial being in feathers and holding a jade tablet presented a white jade tablet, a green jade boundary, and a red jade inscription to Xu. Xu could not recognize them, so the being carried Xu up to heaven. When they ascended to heaven, people working in the fields all saw something but did not know what it was. Soon after, a dense fog appeared and then cleared, making it impossible to see where they had gone. All that could be seen was Xu's carriage and oxen grazing in the fields. Some recognized the oxen as Xu's and told his family members. Fearing it might be an evil spirit taking Xu away, they hid him in a mountain valley and searched for him within a hundred miles but could not find him. After over four hundred years, he suddenly returned to his hometown, and they found his descendant named Huai Xi. Huai Xi heard his ancestors say that a relative had become a celestial being and had not returned for a long time. Xu stayed for several days and described his initial ascent to heaven, saying he did not see the Emperor but only Laozi sitting facing east, with attendants instructing him not to bow but to sit silently. The palace was grand, like clouds with five colors of purple and yellow, indescribable in appearance. There were hundreds of attendants, mostly women, and in the courtyard, there were trees of jade and precious stones, clusters of various types of mushrooms, and dragons and tigers playing together. He heard a sound like metal ringing, but did not know what it was. The walls were brightly lit, with magical inscriptions written on them. Laozi's body was about ten feet tall, with a beard and wearing a robe, radiating light. Soon, several jade maidens brought golden trays and jade cups, offering him a potion called Shen Dan, which grants immortality. They gave him and his wife a cup each, promising them a lifespan of ten thousand years. They instructed him to drink the potion and then bow without thanking them. After taking the medicine, they gave him two pills as large as eggs and five inches thick, telling him to return to the human world temporarily to treat the common people's illnesses. If he wanted to ascend again, he should write this talisman and hang it at the top of a pole, and they would come to meet him. Xu suddenly fell asleep and woke up on the ground, having received many of these talismans, which proved to be effective. [From the Book of Immortals] [67]

    陈安世
    　　陈安世京兆人也为权叔本家佣赁禀性慈仁行见禽兽常下道避之不欲惊之不践生虫未尝杀物年十三四叔本好道思神有二仙人托为书生从叔本游以观试之而叔本不其仙人也乆而意转怠叔本在内方作美食而二仙人复来诣门问安世曰叔本在否答曰在耳入白叔本叔本即欲出其妇引还而止曰饿书生辈复欲求饱腹耳于是叔本使安世出答言不在二人曰前者云在旋言不在何也答曰大家君教我云耳二人善其诚实乃谓叔本勤苦有年今适值我二人而乃懈怠是其不遇防成而败乃问安世曰汝好游戏答曰不好也又曰汝好道乎答曰好而无由知之二人曰汝审好道明日早防道北大下安世 [68]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [68]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 禀性慈仁 - Means 'possesses a kind and compassionate nature.'
    • 行见禽兽常下道避之 - Means 'when walking, he often stepped off the path to avoid disturbing animals.'
    • 不欲惊之 - Means 'did not wish to frighten them.'
    • 不践生虫 - Means 'did not step on living insects.'
    • 未尝杀物 - Means 'never killed any creatures.'
    • 年十三四 - Means 'around thirteen or fourteen years old.'
    • 叔本好道思神 - Means 'Shu Ben loved the Dao and sought spiritual enlightenment.'
    • 托为书生从叔本游 - Means 'posing as scholars, they accompanied Shu Ben on his travels.'
    • 意转怠 - Means 'became increasingly lax.'
    • 饿书生辈复欲求饱腹耳 - Means 'the hungry scholars just want to satisfy their hunger.'
    • 汝好游戏乎 - Means 'Do you like to play?'
    • 好道乎 - Means 'Do you like the Dao?'
    • 无由知之 - Means 'no way to know about it.'
    • 汝审好道 - Means 'If you truly like the Dao.'
    • 明日早防道北大下 - Means 'Tomorrow, early in the morning, go to the north of the Great Marsh on the road.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 陈安世 - The name of the person being described.
    • 京兆 - Refers to the region around modern-day Xi'an, Shaanxi Province.
    • 权叔本 - The name of Tien's master or employer.
    • 道 - Refers to the Dao, a central concept in Daoism, emphasizing naturalness and simplicity.
    • 仙人 - Refers to immortals or divine beings in Chinese mythology.
    • 书生 - Refers to scholars or students.
    • 大家君 - Refers to a respectful term for a master or teacher.
    • 先达 - Refers to respected predecessors or mentors.
    • 勤苦有年 - Means 'diligent and hardworking for many years.'
    • 防成而败 - Means 'to succeed and then fail.'

    Proper Nouns

    • 陈安世 - The name of the person being described.
    • 京兆 - The region around modern-day Xi'an, Shaanxi Province.
    • 权叔本 - The name of Tien's master or employer.
    • 道 - The Dao, a central concept in Daoism.
    • 仙人 - Immortals or divine beings in Chinese mythology.
    • 书生 - Scholars or students.
    • 大家君 - A respectful term for a master or teacher.
    • 先达 - Respected predecessors or mentors.
    • 勤苦有年 - Diligent and hardworking for many years.
    • 防成而败 - To succeed and then fail.

    Tien Anshi
    Tien Anshi, a native of Jingzhao, worked as a hired hand for his master, Quan Shuben. He possessed a kind and compassionate nature. Whenever walking, he often stepped off the path to avoid disturbing animals, not wishing to frighten them. He did not step on living insects and never killed any creatures.
