Around thirteen or fourteen years old, Shuben loved the Dao and sought spiritual enlightenment. Two immortals posed as scholars and accompanied Shuben on his travels to test and observe him. However, Shuben did not recognize them as immortals. Over time, Shuben became increasingly lax. One day, Shuben was inside preparing a delicious meal, and the two immortals returned and knocked on the door. Tien Anshi asked, "Is Master Shuben inside?" They replied, "Yes, he is." Tien went inside to inform Shuben. Shuben immediately wanted to send out his wife to return home, but he stopped himself, saying, "The hungry scholars just want to satisfy their hunger." Shuben then instructed Tien to tell them, "Master is not here." The two immortals asked, "You said he was here, and now you say he is not. Why is that?" Tien replied, "Master told me to say so." The immortals admired his honesty and said, "Shuben has been diligent and hardworking for many years, but now that we have come, he has become lax. This is to succeed and then fail." They then asked Tien, "Do you like to play?" Tien replied, "No, I don't." They asked again, "Do you like the Dao?" Tien replied, "Yes, but I have no way to know about it." The immortals said, "If you truly like the Dao, tomorrow, early in the morning, go to the north of the Great Marsh on the road." [68]

    承言早往期处到日西不见一人乃起欲去曰书生定欺我耳二人已在其侧呼曰安世汝来何晚也答曰早来但不见君耳二人曰吾端坐在汝邉耳频三期之而安世辄早至知可教乃以药二丸与安世诫之曰汝归勿复饮食别止于一处安世承诫二人常来往其处叔本怪之安世处空室何得有人语往辄不见叔本曰向闻多人语声今不见一人何也答曰我独语耳叔本见安世不复食但饮水止息别位疑非常人自知失贤乃叹曰夫道尊徳贵不在年齿父母生我然非师则莫能使我长生先闻道者即为师矣乃执弟子之礼朝夕拜事之为之洒扫安世道成白日升天临去遂以要道术授叔本叔本后亦仙去矣【出神仙传】 [69]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [69]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 承言早往期处到日西不见一人乃起欲去曰书生定欺我耳 - Narrative sequence: 'In response to the words, went early, expecting to arrive at sunset, didn't see anyone, then got up to leave, saying, 'The scholar must have deceived me.'
    • 二人已在其侧呼曰安世汝来何晚也 - Dialogue: 'Two people were already beside him, called out saying, 'An Shi, why did you come so late?'
    • 答曰早来但不见君耳 - Response: 'Replied saying, 'Came early, but didn't see you.'
    • 二人曰吾端坐在汝邉耳频三期之而安世辄早至知可教乃以药二丸与安世诫之曰汝归勿复饮食别止于一处 - Dialogue and instruction: 'The two said, 'We were sitting right beside you, repeatedly waited for three days, but An Shi always came early, knew he could be taught, then gave him two pills, warned him saying, 'You should not eat or drink again, stay in one place separately.'
    • 叔本怪之安世处空室何得有人语往辄不见叔本曰向闻多人语声今不见一人何也答曰我独语耳 - Dialogue and explanation: 'Shu Ben was puzzled, 'How could there be someone speaking in An Shi's empty room, he went and didn't see anyone, Shu Ben said, 'I heard the sound of many people speaking earlier, but now I don't see anyone, why is that? I replied, 'I was just talking to myself.'
    • 叔本见安世不复食但饮水止息别位疑非常人自知失贤乃叹曰夫道尊徳贵不在年齿父母生我然非师则莫能使我长生先闻道者即为师矣乃执弟子之礼朝夕拜事之为之洒扫安世道成白日升天临去遂以要道术授叔本叔本后亦仙去矣 - Narrative sequence: 'Shu Ben saw that An Shi no longer ate, only drank water, and rested in a separate place, suspected he was not an ordinary person, realized he had lost a virtuous person, then sighed and said, 'The way is respected and virtue is precious, not in age, parents give birth to us, but without a teacher, no one can make us live forever, those who first hear the way are our teachers, then treated him with the respect of a disciple, bowing and serving him morning and evening, swept and cleaned for him, An Shi achieved the way and ascended to heaven at noon, before leaving, he taught Shu Ben the essential methods, Shu Ben later also ascended to heaven.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 神仙传 - Refers to the *Shenxian Zhuan* (Records of Immortals), a collection of stories about immortals and their practices.
    • 道尊徳贵 - Refers to the Confucian and Taoist values of respect for the way and virtue.
    • 先闻道者即为师矣 - A philosophical statement emphasizing the importance of those who have first learned the way as teachers.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This sequence must be read as a continuous narrative of the protagonist's actions and thoughts.
    • This dialogue must be read as a direct address from the two people to the protagonist.
    • This response must be read as the protagonist's reply to the two people.
    • This dialogue and instruction must be read as the two people explaining their repeated waits and the protagonist's consistent early arrival, followed by the instruction to take the pills.
    • This dialogue and explanation must be read as Shu Ben's puzzlement and the protagonist's self-explanation.
    • This narrative sequence must be read as Shu Ben's realization and the final events, including the protagonist's ascension and the transmission of the essential methods.

    Proper Nouns

    • 安世 - The name of the protagonist.
    • 叔本 - The name of the person who becomes a disciple.
    • 神仙传 - The title of the text from which this passage is taken.

    In response to the words, he went early, expecting to arrive at sunset, but didn't see anyone. He then got up to leave, saying, "The scholar must have deceived me." Two people were already beside him, calling out, "An Shi, why did you come so late?" He replied, "I came early, but didn't see you." The two said, "We were sitting right beside you. We repeatedly waited for three days, but An Shi always came early. Knowing he could be taught, they gave him two pills and warned him, 'You should not eat or drink again. Stay in one place separately.' An Shi followed the warning, and the two people often came to his place. Shu Ben was puzzled, wondering how there could be someone speaking in An Shi's empty room. He went and didn't see anyone. Shu Ben said, "I heard the sound of many people speaking earlier, but now I don't see anyone, why is that?" An Shi replied, "I was just talking to myself." Shu Ben saw that An Shi no longer ate, only drank water, and rested in a separate place. Suspecting he was not an ordinary person, Shu Ben realized he had lost a virtuous person and sighed, "The way is respected and virtue is precious, not in age. Parents give birth to us, but without a teacher, no one can make us live forever. Those who first hear the way are our teachers." Then, he treated An Shi with the respect of a disciple, bowing and serving him morning and evening, sweeping and cleaning for him. An Shi achieved the way and ascended to heaven at noon. Before leaving, he taught Shu Ben the essential methods. Shu Ben later also ascended to heaven. [From Shenxian Zhuan] [69]

    太平广记卷五
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　太平广记卷六
    　　神仙六
    　　张子房　　　东方朔　　　王乔
    　　周遥　　　刘商
    　　张子房
    　　张子房名良韩国人也避地于南阳徙居于沛后为沛国人焉童幼时过下邳圯桥风雪方甚遇一老叟着乌巾黄单衣坠履于桥下目子房曰孺子为我取之子房无倦色下桥取履以进老叟引足以纳之子房神意愈恭叟笑曰孺子可教也明旦来此当有所教子房昧爽至叟已在矣曰期而后至未可传道如是者三子房先至亦无倦怠老叟喜以书授之曰读此当为帝王师若复求吾乃谷城山下黄石也子房读其书能应机权变佐汉祖定天下后人谓其书为黄石公书修之于身能炼气絶力轻身羽化与绮里季东园公甪里先生夏黄公为云霞之交汉初遇四五小児路上羣戏一 [70]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [70]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 太平广记 - A comprehensive collection of classical Chinese stories, legends, and anecdotes compiled during the Song Dynasty.
    • 钦定四库全书 - A massive collection of Chinese books commissioned by the Qing dynasty, containing a vast array of texts from various genres.
    • 神仙六 - Refers to the sixth section of the 'Sui Shen' (Immortals) category in the *Tai Ping Guang Ji*.
    • 避地于南阳徙居于沛 - Means 'fled to Nanyang and later moved to Pei,' referring to Zhang Liang's relocation.
    • 孺子为我取之 - A polite request meaning 'child, go fetch it for me.'
    • 神意愈恭 - Means 'became even more respectful in spirit.'
    • 明旦来此 - Means 'come here tomorrow morning.'
    • 期而后至 - Means 'arrived late' or 'failed to keep the appointment.'
    • 黄石公书 - The *Huang Shi Gong Shu* (Book of the Yellow Stone Old Man), a legendary text attributed to the Yellow Stone Old Man, who is said to have taught Zhang Liang.
    • 炼气绝力轻身羽化 - Refers to Taoist practices of cultivating qi (vital energy), achieving superhuman strength, lightening the body, and eventually transforming into a spirit (flying off like a feather).
    • 云霞之交 - Means 'companionship with clouds and mist,' symbolizing a life in harmony with nature and immortality.
    • 童幼时过下邳圯桥 - Means 'as a young man, he passed by the Xipu Bridge.'
    • 目子房曰 - Means 'looked at Zhang Liang and said.'
    • 孺子可教也 - Means 'the child can be taught.'
    • 期而后至未可传道 - Means 'since you arrived late, you are not yet ready to receive the teachings.'
    • 明旦来此当有所教 - Means 'come here tomorrow morning, and I will teach you something.'
    • 昧爽至 - Means 'arrived at dawn.'
    • 叟笑曰 - Means 'the old man smiled and said.'
    • 读此当为帝王师 - Means 'by reading this, you will become a teacher to emperors.'
    • 若复求吾乃谷城山下黄石也 - Means 'if you seek me again, I am the Yellow Stone at the foot of Gu Cheng Mountain.'
    • 修之于身能炼气绝力轻身羽化 - Means 'by practicing it, you can cultivate qi, achieve superhuman strength, lighten your body, and transform into a spirit.'
    • 与绮里季东园公甪里先生夏黄公为云霞之交 - Means 'he became companions with the Four Worthies of the Early Han, living in harmony with clouds and mist.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 张子房 - Zhang Liang, a key figure in the establishment of the Han dynasty, known for his strategic brilliance and later for his pursuit of immortality.
    • 东方朔 - Dongfang Shuo, a court jester and scholar during the Han dynasty, known for his wit and wisdom.
    • 王乔 - Wang Qiao, a legendary figure associated with Daoist immortality.
    • 周顥遥 - Zhou Hao Yao, a historical figure whose details are less well-known but likely a scholar or official.
    • 刘商 - Liu Shang, another historical figure, possibly a scholar or official.
    • 下邳圯桥 - The Xipu Bridge, a location in ancient China where Zhang Liang met the Yellow Stone Old Man.
    • 谷城山 - Gu Cheng Mountain, a location in Chinese mythology associated with the Yellow Stone Old Man.
    • 黄石公 - The Yellow Stone Old Man, a legendary figure who is said to have taught Zhang Liang the secrets of immortality.
    • 云霞之交 - A metaphor for a life in harmony with nature and immortality, often used in Daoist literature.
    • 四皓 - The Four Worthies of the Early Han, a group of hermits known for their wisdom and reclusiveness, including Lü Shang, Dongguo Gong, Shangshan Gong, and Yueli Gong.
    • 绮里季、东园公、甪里先生、夏黄公 - The Four Worthies of the Early Han, who are also mentioned in the *Tai Ping Guang Ji*.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张子房 - Zhang Liang
    • 东方朔 - Dongfang Shuo
    • 王乔 - Wang Qiao
    • 周顥遥 - Zhou Hao Yao
    • 刘商 - Liu Shang
    • 下邳圯桥 - Xipu Bridge
    • 谷城山 - Gu Cheng Mountain
    • 黄石公 - The Yellow Stone Old Man
    • 绮里季 - One of the Four Worthies of the Early Han
    • 东园公 - One of the Four Worthies of the Early Han
    • 甪里先生 - One of the Four Worthies of the Early Han
    • 夏黄公 - One of the Four Worthies of the Early Han

    Translation:

    The Tai Ping Guang Ji Volume Five
    The Qin Ding Si Ku Quan Shu
    The Tai Ping Guang Ji Volume Six
    Immortals Six
    Zhang Zi Fang, Dong Fang Shuo, Wang Qiao
    Zhou Hao Yao, Liu Shang

    Zhang Zi Fang
    Zhang Zi Fang, whose name was Liang, was a native of Korea. He fled to Nanyang and later moved to Pei, where he became a resident of Pei. As a young man, he passed by the Xipu Bridge, where the snow was falling heavily. There he met an old man wearing a black cap and a yellow robe. The old man dropped his shoe on the bridge and looked at Zhang Liang, saying, "Child, go fetch it for me." Zhang Liang showed no sign of weariness, went down the bridge to retrieve the shoe, and brought it back to the old man. The old man then extended his foot to receive the shoe, and Zhang Liang's demeanor became even more respectful. The old man smiled and said, "The child can be taught. Come here tomorrow morning, and I will teach you something." The next morning, Zhang Liang arrived at dawn, but the old man was already there. He said, "Since you arrived late, you are not yet ready to receive the teachings." He repeated this three times, and each time Zhang Liang arrived earlier than the old man, showing no sign of weariness. The old man was pleased and gave him a book, saying, "By reading this, you will become a teacher to emperors. If you seek me again, I am the Yellow Stone at the foot of Gu Cheng Mountain." Zhang Liang read the book and was able to apply its teachings with flexibility and strategic insight, assisting Emperor Gaozu of Han in establishing the Han dynasty. Later generations referred to the book as the Huang Shi Gong Shu. By practicing it, he could cultivate qi, achieve superhuman strength, lighten his body, and transform into a spirit, living in harmony with clouds and mist alongside the Four Worthies of the Early Han: Lü Shang, Dongguo Gong, Shangshan Gong, and Yueli Gong. At the beginning of the Han dynasty, he encountered several children playing together on the road. [70]

    児曰着青裙入天门揖金母拜木公时人莫知之子房知之往拜之曰此东王公之玉童也所谓金母者西王母也木公者东王公也此二元尊乃隂阳之父母天地之本源化生万育飬羣品木公为男仙之主金母为女仙之宗长生飞化之士升天之初先觐金母后谒木公然后升三清朝太上矣此歌乃玉童教世人拜王公而揖王母也子房佐汉封留侯为大司徒觧形于世葬于龙首原赤眉之乱人发其墓但见黄石枕化而飞去若流星焉不见其尸形衣冠得素书一篇兵畧数章子房登仙位为太童子常从老君于太清之中其孙道陵得道朝昆仑之夕子房往焉【出仙传拾遗】 [71]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [71]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 児曰 - A phrase indicating a child's statement or question, often used in classical texts to introduce dialogue or commentary.
    • 着青裙入天门 - A ritualistic or symbolic act, where '着青裙' (wearing a green skirt) and '入天门' (entering the heavenly gate) suggest a spiritual or divine journey.
    • 揖金母拜木公 - A formal act of bowing to '金母' (Jinmu, the Queen Mother of the West) and '木公' (Mugong, the East King Father), indicating reverence to deities.
    • 时人莫知之 - A phrase meaning 'no one at the time knew it,' suggesting a hidden or misunderstood event.
    • 子房知之 - 'Zi Fang' (Zhang Liang) is a historical figure known for his wisdom and involvement in the Han dynasty. '知之' means 'knew it,' indicating his awareness of the event.
    • 此东王公之玉童也 - A statement identifying the child as a '玉童' (jade child) of the East King Father, suggesting a divine or supernatural status.
    • 所谓金母者西王母也木公者东王公也 - A clarification that 'Jinmu' is the Queen Mother of the West and 'Mugong' is the East King Father, emphasizing their roles as deities.
    • 此二元尊乃阴阳之父母天地之本源 - A statement that these two deities are the 'parents of yin and yang' and the 'origin of heaven and earth,' highlighting their primordial importance.
    • 化生万物育养群品 - A phrase meaning 'transforming and giving birth to all things, nurturing all beings,' emphasizing their creative and nurturing roles.
    • 木公为男仙之主金母为女仙之宗 - A statement that Mugong is the master of male immortals and Jinmu is the ancestor of female immortals, indicating their roles in the hierarchy of immortals.
    • 长生飞化之士升天之初先觐金母后谒木公 - A ritualistic sequence where immortals first pay homage to Jinmu and then to Mugong before ascending to heaven, emphasizing the order of reverence.
    • 此歌乃玉童教世人拜王公而揖王母也 - A statement that the song is taught by the jade child to teach people to worship the King Father and bow to the Queen Mother, indicating the song's purpose.
    • 子房佐汉封留侯为大司徒 - A statement that Zhang Liang assisted the Han dynasty and was enfeoffed as the Marquis of Liu, holding the position of Grand Tutor, indicating his historical role.
    • 解形于世葬于龙首原 - A phrase meaning 'his physical form was dissolved from the world and he was buried in Longshou Plain,' suggesting his ascension to immortality.
    • 赤眉之乱人发其墓 - A statement that during the Red Eyebrow Rebellion, someone disturbed his tomb, indicating a historical event.
    • 但见黄石枕化而飞去若流星焉不见其尸形衣冠 - A phrase meaning 'only a yellow stone pillow was seen, which transformed and flew away like a shooting star; his body, clothes, and hat were not seen,' suggesting a miraculous transformation.
    • 不得素书一篇兵略数章 - A phrase meaning 'he did not obtain a single volume of white scriptures and several chapters of military strategies,' indicating the loss of important texts.
    • 子房登仙位为太玄童子常从老君于太清之中 - A statement that Zhang Liang ascended to the position of a divine being, becoming a Great Mystery Child and always following Laojun in the Great Purity, indicating his divine status.
    • 其孙道陵得道朝昆仑之夕子房往焉 - A statement that his grandson Tao Ling attained the Dao and, on the evening he visited Mount Kunlun, Zhang Liang went with him, indicating a continuation of his spiritual journey.
    • 出仙传拾遗 - A phrase indicating the source of the text, '拾遗' (Shiyi) meaning 'Miscellaneous Collection,' suggesting it is a compilation of supernatural stories.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 张良 - A historical figure known for his wisdom and involvement in the Han dynasty, often associated with immortality and divine status in later texts.
    • 东王公 - A deity associated with the East and often paired with the Queen Mother of the West in Chinese mythology.
    • 西王母 - A deity associated with the West and often paired with the East King Father in Chinese mythology.
    • 玉童 - A term often used to describe a divine or immortal child, suggesting a high spiritual status.
    • 红眉之乱 - A historical rebellion during the Han dynasty, indicating a specific historical context.
    • 龙首原 - A place name, likely referring to a specific burial site or location.
    • 昆仑山 - A mythical mountain in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortals and the divine.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张良 - A historical figure known for his wisdom and involvement in the Han dynasty.
    • 东王公 - A deity associated with the East and often paired with the Queen Mother of the West in Chinese mythology.
    • 西王母 - A deity associated with the West and often paired with the East King Father in Chinese mythology.
    • 玉童 - A term often used to describe a divine or immortal child.
    • 红眉之乱 - A historical rebellion during the Han dynasty.
    • 龙首原 - A place name, likely referring to a specific burial site or location.
    • 昆仑山 - A mythical mountain in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortals and the divine.

    A Child said, "Wearing a green skirt and entering the heavenly gate, bowing to the Queen Mother of the West and worshipping the East King Father." At the time, no one knew this, but Zhang Liang did. He went to worship them and said, "This is the jade child of the East King Father. The Queen Mother of the West is known as Jinmu, and the East King Father is known as Mugong. These two primordial deities are the parents of yin and yang and the origin of heaven and earth. They transform and give birth to all things, nurturing all beings. Mugong is the master of male immortals, and Jinmu is the ancestor of female immortals. Immortals who have attained eternal life and transformation must first pay homage to Jinmu and then to Mugong before ascending to the Three Clear Heavens and meeting the Supreme One. This song was taught by the jade child to teach people to worship the King Father and bow to the Queen Mother." Zhang Liang assisted the Han dynasty and was enfeoffed as the Marquis of Liu, holding the position of Grand Tutor. His physical form was dissolved from the world and he was buried in Longshou Plain. During the Red Eyebrow Rebellion, someone disturbed his tomb. Only a yellow stone pillow was seen, which transformed and flew away like a shooting star; his body, clothes, and hat were not seen. He did not obtain a single volume of white scriptures and several chapters of military strategies. Zhang Liang ascended to the position of a divine being, becoming a Great Mystery Child and always following Laojun in the Great Purity. His grandson Tao Ling attained the Dao and, on the evening he visited Mount Kunlun, Zhang Liang went with him. [From Miscellaneous Collection of Immortal Transmissions] [71]

    东方朔
    　　东方朔小名曼倩父张氏名夷字少平母田氏夷年二百嵗顔若童子朔生三日而田氏死死时汉景帝三年也邻母拾朔飬之时东方始明因以姓焉年三嵗天下秘识一览暗诵于口恒指挥天上空中独语隣母忽失朔累月暂归母笞之后复去经年乃归母见之大惊曰汝行经年一归何以慰吾朔曰儿暂之紫泥之海有紫水汚衣仍过虞泉湔浣朝发中还何言经年乎母又问曰汝悉经何国朔曰儿湔衣竟暂息都崇台一寤眠王公防儿以丹栗霞浆児食之既多饱闷防死乃饮天黄露半合即醒还遇一苍虎息于路初儿骑虎而还打捶过痛虎啮儿脚伤母便悲嗟乃裂青布裳裹之朔复去家万里见一枯脱布 [72]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [72]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 东方朔小名曼倩 - Direct naming convention for Eastern Shuo's nickname.
    • 父张氏名夷字少平母田氏 - Formal introduction of family members with names and courtesy names.
    • 夷年二百嵗顔若童子 - Hyperbolic expression indicating longevity and youthful appearance.
    • 朔生三日而田氏死 - Chronological event describing Shuo's birth and mother's death.
    • 邻母拾朔飬之时东方始明 - Metaphorical way of saying it was early morning.
    • 因以姓焉 - Explanation of the origin of his surname 'Dong' (east).
    • 年三嵗天下秘识一览暗诵于口 - Exaggerated description of his intelligence at three years old.
    • 恒指挥天上空中独语 - Habit of talking to himself, possibly about what he sees in the sky.
    • 邻母忽失朔累月 - Mysterious disappearance of Shuo for several months.
    • 暂归母笞之 - Brief return and punishment by his mother.
    • 后复去经年乃归 - Another long absence of Shuo.
    • 母见之大惊曰汝行经年一归何以慰吾 - Mother's surprise and concern after Shuo's long absence.
    • 儿暂之紫泥之海有紫水汚衣 - Fantastical description of a journey to the purple mud sea.
    • 仍过虞泉湔浣 - Reference to a mythical place where one can rejuvenate.
    • 朝发中还何言经年乎 - Rhetorical question indicating the time was very short.
    • 汝悉经何国 - Direct question about where Shuo has been.
    • 儿湔衣竟暂息都崇台一寤眠王公防儿以丹栗霞浆 - Fantastical dream sequence involving kings and princes.
    • 儿食之既多饱闷防死乃饮天黄露半合即醒 - Magical experience involving divine or magical water.
    • 还遇一苍虎息于路 - Another fantastical encounter with a black tiger.
    • 初儿骑虎而还打捶过痛虎啮儿脚伤 - Interaction with the tiger, resulting in an injury.
    • 母便悲嗟乃裂青布裳裹之 - Mother's reaction to Shuo's injury.
    • 朔复去家万里见一枯脱布 - Another fantastical journey and encounter.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 东方朔 - Famous figure in Chinese literature, known for his wit and wisdom during the Han Dynasty.
    • 紫泥之海 - Mythical sea, often associated with immortality and magical properties.
    • 虞泉 - Mythical place where one can rejuvenate and gain eternal youth.
    • 都崇台 - Mythical tower, often associated with high places and divine experiences.
    • 丹栗霞浆 - Red chestnuts and rosy juice, often symbolic of divine or magical food.
    • 天黄露 - Yellow dew from the sky, often associated with divine or magical water.
    • 苍虎 - Black tiger, often a symbol of power and danger in Chinese mythology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 东方朔 - Historical figure.
    • 紫泥之海 - Mythical sea.
    • 虞泉 - Mythical place.
    • 都崇台 - Mythical tower.
    • 丹栗霞浆 - Red chestnuts and rosy juice.
    • 天黄露 - Yellow dew from the sky.
    • 苍虎 - Black tiger.

    Translation:

    Dongfang Shuo

    Dongfang Shuo, whose nickname was Manqian, was the son of Zhang, whose name was Yi and courtesy name was Shaoping. His mother was Tian. Yi was two hundred years old, but his appearance was like that of a child. Shuo was born three days after Tian died, which was in the third year of Emperor Jing's reign. When the neighbor's mother picked him up to raise him, it was early morning, and thus he took the surname Dong. At the age of three, Shuo could memorize the secrets of the world and recite them silently. He often pointed at the sky and spoke to himself. One day, the neighbor's mother suddenly lost Shuo for several months. He returned briefly, and his mother scolded him. Later, he left again and did not return for a year. When she saw him, she was greatly surprised and said, "How can you console me after being gone for a year?" Shuo replied, "I went to the purple mud sea, where the purple water dirtied my clothes. I then passed by the Yu Quan to wash. I left in the morning and returned in the middle of the day, how could it be a year?" His mother asked, "Where have you been?" Shuo said, "After washing my clothes, I took a short rest at the lofty tower of Xu Du. I slept and dreamed of being treated by kings and princes with red chestnuts and rosy juice. I ate so much that I felt full and drowsy, almost dying. Then I drank the yellow dew from the sky, half a cup, and woke up. I met a black tiger resting on the road. Initially, I rode the tiger back, hitting it too hard, and the tiger bit my foot, causing injury. My mother then lamented and tore her blue cloth skirt to wrap me. I left again, traveling ten thousand miles and saw a withered tree from which I removed some cloth." [72]

    挂布化为龙因名其地为布龙泽朔以元封中游鸿蒙之泽忽遇母采桑于白海之濵俄而有黄眉翁指母以语朔曰昔为我妻托形为太白之精今汝亦此星之精也吾却食吞气已九千余年目中童子皆有青光能见幽之物三千年一返骨洗髄二千年一剥皮伐毛吾生来已三洗髓五伐毛矣朔既长仕汉武帝为太中大夫武帝暮年好仙术与朔狎昵一日谓朔曰朕欲使爱幸者不老可乎朔曰臣能之帝曰服何药曰东北地有芝草西南有春生之鱼帝曰何知之曰三足乌欲下地食此草羲和以手掩乌目不许下畏其食此草也鸟兽食此即美闷不能动问曰子何知之朔曰小児时掘井防落井下数十年无所托有人 [73]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [73]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 挂布化为龙 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the cloth hung in the wind transformed into a dragon.' Describes a transformation or miracle.
    • 因名其地为布龙泽 - Literally means 'therefore, the place was named 'Bulong Ze'.' The name 'Bulong Ze' is likely a local or regional name with no direct English equivalent.
    • 朔以元封中游鸿蒙之泽 - 'Shuo in the Yuanfeng period traveled through the vast expanse of Hongmeng.' 'Yuanfeng' is a reign title of Emperor Wu of Han, and 'Hongmeng' is a mythical place often associated with the beginning of the universe.
    • 忽遇母采桑于白海之濵 - 'Suddenly encountered his mother picking mulberries by the shore of Baihai.' 'Baihai' is a mythical sea, often associated with the eastern sea in Chinese mythology.
    • 俄而有黄眉翁指母以语朔 - 'Soon, an old man with a yellow beard pointed to his mother and spoke to Shuo.' This is a narrative device to introduce a supernatural or wise figure.
    • 昔为我妻托形为太白之精 - 'Once she was my wife, transformed into the essence of the planet Venus (Tai Bai).' 'Tai Bai' is the Chinese name for Venus, often associated with beauty and divinity.
    • 今汝亦此星之精也 - 'Now you are also the essence of this star.' This suggests a divine or celestial lineage.
    • 吾却食吞气已九千余年 - 'I have abstained from food and inhaled energy for over nine thousand years.' This is a hyperbolic expression of longevity and spiritual practice.
    • 目中童子皆有青光能见幽之物 - 'The pupils in my eyes have a green glow, enabling me to see hidden things.' This describes a supernatural ability.
    • 三千年一返骨洗髓二千年一剥皮伐毛 - 'Every three thousand years, I return to cleanse my bones and marrow; every two thousand years, I shed my skin and feathers.' This describes a cyclical process of renewal and transformation.
    • 吾生来已三洗髓五伐毛矣 - 'Since my birth, I have undergone three bone and marrow cleansings and five skin and feather sheddings.' This emphasizes the speaker's long life and numerous transformations.
    • 朔既长仕汉武帝为太中大夫 - 'Shuo, having grown up, served Emperor Wu of Han as a Taizhong Daifu.' 'Taizhong Daifu' is a high-ranking official position.
    • 武帝暮年好仙术与朔狎昵 - 'In his later years, Emperor Wu was fond of Daoist practices and became close to Shuo.' This indicates a personal relationship between the emperor and Shuo.
    • 一日谓朔曰朕欲使爱幸者不老可乎 - 'One day, he said to Shuo, 'Can I make those I love remain young?'' This is a request for immortality or longevity.
    • 朔曰臣能之 - 'Shuo replied, 'I can do it.' This is a straightforward response.
    • 帝曰服何药 - 'The emperor asked, 'What medicine should be taken?'' This is a practical question.
    • 曰东北地有芝草西南有春生之鱼 - 'Shuo replied, 'In the northeast, there is the grass of immortality; in the southwest, there is the spring-born fish.' These are mythical ingredients for longevity.
    • 帝曰何知之 - 'The emperor asked, 'How do you know this?'' This is a request for the source of the information.
    • 曰三足乌欲下地食此草羲和以手掩乌目不许下畏其食此草也 - 'Shuo replied, 'The three-legged crow wanted to descend to eat this grass, but Xihe covered its eyes and forbade it, fearing it would eat the grass.' This is a mythological explanation.
    • 鸟兽食此即美闷不能动 - 'If birds and beasts eat it, they will become paralyzed and unable to move.' This describes the effects of the grass.
    • 问曰子何知之 - 'He asked, 'How do you know this?'' This is a repetition of the emperor's earlier question.
    • 曰小儿时掘井防落井下数十年无所托有人 - 'Shuo replied, 'When I was a child, I fell into a well and was there for decades, with no one to support me.' This is a personal anecdote to explain his knowledge.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元封 - A reign title of Emperor Wu of Han, indicating the time period of the story.
    • 鸿蒙 - A mythical place often associated with the beginning of the universe, symbolizing vastness and primordial chaos.
    • 白海 - A mythical sea, often associated with the eastern sea in Chinese mythology.
    • 黄眉翁 - An old man with a yellow beard, likely a wise or supernatural figure.
    • 太白之精 - The essence of the planet Venus, often associated with beauty and divinity.
    • 却食吞气 - A Daoist practice of abstaining from food and inhaling energy, symbolizing spiritual cultivation.
    • 三足乌 - A three-legged crow, a mythical bird often associated with the sun.
    • 羲和 - The goddess of the sun, often depicted as the charioteer of the sun.
    • 芝草 - The grass of immortality, a mythical plant believed to grant longevity.
    • 春生之鱼 - A mythical fish that is born in spring, often associated with longevity and rejuvenation.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元封 - A reign title of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 鸿蒙 - A mythical place.
    • 白海 - A mythical sea.
    • 黄眉翁 - An old man with a yellow beard.
    • 太白之精 - The essence of the planet Venus.
    • 三足乌 - A three-legged crow.
    • 羲和 - The goddess of the sun.
    • 芝草 - The grass of immortality.
    • 春生之鱼 - A mythical fish born in spring.
    • 朔 - The name of the character.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 太中大夫 - A high-ranking official position.

    A Cloth Hung in the Wind Transformed into a Dragon, and the Place Was Named Bulong Ze. Shuo, during the Yuanfeng period, traveled through the vast expanse of Hongmeng. Suddenly, he encountered his mother picking mulberries by the shore of Baihai. Soon, an old man with a yellow beard pointed to his mother and spoke to Shuo, saying, "Once she was my wife, transformed into the essence of the planet Venus. Now you are also the essence of this star. I have abstained from food and inhaled energy for over nine thousand years. The pupils in my eyes have a green glow, enabling me to see hidden things. Every three thousand years, I return to cleanse my bones and marrow; every two thousand years, I shed my skin and feathers. Since my birth, I have undergone three bone and marrow cleansings and five skin and feather sheddings." Shuo, having grown up, served Emperor Wu of Han as a Taizhong Daifu. In his later years, Emperor Wu was fond of Daoist practices and became close to Shuo. One day, he said to Shuo, "Can I make those I love remain young?" Shuo replied, "I can do it." The emperor asked, "What medicine should be taken?" Shuo replied, "In the northeast, there is the grass of immortality; in the southwest, there is the spring-born fish." The emperor asked, "How do you know this?" Shuo replied, "The three-legged crow wanted to descend to eat this grass, but Xihe covered its eyes and forbade it, fearing it would eat the grass." If birds and beasts eat it, they will become paralyzed and unable to move. He asked, "How do you know this?" Shuo replied, "When I was a child, I fell into a well and was there for decades, with no one to support me." [73]

    引臣往取此草乃隔红泉不得渡其人与臣一履臣乃乗履泛泉得而食之其国人皆织珠玉为簟要臣入云韨之幕设珉雕枕刻镂为日月云雷之状亦曰镂空枕亦曰雕枕又荐毫之珍褥以百之毫织为褥此毫褥而冷常以夏日舒之因名柔毫水藻之褥臣举手拭之恐水湿席定视乃光也其后武帝寝于光殿召朔于青绮窓绨纨幕下问朔曰汉年运火徳统以何精何瑞为祥朔对曰臣常游昊然之墟在长安之东过扶桑七万里有云山山顶有井云从井中出若土徳则黄云火徳则赤云金徳则白云水徳则黑云帝深信之太初二年朔从西那邪国还得声风木十枝以献帝长九尺大如指此木出因桓之水 [74]

    The emperor invited me to fetch this grass, but it was separated by the Red Spring, which I could not cross. The person gave me a pair of shoes. I then rode on the shoes and crossed the spring, obtaining and eating the grass. The people of that country all wove jade into cushions and invited me into the cloud curtain, setting up black jade pillows carved with images of the sun, moon, clouds, and thunder, also called hollow-carved pillows and black-carved pillows. They also offered a precious cushion made of fine animal hair, woven from a hundred hairs of the finest quality, which was cold to the touch and often used in summer, hence named the soft hair water algae cushion. I raised my hand to wipe it, fearing that the water would wet the mat, but upon closer inspection, it turned out to be luminous.

    Later, Emperor Wu slept in the Bright Light Hall and summoned me under the blue silk windows and fine silk curtains. He asked me, "In the Han dynasty, what kind of spirit and auspicious signs indicate the continuation of the fire element?" I replied, "I often traveled to the mythical place of Hao Ran, east of Chang'an, passing through the mythical land of Fusang, which is seven ten-thousand miles away. There is a mountain with clouds, and at the top of the mountain is a well. Clouds emerge from the well. If the earth element prevails, the clouds are yellow; if the fire element prevails, the clouds are red; if the metal element prevails, the clouds are white; if the water element prevails, the clouds are black." The emperor deeply believed this. In the second year of the Tai-chu era, I returned from the West Na Ya country with ten branches of wind-sounding wood, each nine feet long and as thick as a finger. This wood comes from the River of Huán. [74]

    则禹贡所谓因桓是来即其源也出甜波上有紫燕黄鹄集其间实如细珠风吹珠如玉声因以为名帝以枝遍赐羣臣年百嵗者颁赐此人有疾枝则有汗将死者枝则折昔老在周二千七百年此枝未汗洪崖先生尧时年已三千嵗此枝亦未一折帝乃赐朔朔曰臣见此枝三遍枯死死而复生何翅汗折而已语曰年末年枝忽汗此木五千嵗一湿万嵗一枯也帝以为然又天汉二年帝升苍龙馆思仙术召诸方士言逺国遐乡之事唯朔下席操笔疏曰臣游北极至镜火山日月所不照有龙衔火以照山四极亦有园囿池苑皆植异草木有明茎草如金灯折为烛照见物形仙人寗封尝以此草然为夜朝见腹内外有光亦 [75]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [75]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 則禹貢所謂因桓是來即其源也 - A complex phrase referring to a specific geographical or mythological origin, derived from the *Yugong* (Classic of Great Operations).
    • 出甜波上有紫燕黃鵠集其间 - Describes rare or mythical birds gathering over sweet water.
    • 實如細珠風吹珠如玉聲 - Metaphorically describes the fruit as fine beads that make a sound like jade when blown by the wind.
    • 因以為名 - Means 'thus it is named.'
    • 帝以枝遍賜羣臣年百嵗者 - The emperor gave branches to ministers who are a hundred years old.
    • 有疾枝則有汗 - Conditional relationship: if there is a disease, the branch sweats.
    • 將死者枝則折 - Conditional relationship: if someone is about to die, the branch breaks.
    • 昔老在周二千七百年 - Refers to an ancient figure who lived for 2,700 years.
    • 此枝未汗 - This branch did not sweat.
    • 洪崖先生堯時年已三千嵗 - Refers to a legendary figure who lived for 3,000 years during the time of Emperor Yao.
    • 此枝亦未一折 - This branch also did not break once.
    • 帝乃賜朔朔曰臣見此枝三遍枯死死而復生 - The emperor granted it to Shuo, who had seen the branch wither and die three times but come back to life.
    • 語曰年末年枝忽汗 - Proverbial statement: at the end of the year, the branch suddenly sweats.
    • 此木五千嵗一濕萬嵗一枯也 - This tree wets once every 5,000 years and dries out once every 10,000 years.
    • 帝以為然 - The emperor agreed.
    • 又天漢二年帝升蒼龍館思仙術召諸方士言逺國遐鄉之事 - In the second year of Tianhan, the emperor ascended the Hall of the Azure Dragon, thought about magical techniques, and summoned various sages to speak about matters of distant countries and remote lands.
    • 唯朔下席操筆疏曰臣游北极至鏡火山日月所不照 - Only Shuo stepped down from his seat and wrote, saying he traveled to the North Pole and reached the Mirror Fire Mountain, where the sun and moon do not shine.
    • 有龍衔火以照山四極亦有園囿池苑皆植異草木 - There is a dragon holding fire to illuminate the four corners of the mountain, and there are also gardens and parks planted with exotic plants and trees.
    • 有明茎草如金燈折為燭照見物形 - There is a bright-stemmed grass like a golden lamp, which, when broken, serves as a candle, revealing the shapes of ghosts.
    • 仙人寗封嘗以此草然為夜朝見腹内外有光亦 - The legendary figure Ningfeng once used this grass to light the night court, seeing light inside and outside the body.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 禹貢 - Refers to the *Yugong* (Classic of Great Operations), a chapter in the *Shijing* (Book of Songs).
    • 洪崖先生 - A legendary figure often associated with longevity and immortality.
    • 堯時 - Refers to the time of Emperor Yao, a legendary ruler in Chinese history.
    • 天漢二年 - A specific year during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 蒼龍館 - A mythical or symbolic place, possibly referring to a palace or hall.
    • 鏡火山 - A mythical mountain, possibly a reference to a place where the sun and moon do not shine.
    • 寗封 - A legendary figure associated with immortality and magical practices.

    Proper Nouns

    • 禹貢 - The *Yugong* (Classic of Great Operations).
    • 洪崖先生 - A legendary figure.
    • 堯時 - The time of Emperor Yao.
    • 天漢二年 - The second year of Tianhan.
    • 蒼龍館 - A mythical or symbolic place.
    • 鏡火山 - A mythical mountain.
    • 寗封 - A legendary figure.

    Then, the Yugong (Classic of Great Operations) refers to the origin of the water flowing from the source of the river, emerging from the sweet waters where purple swallows and yellow cranes gather. The fruit is like fine beads, and when the wind blows, the beads make a sound like jade. Thus, it is named. The emperor distributed branches to all the ministers who were a hundred years old. Those who were sick, the branches would sweat; those who were about to die, the branches would break. In the past, the ancient figure Laopi lived for 2,700 years, and this branch did not sweat. The legendary figure Hongya Mr. lived for 3,000 years during the time of Emperor Yao, and this branch also did not break once. The emperor then granted it to Shuo, and Shuo said, "I have seen this branch three times wither and die, but it came back to life." It is said, "At the end of the year, the branch suddenly sweats." This tree wets once every 5,000 years and dries out once every 10,000 years. The emperor agreed. In the second year of Tianhan, the emperor ascended the Hall of the Azure Dragon and thought about magical techniques, summoning various sages to speak about matters of distant countries and remote lands. Only Shuo stepped down from his seat and wrote, saying, "I traveled to the North Pole and reached the Mirror Fire Mountain, where the sun and moon do not shine. There is a dragon holding fire to illuminate the four corners of the mountain, and there are also gardens and parks planted with exotic plants and trees. There is a bright-stemmed grass like a golden lamp, which when broken, serves as a candle, revealing the shapes of ghosts. The legendary figure Ningfeng once used this grass to light the night court, seeing light inside and outside the body." [75]

    名洞腹草帝剉此草为苏以涂明云之观夜坐此观即不加烛亦名照魅草采以借足则入水不沉朔又尝东游吉云之地得神马一匹髙九尺帝问朔何兽曰王母乗云光辇以适东王公之舎税此马于芝田东王公怒弃此马于清津天岸臣至王公坛因骑而反绕日三匝此马入汉闗闗门犹未掩臣于马上睡不还至帝曰其名云何朔曰因事为名名步景驹朔曰自驭之如驽马蹇驴耳朔曰臣有吉云草千顷种于九景山东二千年一花明年应生臣走往刈之以抺马马立不饥朔曰臣至东极过吉云之泽帝曰何为吉云曰其国常以云气占凶吉若有喜庆之事则满室云起五色照人着于草树皆成五色露露味皆甘帝 [76]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [76]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 名洞腹草帝剉此草为苏以涂明云之观 - Complex phrase meaning 'the emperor named the herb 'Dongfucao' and ground it into a paste to coat the Mingyun Pavilion.'
    • 夜坐此观即不加烛亦名照魅草 - Means 'sitting in this pavilion at night, no additional candles are needed, and it is also called the 'Zhaomei Grass.''
    • 采以借足则入水不沉 - Means 'if one picks it and wears it, they will not sink when entering water.'
    • 朔又尝东游吉云之地得神马一匹 - Means 'Shuo once traveled east to the Land of Jiyun and obtained a divine horse.'
    • 王母乘云光辇以适东王公之舍 - Means 'the Queen Mother rode a cloud-lit carriage to the residence of the Eastern Prince Gong.'
    • 税此马于芝田东王公怒弃此马于清津天岸臣至王公坛因骑而反绕日三匝 - Means 'she left the horse at the Mushroom Field, and the Eastern Prince Gong became angry and abandoned the horse at the Heavenly Shore of Qingjin. I, the minister, went to the altar of the Eastern Prince Gong and rode the horse, circling the sun three times.'
    • 此马入汉关关门犹未掩臣于马上睡不觉还至帝曰其名云何 - Means 'the horse entered the Han border gate, and the gate was still not closed. I, the minister, slept on the horse and did not wake up until I returned to the emperor. The emperor asked, 'What is its name?''
    • 朔曰因事为名名步景驹 - Means 'Shuo said, 'Its name is based on its deeds, and it is called Bujingju.''
    • 朔曰自驭之如驽马蹇驴耳 - Means 'Shuo said, 'It is ridden like a slow horse or a lame donkey.''
    • 朔曰臣有吉云草千顷种于九景山东二千年一花明年应生臣走往刈之以抹马马立不饥 - Means 'Shuo said, 'I have a thousand acres of Jiyun Grass planted in the east of Jiuying Mountain. It blooms once every two thousand years. Next year, it should bloom. I will go to cut it and apply it to the horse, and the horse will not feel hungry.''
    • 朔曰臣至东极过吉云之泽帝曰何为吉云曰其国常以云气占凶吉若有喜庆之事则满室云起五色照人着于草树皆成五色露露味皆甘帝 - Means 'Shuo said, 'I reached the easternmost point and passed through the Jiyun Marsh. The emperor asked, 'What is Jiyun?' Shuo replied, 'In that country, they often use cloud vapor to predict good or bad fortune. If there is a joyful event, the room fills with colorful clouds, illuminating people, and the grass and trees all turn five colors. The dew is sweet.''

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 名洞腹草帝 - Refers to the emperor who named the herb 'Dongfucao.'
    • 明云之观 - Refers to the Mingyun Pavilion, a place mentioned in the text.
    • 照魅草 - Refers to the 'Zhaomei Grass,' a type of herb with special properties.
    • 神马 - Refers to a divine horse.
    • 王母 - Refers to the Queen Mother of the West, a figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 东王公 - Refers to the Eastern Prince Gong, another figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 芝田 - Refers to the Mushroom Field, a place mentioned in the text.
    • 清津天岸 - Refers to the Heavenly Shore of Qingjin, a place mentioned in the text.
    • 吉云之泽 - Refers to the Jiyun Marsh, a place mentioned in the text.
    • 五色 - Refers to the five colors, often associated with auspiciousness in Chinese culture.

    Proper Nouns

    • 名洞腹草帝 - The emperor who named the herb 'Dongfucao.'
    • 明云之观 - The Mingyun Pavilion.
    • 照魅草 - The 'Zhaomei Grass.'
    • 神马 - The divine horse.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West.
    • 东王公 - The Eastern Prince Gong.
    • 芝田 - The Mushroom Field.
    • 清津天岸 - The Heavenly Shore of Qingjin.
    • 吉云之泽 - The Jiyun Marsh.
    • 五色 - The five colors.

    The emperor named the herb "Dongfucao" and ground it into a paste to coat the Mingyun Pavilion. Sitting in this pavilion at night, no additional candles are needed, and it is also called the "Zhaomei Grass." If one picks it and wears it, they will not sink when entering water.

    Shuo once traveled east to the Land of Jiyun and obtained a divine horse. The Queen Mother rode a cloud-lit carriage to the residence of the Eastern Prince Gong and left the horse at the Mushroom Field. The Eastern Prince Gong became angry and abandoned the horse at the Heavenly Shore of Qingjin. I, the minister, went to the altar of the Eastern Prince Gong and rode the horse, circling the sun three times. The horse entered the Han border gate, and the gate was still not closed. I, the minister, slept on the horse and did not wake up until I returned to the emperor. The emperor asked, "What is its name?" Shuo said, "Its name is based on its deeds, and it is called Bujingju." Shuo said, "It is ridden like a slow horse or a lame donkey."

    Shuo said, "I have a thousand acres of Jiyun Grass planted in the east of Jiuying Mountain. It blooms once every two thousand years. Next year, it should bloom. I will go to cut it and apply it to the horse, and the horse will not feel hungry." Shuo said, "I reached the easternmost point and passed through the Jiyun Marsh. The emperor asked, "What is Jiyun?" Shuo replied, "In that country, they often use cloud vapor to predict good or bad fortune. If there is a joyful event, the room fills with colorful clouds, illuminating people, and the grass and trees all turn five colors. The dew is sweet." [76]

    曰吉云五露可得否曰臣负吉云草以备马此立可得日可三二往乃东走至夕而还得白青黄露盛以青琉璃各受五合授帝帝徧赐羣臣其得之者老者皆少疾者皆除也又武帝常见彗星朔折指星木以授帝帝指彗星应时星没时人莫之测也朔又善啸每曼声长啸辄尘落漫飞朔未死时谓同舍郎曰天下人无能知朔知朔者太王公耳朔卒后武帝得此语即召太王公问之曰尔知东方朔乎公对曰不知公何所能曰颇善星歴帝问诸星皆具在否曰诸星具独不见嵗星十八年今复见耳帝仰天叹曰东方朔生在朕旁十八年而不知是嵗星哉惨然不乐其余事迹多散在别卷此不备载【出洞防记及朔别传】 [77]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [77]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 曰吉云五露可得否 - A direct question asking if 'the five dews' can be obtained, where '吉云' (Jiyun) is a proper noun, possibly a person or a title.
    • 臣负吉云草以备马 - A statement indicating that the speaker (臣, minister) is carrying 'Jiyun grass' to prepare for a horse, where '负' (fu) means 'to carry' or 'to bear'.
    • 此立可得日可三二往乃东走至夕而还得白青黄露盛以青琉璃各受五合授帝帝徧赐羣臣其得之者老者皆少疾者皆除也 - A complex sentence describing a series of actions and outcomes. '此立可得' means 'this can be obtained immediately', '日可三二往' means 'can go three or four times a day', '乃东走至夕而还得' means 'then go east until evening and return', '白青黄露盛' refers to various colored dews, '以青琉璃各受五合' means 'using green glass containers, each holding five units', '授帝帝徧赐羣臣' means 'presented to the emperor, who then distributed it to all officials', and '其得之者老者皆少疾者皆除也' means 'those who received it, the old became young, and the sick were cured'.
    • 又武帝常见彗星朔折指星木以授帝帝指彗星应时星没时人莫之测也 - Another complex sentence. '又武帝常见彗星' means 'Emperor Wu often saw a comet', '朔折指星木以授帝' means 'Shuo pointed to a star and gave it to the emperor', '帝指彗星应时星没' means 'the emperor pointed to the comet, and it disappeared at the right time', and '时人莫之测也' means 'no one could understand it'.
    • 朔又善啸每曼声长啸辄尘落漫飞朔未死时谓同舍郎曰天下人无能知朔知朔者太王公耳朔卒后武帝得此语即召太王公问之曰尔知东方朔乎公对曰不知公何所能曰颇善星歴帝问诸星皆具在否曰诸星具独不见嵗星十八年今复见耳帝仰天叹曰东方朔生在朕旁十八年而不知是嵗星哉惨然不乐其余事迹多散在别卷此不备载 - A lengthy passage. '朔又善啸' means 'Shuo was also good at whistling', '每曼声长啸辄尘落漫飞' means 'every time he sang a long whistle, dust would fall and fly around', '朔未死时谓同舍郎曰' means 'before Shuo died, he told his fellow chamberlain', '天下人无能知朔知朔者太王公耳' means 'no one in the world knows Shuo except for Taigong', '朔卒后武帝得此语即召太王公问之' means 'after Shuo died, Emperor Wu heard this and summoned Taigong to ask him', '尔知东方朔乎' means 'do you know about Eastern Shuo?', '公对曰不知' means 'Taigong replied, 'I don't know'', '公何所能' means 'what can you do?', '曰颇善星歴' means 'he said, 'I am good at astronomy'', '帝问诸星皆具在否' means 'the emperor asked if all the stars were present', '曰诸星具独不见嵗星十八年今复见耳' means 'he said, 'all the stars are present except for the Star of the Year, which hasn't been seen for 18 years, but it has reappeared now'', '帝仰天叹曰东方朔生在朕旁十八年而不知是嵗星哉' means 'the emperor looked up to heaven and sighed, 'Eastern Shuo lived beside me for 18 years, and I didn't know he was the Star of the Year'', '惨然不乐' means 'he was sad and unhappy', and '其余事迹多散在别卷此不备载' means 'other stories are scattered in other volumes and are not fully recorded here'.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吉云 - Possibly a person or a title, but the exact identity is unclear.
    • 武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Han, a significant figure in Chinese history known for his military campaigns and cultural patronage.
    • 彗星 - Refers to a comet, which was often seen as an omen in ancient Chinese culture.
    • 星木 - Refers to a star, possibly a specific celestial body.
    • 太王公 - Refers to Taigong, a historical figure known for his wisdom and expertise in astronomy.
    • 东方朔 - Refers to Dongfang Shuo, a famous scholar and entertainer in the court of Emperor Wu of Han, known for his wit and knowledge.

    Jiyun asked, "Can the five dews be obtained?" The minister replied, "I carry Jiyun grass to prepare for a horse. This can be obtained immediately; it can be done three or four times a day. Then go east until evening and return. The dews of black, white, blue, and yellow are abundant, and they are stored in green glass containers, each holding five units. They are presented to the emperor, who then distributes them to all officials. Those who receive them, the old become young, and the sick are cured."

    Emperor Wu often saw a comet. Shuo pointed to a star and gave it to the emperor. The emperor pointed to the comet, and it disappeared at the right time. No one could understand it.

    Shuo was also good at whistling. Every time he sang a long whistle, dust would fall and fly around. Before Shuo died, he told his fellow chamberlain, "No one in the world knows Shuo except for Taigong." After Shuo died, Emperor Wu heard this and summoned Taigong to ask him, "Do you know about Eastern Shuo?" Taigong replied, "I don't know." The emperor asked, "What can you do?" Taigong said, "I am good at astronomy." The emperor asked if all the stars were present. Taigong said, "All the stars are present except for the Star of the Year, which hasn't been seen for 18 years, but it has reappeared now." The emperor looked up to heaven and sighed, "Eastern Shuo lived beside me for 18 years, and I didn't know he was the Star of the Year." He was sad and unhappy. Other stories are scattered in other volumes and are not fully recorded here. [Source: Dongfang Ji and Shuo's Separate Biography] [77]

    王乔
    　　王乔河东人也汉显宗时为叶令有神术每月朔望常诣京朝帝怪其来数而不见车骑密令太史伺望之言临至必有防鳬从东南飞来于是鳬至举罗张之但得一舄焉乃四年时所赐尚书官属履也每当朝时叶县门下鼓不击自鸣闻于京师后天忽下玉棺于庭前吏人推排终不揺动乔曰天帝欲召我乃沐浴服饵卧棺中盖便立覆宿昔乃城东土自成坟其夕县中牛羊皆流汗喘乏人莫知之百姓为立庙号叶君祠祷无不应逺近尊崇帝诏迎取其鼓置都亭下畧无复声或云即古仙人王乔也示变化之迹于世耳【出仙传拾遗】
    　　周遥
    　　周遥洞庭山道士自云甪里先生之孙山上有其祖甪里庙甪里邨言其数世得道尝居焦山中学太隂链形之道死于崖窟中嘱其弟子曰检视我尸勿令他物所犯六年后若再生当以衣裳衣我弟子视之初则臭虫壊唯五脏不变依言闭防之至六年往看乃身全却生弟子备汤沐以新衣衣之髪鬒而黑髭麤而直若兽鬛焉十六年又死如 [78]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [78]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 爰及 - Classical connective meaning 'as for' or 'extending to', used to introduce the list that follows.
    • 先達 - Refers to earlier accomplished figures or predecessors who can be learned from.
    • 可為師表 - Means 'can serve as a teacher and model', stressing exemplary status.
    • 博學求之 - Means to seek knowledge broadly from many sources.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 農商工賈 - A classical grouping of farmers, merchants, artisans, and traders.
    • 廝役奴隸 - Refers to servants, laborers, and slaves, indicating the lower end of the social order.
    • 釣魚屠肉 - Names manual occupations such as fishing and butchering.
    • 飯牛牧羊 - Refers to feeding cattle and herding sheep, reinforcing the focus on ordinary labor.
    • 無不利於事也 - A general statement that nothing is unhelpful in matters, suggesting that all these roles or pursuits are valuable in their own right.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王乔 - A proper name, likely referring to a historical or legendary figure.
    • 汉显宗 - The reign title of Emperor Ming of Han.
    • 叶令 - The position of magistrate of Ye.
    • 太史 - The officials responsible for astronomy and astrology.
    • 尚书 - The Ministry of State Affairs.
    • 玉棺 - A jade coffin.
    • 叶君祠 - The Ye Jun Shrine.
    • 都亭 - The market gate in the capital.

    Wang Qiao
    Wang Qiao was from Hedong during the reign of Emperor Ming of Han and served as the magistrate of Ye. He possessed supernatural abilities and regularly traveled to the capital to pay homage to the emperor on the first and fifteenth days of each month. The emperor was puzzled by his frequent visits but saw no signs of his entourage. He secretly ordered the astrologers to observe Wang Qiao, and they reported that whenever he was about to arrive, a large white bird would fly from the southeast. When the bird appeared, they set up a net and caught only one shoe, which was the one given to the officials of the Shangshu four years earlier. Whenever Wang Qiao was about to arrive, the drum at the gate of Ye County would sound without being struck, and this was heard in the capital. Later, a jade coffin suddenly descended in front of the courtyard. The officials tried to move it, but it would not budge. Wang Qiao said, "The Heavenly Emperor wishes to summon me." He then bathed, ate, and lay down in the coffin. The coffin was covered and buried in the eastern part of the city, where a mound formed naturally. On that night, the cattle and sheep in the county sweated and panted, but no one knew why. The people built a temple and called it the Ye Jun Shrine, and all prayers offered there were answered. The shrine was respected and revered far and wide. The emperor ordered the drum to be brought to the capital and placed at the market gate, but it no longer sounded. Some people said that this was the ancient immortal Wang Qiao, demonstrating his magical abilities to the world.

    Zhou Yingyao
    Zhou Yingyao was a Taoist priest from Dongting Mountain who claimed to be the grandson of Lüli Mr. There was a temple dedicated to his ancestor Lüli on the mountain, known as Lüli Village, indicating that his family had been practicing Daoism for generations. He once lived on Jiuyuan Mountain and studied the method of refining the body through the practice of the Great Yin. He died in a cliff cave and instructed his disciples to examine his body and prevent it from being damaged by other creatures. He said, "If I am reborn after six years, dress me in new clothes." His disciples checked his body, which initially smelled and was infested with insects, but only his internal organs remained unchanged. They followed his instructions and sealed the body. Six years later, they opened the seal and found that his body was intact and he had been reborn. The disciples prepared him a bath and dressed him in new clothes. His hair was black and thick, and his beard was coarse and straight, like that of a beast. Sixteen years later, he died again. [78]

    前更七年复生如此三度已四十余年近八十嵗状貎如三十许人隋炀帝闻之征至东都颁赐丰厚恩礼隆异而恳乞归山寻还本郡贞观中召至长安于内殿安置问修习之道对曰臣所修者匹夫之志功不及物利唯一身帝王修道一言之利万国防福得道之效速于人臣区区所学非九重万乗之所修也恳求归山寻亦遂其所适【出仙传拾遗】
    　　刘商
    　　刘商者中山靖王之后举孝亷歴官合淝令而笃好无为清简之道方术服链之门五金八石所难致者必力而求之人有方术未合链施效者必资其药石给其鑪鼎助使成之未尝有所觊觎也因泛舟苕霅间遂卜居武康上强山下有樵童药叟虽常草木之药诣门而售者亦答以善价一旦樵夫鬻樵有术一把商亦厚价致之其庭庑之下篱落之间草木诸药已堆积矣忽闲歩杖防逍遥田畆蹊隧之傍聊自怡适闻藂林间有人相与言曰中山刘商今日已赐真术矣盖隂功笃好之所感乎窥林中杳无人迹奔归 [79]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [79]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 前更七年复生如此三度已四十余年近八十嵗状貎如三十许人 - Describes repeated rebirths and youthful appearance; '复生如此三度' means 'repeatedly returning to life in this manner three times,' and '状貎如三十许人' means 'appearance like someone in their thirties.'
    • 隋炀帝闻之征至东都颁赐丰厚恩礼隆异 - '闻之' means 'upon hearing of it,' and '恩礼隆异' means 'extraordinary favors and honors.'
    • 恳乞归山寻还本郡 - '恳乞' means 'earnestly requested,' and '归山' means 'return to the mountains,' referring to the desire to return to a monastic or secluded life.
    • 贞观中召至长安于内殿安置 - '贞观中' refers to the reign of Emperor Taizong of Tang during the Zhen Guan era, and '内殿安置' means 'lodged in the inner palace.'
    • 问修习之道对曰臣所修者匹夫之志功不及物利唯一身 - '修习之道' means 'the way of cultivation,' and '匹夫之志' means 'the aspiration of an ordinary person.'
    • 帝王修道一言之利万国防福 - '一言之利' means 'the benefit of a single word,' and '万国防福' means 'the protection and blessing of the entire empire.'
    • 得道之效速于人臣区区所学非九重万乗之所修也 - '得道之效' means 'the effect of attaining the Dao,' and '九重万乗' refers to the emperor and his court.
    • 刘商者中山靖王之后举孝亜历官合淝令而笃好无为清简之道 - '孝亜' likely refers to a high-ranking official, and '笃好无为清简之道' means 'firmly adhered to the way of non-action and simplicity.'
    • 方术服链之门五金八石所难致者必力而求之 - '方术' refers to esoteric practices, and '五金八石' refers to rare minerals and stones.
    • 人有方术未合链施效者必资其药石给其鑪鼎助使成之 - '链施效' means 'to achieve success through alchemy,' and '给其鑪鼎' means 'provide furnaces and tools.'
    • 因泛舟苕霅间遂卜居武康上强山下 - '苕霅间' refers to the area between the Tiao and Zhe rivers, and '卜居' means 'choose a place to settle.'
    • 有樵童药叟虽常草木之药诣门而售者亦答以善价 - '樵童药叟' refers to a woodcutter and a herbalist, and '诣门而售' means 'come to sell at the door.'
    • 忽闲歩杖防逍遥田畆蹊隧之傍聊自怡适 - '闲歩杖防' means 'walking leisurely with a cane,' and '聊自怡适' means 'to enjoy oneself casually.'
    • 闻藂林间有人相与言曰中山刘商今日已赐真术矣 - '藂林间' means 'in the dense forest,' and '赐真术' means 'bestowed true elixirs.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 隋炀帝 - Emperor Yang of Sui, a ruler known for his ambitious and often controversial policies.
    • 贞观中 - The Zhen Guan era of Emperor Taizong of Tang, a period of prosperity and cultural flourishing.
    • 长安 - The capital city of the Tang dynasty, known for its grandeur and cultural significance.
    • 内殿 - The inner palace, a place of high honor and proximity to the emperor.
    • 中山靖王之后 - Descendant of Jing Wang of Zhongshan, a noble title indicating aristocratic lineage.
    • 孝亜 - Likely a high-ranking official, possibly a reference to a specific title or position.
    • 方术 - Esoteric practices, including alchemy and divination, common in ancient Chinese culture.
    • 五金八石 - Rare minerals and stones used in alchemical practices.
    • 苕霅间 - The area between the Tiao and Zhe rivers, a scenic and culturally significant region.
    • 武康上强山 - A specific location where the subject settled, likely a mountain retreat.

    Proper Nouns

    • 隋炀帝 - Emperor Yang of Sui.
    • 贞观中 - The Zhen Guan era of Emperor Taizong of Tang.
    • 长安 - The capital city of the Tang dynasty.
    • 内殿 - The inner palace.
    • 中山靖王之后 - Descendant of Jing Wang of Zhongshan.
    • 孝亜 - Likely a high-ranking official.
    • 苕霅间 - The area between the Tiao and Zhe rivers.
    • 武康上强山 - A specific location where the subject settled.

    Here is the final translation of the source text:

    Translation:

    He had been reborn in this manner three times over the course of seven years, and by now had lived for more than forty years, approaching eighty years of age, yet his appearance remained that of a man in his thirties. Upon hearing of this, Emperor Yang of Sui summoned him to the Eastern Capital, where he was bestowed generous gifts and honors. However, he earnestly requested to return to the mountains, and soon thereafter, he returned to his hometown. During the Zhen Guan era, he was summoned to Chang'an and lodged in the inner palace. When asked about his practices of cultivation, he replied, "What I cultivate is the aspiration of an ordinary person; my efforts do not benefit others, but only myself. The emperor’s cultivation, through the power of his words, brings protection and blessing to the entire empire. The effect of attaining the Dao is quicker than what I, a humble subject, can achieve. My simple practices are not something the imperial court would undertake." He then earnestly requested to return to the mountains, and soon his wish was granted. [From Xian Chuan Shi Yi]

    Liu Shang

    Liu Shang was a descendant of Jing Wang of Zhongshan. He was selected for high office through the examination system and served as the magistrate of Hefei. He was deeply devoted to the way of non-action and simplicity. For esoteric practices and alchemy, he sought out rare minerals and stones, no matter how difficult to obtain. If someone had an esoteric practice but had not yet achieved success through alchemy, he would provide them with herbs, minerals, furnaces, and tools to help them succeed. He never sought personal gain. One day, while traveling by boat between the Tiao and Zhe rivers, he chose to settle in the mountains above Wukang. There, he encountered a woodcutter and a herbalist who regularly came to sell their herbs at his door, and he paid them a fair price. One day, a woodcutter brought him a bundle of herbs, and Liu Shang paid a generous price for it. Soon, his courtyard and garden were filled with herbs and medicines. One day, while walking leisurely with a cane along the fields and paths, he heard someone in the dense forest saying, "Liu Shang of Zhongshan has been bestowed true elixirs today." This must have been the result of his hidden virtues and dedication. He looked into the forest but saw no one, and then he rushed back home.

    [79]

    取术修而服之月余齿髪益盛貌如婴童举歩轻速可及驰马登渉云岩无复困惫又月余坐知四方之事騐若符契乃入上彊洞中咸通初有酒家以樵叟稍异尽礼接之累月复一至因谓酒家曰我中山刘商也夙攻水墨愿留一图以酬见待之厚使备绘素而约以再来一日果至酒家援毫运思顷刻而千山万水非世工之所及将去谓酒家曰我祖淮南王今为九海总司居列真之任授我以南溟都水之秩旬日逺别不复来矣如是十许日天色晴霁香风瑞云弥布山谷樵者见空中骑乗飞举南去【出仙传拾遗】 [80]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [80]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 取術修而服之月余 - Practicing and adhering to a certain method for more than a month.
    • 齒髪益盛 - Teeth and hair becoming more abundant, suggesting rejuvenation.
    • 貌如嬰童 - Appearance resembling that of a child, indicating youthful vitality.
    • 舉步輕速可及驰马 - Taking steps that are light and swift, able to keep up with a galloping horse.
    • 登渉云岩 - Ascending and traversing mountains or cliffs, often with a mystical connotation.
    • 無復困惫 - No longer feeling exhausted or tired.
    • 坐知四方之事 - Knowing matters from all directions while seated, suggesting supernatural knowledge.
    • 騐若符契 - Verification like a seal or token, indicating a perfect match or confirmation.
    • 授我以南溟都水之秩 - Being granted a high-ranking position in the management of the Southern Sea.
    • 旬日逺別不复来矣 - Parting for ten days and not returning again.
    • 天色晴霁 - The sky being clear and bright, describing a sunny day.
    • 香风瑞云弥布山谷 - Fragrant winds and auspicious clouds spreading throughout the valleys.
    • 空中騎乗飞举南去 - Riding and flying southward in the air, suggesting a mystical journey.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 中山劉商 - Refers to Liu Shang from Zhongshan, likely a historical or legendary figure.
    • 水墨 - Refers to ink wash painting, a traditional Chinese art form.
    • 九海總司 - Supervisor or administrator of the nine seas, a mythical or vast expanse.
    • 列真之任 - Duty or position of immortals or deities.
    • 南溟都水之秩 - High-ranking position in the management of the Southern Sea.
    • 仙傳拾遺 - Collection of records of immortals or deities with supplementary notes.

    Proper Nouns

    • 中山劉商 - Name of a person, likely a historical or legendary figure.
    • 水墨 - Term referring to ink wash painting, a traditional Chinese art form.
    • 九海總司 - Title referring to the supervisor or administrator of the nine seas.
    • 列真之任 - Term referring to the duty or position of immortals or deities.
    • 南溟都水之秩 - Term referring to a high-ranking position in the management of the Southern Sea.
    • 仙傳拾遺 - Title referring to a collection of records of immortals or deities with supplementary notes.

    After more than a month of practicing and adhering to a certain method, his teeth and hair became more abundant, and his appearance resembled that of a child. He could take steps that were light and swift, keeping up with a galloping horse, and ascend and traverse mountains and cliffs without becoming exhausted. After another month, he could know matters from all directions as if by a seal or token. Then, he entered the Strong Upper Cave. In the first year of the Xiantong era, a tavern owner, noticing that the woodcutter was somewhat unusual, treated him with utmost respect. After several months, the woodcutter returned once more and told the tavern owner, "I am Liu Shang from Zhongshan. I have long practiced ink wash painting. I wish to leave you a painting to repay your generous hospitality." The tavern owner prepared the materials, and Liu Shang promised to return. The next day, Liu Shang indeed returned. The tavern owner provided him with brushes and ink, and Liu Shang began to paint. In a moment, he created a landscape of thousands of mountains and rivers, surpassing the work of ordinary artists. As he was about to leave, Liu Shang told the tavern owner, "My ancestor was the Prince of Huainan and is now the supervisor of the nine seas, holding the duty of immortals. He has granted me the rank of water management in the Southern Sea. We will part ways for ten days and will not return." After about ten days, the sky was clear and bright, with fragrant winds and auspicious clouds spreading throughout the valleys. The woodcutter saw people riding and flying southward in the air. [80]

    太平广记卷六
    <子部,小说家类,异闻之属,太平广记>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　太平广记卷七
    　　神仙七
    　　白石先生　　皇初平　　　王逺
    　　伯山甫　　　马鸣生　　　李八百
    　　李阿
    　　白石先生
    　　白石先生者中黄文人弟子也至彭祖时已二千嵗余矣不肯修升天之道但取不死而已不失人间之乐其所据行者正以交接之道为主而金液之药为上也初以居贫不能得药乃飬羊牧猪十数年间约衣节用置货万金乃大买药服之常煮白石为粮因就白石山居时人故号曰白石先生亦食脯饮酒亦食谷食日行三四百里视之色如四十许人性好朝拜事神好读幽经及太素传彭祖问之曰何不服升天之药答曰天上复能乐比人间乎但莫使老死耳天上多至尊相奉事更苦于人间故时人呼白石先生为遁仙人以其不汲汲于升天为仙官亦犹不求闻逹者也【出神仙传】 [81]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [81]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 太平广记 - A large collection of stories and anecdotes from ancient China, compiled during the Song Dynasty.
    • 钦定四库全书 - The Complete Library of the Four Treasuries, a massive compilation of Chinese literature commissioned by the Qing Dynasty.
    • 神仙 - Literally 'immortals,' referring to deities or enlightened beings in Chinese mythology.
    • 白石先生 - The name of a legendary immortal, known for his longevity and wisdom.
    • 中黄文人 - A reference to a sage or enlightened being, possibly a title or honorific.
    • 彭祖 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology, known for his extraordinary longevity.
    • 金液之药 - A type of elixir or potion believed to grant immortality or longevity.
    • 交接之道 - A term that can refer to various practices, including alchemy, meditation, or sexual techniques, aimed at achieving immortality or longevity.
    • 不失人间之乐 - Literally 'not losing the joys of human life,' suggesting a preference for earthly pleasures over heavenly ones.

    Translation:

    The Taiping Guangji, Volume 6
    <Subcategory: Strange Tales, Novelists' Works, Taiping Guangji>

    The Taiping Guangji, Volume 7
    Chapter 7: Immortals

    White Stone Immortal, Huang Chuping, Wang Yuan, Bo Shanfu, Ma Mingsheng, Li Bagun, Li A

    White Stone Immortal

    The White Stone Immortal was a disciple of Zhong Huang Wenren. By the time of Peng Zu, he had already lived for over two thousand years. He did not pursue the path to ascend to heaven but sought only immortality, and he did not lose the joys of human life. His primary practice was the art of cultivation, and he regarded the elixir of gold as the highest. Initially, due to poverty, he could not obtain the elixir, so he raised sheep and pigs. After several years, he managed to save enough money to purchase the elixir. He often cooked white stones as his food and lived in a cave on White Stone Mountain. People called him the White Stone Immortal because of this. He also ate meat and drank wine, and consumed grains. He could travel three to four hundred miles a day, and his appearance was that of a man in his forties. He enjoyed offering sacrifices to deities and reading esoteric texts and the Tai Su (Great Primordial). Peng Zu asked him, "Why don't you take the elixir to ascend to heaven?" He replied, "Can one be happier in heaven than on earth? I just don't want to grow old and die. In heaven, there are many supreme beings to serve, which is even more difficult than life on earth." Therefore, people called him the Yandun Immortal because he did not eagerly pursue ascension to become an immortal official, much like those who do not seek fame and success. [Source: Xiansheng Zhuan (Biographies of Immortals)] [81]

    皇初平
    　　皇初平者丹溪人也年十五家使牧羊有道士见其良谨便将至金华山石室中四十余年不复念家其兄初起行山寻索初平歴年不得后见市中有一道士初起召问之曰吾有弟名初平因令牧羊失之四十余年莫知死生所在愿道君为占之道士曰金华山中有一牧羊児姓皇字初平是卿弟非疑初起闻之即随道士去求弟遂得相见悲喜语毕问初平羊何在曰近在山东耳初起往视之不见但见白石
    而还谓初平曰山东无羊也初平曰羊在耳兄但自不见之初平与初起俱往看之初平乃叱曰羊起于是白石皆变为羊数万头初起曰弟独得仙道如此吾可学乎初平曰唯好道便可得之耳初起便弃妻子留住就初平学共服松脂茯苓至万日能坐在立亡行于日中无影而有童子之色后乃俱还乡里亲族死终畧尽乃复还去初平改字为赤松子初起改字为鲁班其后服此药得仙者数十人【出神仙传】 [82]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [82]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 四十余年不复念家 - Means 'for more than forty years without thinking of home,' indicating a long period of seclusion.
    • 唯好道便可得之耳 - Means 'If you only love the Tao, you can achieve it,' indicating a spiritual message.
    • 便弃妻子留住就初平学共服松脂茯苓至万日能坐在立亡行于日中无影而有童子之色 - Describes the brother's decision to join his brother in practice, which should be clearly separated from the main narrative.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 皇初平 - A historical figure, a hermit who became an immortal.
    • 丹溪 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific region.
    • 金华山 - A mountain in Zhejiang Province, known for its spiritual significance.
    • 赤松子 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology, associated with immortality.
    • 鲁班 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology, associated with carpentry and craftsmanship.
    • 松脂茯苓 - Ingredients used in traditional Chinese medicine, believed to have health benefits.
    • 神仙传 - A collection of stories about immortals, indicating the source of the text.

    Proper Nouns

    • 皇初平 - The name of the hermit.
    • 丹溪 - A place name.
    • 金华山 - A mountain.
    • 赤松子 - A legendary figure.
    • 鲁班 - A legendary figure.
    • 松脂茯苓 - Ingredients.
    • 神仙传 - The source of the text.

    Huang Chuping, a native of Danxi, was sent by his family to tend sheep when he was fifteen years old. A Taoist saw how diligent and trustworthy he was and took him to a cave in Jinhua Mountain. For more than forty years, he did not think of home. His brother, Chuiqi, went into the mountains to search for Chuping for many years without success. Later, he saw a Taoist in the marketplace and called him over to ask, "I have a younger brother named Chuping whom I sent to tend sheep and lost for more than forty years; I don't know if he is alive or dead. I hope you can tell me where he is." The Taoist said, "There is a shepherd boy named Huang Chuping in Jinhua Mountain, who is your younger brother, not a doubt. Chuiqi heard this and followed the Taoist to find his brother, and they were reunited. After a mix of sadness and joy, they asked Chuping about the sheep. He said, 'They are near the Eastern Mountain.' Chuiqi went to look but saw nothing but white stones. Chuping and Chuiqi went together to see, and Chuping shouted, 'Sheep, come!' Suddenly, the white stones turned into tens of thousands of sheep. Chuiqi said, 'How is it that only my younger brother has attained the way of immortality? Can I learn it too?' Chuping replied, 'If you only love the Tao, you can achieve it.' Chuiqi then abandoned his wife and children to stay with Chuping and practice together, taking pine resin and Poria for ten thousand days. They could sit and stand without moving, walk in the sun without a shadow, and look like young boys. Later, they both returned to their hometown, where most of their relatives had died, and then they returned to their practice. Chuping changed his name to Chi Songzi, and Chuiqi changed his name to Lu Ban. Later, dozens of people who took this medicine became immortals. [82]

    王远
    　　王远字方平东海人也举孝亷除郎中稍加中散大夫学通五经尤明天文图防河洛之要逆知天下盛衰之期九州吉凶如观之掌握后弃官入山修道道成汉孝桓帝闻之连徴不出使郡国逼载以诣京师逺低头闭口不答诏乃题宫门扇板四百余字皆説方来之事帝恶之使削去外字适去内字复见墨皆彻板里削之愈分明逺无子孙乡里人累世相传供飬之同郡太尉陈躭为逺营道室旦夕朝拜之但乞福未言学道也逺在陈家四十余年陈家曽无疾病死丧奴婢皆然六畜繁息田桑倍获逺忽语陈躭曰吾期运当去不得乆停明日日中当发至时逺死躭知其仙去不敢下着地但悲 [83]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [83]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 舉孝廉 - Formal title for the system of recommending virtuous men for government service; literally 'to be recommended as filial and upright.'
    • 除郎中 - Means 'appointed as a Langzhong,' a junior official in the Han imperial court.
    • 稍加中散大夫 - Means 'gradually promoted to the rank of Zhongshangdaifu,' a high-ranking official position.
    • 五经 - Refers to the Five Classics: *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), *Shujing* (Book of Documents), *Yijing* (Book of Changes), *Liji* (Book of Rites), and *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals).
    • 天文图谶 - Refers to astrological and prophetic texts.
    • 河洛之要 - Refers to the profound meanings of the Yellow River and Luo River, which were believed to hold cosmic secrets.
    • 逆知天下盛衰之期 - Means 'to foresee the times of prosperity and decline of the world.'
    • 九州吉凶 - Means 'the fortune or misfortune of the nine provinces' (a traditional Chinese division of the country).
    • 如观之掌 - A metaphor meaning 'as if watching it in the palm of one's hand,' indicating clear foresight.
    • 弃官入山 - Means 'to resign from office and enter the mountains,' referring to a retreat into seclusion for spiritual cultivation.
    • 道成 - Means 'to achieve the Dao' or 'to attain spiritual enlightenment.'
    • 连征不出 - Means 'was repeatedly summoned but did not appear.'
    • 逼载以诣京师 - Means 'forced to be taken to the capital city.'
    • 低头闭口 - Means 'to lower one's head and remain silent.'
    • 题宫门扇板四百余字 - Means 'to inscribe over four hundred characters on the palace door panels.'
    • 方来之事 - Means 'events yet to come.'
    • 恶之 - Means 'to find it distasteful' or 'to dislike.'
    • 削去外字 - Means 'to scrape off the outer characters.'
    • 内字复见墨皆彻板里 - Means 'the inner characters revealed ink that penetrated the board.'
    • 削之愈分明 - Means 'the more it was scraped, the clearer it became.'
    • 无子孙 - Means 'without descendants.'
    • 乡里人累世相传 - Means 'people of the village passed it down through generations.'
    • 供奉之 - Means 'to serve or worship him.'
    • 同郡太尉陈耽 - Refers to Chen Dan, a high-ranking official from the same region.
    • 营道室 - Means 'to build a room for Daoist practice.'
    • 旦夕朝拜之 - Means 'to worship him morning and evening.'
    • 但乞福 - Means 'to merely ask for blessings.'
    • 未言学道 - Means 'without mentioning learning the Dao.'
    • 期运当去 - Means 'his time to depart has arrived.'
    • 不敢下着地 - Means 'did not dare to touch the ground.'
    • 但悲 - Means 'only felt sorrow.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 王远字方平东海人也 - Refers to Wang Yuan, a Daoist figure from the Eastern Han period, known by the courtesy name Fangping, and from the region of Donghai.
    • 孝廉 - A system of selecting officials based on moral virtue and ability.
    • 郎中 - A junior official in the Han imperial court.
    • 中散大夫 - A high-ranking official in the Han bureaucracy.
    • 五经 - The Five Classics, foundational texts of Confucianism.
    • 天文图谶 - Astrological and prophetic texts that were believed to predict future events.
    • 河洛之要 - The Yellow River and Luo River were considered sacred and symbolic of cosmic order.
    • 汉孝桓帝 - Emperor Huan of Han, a ruler of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 京师 - The capital city, in this case, Luoyang.
    • 太尉 - A high-ranking military and political official.
    • 陈耽 - A historical figure, a high-ranking official during the Eastern Han period.
    • 道室 - A room or space dedicated to Daoist practice.
    • 仙去 - To ascend to immortality or leave the mortal realm as a deity.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王远 - A historical Daoist figure from the Eastern Han period.
    • 方平 - The courtesy name of Wang Yuan.
    • 东海 - A region in ancient China, located in present-day Jiangsu and Shandong provinces.
    • 孝廉 - A title for those recommended for government service based on moral virtue.
    • 郎中 - A junior official in the Han imperial court.
    • 中散大夫 - A high-ranking official in the Han bureaucracy.
    • 五经 - The Five Classics of Confucianism.
    • 天文图谶 - Astrological and prophetic texts.
    • 河洛 - The Yellow River and Luo River, symbolizing cosmic order.
    • 汉孝桓帝 - Emperor Huan of Han, a ruler of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 京师 - The capital city, Luoyang.
    • 太尉 - A high-ranking military and political official.
    • 陈耽 - A historical figure, a high-ranking official during the Eastern Han period.
    • 道室 - A room or space dedicated to Daoist practice.

    Wang Yuan

    Wang Yuan, courtesy name Fangping, was from Donghai. He was recommended as filial and upright and appointed as a Langzhong, later being gradually promoted to the rank of Zhongshangdaifu. He was well-versed in the Five Classics and particularly skilled in astrology and prophetic texts, understanding the profound meanings of the Yellow River and Luo River, which allowed him to foresee the times of prosperity and decline of the world, as well as the fortune or misfortune of the nine provinces, as if watching them in the palm of his hand. After resigning from his official duties, he entered the mountains to cultivate the Dao, achieving spiritual enlightenment. Emperor Huan of Han heard of him and repeatedly summoned him, but he did not appear. The imperial court then forced him to be taken to the capital city. Wang Yuan lowered his head and remained silent, not answering the imperial decree. The emperor then inscribed over four hundred characters on the palace door panels, all predicting future events. The emperor found it distasteful and ordered the outer characters to be scraped off. As the outer characters were removed, the inner characters revealed ink that penetrated the board. The more it was scraped, the clearer it became. Wang Yuan had no descendants, but people of the village passed it down through generations, serving and worshipping him. Chen Dan, a high-ranking official from the same region, built a room for Daoist practice for Wang Yuan, worshipping him morning and evening, but merely asking for blessings without mentioning learning the Dao. Wang Yuan stayed in Chen Dan's household for over forty years, during which time Chen Dan's household was free from disease, death, or misfortune, and even the servants and livestock thrived, with crops and mulberry trees yielding bountiful harvests. One day, Wang Yuan suddenly told Chen Dan, "My time to depart has arrived; I cannot stay long. I will leave at noon tomorrow." At the appointed time, Wang Yuan died. Chen Dan realized that he had ascended to immortality, and did not dare to touch the ground, only feeling sorrow. [83]

    啼叹息曰先生舍我我将何怙具棺器烧香就牀衣装之至三日夜忽失其尸衣冠不觧如虵蜕耳逺卒后百余日躭亦卒或谓耽得逺之道化去或谓知躭将终故委之而去也初逺欲东入括苍山过吴住胥门蔡经家蔡经者小民耳而骨相当仙逺知之故住其家遂语经曰汝生命应得度世欲取汝以官僚耳然少不知道今气少肉多不得上去当为尸觧如从狗窦中过耳于是告以要言乃委经而去经后忽身体发如火欲得冷水灌之举家汲水灌之如沃焦石如此三日销耗骨立乃入室以被自覆忽然失之视其被内唯有皮头足具如蝉蜕也去十余年忽还家容色少壮鬂发鬒黒语家人 [84]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [84]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 啼叹息曰 - Phrase indicating sorrowful weeping and sighing, used to describe the emotional state of the subject.
    • 先生舍我我将何怙 - Rhetorical question expressing the speaker's concern about what they will rely on after the departure of the master.
    • 具棺器烧香就牀 - Describes the preparation for death, including setting up a coffin and burning incense at the bedside.
    • 衣装之至三日夜 - Indicates the duration of the preparation, emphasizing the time spent in preparation.
    • 忽失其尸衣冠不觧如虵蜕耳 - Describes the sudden disappearance of the body, with clothes and cap still intact, likened to a snake shedding its skin.
    • 逺卒后百余日躭亦卒 - States that after the death of the first subject, the second subject died after about a hundred days.
    • 或谓耽得逺之道化去 - Suggests a possible interpretation that the second subject attained the way of the first and transformed.
    • 或谓知躭将终故委之而去也 - Another possible interpretation that the first subject knew the second would die and left him.
    • 初逺欲东入括苍山 - Indicates the initial plan of the first subject to travel east to the Kuoch'ang Mountains.
    • 过吴住胥门蔡经家 - Describes the journey and stopping at the home of Cai Jing in Wu.
    • 蔡经者小民耳而骨相当仙 - Describes Cai Jing as a commoner but with a destiny to become a sage.
    • 远知之故住其家 - Indicates that the first subject stayed at Cai Jing's home because he knew his destiny.
    • 汝生命应得度世欲取汝以官僚耳 - A statement about the subject's fate and the intention to take him away from officialdom.
    • 然少不知道今气少肉多不得上去当为尸觧如从狗窦中过耳 - Explains the subject's current state and the process of transformation.
    • 告以要言乃委经而去 - Describes the instruction given and the departure of the first subject.
    • 经后忽身体发如火欲得冷水灌之 - Describes Cai Jing's sudden illness, with his body burning like fire and needing cold water.
    • 举家汲水灌之如沃焦石如此三日销耗骨立 - Describes the family's efforts to cool him down and the extreme state of his body.
    • 乃入室以被自覆忽然失之视其被内唯有皮头足具如蝉蜕也 - Describes Cai Jing's sudden disappearance, with only his skin and head and feet remaining.
    • 去十余年忽还家容色少壮鬂发鬒黑语家人 - Describes Cai Jing's return after many years, looking young and healthy, with black hair and beard, and speaking to his family.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 括苍山 - The Kuoch'ang Mountains, a mountain range in Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 胥门 - A gate in ancient Suzhou, now part of Jiangsu Province.
    • 蔡经 - A historical figure, a commoner who was chosen to follow a spiritual path.
    • 度世 - To transcend the world, often used in the context of achieving immortality or enlightenment.
    • 官僚 - Officials or bureaucrats.
