    • 尸觧 - A term used in Daoist texts to describe the transformation of the body, similar to the concept of '尸体解体' (body dissolution).
    • 狗窦 - A small hole or opening, used metaphorically to describe the process of transformation.
    • 蝉蜕 - The exoskeleton of a cicada, used as a metaphor for the process of transformation or rebirth.
    • 容色少壮 - Describes a youthful and healthy appearance.
    • 鬂发鬒黑 - Describes black hair and beard, indicating youth and vitality.

    Proper Nouns

    • 括苍山 - The Kuoch'ang Mountains.
    • 胥门 - The Xumen gate in ancient Suzhou.
    • 蔡经 - A historical figure.
    • 度世 - Transcending the world.
    • 官僚 - Officials or bureaucrats.
    • 尸觧 - Transformation of the body.
    • 狗窦 - Small hole or opening.
    • 蝉蜕 - Cicada's exoskeleton.
    • 容色少壮 - Youthful and healthy appearance.
    • 鬂发鬒黑 - Black hair and beard.

    The Master wept and sighed, saying, "Master, if you abandon me, what shall I rely on?" He prepared a coffin, burned incense, and lay on the bed, dressed in his clothes. For three days and nights, he suddenly disappeared, his clothes and cap still intact, as if he had shed his skin like a snake. After the Master's death, more than a hundred days later, the disciple also passed away. Some said that the disciple had attained the Master's way and transformed, while others said that the Master knew the disciple would die and left him.

    Initially, the Master wanted to travel east to the Kuoch'ang Mountains, passing through Wu and staying at the home of Cai Jing in the Xumen area. Cai Jing was a commoner, but his destiny was to become a sage. The Master knew this and stayed at his home. He then told Cai Jing, "Your life should lead you to transcend the world, and I want to take you away from officialdom. However, since you are young and ignorant, with less spirit and more flesh, you cannot ascend. You will undergo a transformation like passing through a small hole." He then instructed him in the essential teachings and left him.

    Afterward, Cai Jing suddenly fell ill, with his body burning like fire and needing cold water. His family brought water to cool him down, as if pouring water on a burning rock. After three days, he had lost so much weight that he was left with only bones. He then went into the room, covered himself with a blanket, and suddenly disappeared. When they looked inside the blanket, they saw only his skin, head, and feet, like a cicada's exoskeleton.

    More than ten years later, he suddenly returned home, looking young and healthy, with black hair and beard, and spoke to his family. [84]

    曰七月七日王君当来其日可多作饮食以供从官至其日经家乃借瓮器作饮食百余斛罗列布置庭下是日王君果来未至先闻金鼓箫管人马之声比近皆惊莫知所在及至经舎举家皆见逺冠逺游冠朱衣虎头鞶囊五色绶带劒黄色少髭长短中人形也乗羽车驾五龙龙各异色前后麾节幡旗导从威仪奕奕如大军也有十二队五伯士皆以蜡封其口鼓吹皆乗龙从天而下悬集于庭从官皆长丈余不从道衢既至从官皆不知所在唯独见逺坐耳须臾引见经父母兄弟因遣人召麻姑亦莫知麻姑是何神也言曰王方平敬报乆不到民间今来在此想姑能暂来语否须臾信还不见 [85]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [85]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 曰七月七日王君当来 - Formal statement indicating 'On the seventh day of the seventh month, the Lord will come.'
    • 其日可多作饮食以供从官 - Means 'On that day, one may prepare much food to serve the attendants.'
    • 经家乃借瓮器作饮食百余斛 - Means 'The family then borrowed jars and vessels to prepare over a hundred shi of food.'
    • 罗列布置庭下 - Means 'They arranged and set up the food in the courtyard.'
    • 是日王君果来 - Means 'On that day, the Lord indeed came.'
    • 未至先闻金鼓箫管人马之声 - Means 'Before arriving, they heard the sounds of drums, flutes, and the voices of people and horses.'
    • 比近皆惊莫知所在 - Means 'Those nearby were all startled and did not know where it was coming from.'
    • 及至经舎举家皆见远冠远游冠朱衣虎头鞶囊五色绶带劒黄色少髭长短中人形也 - Means 'When they reached the guesthouse, the entire family saw a distant figure wearing a far-traveling cap, a red robe, a tiger-headed pouch, a five-colored sash, and a sword, with a few strands of beard, of medium height.'
    • 乘羽车驾五龙龙各异色前后麾节幡旗导从威仪奕奕如大军也有十二队五伯士皆以蜡封其口 - Means 'Riding in a feathered carriage, he was drawn by five dragons of different colors, with flags and banners leading the way, and his retinue was majestic like a great army, with twelve divisions and five hundred soldiers, all sealed with wax over their mouths.'
    • 鼓吹皆乘龙从天而下悬集于庭 - Means 'The musicians all rode dragons and descended from the sky, gathering in the courtyard.'
    • 从官皆长丈余不从道衢既至从官皆不知所在唯独见逺坐耳 - Means 'The attendants were all over ten feet tall, not following the usual paths. When they arrived, the attendants disappeared, leaving only the distant figure sitting.'
    • 须臾引见经父母兄弟因遣人召麻姑亦莫知麻姑是何神也 - Means 'After a short while, they were led to see the family members of the guest. They then sent someone to summon Madame Magu, but no one knew who Madame Magu was.'
    • 言曰王方平敬报久不到民间今来在此想姑能暂来语否 - Means 'They said, 'Lord Wang Fangping respectfully reports that he has not been to the common people for a long time. He is here now. Does Madame Magu wish to come and speak?''
    • 须臾信还不见 - Means 'After a short while, the messenger returned but was not seen.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 七月七日 - Refers to the Qixi Festival, a traditional Chinese holiday.
    • 王君 - Refers to a deity or a high-ranking official, possibly Lord Wang Fangping.
    • 金鼓箫管人马之声 - Describes the sounds of a grand procession, including drums, flutes, and the voices of people and horses.
    • 远冠远游冠朱衣虎头鞶囊五色绶带劒 - Describes the attire of the deity, including a distant cap, a far-traveling cap, a red robe, a tiger-headed pouch, a five-colored sash, and a sword.
    • 羽车 - Refers to a feathered carriage, a symbol of divine or supernatural transportation.
    • 五龙 - Refers to five dragons, symbolizing power and divinity.
    • 麾节幡旗 - Refers to flags and banners, symbols of authority and leadership.
    • 麻姑 - Refers to Madame Magu, a mythical figure known for her longevity and supernatural abilities.
    • 王方平 - Refers to Lord Wang Fangping, a deity or high-ranking official.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王君 - Refers to a deity or high-ranking official, possibly Lord Wang Fangping.
    • 王方平 - Refers to Lord Wang Fangping, a deity or high-ranking official.
    • 麻姑 - Refers to Madame Magu, a mythical figure known for her longevity and supernatural abilities.

    On the seventh day of the seventh month, the Lord will come. On that day, one may prepare much food to serve the attendants. When the day arrived, the family borrowed jars and vessels to prepare over a hundred shi of food, arranging and setting it up in the courtyard. On that day, the Lord indeed came. Before arriving, they heard the sounds of drums, flutes, and the voices of people and horses. Those nearby were all startled and did not know where it was coming from. When they reached the guesthouse, the entire family saw a distant figure wearing a far-traveling cap, a red robe, a tiger-headed pouch, a five-colored sash, and a sword, with a few strands of beard, of medium height. Riding in a feathered carriage, he was drawn by five dragons of different colors, with flags and banners leading the way, and his retinue was majestic like a great army, with twelve divisions and five hundred soldiers, all sealed with wax over their mouths. The musicians all rode dragons and descended from the sky, gathering in the courtyard. The attendants were all over ten feet tall, not following the usual paths. When they arrived, the attendants disappeared, leaving only the distant figure sitting. After a short while, they were led to see the family members of the guest. They then sent someone to summon Madame Magu, but no one knew who Madame Magu was. They said, "Lord Wang Fangping respectfully reports that he has not been to the common people for a long time. He is here now. Does Madame Magu wish to come and speak?" After a short while, the messenger returned but was not seen. [85]

    其使但闻信语曰麻姑载拜不相见忽已五百余年尊卑有序拜敬无阶烦信承来在彼食顷即到先受命当按行蓬莱今便暂往如是当还还便亲觐愿未即去如此两时闻麻姑来来时亦先闻人马声既至从官半于逺也麻姑至蔡经亦举家见之是好女子年可十八九许于顶上作髻余发散垂至腰衣有文采又非锦绮光彩耀目不可名状皆世之所无也八拜逺逺为之起立坐定各进行厨皆金盘玉杯无限也肴膳多是诸花而香气达于内外擘脯而食之云麟脯麻姑自説云接侍以来已见东海三为桑田向到蓬莱又水浅于往日防时畧半耳岂将复为陵陆乎逺叹曰圣人皆言海中行复扬 [86]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [86]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 但闻信语 - Only heard the words of the messenger, emphasizing the limited information conveyed.
    • 载拜 - To pay respects repeatedly, indicating a formal and respectful greeting.
    • 忽已 - Suddenly it has been, emphasizing the passage of time.
    • 尊卑有序拜敬无阶 - Respecting hierarchical order and paying respect without rank, highlighting the egalitarian nature of the greeting.
    • 烦信承来在彼食顷即到 - Trouble the messenger to come and will arrive in the time it takes to eat a meal, indicating a quick journey.
    • 先受命当按行蓬莱 - First receiving an order to inspect the Isles of the Immortals, indicating a mission.
    • 来时亦先闻人马声 - When coming, one also first hears the sound of people and horses, describing the approach.
    • 从官半于远也 - Half the officials were far away, indicating a partial presence.
    • 擘脯而食之 - To split the meat and eat it, describing a method of eating.
    • 向到蓬莱又水浅于往日防时略半耳 - When arriving at the Isles of the Immortals, the water is shallower than before and it is about half as deep as it was, describing a change in the environment.
    • 岂将复为陵陆乎 - Will it become land again? Expressing a question about the future.
    • 远叹曰圣人皆言海中行复扬 - The distant one sighed and said that sages all say that the sea will rise again, indicating a reflection on the cyclical nature of the world.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 麻姑 - A legendary immortal in Chinese mythology, known for her longevity and magical abilities.
    • 蓬莱 - One of the Three Islands of Immortality in Chinese mythology, a place of eternal youth and abundance.
    • 麟脯 - Meat of the qilin, a mythical creature symbolizing prosperity and good fortune.
    • 圣人 - Sages or wise men, often referring to ancient philosophers or rulers.

    Proper Nouns

    • 麻姑 - Ma Gu, the immortal.
    • 蓬莱 - The Isles of the Immortals.
    • 麟脯 - Meat of the qilin.
    • 圣人 - Sages.

    The messenger only heard the words of the envoy: "Ma Gu pays repeated respects, not having seen each other for five hundred years. Respect is shown according to rank, with no hierarchy. Please trouble the messenger to come; you will arrive in the time it takes to eat a meal. First receiving an order, I am to inspect the Isles of the Immortals. Now I will go there temporarily. If I return, I will personally pay homage. I hope you do not leave immediately. In this way, within two hours, I heard Ma Gu coming. When she came, one also first heard the sound of people and horses. When she arrived, half the officials were far away. Ma Gu arrived, and Cai Jing also saw her with his whole family. She was a beautiful woman, about eighteen or nineteen years old, with a bun on her head and her remaining hair hanging down to her waist. Her clothes had intricate patterns and were not made of brocade or silk, but their brilliance was dazzling and beyond description, all things of the world had never seen before. Eight bows, Yuan stood up and paid respects. After sitting down, each proceeded to the kitchen, which had golden plates and jade cups without limit. The dishes and meals were mostly flowers, and their fragrance reached inside and out. They split the meat and ate it, saying it was qilin meat. Ma Gu said, 'Since I began serving, I have seen the East Sea turn into land three times. When I arrived at the Isles of the Immortals, the water was shallower than before, about half as deep as it was. Will it become land again?' Yuan sighed and said, 'Sages all say that the sea will rise again.' [86]

    尘也麻姑欲见蔡经母及妇等时经弟妇新产数日姑见知之曰噫且立勿前即求少许米来得米掷之堕地谓以米祛其也视其米皆成丹砂逺笑曰姑故年少也吾老矣不喜复作如此狡狯变化也逺谓经家人曰吾欲赐汝辈美酒此酒方出天厨其味醇醲非俗人所宜饮饮之或能烂肠今当以水和之汝辈勿怪也乃以斗水合升酒搅之以赐经家人人饮一升许皆醉良乆酒尽逺语左右曰不足复还取也以千钱与余杭姥乞酒湏臾信还得一油囊酒五斗许使传余杭姥答言恐地上酒不中尊饮耳麻姑手爪似鸟经见之心中念曰背大痒时得此爪以爬背当佳也逺已知经心中所言即使 [87]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [87]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 尘也 - Unclear phrase, possibly a typo or error.
    • 麻姑欲见蔡经母及妇等 - Mazu wishes to see Cai Jing's mother and wife.
    • 时经弟妇新产数日 - Cai Jing's younger sister-in-law had given birth a few days prior.
    • 姑见知之曰噫且立勿前 - Mazu, upon seeing the situation, says, 'Ah, stand back.'
    • 即求少许米来得米掷之堕地 - Mazu asks for a little rice, receives it, and throws it to the ground.
    • 谓以米祛其也 - The rice was used to drive away something undesirable (the character  is unclear and may be a typo or error).
    • 视其米皆成丹砂 - The rice turned into cinnabar (a red mineral).
    • 远笑曰姑故年少也吾老矣不喜复作如此狡狯变化也 - Someone (possibly a deity or another figure) laughs and comments on the age difference between Mazu and themselves, expressing a dislike for such playful transformations.
    • 远谓经家人曰吾欲赐汝辈美酒此酒方出天厨其味醇醲非俗人所宜饮饮之或能烂肠今当以水和之汝辈勿怪也 - The same figure tells Cai Jing's family that they want to give them some fine wine, which comes from a heavenly kitchen and is very strong, not suitable for ordinary people to drink, and might even cause digestive issues. They will dilute it with water.
    • 乃以斗水合升酒搅之以赐经家人人饮一升许皆醉良久酒尽 - They mix a certain amount of water with the wine and give it to Cai Jing's family, who all get drunk after drinking a certain amount.
    • 远语左右曰不足复还取也以千钱与余杭姥乞酒须臾信还得一油囊酒五斗许使传余杭姥答言恐地上酒不中尊饮耳 - The figure tells their attendants that it is not enough and asks them to go back and get more wine from an old woman in Yuhang (a place name). They return with a bag of wine and the old woman says that earthly wine might not be suitable for them to drink.
    • 麻姑手爪似鸟经见之心中念曰背大痒时得此爪以爬背当佳也 - Cai Jing notices that Mazu's fingers are like those of a bird and thinks to himself that if he were to scratch his back with them when it itches, it would be great.
    • 远已知经心中所言即使 - The figure already knows what Cai Jing is thinking and acts accordingly.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 麻姑 - A deity in Chinese mythology, often associated with longevity and immortality.
    • 蔡经 - A historical figure or a person mentioned in the text, possibly a family member.
    • 余杭姥 - An old woman from Yuhang, a place name. In Chinese mythology, old women often represent wisdom and longevity.
    • 丹砂 - Cinnabar, a red mineral often associated with alchemy and immortality in Chinese culture.
    • 天厨 - Heavenly kitchen, a metaphor for divine or celestial sources of food and drink.

    Proper Nouns

    • 麻姑 - A deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 蔡经 - A historical figure or a person mentioned in the text.
    • 余杭姥 - An old woman from Yuhang, a place name.
    • 丹砂 - Cinnabar, a red mineral.
    • 天厨 - Heavenly kitchen, a metaphor for divine sources.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. Her younger sister-in-law had given birth a few days prior. Mazu, upon seeing the situation, said, "Ah, stand back." She then asked for a little rice, received it, and threw it to the ground, saying that the rice was used to drive away something undesirable. Upon seeing the rice turn into cinnabar, the figure laughed and commented, "Mazu, you are still young, while I am old. I do not like to perform such playful transformations anymore." The figure then told Cai Jing's family that they wanted to give them some fine wine, which came from a heavenly kitchen and was very strong, not suitable for ordinary people to drink, and might even cause digestive issues. They would dilute it with water. They mixed a certain amount of water with the wine and gave it to Cai Jing's family, who all got drunk after drinking a certain amount. The figure then told their attendants that it was not enough and asked them to go back and get more wine from an old woman in Yuhang. They returned with a bag of wine, and the old woman said that earthly wine might not be suitable for them to drink. Cai Jing noticed that Mazu's fingers were like those of a bird and thought to himself that if he were to scratch his back with them when it itched, it would be great. The figure already knew what Cai Jing was thinking and acted accordingly. [87]

    病有效事之者数百家夀一百一十嵗而死死后子弟行其符不复騐矣逺去后经家所作饮食数百斛皆尽亦不见有人饮食也经父母私问经曰王君是何神人复居何处经曰常在昆仑山往来罗浮括苍等山山上皆有宫室主天曺事一日之中与天上相反覆者数十过地上五岳生死之事皆先来吿王君王君出城尽将百官从行唯乗一黄麟将十数侍人每行常见山林在下去地常数百丈所到则山海之神皆来奉迎拜谒其后数十年经复暂归家逺有书与陈尉其书廓落大而不工先是人无知方平名逺者因此乃知之陈尉家于今世世存録王君手书并符传于小箱中【出神仙传】
    　　伯山甫 [89]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [89]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 病有效事之者数百家 - There were hundreds of people who followed the practices described in the text.
    • 夀一百一十嵗而死 - Lived to be one hundred and ten years old and then died.
    • 死后子弟行其符不复騐矣 - After his death, his descendants tried to use his talismans, but they no longer worked.
    • 远去后经家所作饮食数百斛皆尽亦不见有人饮食也 - After the person left, hundreds of measures of food prepared by his family disappeared, and no one was seen eating it.
    • 经父母私问经曰王君是何神人复居何处 - His parents privately asked him, 'Who is this Master Wang? Where does he live?'
    • 常在昆仑山往来罗浮括苍等山 - He often travels between Mount Kunlun and other mountains like Luofu and Kuocang.
    • 山上皆有宫室主天曹事 - There are palaces on the mountains, and he is in charge of celestial affairs.
    • 一日之中与天上相反覆者数十过 - In one day, he changes places with the heavens dozens of times.
    • 地上五岳生死之事皆先来吿王君 - All matters concerning life and death in the five sacred mountains on earth come to him first.
    • 王君出城尽将百官从行唯乘一黄麟将十数侍人 - When Master Wang leaves the city, he takes all his officials with him, riding a yellow dragon and accompanied by several servants.
    • 每行常见山林在下去地常数百丈 - Every time he travels, the mountains and forests seem to be several hundred feet below him.
    • 所到则山海之神皆来奉迎拜谒 - Wherever he goes, the gods of the mountains and seas come to greet and pay homage to him.
    • 其后数十年经复暂归家远有书与陈尉 - After several decades, he temporarily returned home and sent a letter to Chen Wei from afar.
    • 其书廓落大而不工 - The letter was broad and grand but not well-crafted.
    • 先是人无知方平名远者因此乃知之 - Before this, no one knew of Fang Ping, but because of this, people came to know him.
    • 陈尉家于今世世存录王君手书并符传于小箱中 - Chen Wei's family has kept Master Wang's handwritten letters and talismans in a small box, passing them down through generations.
    • 【出神仙传】 - The story is from the *Book of Immortals*.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 王君 - Refers to a deity or immortal figure.
    • 昆仑山 - A mythical mountain in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortals and the divine.
    • 罗浮 - A mountain range in Guangdong Province, known for its association with Taoist immortals.
    • 括苍 - A mountain range in Zhejiang Province, also associated with Taoist immortals.
    • 五岳 - Refers to the five sacred mountains in China: Mount Tai, Mount Heng (in Shanxi), Mount Heng (in Hunan), Mount Hua, and Mount Song.
    • 方平 - A legendary figure known for his immortality and supernatural abilities.
    • 陈尉 - A person who received a letter from Fang Ping.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王君 - A deity or immortal figure.
    • 昆仑山 - A mythical mountain in Chinese mythology.
    • 罗浮 - A mountain range in Guangdong Province.
    • 括苍 - A mountain range in Zhejiang Province.
    • 五岳 - The five sacred mountains in China.
    • 方平 - A legendary figure known for his immortality and supernatural abilities.
    • 陈尉 - A person who received a letter from Fang Ping.

    A number of people followed the practices described in the text, and there were hundreds of them. One such person lived to be one hundred and ten years old and then died. After his death, his descendants tried to use his talismans, but they no longer worked. After he left, hundreds of measures of food prepared by his family disappeared, and no one was seen eating it. His parents privately asked him, "Who is this Master Wang? Where does he live?" He replied, "He often travels between Mount Kunlun and other mountains like Luofu and Kuocang. There are palaces on the mountains, and he is in charge of celestial affairs. In one day, he changes places with the heavens dozens of times. All matters concerning life and death in the five sacred mountains on earth come to him first. When Master Wang leaves the city, he takes all his officials with him, riding a yellow dragon and accompanied by several servants. Every time he travels, the mountains and forests seem to be several hundred feet below him. Wherever he goes, the gods of the mountains and seas come to greet and pay homage to him." After several decades, he temporarily returned home and sent a letter to Chen Wei from afar. The letter was broad and grand but not well-crafted. Before this, no one knew of Fang Ping, but because of this, people came to know him. Chen Wei's family has kept Master Wang's handwritten letters and talismans in a small box, passing them down through generations. [From the Book of Immortals]
    　　Boshi Fu [89]

    李八百
    　　李八百蜀人也莫知其名歴世见之时人计其年八百嵗因以为号或山林或居市防知汉中唐公房有志不遇明师欲教授之乃先往试之为作客佣赁者公昉不知也八百驱使用意异于他客公昉爱异之八百乃伪病困当欲死公昉即为迎医合药费数十万钱不以为损忧念之意形于顔色八百又转作恶疮周徧身体脓血臭恶不可忍近公昉为之流涕曰卿为吾家使者勤苦歴年常得笃疾吾取医欲令卿愈无所恡惜而犹不愈当如卿何八百曰吾疮不愈须人舐之
    当可公昉乃使三婢三婢为舐之八百又曰婢舐不愈若得君为舐之即当愈耳公昉即舐复言无益欲公昉妇舐之最佳又复令妇舐之八百又告曰吾疮乃欲差当得三十斛美酒浴身当愈公昉即为具酒置大器中八百即起入酒中浴疮即愈体如凝脂亦无余防乃吿公昉曰吾是仙人也子有志故此相试子真可教也今当授子度世之诀乃使公昉夫妻并舐疮三婢以其浴酒自浴即皆更少顔色美悦以丹经一卷授公昉公昉入云台山中作药药成服之仙去【出神仙传】 [91]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [91]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 歴世見時 - A phrase meaning 'seen through many generations,' indicating longevity or enduring presence.
    • 莫知其名 - Literally 'no one knows his name,' suggesting anonymity or mystery.

    Li Ba Ba
    Li Ba Ba was a man from Shu, whose name was unknown, but he was seen across many generations. People estimated his age to be eight hundred years, so he was called by this number. Sometimes he dwelled in the mountains and forests, and sometimes he lived in the city. He knew Han Zhong Tang Gongfang, who had aspirations but had not met a great teacher and wished to be instructed by him. Li Ba Ba first went to test him, working as a hired laborer for Tang Gongfang, who did not recognize him. Li Ba Ba treated him differently from other guests, and Tang Gongfang found him strange and unique. Li Ba Ba then pretended to be seriously ill, as if he were about to die. Tang Gongfang immediately sought a doctor and spent tens of thousands of coins on medicine, but he was not concerned about the cost, and his worry and concern were evident on his face.

    Li Ba Ba then changed his condition to a severe and foul-smelling ulcer that covered his entire body, making it unbearable to approach. Tang Gongfang wept and said, "You are a servant of my household, and you have worked hard for years, often suffering from serious illnesses. I have sought a doctor to help you recover, spending no expense, but you still do not get better. What should I do?" Li Ba Ba replied, "My ulcer will not heal unless someone licks it." Tang Gongfang then had three maids lick the ulcer, but Li Ba Ba said, "The maids' licking does not heal it. If you can lick it, it will heal." Tang Gongfang then licked it, but Li Ba Ba said it was still not enough. He then wanted Tang Gongfang's wife to lick it, which would be the best, and Tang Gongfang's wife also licked it. Li Ba Ba then said, "My ulcer is almost healed, but I need thirty shi of fine wine to bathe my body to heal it." Tang Gongfang immediately prepared the wine and placed it in a large container. Li Ba Ba then stood up and entered the wine, bathing in it, and the ulcer healed instantly. His body became as smooth as congealed fat, and there were no more scars. He then told Tang Gongfang, "I am a celestial being. You have aspirations, so I tested you. You are truly worthy of instruction. Now I will teach you the secret of transcending the world." He then made Tang Gongfang and his wife lick the ulcer, and the three maids also bathed in the wine Tang Gongfang had prepared. They all became younger, their faces radiant and joyful. He then gave Tang Gongfang a scroll of the Dan Jing (Alchemical Scripture) and instructed him to go to the Yuntai Mountain, where he made medicine. After the medicine was completed, Tang Gongfang took it and became an immortal. [From the Records of Immortals] [91]

    太平广记卷七
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　太平广记卷八
    　　神仙八
    　　刘安　　　　隂长生　　　张道陵
    　　刘安
    　　汉淮南王刘安者汉髙帝之孙也其父厉王长得罪徙蜀道死文帝哀之而裂其地尽以封长子故安得封淮南王时诸王子贵侈莫不以声色游猎犬马为事唯安独折节下士笃好儒学兼占方术飬士数千人皆天下俊士作内书二十二篇又中篇八章言神仙黄白之事名为鸿寳万毕三卷论变化之道凡十万言武帝以安辩博有才属为诸父甚重尊之特诏及报书常使司马相如等共定草乃遣使召安入朝尝诏使为离骚经旦受诏食时便成奏之安每宴见谈説得失及献诸赋颂晨入夜出乃天下道书及方术之士不逺千里卑辞重币请致之于是乃有八公诣门皆眉皓白门吏先宻以白王王使阍人自以意难问之曰我王上欲求 [93]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [93]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 折节下士 - To humble oneself before scholars or intellectuals, indicating a change in behavior to show respect.
    • 笃好儒学 - To be deeply devoted to Confucian learning.
    • 兼占方术 - To engage in divination and magical arts alongside other pursuits.
    • 飬士数千人 - To support or maintain thousands of scholars or students.
    • 内书二十二篇 - Twenty-two internal books, likely referring to texts on various subjects.
    • 中篇八章 - Eight chapters of middle-length works, possibly detailing specific topics.
    • 鸿寳万毕三卷 - Three volumes of 'Hongbao Wanbi,' which might contain esoteric knowledge or alchemical recipes.
    • 论变化之道 - Discussing the principles of transformation or change.
    • 凡十万言 - A total of 100,000 words.
    • 辩博有才属 - To be eloquent, knowledgeable, and talented.
    • 诸父 - Referring to paternal relatives or respected elders.
    • 重尊之 - To honor and respect highly.
    • 特诏及报书 - Special edicts and replies from the emperor.
    • 司马相如等共定草 - Sima Xiangru and others together drafting or reviewing the content.
    • 召入朝 - To summon to the imperial court.
    • 旦受诏食时便成奏之 - Receiving the imperial decree in the morning and completing the task by the time of the meal.
    • 八公诣门 - Eight sages or scholars arriving at the door.
    • 鬂眉皓白 - Having white hair and eyebrows, indicating old age.
    • 卑辞重币 - Using humble words and offering valuable gifts.
    • 请致之 - To invite or request their presence.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 汉高帝 - Emperor Gaozu of Han, the founder of the Han Dynasty.
    • 汉文帝 - Emperor Wen of Han, known for his benevolent rule and reforms.
    • 厉王 - Liu An's father, the Prince of Chu, who was exiled to Sichuan and later died.
    • 淮南 - A region in present-day Anhui Province, where Liu An ruled as prince.
    • 离骚经 - The 'Lisao' (also known as 'Li Sao'), a famous poem by Qu Yuan, often associated with themes of exile and loyalty.
    • 声色游猎犬马 - Refers to indulgence in music, women, hunting, and horse racing, activities often criticized as frivolous.
    • 神仙黄白之事 - Matters related to immortals, alchemy, and the pursuit of longevity.
    • 鸿寳万毕 - A term that may refer to esoteric texts or alchemical works attributed to Liu An.

    Proper Nouns

    • 太平广记 - A collection of Chinese legends and stories.
    • 钦定四库全书 - A massive collection of Chinese literature.
    • 汉淮南王刘安 - Liu An, Prince of Huainan.
    • 汉高帝 - Emperor Gaozu of Han.
    • 汉文帝 - Emperor Wen of Han.
    • 厉王 - Liu An's father, the Prince of Chu.
    • 淮南 - The region where Liu An ruled.
    • 离骚经 - The 'Lisao' poem by Qu Yuan.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 司马相如 - Sima Xiangru, a famous poet and statesman.
    • 八公 - Eight wise men or scholars.

    The Book of Taiping Guangji, Volume Seven
    The Qinding Siku Quanshu
    The Book of Taiping Guangji, Volume Eight
    The Immortals, Volume Eight

    Liu An
    Yin Changsheng
    Zhang Daoling

    Liu An
    Liu An, the Prince of Huainan, was the grandson of Emperor Gaozu of Han. His father, the Prince of Chu, was exiled to Sichuan and died there. Emperor Wen of Han felt sorry for the family and divided the territory to grant it to Liu An, making him the Prince of Huainan. During this period, the sons of the nobility were extravagant, indulging in music, women, hunting, and horse racing. Only Liu An changed his ways, showing respect to scholars, and was deeply devoted to Confucian learning and divination, supporting thousands of talented individuals. He wrote twenty-two internal books, eight chapters of middle-length works, and three volumes of "Hongbao Wanbi," discussing transformations and alchemy, totaling 100,000 words. Emperor Wu of Han valued Liu An for his eloquence and talent, treating him with great respect, and often had him draft important documents, summoning him to the court. Once, he was ordered to write the "Lisao," completing it by the time of the meal. Liu An often participated in banquets, discussing gains and losses, and presenting various poems and hymns, entering the palace in the morning and leaving at night. Scholars and practitioners of Daoist arts from far away came to see him, offering valuable gifts and humble words. Eight wise men arrived at the door, all with white hair and eyebrows. The gatekeeper reported this to the prince, who instructed the gatekeeper to ask them if they had come to seek something. [93]

    延年长生不老之道中欲得博物精义入妙之大儒下欲得勇敢武力扛鼎暴虎横行之壮士今先生年已耆矣似无驻衰之术又无贲育之气岂能究于三坟五典八索九丘钩深致逺穷理尽性乎三者既乏余不敢通八公笑曰吾闻王尊礼贤士吐握不倦茍有一介之善莫不毕至古人贵九九之学飬鸣吠之技诚欲市马骨以致骐骥师郭生以招羣英吾年虽鄙陋不合所求故逺致其身且欲一见王虽使无益亦岂有损何以年老而逆见嫌耶王必见年少则谓之有道皓首则谓之庸叟恐非发石采玉探渊索珠之谓也薄吾老今则少矣言未竟八公皆变为童子年可十四五角髻青丝色如桃花门吏大惊走以白王王闻之足不履跣而迎登思 [94]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [94]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 延年长生不老之道 - Method or way of achieving immortality or eternal life.
    • 博物精义入妙之大儒 - Great scholar who is deeply knowledgeable and has mastered the profound principles of various disciplines.
    • 勇敢武力扛鼎暴虎横行之壮士 - Brave warrior who is strong enough to lift a cauldron and fight tigers barehanded, symbolizing extraordinary physical prowess.
    • 三坟五典八索九丘 - Ancient texts or books, often used to denote comprehensive and authoritative knowledge.
    • 钩深致远穷理尽性 - Explore deeply, reach far, exhaust the principles, and fully understand the nature of things.
    • 九九之学 - Basic arithmetic skills, specifically the multiplication table up to nine.
    • 养鸣吠之技 - Skills of training animals like dogs to bark or birds to sing.
    • 市马骨以致骐骥 - Metaphor for seeking out rare talents or resources.
    • 师郭生以招群英 - Using the example of Guo Sheng to attract many talented individuals.
    • 年虽鄙陋不合所求 - Despite being old and perhaps lacking in certain qualities, one still seeks to contribute.
    • 远致其身 - Come from afar to offer oneself.
    • 发石采玉探渊索珠 - Metaphors for seeking valuable knowledge or treasures.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 延年长生不老 - Common theme in Chinese literature and philosophy, reflecting the pursuit of longevity and immortality.
    • 三坟五典八索九丘 - Ancient texts that are often mentioned in classical Chinese literature, representing comprehensive knowledge.
    • 钩深致远穷理尽性 - Philosophical concepts from Confucian and Daoist traditions, emphasizing deep understanding and mastery.
    • 王尊礼贤士吐握不倦 - Reference to King W's virtue of respecting and valuing talented scholars, a common theme in Chinese historical and literary works.
    • 九九之学 - Basic arithmetic skills, reflecting the importance of education and practical knowledge.
    • 养鸣吠之技 - Training animals, which is a practical skill often mentioned in classical texts.
    • 市马骨以致骐骥 - Metaphorical reference to seeking out rare talents, similar to the story of King Mu of Qin.
    • 师郭生以招群英 - Reference to using the example of a respected figure to attract talented individuals, reflecting the importance of role models in Chinese culture.
    • 年虽鄙陋不合所求 - Reflects the humility and determination of an older person to contribute despite perceived limitations.

    Proper Nouns

    • 八公 - The eight sages or scholars, a common reference in classical Chinese literature.
    • 王 - Refers to King W, a specific historical or legendary figure.
    • 郭生 - A respected figure used as an example to attract talent.

    In the pursuit of immortality and eternal life, one seeks a great scholar who is deeply knowledgeable and has mastered the profound principles of various disciplines, and also a brave warrior who is strong enough to lift a cauldron and fight tigers barehanded. But now, Master, you are already advanced in age, seemingly lacking the means to halt the decline of your body, and without the strength and courage of Bi and Yu. How can you delve into the depths of the Three Tombs, Five Classics, Eight Records, and Nine Histories, exploring deeply, reaching far, exhausting the principles, and fully understanding the nature of things? If these three qualities are lacking, I dare not proceed further. The Eight Sages laughed and said, "I have heard that King W respects and values talented scholars, never tiring of welcoming them. If there is even a small virtue, none fails to arrive. Ancient people valued basic arithmetic skills and the training of animals like dogs to bark or birds to sing. Truly, they sought out rare talents, just as King Mu of Qin sought out the bones of a horse to attract a thousand-mile steed. They used the example of Guo Sheng to attract many talented individuals. Though I am old and perhaps lacking in certain qualities, I still seek to contribute. I have come from afar to offer myself, and I wish to meet King W. Even if it is of no benefit, how could it be harmful? Why should age be a reason to be seen as undesirable? If King W sees me as young, he will consider me wise; if he sees me as old, he will consider me a fool. I fear this is not the way to seek valuable knowledge or treasures. Despite my age, I am still young. Before I finished speaking, the Eight Sages all transformed into children, about fourteen or fifteen years old, with braided hair, black hair, and faces as rosy as peach blossoms. The gatekeeper was greatly surprised and ran to inform King W. Upon hearing this, King W did not wear shoes and rushed to welcome them, ascending to the throne. [94]

    隂长生
    　　隂长生者新野人也汉皇后之亲属少生富贵之门而不好荣贵唯専务道术闻马鸣生得度世之道乃寻求之遂得相见便执奴仆之役亲运履之劳鸣生不教以度世之法但日夕别与之髙谈论当世之事治农田之业如此十余年长生不懈同时共事鸣生者十二人皆悉归去唯长生执礼弥肃鸣生告之曰子真能得道矣乃将入青城山中煮黄土为金以示之立坛西面乃以太清神丹经授之鸣生别去长生乃归合之丹成服半剂不尽即升天乃大作黄金十数万斤以布恵天下贫乏不问识与不识者周行天下与妻子相随一门皆夀而不老在民间三百余年后于平都山东白日升天而去著书九篇云上古仙者多矣不可 [99]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [99]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 少生富贵之门而不好荣贵 - Classical expression meaning 'born into a wealthy family but not desiring honor and wealth,' highlighting the protagonist's unique character.
    • 唯専务道术 - Means 'only devoted to Daoist practices,' emphasizing the character's dedication to spiritual pursuits.
    • 闻马鸣生得度世之道乃寻求之 - Means 'heard that Master Ma Ming had the way to transcend the world and sought him out,' indicating the protagonist's quest for spiritual enlightenment.
    • 遂得相见便执奴仆之役亲运履之劳 - Means 'and upon meeting, he willingly took on the role of a servant, performing menial tasks,' showing the character's humility and dedication.
    • 鸣生不教以度世之法但日夕别与之髙谈论当世之事治农田之业 - Means 'Master Ma Ming did not teach him the way to transcend the world but instead talked with him about current affairs and farming,' indicating the unconventional nature of their relationship.
    • 如此十余年长生不懈 - Means 'for over ten years, Longsheng remained steadfast,' emphasizing the character's persistence.
    • 同时共事鸣生者十二人皆悉归去唯长生执礼弥肃 - Means 'twelve others who served Master Ma Ming all left, but Longsheng remained even more respectful,' highlighting the character's exceptional dedication.
    • 乃将入青城山中煮黄土为金以示之 - Means 'he then took him into Qingcheng Mountain, where he turned clay into gold to show him,' indicating a miraculous demonstration.
    • 立坛西面乃以太清神丹经授之 - Means 'he set up an altar facing west and gave him the *Taiqing Shen Dan Jing* (Great Clarity Divine Elixir Scripture),' showing the transmission of sacred knowledge.
    • 鸣生别去长生乃归合之丹成服半剂不尽即升天 - Means 'Master Ma Ming left, and Longsheng returned home, completed the elixir, and after consuming half of it, ascended to heaven,' indicating the culmination of his spiritual journey.
    • 乃大作黄金十数万斤以布惠天下贫乏不问识与不识者周行天下与妻子相随一门皆夀而不老在民间三百余年后于平都山东白日升天而去著书九篇云上古仙者多矣不可 - Means 'he then created tens of thousands of pounds of gold, distributed it to the poor throughout the land, regardless of whether they knew him or not, traveled the world with his wife and children, and all his family lived long and did not age. After three hundred years, he ascended to heaven during the day in Pingdu Mountain in the east. He wrote nine books, saying that there were many immortals in ancient times, but it is not possible to list them all,' indicating the character's legacy and the mystical nature of his existence.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 阴长生 - The name of the protagonist, a historical figure known for his Daoist practices and ascension to immortality.
    • 新野人 - Refers to the place of origin, Xinye, in Henan Province.
    • 汉皇后之亲属 - Indicates that the protagonist is related to the Han empress, placing him in a high social status.
    • 马鸣生 - The name of the Daoist master, a historical figure known for his spiritual practices and teachings.
    • 度世之道 - Refers to the Daoist concept of transcending the world, achieving immortality.
    • 青城山 - A famous Daoist mountain in Sichuan Province, known for its spiritual significance.
    • 太清神丹经 - The *Taiqing Shen Dan Jing* (Great Clarity Divine Elixir Scripture), a Daoist text on alchemy and immortality.
    • 平都山 - A mountain in Sichuan Province, known for its association with immortals.
    • 著书九篇 - Refers to the nine books written by the protagonist, which are not specified in the text.
    • 上古仙者多矣不可 - Indicates that there were many immortals in ancient times, but it is not possible to list them all, suggesting the vastness and mystery of Daoist lore.

    Proper Nouns

    • 阴长生 - The protagonist's name.
    • 新野 - The place of origin, Xinye, in Henan Province.
    • 汉皇后 - The Han empress, indicating the protagonist's high social status.
    • 马鸣生 - The Daoist master's name.
    • 度世之道 - The Daoist concept of transcending the world, achieving immortality.
    • 青城山 - A famous Daoist mountain in Sichuan Province.
    • 太清神丹经 - The *Taiqing Shen Dan Jing* (Great Clarity Divine Elixir Scripture), a Daoist text on alchemy and immortality.
    • 平都山 - A mountain in Sichuan Province, known for its association with immortals.
    • 著书九篇 - The nine books written by the protagonist.
    • 上古仙者 - Immortals in ancient times.

    Yin Changsheng
    Yin Changsheng, a native of Xinye, was related to the Han empress. Born into a wealthy family, he did not desire honor and wealth but was solely devoted to Daoist practices. Upon hearing that Master Ma Ming had the way to transcend the world, he sought him out and, upon meeting, willingly took on the role of a servant, performing menial tasks. Master Ma Ming did not teach him the way to transcend the world but instead talked with him about current affairs and farming. For over ten years, Changsheng remained steadfast. Twelve others who served Master Ma Ming all left, but Changsheng remained even more respectful. Master Ma Ming told him, "You truly can attain the Dao." He then took him into Qingcheng Mountain, where he turned clay into gold to show him. He set up an altar facing west and gave him the Taiqing Shen Dan Jing (Great Clarity Divine Elixir Scripture). Master Ma Ming left, and Changsheng returned home, completed the elixir, and after consuming half of it, ascended to heaven. He then created tens of thousands of pounds of gold, distributed it to the poor throughout the land, regardless of whether they knew him or not, traveled the world with his wife and children, and all his family lived long and did not age. After three hundred years, he ascended to heaven during the day in Pingdu Mountain in the east. He wrote nine books, saying that there were many immortals in ancient times, but it is not possible to list them all. [99]

    防泥土为俦奔驰索死不肯暂休其二章曰余之圣师体道之真升降变化乔松为隣唯余同学十有二人寒苦求道歴二十年中多怠堕志行不坚痛乎诸子命也自天天不妄授道必归贤身没幽壤何时可还嗟尔将来勤加精研勿为流俗富贵所牵神道一成升彼九天夀同三光何但亿千其三章曰惟余束髪少好道徳弃家随师东西南北委放五浊避世自匿三十余年名山之侧寒不遑衣饥不暇食思不敢归劳不敢息奉事圣师承欢悦色面垢足胝乃见诚实遂受要诀恩深不测妻子延年咸享无极黄白已成货财千亿使役神玉女侍侧今得度世神丹之力隂君处民间百七十年色如女子白日升天而去【出神仙传】 [102]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [102]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 防泥土为俦奔驰索死不肯暂休 - Compound expression describing a person of lowly status who runs tirelessly in pursuit of something, even to the point of death.
    • 唯余同学十有二人寒苦求道历二十年中多怠堕志行不坚 - Describes a group of students who, despite their hardships, have struggled to maintain their resolve and commitment to their path.
    • 痛乎诸子命也自天天不妄授道必归贤身没幽壤何时可还 - Expresses sorrow and the belief that the Dao is only given to the worthy.
    • 嗟尔将来勤加精研勿为流俗富贵所牵神道一成升彼九天寿同三光何但亿千 - Offers advice and encouragement, advising against materialism and emphasizing the ultimate goal of achieving the spiritual path.
    • 惟余束髪少好道德弃家随师东西南北委放五浊避世自匿三十余年名山之侧寒不遑衣饥不暇食思不敢归劳不敢息奉事圣师承欢悦色面垢足胝乃见诚实遂受要诀恩深不测妻子延年咸享无极黄白已成货财千亿使役神玉女侍侧今得度世神丹之力阴君处民间百七十年色如女子白日升天而去 - Describes the author's journey and achievements, including early dedication, nomadic life, seclusion, hardships, and ultimate success in achieving immortality.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 防泥土为俦 - Refers to the lowly status of the person, often used to describe someone of humble origins.
    • 寒苦求道 - Refers to the hardships faced by those seeking the Dao, a central concept in Daoism.
    • 五浊 - Refers to the five turbid worlds, a concept in Buddhism and Daoism that describes the impurities of the world.
    • 名山之侧 - Refers to the practice of seeking spiritual enlightenment in remote, sacred mountains.
    • 黄白已成 - Refers to the alchemical transformation of base metals into gold and silver, a common theme in Daoist texts.
    • 神丹 - Refers to the elixir of immortality, a key element in Daoist practices.
    • 阴君 - Refers to a female deity or immortal, often associated with longevity and spiritual power.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 防泥土为俦奔驰索死不肯暂休 - Can be read as a single, complex sentence describing a person's relentless pursuit despite their lowly status.
    • 唯余同学十有二人寒苦求道历二十年中多怠堕志行不坚 - Can be read as a single, complex sentence describing the struggles of a small group of students over a long period.
    • 痛乎诸子命也自天天不妄授道必归贤身没幽壤何时可还 - Can be read as a single, complex sentence expressing sorrow and the belief that the Dao is only given to the worthy.
    • 嗟尔将来勤加精研勿为流俗富贵所牵神道一成升彼九天寿同三光何但亿千 - Can be read as a single, complex sentence offering advice and encouragement.
    • 惟余束髪少好道德弃家随师东西南北委放五浊避世自匿三十余年名山之侧寒不遑衣饥不暇食思不敢归劳不敢息奉事圣师承欢悦色面垢足胝乃见诚实遂受要诀恩深不测妻子延年咸享无极黄白已成货财千亿使役神玉女侍侧今得度世神丹之力阴君处民间百七十年色如女子白日升天而去 - Can be read as a single, complex sentence describing the author's journey and achievements.

    Translation

    Those who are like mud and dirt, running tirelessly in pursuit of their goals until death, do not rest for a moment. Chapter Two: My sage teacher embodies the true Dao, ascending and descending through transformations, with cypress and pine trees as neighbors. Only I and eleven other students, enduring hardships in seeking the Dao, have struggled over twenty years, many becoming lazy and falling away due to a lack of firm resolve. How pitiful are the fates of these children! From heaven, the Dao is not given lightly; it is only given to the worthy. When will the body return from the underworld? Alas, future generations, diligently study and research, do not be swayed by worldly wealth and status. Once the spiritual path is achieved, ascend to the nine heavens, living as long as the sun, moon, and stars, far beyond a thousand years.

    Chapter Three: Only I, when young, loved morality, leaving home to follow the teacher, traveling east, west, south, and north, abandoning the five turbid worlds to avoid the world and hide. For over thirty years by the side of famous mountains, cold without time to wear clothes, hungry without time to eat, thinking of returning but too tired, unable to rest, serving the sage teacher with joy and pleasure, with a dirty face and calloused feet, finally seeing sincerity, finally receiving the essential teachings, the depth of gratitude is unfathomable. Wife and children live long, enjoying boundless life, alchemical transformations complete, wealth in the billions, servants of ghosts and spirits, jade maidens on the side, now having achieved immortality through the elixir of the gods. The Yin Lord lived in the world for 170 years, appearing like a woman, ascending to heaven by day. [From the Sage Immortal Transmission] [102]

    修复者皆使疾病县有应治桥道于是百姓斩草除溷无所不为皆出其意而愚者不知是陵所造将为此文从天上下也陵又欲以亷耻治人不喜施刑罚乃立条制使有疾病者皆疏记生身以来所犯之辠乃手书投水中与神明共盟约不得复犯法当以身死为约于是百姓计念邂逅疾病辄当首过一则得愈二使羞慙不敢重犯且畏天地而改从此之后所违犯者皆改为善矣陵乃多得财物以市其药合丹丹成服半剂不愿即升天也乃能分形作数十人其所居门前水池陵常乗舟戏其中而诸道士賔客往来盈庭盖座上常有一陵与賔客对谈共食饮而 [104]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [104]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 修复者皆使疾病县有应治桥道 - Elliptical phrase: '修复者' refers to repairers/workers, '皆使' means 'all caused,' '疾病县' could be a proper noun or descriptive phrase, '有应治桥道' means 'bridges and roads to be repaired.' Overall, it means the repairers caused the county to have bridges and roads that needed repair.
    • 百姓斩草除溷无所不为 - Metaphorical expression: '斩草除溷' means 'clearing weeds and removing filth,' symbolizing thorough cleaning or improvement. '无所不为' means 'nothing is left undone,' indicating comprehensive effort.
    • 以亷耻治人 - Means 'to govern people with shame and integrity,' emphasizing moral governance.
    • 乃立条制使有疾病者皆疏记生身以来所犯之辠 - Complex sentence: '立条制' means 'establish rules or regulations,' '疏记' means 'list or record,' '生身以来所犯之辠' means 'sins committed since birth.' Overall, it means the rules required people to list all their sins since birth.
    • 乃手书投水中与神明共盟约 - Complex sentence: '手书' means 'written by hand,' '投水中' means 'thrown into the water,' '与神明共盟约' means 'make an oath with the divine.' Overall, it means they wrote their oaths by hand and threw them into the water as a pact with the divine.
    • 陵乃多得财物以市其药合丹 - Complex sentence: '多得财物' means 'obtained many riches,' '以市其药' means 'to buy his medicine,' '合丹' means 'to concoct elixirs.' Overall, it means he obtained many riches to buy his medicine and concoct elixirs.
    • 陵常乗舟戏其中 - Simple sentence: '乗舟' means 'ride a boat,' '戏' means 'play,' '其中' means 'in it.' Overall, it means he often rode a boat and played in the water pool in front of his residence.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 陵 - Likely a proper noun, possibly referring to a historical figure or person of significance. Without additional context, it is difficult to determine the exact identity.
    • 道士賔客 - '道士' refers to Taoist priests or practitioners, '賔客' refers to guests or visitors. Indicates the presence of Taoist priests and guests at the residence.
    • 盖座上常有一陵与賔客对谈共食饮 - Suggests a supernatural or magical element, implying that there was always one '陵' sitting with the guests, engaging in conversation and sharing meals. Could be a reference to a spiritual or mystical presence.

    Proper Nouns

    • 陵 - Proper noun, likely referring to a historical figure or person of significance.
    • 道士 - Proper noun, referring to Taoist priests or practitioners.
    • 賔客 - Proper noun, referring to guests or visitors.

    The repairers caused the county to have bridges and roads that needed repair. The common people cleared weeds and removed filth, leaving nothing undone, all according to the will of the county, while the foolish did not understand that these repairs were made by the Lord to prepare for the descent of heavenly writings. The Lord also wished to govern people with shame and integrity, disliking the use of punishment. He therefore established rules requiring those with illnesses to list all the sins they had committed since birth, and they wrote these lists by hand and threw them into the water as an oath with the divine, promising not to commit further crimes and to die if they did. Thus, the common people thought that if they ever fell ill, they should confess their sins, for this would lead to healing and also make them feel ashamed and unwilling to repeat their offenses, and they would fear heaven and earth and reform. From then on, all those who violated the laws and regulations reformed and became good. The Lord obtained many riches to buy his medicine and concoct elixirs. After the elixirs were completed, he took half a dose but did not wish to ascend to heaven immediately. Instead, he could divide his form into dozens of people. In front of his residence, there was a water pool where he often rode a boat and played. Taoist priests and guests came and went, filling the courtyard, and there was always one Lord sitting with the guests, engaging in conversation and sharing meals. [104]

    修复者皆使疾病县有应治桥道于是百姓斩草除溷无所不为皆出其意而愚者不知是陵所造将为此文从天上下也陵又欲以亷耻治人不喜施刑罚乃立条制使有疾病者皆疏记生身以来所犯之辠乃手书投水中与神明共盟约不得复犯法当以身死为约于是百姓计念邂逅疾病辄当首过一则得愈二使羞慙不敢重犯且畏天地而改从此之后所违犯者皆改为善矣陵乃多得财物以市其药合丹丹成服半剂不愿即升天也乃能分形作数十人其所居门前水池陵常乗舟戏其中而诸道士賔客往来盈庭盖座上常有一陵与賔客对谈共食饮而

    The repairers caused the county to have bridges and roads that needed repair. The common people cleared weeds and removed filth, leaving nothing undone, all according to the will of the county, while the foolish did not understand that these repairs were made by the Lord to prepare for the descent of heavenly writings. The Lord also wished to govern people with shame and integrity, disliking the use of punishment. He therefore established rules requiring those with illnesses to list all the sins they had committed since birth, and they wrote these lists by hand and threw them into the water as an oath with the divine, promising not to commit further crimes and to die if they did. Thus, the common people thought that if they ever fell ill, they should confess their sins, for this would lead to healing and also make them feel ashamed and unwilling to repeat their offenses, and they would fear heaven and earth and reform. From then on, all those who violated the laws and regulations reformed and became good. The Lord obtained many riches to buy his medicine and concoct elixirs. After the elixirs were completed, he took half a dose but did not wish to ascend to heaven immediately. Instead, he could divide his form into dozens of people. In front of his residence, there was a water pool where he often rode a boat and played. Taoist priests and guests came and went, filling the courtyard, and there was always one Lord sitting with the guests, engaging in conversation and sharing meals.
